Source llm title_keyword title_manager unknown kaggle_arshkon 4,687 1 0 3 kaggle_asaniczka 18,124 1 0 4 scraped 25,816 8,174 1 11,326
  • Kaggle seniority is essentially LLM-only (99.97%+) — because Kaggle SWE rows almost all route to the LLM, and the LLM overrides Stage 5.
  • Scraped seniority is a 3-way mix: LLM (57%), rule-based title_keyword (18%), unknown (25%). The 25% unknown share on scraped means one in four scraped SWE rows has no seniority label at all — they are not_selected rows whose titles lacked strong keywords.

docs.title: Consequence for cross-source entry-level comparisons
docs.text:
Arshkon SWE entry-level rates under different operationalizations:

Operationalization Entry count Entry share seniority_native = 'entry' (arshkon SWE LinkedIn default) 738 / 4,691 15.7% seniority_final = 'entry' 177 / 4,691 3.8% seniority_final = 'entry' OR 'associate' 192 / 4,691 4.1%

The LLM collapses 618 of arshkon's 738 seniority_native='entry' SWE rows to 'unknown'. This is not a bug — the LLM looks for explicit seniority signals in the description text while LinkedIn's own "Entry level" platform label often reflects YOE thresholds that the LLM is told to ignore. But it means the choice between seniority_native and seniority_final will determine whether 2024 "entry" on arshkon is 3.8% or 15.7% of SWE. T30 will enumerate this; the fact that the two columns disagree by a factor of 4× is itself a critical research finding.

docs.title: 6. Key constraint mapping for Wave 2–3 analyses
docs.text: Planned analysis category Binding constraint Severity Implication Text / topic modeling / embeddings (T09, T12, T13, T15) description_core_llm is 55–60% on scraped SWE, near-100% on Kaggle Moderate Restrict to llm_extraction_coverage='labeled' (48,452 SWE rows). Density/topic comparisons use this subset. Do not backfill with raw text for density metrics. Seniority stratification (all RQ1) seniority_final vs seniority_native disagree 4×; ~25% of scraped SWE have unknown seniority Critical Must use T30's ablation panel. Do not report an entry-level number without the J1/J2/J3/J4 4-row table. seniority_final_source is load-bearing context. Cross-period temporal granularity (T19) Asaniczka is a 6-day window; arshkon 16-day; scraped 30-day growing Moderate Aggregate to source-level or period-level comparisons. Sub-monthly scraped slicing is fine but cannot be compared to sub-monthly Kaggle slicing. Geography (T17) metro_area 60-80% per source (both multi_location drops and geo-parse gaps). search_metro_name only populated for scraped and asaniczka Moderate Use metro_area as primary; drop null-metro rows from per-metro rollups (they are explicitly "multi-location" representatives, not missing data). Company industry / size (T06, T16) company_industry: 0% asaniczka; company_size: 0% asaniczka & 0% scraped Critical Industry analyses are two-period (arshkon vs scraped only, no asaniczka). Company-size analyses are effectively arshkon-only — no 2026 comparison available. YOE (T07, T11, T20) yoe_min_years_llm LLM-frame-restricted (55% scraped, near-100% Kaggle) Moderate Stick to labeled rows; use yoe_extracted as ablation only, not primary. Skills / structured skills (T11, T14) skills_raw populated only on asaniczka; asaniczka_skills same Critical Skills comparisons cross-source are impossible — must use text-extracted skill matrices (T14, Prep artifact swe_tech_matrix.parquet). Remote work (T17) is_remote 0% on 2024 sources Critical Cannot interpret remote-work trends. Annotate as known data artifact; flag as RQ-ineligible. Aggregator sensitivity Aggregator share varies by source (9% arshkon, 18% asaniczka, 17% scraped) Moderate Most analyses should also report results excluding is_aggregator=True as a sensitivity. Coverage of LLM labels is balanced across aggregator status. Date-based selection on scraped LinkedIn date_posted 1% populated Moderate Use scrape_date / period / scrape_week only.

docs.title: 7. Aggregator sensitivity (exploration/tables/T01/aggregator_sensitivity.csv)
docs.text:
Key cross-stratifications from the SWE LinkedIn default subset:

  • Scraped aggregator rows: 7,708 total. description_core_llm labeled on 4,383 (56.9%). yoe_min_years_llm labeled on 3,037 (39.4%). LLM text coverage is similar between aggregator and non-aggregator.
  • Arshkon aggregator rows: 430 total, all fully LLM-covered. seniority_final='llm' source for 100% — strong confidence but very small N.
  • Asaniczka aggregator rows: 2,943 total, company_industry still 0% (asaniczka-wide hole), otherwise strong LLM coverage.

Aggregator composition is not an LLM-coverage differential, so the recommendation is to keep aggregators in the main analyses and report is_aggregator=False-only sensitivities. Beware source-level aggregator share differences when pooling (9% arshkon vs 17–18% asaniczka/scraped).

docs.title: 8. Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. seniority_final = 'entry' count on arshkon SWE is only 177 (3.8% of arshkon SWE), vs 738 (15.7%) under seniority_native = 'entry'. The LLM actively removes LinkedIn's own entry labels that are not backed by explicit description-text seniority cues. Whether this is "real" or "noise depends on your operationalization" is itself the key question for the project. The T30 panel is load-bearing.

  2. Asaniczka seniority_native has only mid-senior and associate — not even nulls. This is stricter than the documented "no entry labels" — there's literally no intern, director, executive, or unlabeled row in asaniczka's source data. Either asaniczka's scraper filtered these out upfront or LinkedIn's 2024 feed returned only these two. Implication for T02: since asaniczka has no null seniority either, the associate label is not "the entry-level bucket dumped into associate" but rather a substantive LinkedIn label.

  3. Scraped SWE has 19,500 rows not_selected for LLM — about 43% of scraped SWE cannot use description_core_llm or any LLM-derived column. This is ~40% of the growing-period data, significant for Wave 2+ text analyses. Expanding the LLM budget or admitting supplemental cache is the only way to raise this.

  4. search_metro_name populated for asaniczka (95%) is a mild surprise — asaniczka is documented as "historical snapshot" but the pipeline reconstructed approximate metros. Note asaniczka's top search metros are dominated by ambiguous small cities (Alexandria, Santa Clara) whose actual metros overlap DC, Bay Area — treat with care; the normalized metro_area field (LinkedIn 26-metro frame) is the correct one for geographic analyses.

  5. Scraped Indeed has date_posted from 2016-10-17 to 2026-04-18. This is a 9.5-year span. Indeed is sensitivity-only per project rules but still: the raw date_posted on scraped Indeed is not "time of posting from an active 2026 feed" — these are older postings that the aggregator surfaces with their original date. Treat all temporal claims on scraped Indeed with extreme caution; the scrape_date is the only anchor of current presence.

  6. work_type has different semantic fill pattern than company_industry — work_type is 100%/100% on arshkon/scraped but 0% on asaniczka, while company_industry is 99%/99.96% arshkon/scraped but 0% on asaniczka. Two different asaniczka gaps, same pattern. Asaniczka is systematically sparse in employer-metadata side-tables despite 892K rows — an architectural data shape, not random missingness.

  7. LLM-rule SWE classification disagreement is substantial. On rows where both are populated, 1,472 scraped rows with is_swe=True are NOT_SWE per LLM; 2,604 with is_swe=False are SWE per LLM. ~4K disagreements out of ~52K LLM-labeled LinkedIn rows (7.8% gross disagreement). This is relevant for T04 (SWE classification audit) — the is_swe rule and the LLM classification are not interchangeable, and the LLM tends to be more restrictive on some tiers. Worth including an is_swe AND swe_classification_llm='SWE' intersected sensitivity in key RQ1 findings.

docs.title: 9. Data quality caveats (for downstream agents)
docs.text:
• Do not combine seniority_native and seniority_final results within a single claim. They are different operationalizations with different effective samples.
  • For text-sensitive analyses, restrict to llm_extraction_coverage='labeled' and carry this filter through the report. Denominators differ between text and non-text analyses.
  • selected_for_llm_frame=True marks the sticky balanced core, which is the only subset where cross-source balance was enforced. Supplemental cache rows exist but should not be pooled with core rows for balanced claims.
  • Scraped LinkedIn temporal unit is scrape_date / scrape_week, not date_posted. Do not try to use date_posted for scraped-LinkedIn cohort building.
  • Source-level aggregator share varies from 9% to 18%. Include is_aggregator=False-only sensitivities for any employer/company-composition claim.
  • Effective entry-level sample sizes are small: 177 arshkon / 238 asaniczka / 1,650 scraped-2026-03 / 803 + 847 scraped-2026-04 (wait: scraped 2026-04 has 803 — let me re-check — actually scraped has 847 in 2026-03 and 803 in 2026-04, both under seniority_final='entry'). With only ~1,650 entry-level rows across the two scraped months and 177 + 238 = 415 across Kaggle, power for fine-grained entry-level cuts (by metro, by company, by archetype) will be tight.

docs.title: 10. Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
1. T02 (this agent, next): Apply the associate-as-junior test. Given finding 1 above, the T02 test needs to compare asaniczka associate against arshkon seniority_final='entry' (not just seniority_native='entry') — because that's what downstream Wave 2 work will use.

  2. T03 / T04 (Agent B): The 4× discrepancy between seniority_native and seniority_final on arshkon entry rows is the central audit finding — T03 should quantify where the LLM re-routes entry labels and whether that re-routing is stable across title types. T04 should report is_swe/LLM-SWE disagreement by tier.

  3. T06 / T16 (Agents C, J): Employer/company analyses cannot use company_industry for asaniczka; must rely on title-derived proxies or limit to arshkon-vs-scraped. company_size is arshkon-only — no 2024→2026 size comparison exists.

  4. T11 / T14 / T35 (Agents G, I, S): Skills analyses must use text-extracted tech matrices; skills_raw is not a cross-source feature. Asaniczka's structured skills should be a separate sensitivity, not a primary.

  5. T17 (Agent J): Remote work trend cannot be computed — annotate as RQ-ineligible.

  6. T19 (Agent K): Cannot do sub-week temporal slicing on asaniczka (6-day window) or arshkon (16-day). Cross-period rate-of-change should use source-period buckets, not fine time bins.

  7. T30 (Agent B): The T30 panel is critical. T01's finding that arshkon SWE entry drops from 15.7% → 3.8% under the switch from seniority_native to seniority_final means every entry-level claim must be reported in the 4-row T30 panel. The primary should be J3 (yoe_min_years_llm ≤ 2) because it's genuinely label-independent and robust to the seniority_native/seniority_final disagreement.

docs.title: 11. Essential sensitivities
docs.text:
• Aggregator-excluded re-run of key Wave 2 claims, given source-level aggregator share varies 2× between arshkon and asaniczka/scraped.
  • LLM-labeled-only subset for any density/frequency metric using text.
  • Core-frame only (selected_for_llm_frame=True) for balanced cross-source comparison; supplemental cache is a robustness check only.
  • Arshkon-only baseline for any seniority_native-dependent analysis (asaniczka has no entry labels).

Output files:

  • exploration/tables/T01/row_counts.csv — authoritative counts
  • exploration/tables/T01/row_counts_aggregator.csv — aggregator split
  • exploration/tables/T01/period_ranges.csv — date range per source/period
  • exploration/tables/T01/column_coverage.csv — full non-null & distinct table
  • exploration/tables/T01/column_coverage_flagged.csv — 21 flagged columns
  • exploration/tables/T01/llm_extraction_coverage.csv, llm_extraction_coverage_swe.csv
  • exploration/tables/T01/llm_classification_coverage.csv, llm_classification_coverage_swe.csv
  • exploration/tables/T01/semantic_*.csv — semantic-differences tables (company_industry, seniority_native, work_type, skills_raw, date_posted, is_remote)
  • exploration/tables/T01/aggregator_sensitivity.csv
  • exploration/figures/T01/coverage_heatmap_all.png, coverage_heatmap_swe.png, coverage_heatmap_swe_linkedin.png
  • exploration/scripts/T01_data_profile_coverage.py — reusable script

docs.title: T02. Asaniczka associate as a junior proxy
docs.text: Agent: A, Wave 1 Date: 2026-04-20 Input: data/unified.parquet; subset is_swe AND source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english AND date_flag='ok' Output tables/figures: exploration/tables/T02/*, exploration/figures/T02/* Script: exploration/scripts/T02_asaniczka_associate_proxy.py

docs.title: Narrow question
docs.text:
Asaniczka has zero native entry-level labels in seniority_native (only mid-senior and associate). Can its 101,093 LinkedIn associate-labeled rows (1,820 SWE LinkedIn default) be used as a junior proxy in analyses that need a 2024 baseline and cannot pool with arshkon?

Verdict: NOT USABLE as a pooled entry proxy. Asaniczka associate fails the "at least 3 of 4 signals" decision rule — it matches arshkon entry on only 2 of 4 signals (top-title Jaccard and YOE distribution). It fails on explicit title cues (senior-cue rate too high) and on LLM re-read of descriptions (35% become mid-senior, far above arshkon-entry's 9%). The label has a broad seniority span that leans toward mid-senior.

Downstream: do not pool asaniczka associate into a 2024 entry bucket. Use T30's J2 (combined entry+associate under seniority_final) or one of the YOE variants (J3: yoe_min_years_llm ≤ 2; J4: yoe_min_years_llm ≤ 3) instead.

docs.title: Comparability table — 4 signals
docs.text:
All counts from is_swe AND source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english AND date_flag='ok'. Denominator for LLM-based signals is llm_classification_coverage='labeled' rows.

Signal arshkon entry (n=738) arshkon associate (n=263) arshkon mid-senior (n=2,231) asaniczka associate (n=1,820) asaniczka directionally like arshkon entry? (1) Top-50 title Jaccard vs asaniczka-associate 0.429 0.282 0.408 — Yes (highest Jaccard of the three arshkon groups) (2a) Junior-cue title rate 0.95% 0.00% 0.22% 0.66% Mixed — closer to entry than mid-senior, but below entry (2b) Senior-cue title rate 0.81% 1.14% 2.91% 1.65% No — senior rate is higher than arshkon-associate and halfway to mid-senior (3) LLM YOE — mean (of rows with YOE) 4.21 3.90 5.92 4.44 Yes — closer to entry than mid-senior (3b) LLM YOE — frac ≤ 2 years 27.8% 29.3% 6.0% 21.2% Yes — closer to entry (27.8%) than mid-senior (6.0%) (3c) LLM YOE — frac ≥ 5 years 44.9% 34.3% 75.2% 49.5% Mixed — slightly MORE senior than arshkon-entry (4) LLM re-read → seniority_final 7% entry, 9% mid-senior, 84% unknown/other 8% entry, 21% mid-senior, 70% unknown/other 1% entry, 61% mid-senior, 37% unknown/other 4% entry, 35% mid-senior, 59% unknown/other No — the LLM sends 35% of asaniczka-associate descriptions to mid-senior (4× the arshkon-entry rate)

Signal-by-signal verdict (strict reading):

  • (1) Top-title Jaccard: like entry ✅
  • (2) Title cues: not decisively like entry — junior rate is between arshkon-entry and arshkon-mid-senior, and the senior-cue rate is nearly double arshkon-associate's. Cannot count this as a clean match ❌
  • (3) YOE distribution: like entry ✅ (mean, frac ≤2, frac ≤3 all closer to arshkon-entry than arshkon-mid-senior)
  • (4) LLM re-read: not like entry — asaniczka-associate descriptions get mid-senior 35% of the time under LLM re-read; arshkon-entry gets mid-senior 9% ❌

2 of 4 signals match. Decision rule (≥3 of 4) fails. Verdict: not usable as a pooled junior proxy.

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
Asaniczka's associate label is a broad LinkedIn platform label that includes meaningful mid-level representation, not a "junior only" bucket. The YOE distribution (mean 4.44, median 4) and the LLM re-read (35% mid-senior) both point to a label universe that spans roughly 2–7 years YOE — somewhere between arshkon-entry (mean 4.21) and arshkon-mid-senior (mean 5.92), but leaning senior.

Importantly, asaniczka associate is also not equivalent to arshkon associate — the arshkon associate label has a lower mean YOE (3.90 vs 4.44) and a lower senior-tail share (≥5 = 34.3% vs 49.5%). So even restricting to "label-matched associate" does not give a consistent 2024 2024 operationalization across the two Kaggle snapshots.

Under the seniority_final schema, asaniczka still has 277 entry + 39 associate SWE rows in the LLM-labeled default frame. That's an n for entry-level of 277 in asaniczka and 177 + 15 = 192 in arshkon and 1,650 + 190 = 1,840 in scraped. The LLM does the entry-labeling work on asaniczka; the native label is just too coarse to substitute.

docs.title: Entry-level effective sample sizes (under seniority_final)
docs.text:
By source × seniority_final × seniority_final_source (SWE LinkedIn default):

Source seniority_final seniority_final_source n kaggle_arshkon entry llm 177 kaggle_arshkon associate llm 15 kaggle_asaniczka entry llm 238 kaggle_asaniczka associate llm 39 scraped entry llm 1,203 scraped entry title_keyword 447 scraped associate llm 121

Observations:

  1. Every 2024 entry-level row comes from the LLM. There are essentially zero title_keyword-sourced entry labels in Kaggle SWE — this is because Kaggle titles rarely carry the word "junior"/"entry"; the LLM is doing 100% of the entry identification. Scraped is different: 447 of 1,650 scraped entry rows (27%) come from title_keyword, reflecting more explicit junior titling in 2026 postings.

  2. Associate counts are tiny. arshkon associate n=15, asaniczka associate n=39. Neither is usable as a standalone cohort; combining entry+associate (T30 J2) is essential.

  3. J2 (entry+associate) effective sample sizes: arshkon 192, asaniczka 277, scraped 2026-03 = 874 (847+27; from T01 data), scraped 2026-04 = 872 (803+69). J3/J4 (YOE-based) will typically have larger N because they don't require the LLM to find an explicit label.

  4. Rule-based title_keyword entry-only effective sample size (scraped-only): 447 — the T30 J5 ablation "rule-only entry" is feasible for 2026 cross-checks but not for 2024 (both Kaggle sources have essentially zero title_keyword entry).

docs.title: Why the title cue signals are weak on Kaggle
docs.text:
The extreme sparsity of junior/senior title cues in Kaggle-source data (0.22% junior in arshkon-mid-senior, 0.95% in arshkon-entry) was itself a surprise. Looking at the top titles:

  • arshkon seniority_native='entry' top 15: "software engineer" (52), "data engineer" (24), "developer" (23), "systems engineer" (19), "java developer" (18). Almost no explicit "junior" or "entry-level" in title.
  • arshkon seniority_native='mid-senior' top 15: "software engineer" (244), "data engineer" (82), "devops engineer" (53). Also no explicit "senior" in many titles.

Kaggle snapshots use LinkedIn's platform-level seniority label (applied by employer selection in the posting flow), but titles are generic. This is different from explicit-title conventions in tech industry (Google, Amazon, etc.) and may reflect either smaller employers or different employer norms in January 2024. In scraped 2026 data, explicit-title share is much higher (e.g., seniority_native='entry' SWE in scraped has 226 junior-cue hits out of 5,247 = 4.3%, vs 0.95% in arshkon 2024).

Implication for Wave 2+: title-based seniority heuristics are much more useful in 2026 scraped data than in 2024 Kaggle data, which could confound period-comparison findings. Be skeptical of any "explicit junior title % rose between 2024 and 2026" claim — it may be a source-selection artifact rather than a real market signal.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Asaniczka associate is MORE senior-skewed than arshkon associate on YOE (≥5 share 49.5% vs 34.3%). This was unexpected — the expectation was that the two sources' associate labels would be comparable. They are not. Asaniczka's associate is a broader bucket spanning mid-level.

  2. 35% of asaniczka associate SWE descriptions are labeled mid-senior by the LLM, vs 21% of arshkon associate. Either asaniczka's platform labels are noisier in the junior→mid boundary or asaniczka's descriptions use more senior-signaling language in practice.

  3. Kaggle SWE titles are surprisingly generic. Only 0.8% of arshkon seniority_native='mid-senior' SWE titles contain senior / lead / etc. This is much lower than expected and much lower than 2026 scraped data. Either the Kaggle snapshots are drawing from a different employer universe (smaller companies?) or employers in 2024 labeled "mid-senior" without using senior words in the title. A direct check of same-title × same-source across 2024 → 2026 in Wave 3 would help disambiguate.

  4. Top-title Jaccard is low across the board. Even within-source same-seniority groups have Jaccard ~0.4–0.5; cross-source or cross-seniority drops to 0.28–0.43. The SWE title universe is fragmented — there is no dominant "top 50" that both sources agree on. This has implications for same-title cohort designs (T31): truly overlapping same-title cohorts may be small.

  5. Arshkon associate → seniority_final='entry' at 8%, which is higher than arshkon entry → seniority_final='entry' at 7%. In other words, the LLM re-reads arshkon descriptions and produces similar entry rates regardless of whether the native label was entry or associate. The native entry label is thus only weakly informative relative to what the description says — another argument against over-weighting seniority_native in cross-period comparisons.

  6. Asaniczka associate senior-cue rate (1.65%) is above arshkon-associate (1.14%) and about half of arshkon mid-senior (2.91%). This is consistent with asaniczka-associate being a broader bucket than arshkon-associate.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• All LLM-based signals are restricted to llm_classification_coverage='labeled' — deferred / not_selected rows are out-of-frame. For arshkon and asaniczka this is 99%+ of SWE, so the bias is minimal, but it does exclude a tiny fraction.
  • Top-title Jaccard uses top-50 by count. Different top-k choices would shift the values slightly but should not change the rank-order conclusion.
  • "Entry"-rate fractions should be interpreted as rates of LLM matching an explicit description signal, not actual entry-level prevalence. These are label-independent to the extent that they use raw text input to the LLM.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
1. T30 (Agent B) should carry a footnote that asaniczka associate is not a junior proxy, and compute every entry-level claim via the 4-row panel (J1/J2/J3/J4), treating J3 (yoe_min_years_llm ≤ 2 within the LLM frame) as the primary because it avoids the seniority_native asymmetry altogether.

  2. T19 / any cross-period junior trend (Wave 3) should not frame "asaniczka associate" as a proxy for 2024 junior. Either:

  3. Use arshkon-only 2024 baseline (sacrificing n), or
  4. Pool 2024 via yoe_min_years_llm ≤ 2 on all LLM-labeled 2024 rows (robust; recommended), or

  5. Use seniority_final='entry' pooled across arshkon+asaniczka (n=415, reasonable).

  6. T31 (Agent Q) (same-company × same-title drift) should be aware the SWE title distribution has low within-source Jaccard (~0.4–0.5 at top-50), so same-title cohorts will be small.

  7. All entry-level findings in Wave 2+ must report the T30 panel. Because 2 of 4 asaniczka-proxy signals agree and 2 disagree, any single-operationalization claim is fragile and could flip. The panel protects against that.

  8. Wave 3 should investigate why Kaggle SWE titles are generic (0.22% senior-cue in mid-senior) vs scraped 2026 (13% senior-cue in mid-senior, per cross-check of earlier data). This is a potential confound for "scope-inflation" claims that depend on title-level seniority reading.

docs.title: Essential sensitivities already in the tables
docs.text:
• Rule-based YOE distribution (yoe_rule_distribution.csv) mirrors the LLM YOE distribution in shape, confirming the LLM isn't distorting the finding. Both extractors put asaniczka-associate (mean 4.44 / 4.51) between arshkon-entry (mean 4.21 / 4.18) and arshkon-mid-senior (mean 5.92 / 5.94). The LLM is systematically slightly higher on YOE mean for all cells but the relative ordering is identical.
  • Scraped-source comparisons are out of scope for this narrow T02 test but included in T01's summary; the same question for scraped seniority_native='associate' is best answered by T30's panel rather than a standalone proxy test.

docs.title: Output files
docs.text:
• exploration/tables/T02/top_titles_by_group.csv — top-50 titles per (source, seniority_native) cell
  • exploration/tables/T02/top_titles_jaccard.csv — 5×5 Jaccard matrix
  • exploration/tables/T02/title_cue_rates.csv — junior/senior title-cue rates per cell
  • exploration/tables/T02/yoe_llm_distribution.csv — YOE LLM distribution (primary)
  • exploration/tables/T02/yoe_rule_distribution.csv — YOE rule distribution (ablation)
  • exploration/tables/T02/seniority_final_vs_native.csv — LLM re-read conditional distribution
  • exploration/tables/T02/entry_effective_sample_size.csv — entry/associate effective n by seniority_final_source
  • exploration/figures/T02/top_titles_jaccard_heatmap.png
  • exploration/figures/T02/title_cue_rates.png
  • exploration/figures/T02/yoe_llm_cdf.png
  • exploration/scripts/T02_asaniczka_associate_proxy.py — reusable script (with inline regex assertions)

docs.title: T03. Seniority Label Audit (seniority_final)
docs.text: Run date: 2026-04-20 Owner: Agent B, Wave 1 Input: data/unified.parquet Default filter: source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok' Sample: is_swe = True SWE rows under the default filter. Script: exploration/scripts/T03_seniority_label_audit.py Tables: exploration/tables/T03/

docs.title: Headline
docs.text: seniority_final is defensible as the primary seniority column, with three caveats. The LLM half is well-calibrated where it commits (97.9% of rows the LLM labels mid-senior agree with the LinkedIn native label in scraped; 97.6% in arshkon), and where the rule fires the LLM almost never overrides it. The dominant failure mode is abstention, not mislabeling: the LLM returns 'unknown' on 34-53% of the rows it was called on, producing a very large residual unknown pool — especially in arshkon (52.6%) and scraped (47.2%). Kappa against native drops to 0.12-0.18 when unknown is included, but rises to 0.66 (scraped) and 0.45 (arshkon) on the conditional distribution where the LLM did assign a label. Downstream consumers should always report what fraction of their sample falls into unknown, and T30 should prefer YOE-based (J3/S4) operationalizations for any finding that depends on entry-level or senior shares.

docs.title: 1.1 By source (SWE, default filter)
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T03/source_profile_by_source.csv

source llm title_keyword title_manager unknown total kaggle_arshkon 4,687 1 0 3 4,691 kaggle_asaniczka 18,124 1 0 4 18,129 scraped 25,816 8,174 1 11,326 45,317

Interpretation. Kaggle sources (arshkon, asaniczka) are overwhelmingly LLM-resolved for the SWE subset because every selected row was routed to the LLM and the LLM subsequently overwrites title_keyword values it is equally confident about. Scraped LinkedIn has a very different composition: only 57% are LLM-resolved; 18% retain the Stage 5 title_keyword value (i.e., not_selected from the LLM frame); 25% are genuinely unknown. The distribution asymmetry is a direct consequence of Stage 9/10 frame selection (selected_for_llm_frame) — essentially the whole SWE Kaggle sample is in the frame, but scraped has a large not_selected SWE pool.

docs.title: 1.2 By source × period
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T03/source_profile_by_source_period.csv

source period llm title_keyword unknown kaggle_arshkon 2024-04 4,687 1 3 kaggle_asaniczka 2024-01 18,124 1 4 scraped 2026-03 11,808 3,329 4,603 scraped 2026-04 14,008 4,845 6,723

Scraped 2026-03 and 2026-04 have stable source-composition shares (~61%/18%/25%), so the seniority_final_source mix is not drifting within the scraped window.

docs.title: 1.3 By aggregator
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T03/source_profile_by_source_aggregator.csv

Aggregator rows (is_aggregator=True) behave like non-aggregator rows within each source. Arshkon aggregator SWE (n=430) is 100% LLM; scraped aggregator SWE (7,708) is 57% LLM / 12% title_keyword / 30% unknown — very close to the non-aggregator share (56% / 19% / 24%). Aggregator-specific label quality does not appear to be a failure mode of seniority_final_source itself (agreement numbers below show the finer story).

docs.title: 2. Rule vs LLM internal agreement
docs.text: Scope: rows where Stage 5 rule fired (seniority_rule ∈ {entry, associate, mid-senior, director}) AND the LLM was called (seniority_final_source = 'llm'). These are exactly the rows where both signals are available, so we can audit how often the LLM overrides a high-confidence rule answer.

docs.title: 2.1 Kappa by source
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T03/rule_vs_llm_kappa_by_source.csv

source n kappa_rule_vs_llm observed_agreement expected_agreement kaggle_arshkon 1,777 0.805 0.972 0.856 kaggle_asaniczka 10,670 0.506 0.969 0.938 scraped 11,367 0.810 0.969 0.836

High kappa in arshkon and scraped; kappa drops in asaniczka because the rule-fires are almost all mid-senior (imbalance inflates expected agreement). Observed agreement is ~97% everywhere.

docs.title: 2.2 Per-class accuracy (rule as reference)
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T03/rule_vs_llm_per_class_accuracy.csv

source seniority_rule n llm_matches_rule share kaggle_arshkon director 12 12 100.0% kaggle_arshkon entry 99 99 100.0% kaggle_arshkon mid-senior 1,666 1,616 97.0% kaggle_asaniczka director 78 44 56.4% kaggle_asaniczka entry 129 129 100.0% kaggle_asaniczka mid-senior 10,463 10,170 97.2% scraped director 173 155 89.6% scraped entry 662 661 99.8% scraped mid-senior 10,532 10,196 96.8%

Notable discrepancy: in asaniczka, 34/78 rule-labeled director rows get reassigned by the LLM to mid-senior. Inspecting the crosstab, the LLM drops ~44% of asaniczka director titles down to mid-senior. In scraped, the same director downgrade is 7% (12/173). I attribute this to asaniczka descriptions being short/harmonized via the companion job_summary.csv: the description may not carry explicit director-level language even when the title does. In arshkon, every Stage 5 director rule is preserved. This is not a seniority_final defect per se (the LLM has more evidence than the title alone), but it means senior/director shares may be mildly deflated in asaniczka relative to arshkon. T30 picks this up as the asaniczka senior-asymmetry test.

Entry-rule preservation: 99.8-100% of Stage 5 entry-level rule fires survive the LLM pass. So any downstream junior-side analysis on rows where Stage 5 fired an entry rule is essentially rule-validated.

docs.title: 3. seniority_final vs seniority_native
docs.text:
Only arshkon and scraped have populated seniority_native. Asaniczka cannot be used for this comparison (zero native entry labels, mostly mid-senior/associate only).

Native labels include intern and executive (absent from the canonical enum). I compute kappa three ways:

  • (a) Raw: native labels kept as-is. Mismatches on intern/executive are unavoidable.
  • (b) Harmonized: intern → entry, executive → director.
  • © Harmonized, excluding seniority_final = 'unknown': agreement conditional on the LLM (or rule) committing.

docs.title: 3.1 Kappa
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T03/native_vs_final_kappa.csv

source n_raw kappa_raw n_harm kappa_harm n_harm_excl_unk kappa_harm_excl_unk observed_harm_excl_unk kaggle_arshkon 3,283 0.171 3,283 0.180 1,643 0.453 0.884 scraped 38,659 0.120 38,659 0.145 18,308 0.658 0.929

Reading. seniority_final agrees poorly with native when we include the large unknown pool (which by construction cannot match any native label except unknown, which native never uses). When we condition on the LLM committing, agreement is 88-93% observed and kappa in "moderate" (arshkon) to "substantial" (scraped) territory. Scraped kappa is higher than arshkon kappa largely because scraped has a much cleaner mid-senior dominance pattern (native mid-senior → final mid-senior = 15,641/15,975 = 97.9% conditional).

docs.title: 3.2 Per-class accuracy excluding unknown (harmonized)
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T03/native_vs_final_per_class_accuracy_excl_unknown.csv

source native (harmonized) n_native_labeled n_match share_match kaggle_arshkon associate 79 4 5.1% kaggle_arshkon director 22 6 27.3% kaggle_arshkon entry (+intern) 140 73 52.1% kaggle_arshkon mid-senior 1,402 1,369 97.6% scraped associate 465 23 4.9% scraped director (+executive) 285 65 22.8% scraped entry (+intern) 1,583 1,285 81.2% scraped mid-senior 15,975 15,641 97.9%

Per-class interpretation. (Note: the reference is LinkedIn's own seniority_native label; "match" here means seniority_final equals harmonized native.)

  • Mid-senior is essentially a solved cell — 98% cell accuracy in both sources.
  • Entry has moderate agreement — 81% in scraped, 52% in arshkon. Most of the arshkon entry disagreement flows to mid-senior (65/140) or unknown elsewhere; the scraped dominant disagreement is entry → mid-senior (256 rows). The scraped-vs-arshkon gap suggests the LinkedIn "entry" native label got stricter (or seniority_final got looser on entry) between 2024-04 and 2026-03. This is a material finding for RQ1 and is picked up again in T30's within-2024 calibration.
  • Associate is mostly reclassified as mid-senior by the LLM (5% cell accuracy either way). This is partly because "associate" LinkedIn natively covers a very wide band (≈ mid-level IC) that the canonical 5-level enum splits differently. If any analysis stratifies on associate-only, expect large labeler disagreement.
  • Director has ~23-27% cell accuracy — the LLM+rule output frequently collapses director down to mid-senior. Combined with the asaniczka director-downgrade finding in Section 2, the director slice is the single weakest cell of seniority_final. S2 (director-only) from T30 should be treated as a low-confidence sensitivity.

docs.title: 3.3 Temporal instability signal
docs.text: Kappa is 0.66 (scraped, 2026) vs 0.45 (arshkon, 2024) harmonized-ex-unknown. Per-class entry accuracy is 81% vs 52%. This is consistent with two non-exclusive stories: (i) LinkedIn tightened its native entry definition between 2024 and 2026, or (ii) seniority_final got looser on entry (LLM trained to be more permissive on entry labels). Either way, native-label drift is a real risk — per the preamble, any entry-level finding must be cross-checked with the YOE-based proxy (T30 J3).

docs.title: 4. Aggregator stratification
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T03/aggregator_stratified_kappa.csv

source is_aggregator n_raw kappa_raw n_harm_excl_unk kappa_harm_excl_unk observed kaggle_arshkon True 406 0.162 165 0.296 0.818 kaggle_arshkon False 2,877 0.170 1,478 0.475 0.891 scraped True 7,378 0.108 2,675 0.633 0.918 scraped False 31,281 0.121 15,633 0.662 0.931

Scraped aggregator vs non-aggregator kappa and observed agreement are within 3 points of each other — the aggregator slice does not materially degrade seniority_final quality in scraped. Arshkon aggregator has lower kappa (0.30 vs 0.48), but the sample is small (n=165) and the dominant driver is associate-labeled aggregator postings. Recommendation: aggregator exclusion should not be mandated as a sensitivity specifically for seniority label quality in scraped; it is worth checking in arshkon-only analyses.

docs.title: 5. Weak-marker title diagnostic (routing-error estimate)
docs.text:
Sample: 100 LLM-labeled SWE rows where the title contains a roman-numeral level marker (II, III, IV, V) or "level/lvl N".

exploration/tables/T03/weak_marker_llm_sample.csv

Summary (weak_marker_llm_summary.csv):

source final='unknown' final='mid-senior' final=other total kaggle_arshkon 143 (83%) 27 2 172 kaggle_asaniczka 841 (79%) 226 5 1,072 scraped 1,252 (77%) 330 37 1,619

In the 100-row qualitative sample, the LLM committed to a label on 19 rows — all mid-senior. Cross-referencing with yoe_min_years_llm:

  • "Software Engineer II" (n=3 committed): YOE 3, 5, and one null → labeled mid-senior. The "II" suffix typically indicates associate-tier (≈ 2-3 YOE) in practice, so labeling as mid-senior is arguably upward-biased on these rows. However, the mid-senior label is reasonable when YOE is ≥ 3.
  • "Software Engineer III" / "SDE III" / etc. (n=8 committed): YOE 5-9 → mid-senior. Correct.
  • "Software Engineer IV" (n=3 committed): YOE 4-12 → mid-senior. Correct.
  • "Senior II" / "Tech Lead - Level 6" / "Staff - Level 4" — all correct mid-senior.

Qualitative routing-error estimate. When the LLM commits on weak-marker titles (19/100 = 19% of the time), I see no obvious downward errors (never labels a higher level as entry) but a small upward tilt on II-suffix titles (plausibly 2-3 of the 4 "II" commitments should have been associate). Extrapolating to the ~2,860 total weak-marker LLM-labeled rows: ~540 commitments of which perhaps ~100-200 II-titled rows are labeled one tier high. This would mildly inflate the mid-senior share relative to a title-rule baseline, but the overall effect size is small (~0.5-1 pp of the mid-senior share in each source).

However, the bigger effect is the 77-83% abstention rate. A weak-marker title that doesn't carry through to description-level explicit seniority phrasing gets dropped to unknown — losing an entire population of correctly-leveled engineering roles from seniority-stratified analyses. This is the primary rationale for T30's YOE-based primary (J3/S4): yoe_min_years_llm is populated on the unknown-seniority subset too, and recovers that signal.

docs.title: 6. LLM unknown share
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T03/llm_unknown_share_by_source.csv

source total LLM-called n_unknown share_unknown kaggle_arshkon 4,687 2,465 52.6% kaggle_asaniczka 18,124 6,214 34.3% scraped 25,816 12,192 47.2%

The LLM declines to assign a seniority on 34-53% of rows it was called on. Per the schema design rationale, this is by design — the LLM only commits on explicit signals. But the aggregate effect is large: in the default-filtered SWE sample, seniority_final = 'unknown' is 26% of arshkon, 34% of asaniczka, 52% of scraped (which has the additional not_selected pool on top).

Implication for all downstream seniority-stratified tasks: the "unknown" slice is not small and is not balanced across sources. Any denominator that uses "all SWE" (rather than "known-seniority SWE") will shift its share estimate simply because the unknown share differs between 2024 and 2026. T30 handles this by reporting both "of all SWE" and "of known-seniority" denominators for every definition.

docs.title: 7. Defensibility verdict
docs.text:
seniority_final is defensible as the production seniority column, subject to three explicit caveats that downstream consumers must respect:

  1. Abstention, not mislabeling, is the dominant failure. The unknown share is 26-52% across sources and shifts across periods. Never report entry-level or senior shares without documenting which denominator is used and what the corresponding unknown share is.
  2. Director slice is weak. Only 22-27% of native director rows get a director label in seniority_final, and the LLM specifically downgrades 44% of asaniczka rule-labeled directors to mid-senior. Any analysis that isolates directors (e.g., T30 S2) should be treated as a low-confidence sensitivity, not a primary claim.
  3. Associate slice is essentially unusable at present. Native associate → final associate cell accuracy is 5% in both sources; the LLM routes most of this pool to mid-senior. Do not stratify on associate as a standalone cell. The T30 J2 definition (entry ∪ associate) is the only safe way to use associate.

No remediation is proposed for the core pipeline — the conservative-abstention behavior is working as intended. The remediation for analysis is to (a) report the abstention share alongside every seniority-stratified claim, and (b) prefer YOE-based primaries (T30 J3/S4) for claims that need label-independent validation. T30 encodes this recommendation in the shared panel.

docs.title: Essential sensitivities
docs.text:
• Aggregator exclusion (a). Scraped aggregator kappa is within 3 pp of non-aggregator; arshkon aggregator kappa is weaker but the sample is small. Report separately where downstream consumers need it, but no mandatory aggregator-only sensitivity for seniority label quality.
  • Within-2024 calibration. Arshkon vs asaniczka comparison is complicated by asaniczka's lack of entry labels; both sources behave similarly on mid-senior but diverge on director (asaniczka downgrades ~44% of director titles).

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. The LLM unknown rate is much higher than I'd expect for a modern LLM classifier — 34-53% on SWE rows it was called on. The LLM design rationale (explicit-signals-only) produces this directly. Downstream tasks need to internalize that seniority_final is ⅔ a crisp labeler and ⅓ an explicit abstainer.
  2. Director accuracy is dramatically worse than mid-senior accuracy (22-27% vs 97-98% conditional cell accuracy). The director failure mode is one-way: the LLM drops director titles to mid-senior when the description lacks director-level management language. This is plausibly correct on the underlying role composition (many "Director" titles in software are really IC/TL roles), but it means S2 (director-only from T30) cannot be trusted as a primary measure.
  3. Weak-marker titles (II/III/IV) almost never get labeled by the LLM (77-83% abstention). The implicit policy is "if the description doesn't restate the level, don't infer from the title number." This preserves YOE-based analyses but collapses a large, clean class-informative signal into unknown.
  4. Asaniczka director-downgrade is larger than arshkon's (44% vs 0% LLM downgrade of rule-labeled directors). This is a direct compositional difference between the two 2024 sources that will surface in T30's asaniczka senior-asymmetry test.
  5. Scraped-vs-arshkon entry accuracy gap (81% vs 52% conditional on native=entry). Either LinkedIn tightened its entry label between 2024-04 and 2026-03, or the LLM got looser on entry. Either way, the native label cannot be a stable cross-period reference — yet another reason to prefer YOE-based primaries in T30.

docs.title: Data caveats
docs.text:
• Asaniczka cannot be audited via seniority_native (no native entry labels, only mid-senior/associate). All native-vs-final analyses in this report are arshkon-only or scraped-only.
  • Indeed has no seniority_native (0% populated), so seniority_final cannot be validated on the scraped Indeed subset. Indeed is out of scope for the primary LinkedIn analysis anyway.
  • seniority_rule is written by Stage 5 and is preserved through Stage 10. Rows where seniority_rule = 'unknown' AND seniority_final_source = 'llm' are the normal majority case (the LLM adds value where the rule is silent). The schema-guaranteed impossible case (rule non-unknown AND final_source='llm' that contradicts rule strongly) is rare in practice (e.g., 13 rows in scraped where rule=mid-senior but final=entry).

docs.title: Downstream action items
docs.text:
1. T30 must prefer J3 (YOE ≤ 2) and S4 (YOE ≥ 5) as primaries for entry and senior claims. Label-based definitions (J1, S1, S2) should be sensitivities.
  2. T30 must run the asaniczka senior-asymmetry test (S4 and S1 shares in asaniczka vs arshkon 2024). Section 2.2 provides the prior expectation: asaniczka directors get downgraded; check whether this propagates to S4/S1 senior shares.
  3. T30 must report known-seniority denominators alongside all-SWE denominators for every label-based definition, because the unknown share differs across sources (26% arshkon, 34% asaniczka, 52% scraped).
  4. Any Wave 2+ task that isolates directors (T21, T34) must note this audit — director precision is too low to support director-only primary claims. Use mid-senior+director as the safe senior aggregate.
  5. Entry-level findings (T20, Wave 3 RQ1 tasks) must be validated against yoe_min_years_llm ≤ 2 even after consulting seniority_final. The 2024→2026 entry native-label drift is a real risk.

docs.title: T04. SWE Classification Audit
docs.text: Run date: 2026-04-20 Owner: Agent B, Wave 1 Input: data/unified.parquet Default filter: source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok' (applied to all metrics unless stated otherwise). Script: exploration/scripts/T04_swe_classification_audit.py Tables: exploration/tables/T04/

docs.title: Headline
docs.text: SWE classification (is_swe) is defensible for primary analysis, with three caveats. Zero dual-flag violations (SWE/adjacent/control are mutually exclusive as required). Rule-vs-LLM agreement where both fire is 80.6% (39,172 / 48,627 rule-SWE rows agree with LLM=SWE). The 10.9% rule-SWE → LLM-NOT_SWE disagreement is concentrated in genuinely ambiguous roles (systems/test/reliability/automation engineers) where title alone is insufficient and the LLM disambiguates via description context. False negatives (rule-non-SWE → LLM-SWE) are much smaller (2,914 rows in the labeled frame, ~6%) and concentrated in emerging roles (architect variants, data scientist, AI/GenAI developer). The one material finding downstream consumers must respect: ML Engineer classification is temporally/source-inconsistent — asaniczka 2024 classifies 59% of ML Engineer titles as SWE vs arshkon 2024 78%, a 19 pp within-2024 gap that will appear as spurious temporal change if not controlled.

docs.title: 1. Classification tier breakdown
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T04/tier_breakdown.csv

tier n swe adjacent control avg_confidence unresolved 1,026,129 0 0 41,122 0.06 regex 192,483 62,173 10,885 119,405 0.99 title_lookup_llm 10,370 4,827 5,248 0 0.87 embedding_adjacent 8,573 0 8,573 0 0.76 embedding_high 1,137 1,137 0 0 0.89

Reading. The vast majority of SWE rule hits come from regex tier (62,173 of 68,137 SWE = 91.3%) with mean confidence 0.99 — these are essentially solved. title_lookup_llm adds 4,827 SWE and 5,248 adjacent at confidence ~0.87. embedding_high adds the remaining 1,137 SWE. Most control rows come from the regex tier (119,405 of 160,527 = 74.4%); the remainder come from the "unresolved" tier's confident-enough negatives. Nothing looks off at this level.

exploration/tables/T04/swe_confidence_bins.csv shows:

conf_bin total swe adjacent control <0.3 898,766 0 0 36,834 0.3-0.5 74,142 0 0 3,132 0.5-0.7 53,221 0 0 1,156 0.7-0.85 19,458 0 19,458 0 0.85-1.0 11,507 5,964 5,248 0 1.0 181,598 62,173 0 119,405

SWE rows never land below confidence 0.85 (by construction of the tier thresholds). Borderline cases are title_lookup_llm at 0.85-0.99. The embedding_adjacent bucket at 0.7-0.85 is the borderline for SWE-adjacent.

docs.title: 2. Borderline SWE sample (qualitative review, n=50)
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T04/borderline_swe_sample.csv — 50 SWE rows with swe_confidence in [0.3, 0.7) or tier = 'title_lookup_llm'.

Reviewing the sample titles and their LLM cross-check labels:

  • Clear SWE, LLM agrees (~27 rows): "Lead Developer", "Senior Lead Engineer- Generative AI", "Computer Scientist", "Senior Embedded Engineer", "Database Developer", "Mobile Architect", "Full Stack Developer" variants.
  • Correctly SWE-adjacent, LLM says SWE_ADJACENT (~13 rows): "Quality Assurance Automation Engineer", "Senior QA Engineer", "Sr. QA Engineer III", "Senior Software QA Engineer" — these are test-automation roles that the rule flagged as SWE but the LLM correctly down-classifies. These are genuine borderline cases where the LLM is more conservative than the rule. Downstream work using swe_classification_llm instead of is_swe will have a cleaner SWE vs adjacent split.
  • Correctly non-SWE, LLM says NOT_SWE (~5 rows): "Reliability Engineer" (petroleum), "Senior Automation Engineer" (pharma manufacturing) — these are miscategorized by the rule because the title contains an SWE-like keyword but the role is mechanical/process engineering. True FP in is_swe.
  • No LLM label (~5 rows): Stage 10 did not fire for these — the rule classification stands.

Estimated true FP rate within the borderline SWE subset: ~5/50 = 10% (non-SWE roles mislabeled as SWE). Scaled to the 4,827 title-lookup-LLM SWE rows: roughly ~480 FP. Against the 68,137 total SWE rows this is ~0.7% — small but nonzero.

docs.title: 3. Borderline non-SWE sample (qualitative review, n=50)
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T04/borderline_non_swe_sample.csv — 50 non-SWE rows with titles containing "engineer", "developer", "software", or "programmer".

  • Correctly non-SWE (~42 rows): Civil/mechanical/chemical/process/field/controls/electrical engineers; project engineers; manufacturing engineers. The exclusion rules are working correctly on these.
  • Likely false negatives (~3-5 rows):
  • "Mean stack Developer" (asaniczka, adjacent) — full-stack JS web dev, should be SWE. LLM correctly calls it SWE.
  • "AEM Developer" (asaniczka, unresolved) — Java/Spring Boot CMS development, likely SWE. No LLM label.
  • "Java Mulesoft Lead Developer" (asaniczka, unresolved) — Java integration development, likely SWE. No LLM label.
  • Legitimately non-SWE despite the keyword (~3 rows): "Dev/Research Engineer 2" (mechanical R&D), "Staff Engineer, R&D" (medical devices, J&J).

Estimated FN rate in this keyword-filtered subset: ~4/50 = 8%. Scaled to the 115,973 non-SWE rows with SWE keywords in title: ~9,300 potential FN. However, the LLM cross-check (Section 5) identifies 2,914 of these in the labeled frame (i.e., ~6% of labeled-subset non-SWE keyword rows). My 8% qualitative estimate is in the same range as the LLM's 6% cross-check, so both estimates agree.

docs.title: 4.1 Top adjacent titles
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T04/adjacent_top_titles.csv (top 10):

  1. quality engineer (1,491)
  2. data scientist (1,484)
  3. network engineer (1,328)
  4. data analyst (1,204)
  5. architect (467)
  6. solutions architect (392)
  7. security engineer (344)
  8. systems administrator (261)
  9. system administrator (194)
  10. cyber security engineer (176)

Reading. The adjacent bucket captures tech-adjacent roles cleanly: data scientist/analyst, network engineer, security engineer, architect variants, systems administrator. This is the expected composition for "technical roles involving some code but not primarily software development." No obvious contamination with pure SWE.

docs.title: 4.2 Top control titles
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T04/control_top_titles.csv (top 10):

  1. accountant (8,200)
  2. registered nurse (3,773)
  3. electrical engineer (3,433)
  4. financial analyst (2,718)
  5. mechanical engineer (2,615)
  6. accounting manager (2,506)
  7. registered nurse (rn) (1,429)
  8. nurse practitioner (1,129)
  9. civil engineer (965)
  10. tax accountant (660)

Reading. Control bucket is accountants + RNs + non-software engineers (electrical, mechanical, civil). Clean separation from SWE — these are the intended baselines for cross-occupation comparisons. No SWE contamination.

docs.title: 5. False-positive and false-negative estimates (LLM cross-check)
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T04/fp_fn_summary.csv:

measure n denominator rate rule SWE → LLM SWE 39,172 48,627 (rule-SWE with LLM label) 80.6% (agreement) rule SWE → LLM SWE_ADJACENT 4,170 48,627 8.6% (soft FP) rule SWE → LLM NOT_SWE 5,285 48,627 10.9% (hard FP) rule non-SWE → LLM SWE (FN) 4,064 — — of which rule-adjacent → LLM SWE 4,043 16,707 rule-adj labeled 24.2% of which rule-control → LLM SWE 21 36,696 rule-ctrl labeled <0.1%

Rule-vs-LLM agreement on the SWE class: 80.6% exact. Hard FP rate (rule-SWE but LLM-NOT_SWE) is 10.9%. Soft FP (rule-SWE but LLM downgrades to ADJACENT) is 8.6%. Combined precision of is_swe against the LLM standard is 80.6%.

False negatives are concentrated in the adjacent bucket, not control: 4,043 of 4,064 FN are rule-adjacent rows the LLM promotes to SWE. The control bucket is essentially FN-free (21 rows of 36,696 = 0.06%). This means:

  • Using is_swe for primary SWE claims gives a conservative undercount (misses ~4,000 LLM-SWE rows now classified as adjacent).
  • Using is_swe OR is_swe_adjacent for broader tech analyses gives a high-recall but noisy set that includes ~6,000-10,000 truly non-SWE roles (LLM NOT_SWE among rule-adjacent) as well as the true SWE-adjacent population.
  • swe_classification_llm = 'SWE' gives the most precise SWE cell at the cost of coverage: 43,236 rows (labeled only) vs 68,137 rule-SWE rows. For any claim where precision matters more than coverage, use the LLM label where available.

docs.title: 5.1 Top titles where rule and LLM disagree
docs.text:
Hard FP (rule-SWE, LLM-NOT_SWE) — top titles:

  1. systems engineer (589)
  2. system engineer (263)
  3. test engineer (200)
  4. reliability engineer (161)
  5. product engineer (137)
  6. application engineer (135)
  7. automation engineer (135)
  8. quality assurance engineer (76)

These are real ambiguity cases, not rule bugs. "Systems engineer" at a software company usually means platform/infra engineering (SWE); at an aerospace or defense company it usually means systems integration/hardware (NOT SWE). The rule has to pick one interpretation; the LLM picks by description. Downstream action: for primary SWE claims, intersect is_swe with swe_classification_llm='SWE' where the LLM is labeled. Precision rises; coverage drops.

Hard FN (rule-non-SWE, LLM-SWE) — top titles:

  1. data scientist (118)
  2. analytics engineer (93)
  3. security engineer (31)
  4. manager, data engineering (24)
  5. manager, platform engineering (24)
  6. servicenow architect (23)
  7. architect (22)
  8. application architect (22)
  9. devops engineer (19)
  10. data architect (18)

These are emerging-role titles. The rule treats data scientist / analytics engineer / architect as SWE-adjacent (correctly for 2024); the LLM sometimes labels these as SWE based on description context (for modern roles that are 80%+ code-writing). Downstream action: if an analysis is interested in the full "software-producing role" population including architects and analytics engineers, union is_swe with swe_classification_llm='SWE' on labeled rows.

docs.title: 6. Dual-flag violation check
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T04/dual_flag_violations.csv

swe_and_adj swe_and_ctrl adj_and_ctrl any_dual_flag 0 0 0 0

Zero dual-flag violations on the full 1,452,875-row parquet. Exclusivity invariant holds.

docs.title: 7. Boundary cases: ML/Data/DevOps/AI/Security/QA Engineers
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T04/boundary_cases.csv and boundary_cases_shares.csv

boundary_class source total swe_share adj_share AI Engineer arshkon 56 66.1% 7.1% AI Engineer asaniczka 66 60.6% 4.5% AI Engineer scraped 1,928 86.6% 2.4% Data Engineer arshkon 369 91.6% 3.5% Data Engineer asaniczka 1,076 89.7% 5.1% Data Engineer scraped 3,609 95.9% 1.9% DevOps/SRE/Platform arshkon 397 93.7% 1.8% DevOps/SRE/Platform asaniczka 986 94.6% 1.4% DevOps/SRE/Platform scraped 3,647 96.4% 0.9% ML Engineer arshkon 142 78.2% 2.1% ML Engineer asaniczka 437 58.6% 9.4% ML Engineer scraped 2,568 81.0% 5.1%

Key findings:

  • ML Engineer within-2024 gap: arshkon 78.2% SWE vs asaniczka 58.6% SWE = 19.6 pp. Scraped 2026 is 81.0%. This means any "ML Engineer share trend 2024→2026" will be dominated by the asaniczka-vs-arshkon classification gap rather than by real market change. Downstream tasks must stratify by source for ML Engineer claims or rely on the title-prefix subset rather than the is_swe label.
  • Data Engineer is stable across sources (89.7-95.9% SWE). Safe to use.
  • DevOps/SRE/Platform is stable (93.7-96.4% SWE). Safe to use.
  • AI Engineer has a moderate gap (arshkon 66%, asaniczka 61%, scraped 87%) — mostly a scraped-vs-Kaggle gap, likely because scraped queries are AI-targeted so AI Engineer titles in scraped are more consistently labeled as SWE. A 21 pp gap between Kaggle (60-66%) and scraped (87%) is larger than the ML Engineer within-2024 gap but moves in a consistent direction; downstream claims about AI Engineer trends should use the LLM cross-check.
  • QA/Test Engineer is mixed (15-26% SWE depending on source) and largely classified as adjacent. Stable directionally.
  • Security Engineer is consistently adjacent (~0% SWE everywhere) — no boundary issue.

docs.title: 8. Temporal consistency check
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T04/temporal_consistency.csv

source period swe_share adj_share ctrl_share kaggle_arshkon 2024-04 4.25% 2.17% 7.81% kaggle_asaniczka 2024-01 2.14% 1.11% 12.43% scraped 2026-03 16.24% 4.60% 16.26% scraped 2026-04 16.06% 4.59% 16.84%

Reading. The huge jump in SWE share from ~2-4% (Kaggle) to ~16% (scraped) is not a classification error — it is a direct consequence of the scraper's query-stratified design. The scraper explicitly issues SWE, SWE-adjacent, and control queries, so the scraped sample has roughly balanced counts of each category, while Kaggle samples are general LinkedIn dumps with naturalistic composition.

Confirmation from search_query × is_swe breakdown: - scraped SWE-targeted queries (query_tier='swe'): 34,328 / 51,285 = 66.9% SWE - scraped adjacent-targeted queries (query_tier='adjacent'): 9,854 / 69,832 = 14.1% SWE (plus adjacent) - scraped control queries (query_tier='control'): 1,130 / 159,684 = 0.7% SWE

Downstream consequence. Any SWE-share or SWE-level metric comparing 2024 to 2026 must control for this sample-design difference. The typical way is to compute shares within SWE (e.g., junior share of SWE, not junior share of all postings). This is already the T30 approach and is the right approach for RQ1/RQ2 seniority analyses.

docs.title: 9. Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Rule-vs-LLM SWE agreement is 80.6% — lower than I expected for a post-preprocessing-fixes classifier. The gap is dominated by genuinely ambiguous systems/test/reliability engineer titles where only description context can disambiguate.
  2. The ML Engineer classification gap within 2024 (78% arshkon vs 59% asaniczka) is the largest non-design-driven source inconsistency I found. This will matter for any task that stratifies by ML Engineer role — T30's panel uses is_swe as the filter, so ML Engineer composition within the SWE sample differs between asaniczka and arshkon.
  3. Data scientist and analytics engineer dominate the rule-non-SWE / LLM-SWE FN list. The rule treats them as adjacent; the LLM promotes them to SWE when description context emphasizes engineering over analysis. This is an emerging-role boundary that T04 surfaces but does not resolve — downstream tasks that want to include data scientists in "SWE-like populations" should use swe_classification_llm = 'SWE' as an expansion.
  4. Architect variants account for ~100 FN rows. "ServiceNow Architect", "Solutions Architect", "Data Architect", "Application Architect" — the rule sends these to adjacent; the LLM sometimes promotes to SWE. For tasks analyzing senior IC archetypes (T21, T34), this is a meaningful boundary issue.
  5. Zero dual-flag violations across 1,452,875 rows. The invariant is clean.
  6. Agg-free: Aggregator exclusion check (not reported in detail) shows SWE share is roughly constant with and without aggregators; aggregator-specific classification artifacts are not a visible issue at this level.
  7. The 16% scraped SWE share vs 2-4% Kaggle SWE share is not a bug but it is a trap. Any naive computation of "is the SWE labor market growing or shrinking 2024→2026" from these shares will produce nonsense. Compositional shares within SWE are the right approach and are already what T30/Wave 2+ tasks do.

docs.title: 10.1 Aggregator exclusion (a)
docs.text: Not separately reported. Spot-check suggests aggregator SWE share does not differ materially from non-aggregator SWE share within each source, and the FP/FN pattern (systems engineer ambiguity) is structural rather than aggregator-driven. Downstream tasks that exclude aggregators can apply their filter on top of is_swe without re-auditing classification.

docs.title: 11. Defensibility verdict
docs.text:
is_swe is defensible for primary SWE analyses with these caveats for downstream consumers:

  1. Use is_swe for coverage; use swe_classification_llm='SWE' for precision. When a finding is sensitive to FP (e.g., precise senior-IC compensation claims), restrict to the LLM-labeled subset where the LLM confirms SWE.
  2. Do not report "SWE share of all postings" trends across the 2024→2026 break — the scraped data is query-stratified and this metric is not comparable. Use within-SWE shares instead.
  3. ML Engineer is the one boundary category with a material within-2024 source inconsistency (59% asaniczka vs 78% arshkon). Stratify by source or use title-prefix filters for ML Engineer claims.
  4. Data scientist and architect variants are rule→LLM boundary cases. Tasks interested in "software-producing" populations broadly should union is_swe with swe_classification_llm='SWE' on labeled rows.

No preprocessing change is requested from this audit. The rule-based classifier is operating within documented specifications; the findings here are guidance for how downstream consumers should interpret and optionally tighten the SWE sample.

docs.title: 12. Downstream action items
docs.text:
1. Wave 2+ tasks that need a precise SWE cell should consider is_swe AND (swe_classification_llm='SWE' OR llm_classification_coverage != 'labeled') — keeping rule-SWE rows where the LLM wasn't called, dropping rule-SWE rows the LLM reclassified. This roughly matches the "80.6% rule-LLM agreement" precision bar.
  2. T20 (seniority boundary clarity), T21 (senior role evolution), T34 (emergent senior archetypes) should avoid isolating "architect" as a separate seniority tier without first checking whether architect rows would be promoted to SWE by the LLM.
  3. T28 (domain-stratified scope), T18 (cross-occupation boundary) should stratify by query_tier for scraped when computing any cross-source metric, to avoid the sample-design artifact.
  4. ML Engineer within-2024 gap (19.6 pp) should be documented in the Gate 1 memo and respected by any downstream ML-engineer-specific analysis.

docs.title: T05. Cross-dataset comparability
docs.text:
Agent C, Wave 1. Generated 2026-04-20.

Question. Are the three LinkedIn datasets (kaggle_arshkon, kaggle_asaniczka, scraped) measuring the same underlying labor market, or are their differences artifacts of data collection?

TL;DR. - All three pairs differ significantly on every dimension, but the arshkon↔asaniczka gap (both 2024, same platform, different snapshots) is roughly the within-period noise floor. The 2024↔2026 (scraped) gaps consistently sit well above that floor on length and geography, and well below it on seniority. - Description length is the most differentiated signal. KS D = 0.32 for arshkon↔scraped (non-agg), vs 0.11 for arshkon↔asaniczka. Scraped postings are ~1750 chars longer on average than 2024 arshkon. - Seniority_final distributions differ mainly between 2024 sources and asaniczka. arshkon↔scraped Cramér's V = 0.022 (trivial); asaniczka↔scraped V = 0.117 (moderate). Asaniczka is the odd one out on seniority — 97% mid-senior — reflecting the known zero-native-entry gap, not a real labor market fact. - Geographic distributions differ substantially (V ≈ 0.27–0.34 between 2024 and scraped). California share jumps from 17% to 25% in scraped. Mostly an artifact of the scraper's metro-weighted search design, not a 2024→2026 geographic shift. - Company overlap is low everywhere. Pairwise Jaccard ranges 0.10–0.13. The "measured-market" defined by these three sources is mostly disjoint at the company level. Top-50 employers are more stable (Jaccard 0.10–0.28). - Title vocabulary overlap is even lower — raw Jaccard 0.03–0.05. Filtering to titles with ≥3 occurrences raises overlap to 0.10–0.25. - Platform labeling stability test (novel finding). Within the same 20 most-common SWE titles, the seniority_native mid-senior share rises systematically from 2024 arshkon to 2026 scraped — often from ~50% to ~75%. YOE means are roughly stable across the same cells. This is the platform's own labeling drifting toward mid-senior, not the market changing underneath. The entry-share drop in native labels 2024→2026 is partly a platform relabeling artifact. - Indeed cross-validation supports the platform-artifact reading. On the same 20 titles, scraped-Indeed entry indicators (rule-based yoe_extracted ≤ 2) track LinkedIn-scraped reasonably closely, suggesting the 2026 entry-share number is not a LinkedIn-only artifact. - Aggregator sensitivity is small. Excluding aggregators moves Cramér's V numbers by at most 0.03 and never flips a sign. Aggregator exclusion does not save any comparison. - Trustworthy comparisons (in order): YOE means per title > seniority_final distributions (mid/senior only) > description length (interpret carefully). Least trustworthy: geographic distributions (scraper design artifact), seniority_native-based trends (platform drift), raw title vocabulary (long-tail noise).

Scripts: exploration/scripts/T05_cross_dataset_comparability.py, exploration/scripts/T05_figures.py. Tables in exploration/tables/T05/. Figures in exploration/figures/T05/.

docs.title: Data scope and filters
docs.text:
SWE (is_swe = true), LinkedIn (source_platform = 'linkedin'), is_english = true, date_flag = 'ok'. For the top-20 title stability test (step 9), Indeed is included as a cross-platform sensitivity for scraped only.

Filtered SWE counts on LinkedIn:

source total LinkedIn LinkedIn SWE filtered non-agg agg multi-loc kaggle_arshkon 110,619 4,691 4,261 430 116 kaggle_asaniczka 892,554 18,129 15,186 2,943 894 scraped 281,008 45,317 37,609 7,708 3,344

Indeed SWE filtered: 14,245 (scraped only).

docs.title: Summary effect-size table (non-aggregator)
docs.text:
source_a source_b desc_len_ks_D company_jac top50_co_jac seniority_V state_V title_jac arshkon asaniczka 0.115 0.108 0.099 0.127 0.142 0.051 arshkon scraped 0.321 0.103 0.220 0.022 0.269 0.026 asaniczka scraped 0.252 0.132 0.163 0.117 0.342 0.037

(Table: effect_size_summary.csv; aggregator sensitivity: agg_sensitivity_effect_sizes.csv.)

Baseline noise floor (within-2024 calibration — arshkon vs asaniczka): these two are both 2024 LinkedIn snapshots 3 months apart from different scrapers. The values in that row set the expected "same market, different snapshot" magnitudes. Anything substantially above those numbers in 2024↔2026 comparisons is in principle candidate real change + artifact; anything similar or below is within the noise floor.

docs.title: Step 1. Description length
docs.text:
KS test, all three pairwise, non-aggregator:

pair n_a n_b mean_a mean_b D p arshkon vs asaniczka 4,261 15,186 3,359 3,805 0.115 ~0 arshkon vs scraped 4,261 37,609 3,359 5,097 0.321 ~0 asaniczka vs scraped 15,186 37,609 3,805 5,097 0.252 ~0

Within-2024 mean gap ≈450 chars. 2024→2026 gap ≈1,300–1,750 chars. The 2024→2026 shift is large relative to the noise floor — it is not purely a scrape-design artifact, because arshkon and asaniczka also differ from each other substantially below the 2024↔2026 gap.

Artifact diagnosis. Part of the scraped excess is likely LinkedIn's own boilerplate growth (disclaimers, EEO/DEI blocks, benefits boilerplate); the rest plausibly reflects job-content inflation. Downstream analyses must use description_core_llm (LLM-cleaned text) rather than raw description when measuring content — T05 shows the raw length signal is a mix of content and boilerplate.

Figure: figures/T05/description_length_hist.png.

docs.title: Step 2. Company overlap (Jaccard)
docs.text:
Pairwise Jaccard of company_name_canonical sets (non-aggregator):

pair n_a n_b intersect union Jaccard top-50 Jaccard arshkon vs asaniczka 2,235 4,814 687 6,362 0.108 0.099 arshkon vs scraped 2,235 7,740 932 9,043 0.103 0.220 asaniczka vs scraped 4,814 7,740 1,461 11,093 0.132 0.163

Key observation. The arshkon↔scraped top-50 Jaccard (0.22) is actually higher than arshkon↔asaniczka top-50 (0.10) — despite being 2 years apart. This is because the top-50 in each LinkedIn snapshot is dominated by persistent large employers (Amazon, Capital One, Microsoft, Google, Meta), and the arshkon top-50 is a reasonable snapshot of those. Asaniczka's top-50, however, is dominated by aggregator-like names (ClearanceJobs, Jobs for Humanity, ClickJobs.io) that are almost absent from scraped.

docs.title: Step 3. Geographic (state-level chi-squared)
docs.text:
Non-aggregator, multi-location rows excluded:

pair n_states total_a total_b chi2 V arshkon vs asaniczka 50 3,152 14,129 347 0.142 arshkon vs scraped 43 3,142 32,166 2,559 0.269 asaniczka vs scraped 50 14,129 32,166 5,403 0.342

California share: arshkon 16.6%, asaniczka 16.8%, scraped 25.1%. The +8 percentage-point California jump is the single largest driver of the state-chi2. WA (+3pp), NY (+3pp), MA (+2pp) also move up; FL (-2.5pp), OH (-1.4pp), MI (-0.5pp) move down.

Artifact diagnosis. The scraper's metro-targeted search design over-represents tech-heavy metros (per docs/data-sources-and-prompts.md). This is almost certainly a scrape design artifact, not a 2024→2026 geographic re-concentration of SWE hiring. Any geographic finding from the T05–T38 pipeline must not interpret raw scraped state shares as a national measure — either restrict to per-metro analyses (where the frame is stable) or use arshkon/asaniczka as the market-wide benchmark.

See state_shares.csv; figure state_shares_top15.png.

docs.title: Step 4. Seniority (seniority_final)
docs.text:
Non-aggregator, excluding unknown:

source entry associate mid-senior director n_labeled kaggle_arshkon 8.1% 0.7% 90.4% 0.8% 2,051 kaggle_asaniczka 2.2% 0.4% 96.8% 0.6% 9,608 scraped 7.5% 0.6% 90.3% 1.6% 18,960

Chi-squared (non-aggregator):

pair chi2 dof p V arshkon vs asaniczka 187.5 3 ~0 0.127 arshkon vs scraped 10.1 3 0.018 0.022 asaniczka vs scraped 391.5 3 ~0 0.117

Finding — arshkon and scraped are effectively interchangeable on seniority_final distribution. Cramér's V = 0.022 is trivial. Asaniczka is the outlier (entry share 2.2%, pulled down by zero native entry labels), consistent with its documented caveat.

Bar chart: figures/T05/seniority_final_bar.png.

docs.title: Step 5. Title vocabulary Jaccard
docs.text:
Raw Jaccard is driven by long-tail uniqueness:

pair non-agg, min_count=1 min_count=3 arshkon vs asaniczka 0.051 0.245 arshkon vs scraped 0.026 0.102 asaniczka vs scraped 0.037 0.185

With the min_count ≥ 3 filter, arshkon↔asaniczka Jaccard (0.245) is ~2.4× the arshkon↔scraped Jaccard (0.102). This is larger than the description-length ratio. Titles move more than seniority but less than description length.

Titles unique to each source (sample, non-agg, ≥3 occurrences): see titles_unique_per_source.csv. Scraped-only titles include ai engineer, applied ai engineer, llm engineer, forward deployed engineer, solutions engineer - ai — consistent with a new generative-AI role family.

docs.title: Step 6. Industry chi-squared (arshkon vs scraped — asaniczka has no industry)
docs.text:
Non-aggregator, 227 industry labels, n=34,918 (scraped) and 4,146 (arshkon): chi2=2,536, V=0.255.

Largest industry drops (scraped share minus arshkon share):

industry arshkon scraped Δ IT Services and IT Consulting 25.6% 14.0% −11.7pp Information Services 3.4% 0.5% −2.9pp Staffing and Recruiting 4.6% 2.3% −2.3pp Information Technology & Services 2.7% 1.0% −1.7pp

Largest industry rises:

industry arshkon scraped Δ Software Development 12.9% 19.2% +6.4pp Technology, Information and Internet 2.5% 6.2% +3.6pp Defense and Space Manufacturing 2.5% 5.0% +2.4pp Information Services + Tech, Information and Internet 0.0% 2.3% +2.3pp

Artifact diagnosis. LinkedIn changed/merged some industry taxonomy labels between 2024 and 2026 (e.g. the merged "Information Services and Technology, Information and Internet" has 0% in 2024 but 2.3% in 2026). Some of the IT Services drop is real re-categorization into Software Development and Technology/Information. Industry-level change should be interpreted carefully; a crosswalk table would be needed for proper 2024→2026 attribution.

docs.title: Step 7. Artifact diagnostic summary
docs.text: metric noise-floor (arshkon↔asaniczka) arshkon↔scraped asaniczka↔scraped trustworthy? description length KS D 0.115 0.321 0.252 yes for direction, caveat boilerplate company Jaccard 0.108 0.103 0.132 yes seniority V 0.127 0.022 0.117 yes — direction: asaniczka is odd state V 0.142 0.269 0.342 no — scraper frame artifact title Jaccard (≥3) 0.245 0.102 0.185 yes industry V n/a n/a (V=0.255 for this single pair) n/a partly — taxonomy change

docs.title: Step 8. Within-2024 calibration — arshkon vs asaniczka
docs.text:
Already reported throughout (table calibration_2024.json). Highlights: - Description length KS D = 0.115 (both 2024 LinkedIn, differ by ~450 chars) - Seniority V = 0.127 (driven by asaniczka's zero-entry artifact) - Company Jaccard = 0.108 - Title (≥3) Jaccard = 0.245 - State V = 0.142

These are the within-period baseline magnitudes; anything similar in 2024→2026 is noise-level, anything substantially larger is candidate real change + frame artifact.

docs.title: Step 9. Platform labeling stability test
docs.text:
Top 20 SWE titles shared between arshkon and scraped (see shared_titles_top20.csv): software engineer, data engineer, devops engineer, java developer, full stack engineer, site reliability engineer, systems engineer, machine learning engineer, system engineer, salesforce developer, embedded software engineer, automation engineer, cloud engineer, software developer, software development engineer, test engineer, frontend developer, java software engineer, product engineer, full stack developer.

Native-label mid-senior share rises from 2024 to 2026 across nearly every title. A sampling:

title mid-senior arshkon mid-senior scraped Δ software engineer 58.7% 66.2% +7.5pp data engineer 51.8% 75.1% +23.3pp devops engineer 61.3% 77.1% +15.8pp frontend developer 50.0% 85.1% +35.1pp full stack engineer 47.5% 78.9% +31.3pp java developer 30.3% 72.9% +42.6pp site reliability engineer 54.3% 73.2% +18.9pp system engineer 54.5% 79.1% +24.5pp systems engineer 53.3% 69.7% +16.4pp test engineer 39.3% 61.6% +22.3pp salesforce developer 59.1% 81.2% +22.1pp product engineer 20.8% 51.5% +30.7pp

(Full table: shared_titles_seniority_native.csv; figure: title_native_seniority_drift.png.)

By contrast, YOE means on the same titles are roughly stable. Mean yoe_min_years_llm per title, arshkon vs scraped:

title YOE arshkon YOE scraped Δ software engineer 5.05 5.30 +0.25 data engineer 5.31 5.41 +0.10 devops engineer 6.06 5.24 -0.82 java developer 6.58 5.56 -1.01 site reliability engineer 5.45 5.51 +0.06 full stack engineer 5.08 5.21 +0.13 cloud engineer 4.88 5.74 +0.86 product engineer 3.06 3.28 +0.22 frontend developer 5.65 5.78 +0.13

Interpretation. If the same title sees a large seniority_native mid-senior share increase but near-zero YOE change, the parsimonious reading is that LinkedIn's seniority labeling policy — how employer-supplied level fields map to platform labels — shifted between 2024 and 2026. The apparent 2024→2026 "entry-share decline" in seniority_native-only analyses is partly a platform relabeling artifact.

This is why the T30 panel provides four junior variants — J1/J2 use seniority_final (also affected, since Stage 5 relies on title keywords and Stage 10 labels the LLM frame only), J3/J4 use yoe_min_years_llm (independent of both platform labels and rule-based title keywords). Any entry-share comparison 2024↔2026 should report J3 as primary and J1/J2 as sensitivities.

Figure: title_native_seniority_drift.png, title_yoe_drift.png.

docs.title: Indeed cross-validation for the platform-artifact reading
docs.text:
For the same 20 titles, we have scraped-Indeed rows in addition to scraped-LinkedIn. Indeed is excluded from the LLM frame so yoe_min_years_llm is null — we use rule-based yoe_extracted ≤ 2 as the entry indicator.

Examples (n ≥ 50 on both platforms):

title scraped LinkedIn % rule_yoe≤2 scraped Indeed % rule_yoe≤2 software engineer 20.6% 21.8% data engineer 12.2% 14.5% devops engineer 12.5% 17.6% java developer 20.7% 25.0% machine learning engineer n/a n/a full stack engineer 14.5% 9.8%

Rule-based entry-rates are similar between LinkedIn and Indeed for the overlapping title set (Pearson r = 0.65 across 20 shared titles on rule-based yoe≤2 share). Indeed tends slightly toward higher entry rates on some titles (automation engineer, devops engineer, software developer) but the rank-correlation is strong. The LinkedIn 2024→2026 entry-share movement is not a LinkedIn-only platform artifact on YOE-based measures — Indeed shows similar rates in 2026. This weakens a strict "LinkedIn-only artifact" hypothesis for the YOE-based entry measure, even while the seniority_native drift above strongly suggests platform relabeling on the label side.

Full table: shared_titles_platform_cross.csv.

docs.title: Aggregator sensitivity
docs.text:
Re-run of all pairwise tests with aggregators included vs excluded (agg_sensitivity_effect_sizes.csv, seniority_shares_by_agg_mode.csv). Effect-size shifts are small (≤ 0.03 V or KS D). Direction never flips. Aggregator exclusion is a real-decision sensitivity, but it is not a confound that rescues or breaks any of the T05 findings.

Aggregator rows differ substantively from non-aggregator in seniority shares (see table): aggregators have lower entry share on asaniczka (1%) and higher entry share on arshkon (6%) and scraped (8%). Aggregator exclusion is still warranted for content-level analyses, not because aggregator exclusion fixes the comparability gap but because aggregator postings are a qualitatively different artifact (short descriptions, generic template). See T06 for dedicated aggregator profiling.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. arshkon and scraped are effectively twins on seniority_final distribution (V=0.022). The 2024↔2026 gap on this variable is smaller than the within-2024 gap between arshkon and asaniczka. The common narrative that "entry-level share is collapsing" does not show up in this basic seniority_final-level comparison — they are near-identical. Whatever the entry-share story is, it is not in the aggregate seniority_final distribution.

  2. LinkedIn's own native seniority labels drifted substantially 2024→2026, and this drift is visible at the same-title level. The seniority_native mid-senior share for data engineer rose 23pp; for java developer, 43pp; for frontend developer, 35pp. YOE distributions on the same cells barely moved. The platform-relabeling-not-market-change reading is strong on the native label.

  3. Title vocabulary overlap is surprisingly low even within 2024. Arshkon↔asaniczka raw title Jaccard is 0.05. Both are LinkedIn SWE 2024. This tells us either (a) the SWE classifier is picking up a lot of title long-tail, or (b) LinkedIn's title population has a very long tail of one-offs with mild variation. This matters for any "title vocabulary expansion" Wave 2 claim — the baseline vocab is already very unstable within period.
