  4. California's share jumped +8pp in scraped — but this is the scraper design. Any geographic finding based on scraped state shares, or on scraped/arshkon state-share ratios, is unreliable. Metro-restricted analyses will be more defensible.

  5. Asaniczka top-50 is aggregator-heavy (ClearanceJobs, Jobs for Humanity, ClickJobs.io). If the top-company set drives a finding, asaniczka's top is systematically different in genre from arshkon/scraped — it is plausibly overweighted in defense/cleared roles.

  6. "Jobs for Humanity" appears twice in asaniczka's top companies — 571 postings under the canonical name "Capital One" (because it is detected as an aggregator and the real employer extracted to Capital One) and 185 under its own name. This dual-resolution is a real company-canonicalization edge case — the aggregator splits into a real-employer-resolved cohort and an unresolved cohort. Downstream analyses should be aware that aggregator real-employer resolution is partial.

  7. Indeed YOE-based entry rates track LinkedIn-scraped on the same titles. This was not guaranteed — Indeed could have had a different seniority-entry composition, but it does not. This is weak corroboration that scraped-LinkedIn's YOE-based entry-share shifts are not a LinkedIn-only platform artifact.

docs.title: What to trust downstream
docs.text:
Trustworthy for 2024↔2026 comparison: - Mean yoe_min_years_llm per title (stable across the platform-stability check) - seniority_final distribution at aggregate level (V=0.022 for arshkon↔scraped — so any entry-share direction must be carefully checked, but the aggregate is stable) - Top-50 employer overlap (Jaccard 0.22 arshkon↔scraped)

Interpretable but use care: - Description length (partly boilerplate growth, partly content growth — use description_core_llm) - Company Jaccard on the full population (low everywhere — the "measured market" is mostly disjoint) - Industry distribution (taxonomy changes — needs crosswalk)

Avoid as primary: - seniority_native as a 2024→2026 entry measure (platform drift) - Geographic state shares on scraped vs Kaggle (scraper frame artifact) - Title vocabulary overlap without a count floor (long-tail noise)

docs.title: T06. Company concentration deep investigation
docs.text:
Agent C, Wave 1. Generated 2026-04-20.

Question. Does a handful of companies drive the aggregate patterns in each dataset? Which downstream findings are likely concentration-driven, and what mitigation does each need?

TL;DR. - Per-source concentration is moderate. Non-aggregator HHI ranges 14 (arshkon) to 23 (scraped) on a 0–10,000 scale; Gini 0.40–0.68. Scraped is more skewed than arshkon despite having 3.5× more companies. - The top 5 non-aggregator employers explain 4.4% (arshkon), 5.8% (asaniczka), 7.1% (scraped) of SWE postings. No single source is dominated by any one employer: max company share is 1.2%–2.2%. - Entry-level posting is highly concentrated at the company level. Among companies with ≥5 SWE postings, 58% (arshkon), 62% (asaniczka), 63% (scraped) have ZERO entry-labeled rows under J3 (yoe_llm ≤ 2). Under J1 (seniority_final='entry'), 77–87% have zero. Entry-share is a minority-employer activity, not a market-wide one. Any 2024→2026 "entry-share decline" is about a specific sub-population of employers. - Within-vs-between decomposition (arshkon∩scraped panel, 125 companies with ≥5 postings each): - Under J3 (yoe_llm ≤ 2): total change +5.0pp is almost entirely BETWEEN-company (+5.3pp); within-company is zero (−0.03pp). The entry-share rise 2024→2026 is compositional — different companies are in the 2026 sample, not the same companies posting more entry roles. - Under J4 (yoe_llm ≤ 3): total change +7.5pp — again almost entirely between (+7.9pp); within is +0.2pp. - Under J1 (seniority_final='entry'): total change +0.06pp — within component −0.48pp, between +0.34pp. Direction flips between J3 and J1 but both are small magnitudes. - AI mention share: total change +39pp, within +31pp, between +14pp. Mostly within — same companies adding AI mentions. - Description length: total change +1752 chars, within +970, between +777. Mostly within-company growth. - Tech-token count: total change +0.99 tokens/post, within +1.69, between −0.29. Within dominates; between is slightly negative. - 206 companies flagged as "entry specialists" (any J1/J2/J3/J4 variant > 60%) across all sources. 148 of these are flagged exclusively by J4 (yoe_llm ≤ 3) — this confirms J4 is too loose as an entry threshold. 53 are exclusively J3-flagged; 3 exclusively J1-flagged. Tech giants (Amazon, AWS, Microsoft, Google) flag under J4 because median YOE for big-tech entry tiers is 3 years (quantized at 2, 5, 8 in the YOE distribution). Use J3 as the primary entry-specialist flag. - Duplicate-template audit essentially clean. Stage-4 multi-location dedup is working: max-dup-ratio ≥ 2 with n≥5 exists for only 2 companies in arshkon, 1 in asaniczka, 6 in scraped. The residual 6 in scraped are worth checking but do not change the corpus. - Aggregator profile. 9.1%–16.2% of filtered SWE postings per source are from aggregators. Aggregators have lower description length in arshkon/scraped (2780 vs 3359 chars; 3899 vs 5097 chars) but higher description length in asaniczka (4276 vs 3805 chars). Aggregator entry shares are lower than non-aggregator in all sources except arshkon J1. Aggregator share differs across sources (arshkon 9.1% → asaniczka 16.2% → scraped 17.0%), so aggregator-stratified analyses are warranted for any cross-source comparison. - Returning-companies cohort (arshkon∪asaniczka ∩ scraped): 2,109 companies. Accounts for 55% of scraped SWE postings (24,927/45,317) while representing 25% of 2026 unique companies. The other 6,368 "new entrant" companies in 2026 account for 45% of postings. Returning companies have slightly higher J3 entry share in scraped (15.3% vs 12.8% for new entrants), slightly longer descriptions (5121 vs 4615 chars).

Artifacts produced: - exploration/artifacts/shared/entry_specialist_employers.csv — 206 rows, consumed by T16 and concentration-sensitivity checks - exploration/artifacts/shared/returning_companies_cohort.csv — 2,109 rows, consumed by T37 and T38

Scripts: exploration/scripts/T06_company_concentration.py, exploration/scripts/T06_figures.py. Tables in exploration/tables/T06/. Figures in exploration/figures/T06/.

docs.title: Data scope and filters
docs.text:
SWE (is_swe = true), LinkedIn (source_platform = 'linkedin'), is_english = true, date_flag = 'ok'. Grouping column: company_name_canonical (excluding NULLs where grouping is needed). 45 of 4,691 arshkon SWE rows have NULL canonical names (0.96%) — excluded from the specialist list and the returning/new-entrant calculation.

T30 panel used locally per the dispatch spec: - J1 seniority_final = 'entry' - J2 seniority_final IN ('entry', 'associate') - J3 yoe_min_years_llm ≤ 2 with llm_classification_coverage = 'labeled' (primary) - J4 yoe_min_years_llm ≤ 3 with llm_classification_coverage = 'labeled'

docs.title: Step 1. Concentration metrics per source
docs.text:
source agg_mode n_companies n_postings HHI Gini top1 top5 top10 top20 top50 max arshkon all 2,299 4,691 16.2 0.438 1.1% 4.9% 7.9% 12.7% 21.4% 52 arshkon non-agg 2,236 4,261 14.1 0.405 1.2% 4.4% 7.3% 11.1% 19.0% 52 asaniczka all 4,948 18,129 54.4 0.641 5.0% 12.2% 16.0% 21.1% 30.6% 898 asaniczka non-agg 4,814 15,186 19.1 0.588 1.9% 5.8% 9.1% 14.1% 23.5% 292 scraped all 8,477 45,317 36.3 0.699 4.3% 9.3% 12.8% 18.0% 27.1% 1,934 scraped non-agg 7,740 37,609 23.1 0.679 2.2% 7.1% 11.0% 16.5% 25.1% 820

(HHI scaled to 0–10,000; US DOJ thresholds: <1500 unconcentrated, 1500–2500 moderate, >2500 high. All three sources are well below "moderate concentration" by HHI.)

Notable: - Asaniczka "all" HHI is 3.8× higher than non-aggregator, driven by ClearanceJobs (898 postings, 5.0% share) and the aggregator-resolved "Capital One" pool (658 postings, 3.6% share). Aggregators inflate asaniczka concentration substantially. - Scraped has the widest company base (8,477 companies, 7,740 non-aggregator) but the biggest single-employer scale (max 1,934 postings = "Jobs via Dice"; 820 for Google non-aggregator). - Gini is 0.40 (arshkon non-agg), 0.59 (asaniczka), 0.68 (scraped). Scraped has the most uneven distribution despite having more companies — mass in the long tail.

Figure: cumulative_posting_share.png.

docs.title: Step 2. Top-20 employer profile per source
docs.text:
Full table: top20_employer_profile.csv. Excerpt (arshkon):

rank company n share agg industry mean YOE llm mean desc J1% J3% 1 Apex Systems 52 1.1% T IT Services and IT Consulting 4.94 4,333 3.8% 17.1% 2 Capital One 52 1.1% F Financial Services 5.46 5,585 0.0% 5.8% 3 Motion Recruitment 40 0.9% T Staffing and Recruiting 5.92 1,681 0.0% 4.0% 4 Tata Consultancy Services 39 0.8% T IT Services and IT Consulting 7.22 1,114 0.0% 0.0% 5 Amazon 31 0.7% F Software Development 3.23 4,044 12.9% 29.0% ... ... ... ... ... ... ... ... ... ... 18 Microsoft 19 0.4% F Software Development 3.26 5,966 0.0% 63.2%

Scraped top 5:

rank company n share agg industry mean YOE llm mean desc J1% J3% 1 Jobs via Dice 1,934 4.3% T Software Development 5.31 5,139 2.8% 17.5% 2 Google 820 1.8% F Tech/Info 4.39 4,774 1.2% 43.7% 3 JPMorgan Chase 680 1.5% F Financial Services 5.27 5,823 0.0% 4.3% 4 Microsoft 418 0.9% F Software Development 3.60 6,437 0.2% 40.4% 5 AWS 376 0.8% F IT Services and IT Consulting 3.79 6,785 0.0% 6.9%

Observations: - Tech giants (Google, Microsoft) have huge J3 shares — 40%+ of their postings have yoe_llm ≤ 2. This is a real structural feature of big-tech SWE hiring — entry-tier bands quantized at YOE=2. - IT services and staffing firms have the longest tenure profiles (mean YOE 5–7). - Big financials (Capital One, JPMorgan, Fidelity) are low J1 / low J3 — almost no posted entry roles on the public frame. - "Jobs via Dice" is an aggregator; its 4.3% share of scraped is a real concentration concern — ~1,934 aggregator postings are influencing corpus averages.

Figure: top20_volume_bar.png.

docs.title: Step 3. Duplicate-template audit
docs.text:
source companies total with dup_ratio ≥ 2 and n ≥ 5 mean dup_ratio among n≥5 arshkon 2,299 2 1.04 asaniczka 4,948 1 1.01 scraped 8,477 6 1.02

Finding. Stage-4 multi-location dedup is working — duplicates essentially gone. Multi-location flag is doing the real work: scraped has 3,344 SWE rows with is_multi_location=True (7.4% of the filtered corpus), concentrated in Meta (69% of company postings), Deloitte (74%), CoreWeave (71%), Turing (83%). The 6 residual scraped dup-cases are worth spot-checking but do not change the corpus-level picture.

Tables: dup_audit_all_companies.csv, dup_audit_top10_per_source.csv.

docs.title: Step 4. Entry-level posting concentration (T30 panel)
docs.text:
source total co any J1 any J2 co with LLM any J3 any J4 co ≥5 posts % zero J1 % zero J2 co ≥5 with LLM % zero J3 % zero J4 arshkon 2,299 149 162 1,807 327 630 165 78% 76% 161 58% 34% asaniczka 4,948 168 192 4,242 685 1,425 632 87% 85% 627 62% 39% scraped 8,477 812 866 5,239 1,153 2,215 1,653 77% 75% 1,581 63% 39%

Read this carefully. Among companies with ≥5 SWE postings that have ≥1 LLM-labeled row, 58–63% have ZERO entry-labeled postings under J3 (yoe_llm ≤ 2). Under J1 (seniority_final='entry'), 77–87% have zero.

Entry posting is a specialty activity. The story of "does entry share rise or fall" is a story about a minority subset of employers that do entry hiring. This has several implications downstream:

  1. Aggregate entry-share changes are driven by changes in that minority's behavior, not by a market-wide redistribution of hiring activity to more junior levels.
  2. Entry-share changes driven by composition of the subset (different firms in the flagged set) may look very different from changes driven by the same firms changing behavior. See the decomposition results in Step 5.
  3. A 1pp shift in aggregate entry share can represent a substantial change in entry-posting density among the minority of firms that post entry roles.

Entry-share distribution per company (see entry_share_distribution_{J1,J2,J3,J4}.csv):

source variant n companies (≥5, any entry) mean entry-share median q25 q75 arshkon J3 67 0.268 0.200 0.119 0.333 asaniczka J3 237 0.217 0.167 0.083 0.250 scraped J3 583 0.297 0.250 0.143 0.375 arshkon J1 36 0.147 0.118 0.076 0.157 asaniczka J1 81 0.100 0.071 0.034 0.143 scraped J1 378 0.146 0.110 0.059 0.192

Among companies that post any entry-labeled rows at all, the typical firm has 10–25% of its postings labeled entry. Arshkon and scraped are broadly similar here; asaniczka is lower (driven by the absence of native entry labels).

Figures: j3_entry_share_distribution.png, entry_share_all_variants_scraped.png.

docs.title: Step 5. Within-company vs between-company decomposition
docs.text:
Panel: companies with ≥5 SWE postings in BOTH arshkon AND scraped. n = 125 companies.

Shift-share decomposition (weighted mean change from 2024 to 2026):

metric def mean 2024 mean 2026 total Δ within between interaction n entry_share_J1 sen_final='entry' 0.032 0.032 +0.001 −0.005 +0.003 +0.002 125 entry_share_J2 entry+associate 0.035 0.036 +0.000 −0.005 +0.003 +0.002 125 entry_share_J3 yoe_llm≤2 0.120 0.170 +0.050 −0.000 +0.053 −0.002 121 entry_share_J4 yoe_llm≤3 0.244 0.319 +0.075 +0.002 +0.079 −0.007 121 ai_mention_share regex 0.132 0.522 +0.390 +0.314 +0.138 −0.062 125 desc_length_mean chars 3,489 5,241 +1,752 +970 +777 +5 125 tech_per_post_mean curated regex 6.03 7.02 +0.99 +1.69 −0.29 −0.41 125

Direction flips between J1 and J3. Under J1 (platform/rule labels), the same 125 companies on average dropped their entry-share 0.5pp within-company. Under J3 (yoe_llm ≤ 2), they were flat within-company (~0) but the panel grew +5pp overall because the 2026 snapshot is tilted toward companies with higher YOE-based entry rates.

Translation for Wave 2+. The "entry-share rise 2024→2026 in SWE" finding, under J3, is a composition story. It is NOT "the same companies are posting more junior roles." It is "a different set of companies is in the 2026 sample."

AI mention rate (within-company 0.132 → 0.522, Δ=+0.39) is primarily within-company (+0.31) with a substantial between component (+0.14). This is one of the clearest "same employer, new behavior" patterns in the panel.

Description length rose 1,752 chars on average — half within, half between. Same companies added ~970 chars; 2026 company mix adds another ~780 chars.

Tech-token count dynamics: within +1.69 tokens, between −0.29. Same companies widened their tech vocabulary; 2026 newcomers are slightly narrower in tech-keyword density.

Table: within_between_decomposition.csv; raw panel: overlap_panel_arshkon_scraped.csv.

docs.title: Step 6. Entry-specialist employer identification
docs.text:
206 companies with ≥5 SWE postings and any J1/J2/J3/J4 share > 60%.

Total variant hits (threshold 60%): - J1 > 60%: 5 companies - J2 > 60%: 5 companies (overlap heavy with J1) - J3 > 60%: 55 companies - J4 > 60%: 203 companies

Binding (exclusively — meets no stricter variant): - 148 flagged only by J4 (yoe_llm ≤ 3) — many are big-tech with quantized YOE=3 roles - 53 flagged only by J3 (yoe_llm ≤ 2) without meeting J1/J2 — true LLM-YOE-based junior-heavy - 3 flagged only by J1 (rule-based platform label) without meeting J3/J4

Of the 206: 197 non-aggregator, 9 aggregator. Category: 9 aggregators, 6 tech-giant-intern, 18 staffing, 4 college-jobsite, 169 direct-employer.

Top specialists by volume (from entry_specialist_details.csv):

company n_swe J1% J3% J4% type Amazon 549 5.1% 11.2% 67.3% tech-giant-intern Amazon Web Services (AWS) 485 0.0% 6.1% 63.9% tech-giant-intern TikTok 176 60.8% 27.3% 54.5% direct-employer PNC 135 1.5% 12.0% 66.7% direct-employer TikTok USDS Joint Venture 126 6.3% 39.1% 80.4% direct-employer Discover Financial Services 96 0.0% 12.5% 72.9% direct-employer Stealth Startup 49 4.1% 34.8% 65.2% direct-employer Applied Intuition 47 2.1% 25.8% 67.7% direct-employer KLA 46 15.2% 61.3% 87.1% direct-employer SynergisticIT 25 100.0% — — direct-employer WayUp 21 95.2% 100% 100% direct-employer

Interpretation. The true junior-heavy set is small (~40 companies under J3). Under J4, the list is inflated by tech giants whose entry tiers quantize at YOE=3. Under J1 alone, the real entry-specialist employers are: - SynergisticIT (staffing/training pipeline) - WayUp (college-jobsite intermediary) - TikTok (real employer with unusual entry-share) - AaraTechnologies (aggregator)

Recommendation. Downstream tasks should use the J3 variant of the specialist list as the primary flag, not the "any-variant" list. The file entry_specialist_employers.csv has junior_share_j3 and junior_share_j1_j2 columns so downstream can filter as needed. Specialists with junior_share_j3 > 0.60 AND junior_share_j1_j2 > 0.30 are the unambiguous junior-heavy employers.

docs.title: Step 7. Aggregator profile
docs.text:
source agg n_postings n_companies mean desc J1% J2% mean YOE J3% arshkon F 4,261 2,235 3,359 3.9% 4.2% 5.48 13.4% arshkon T 430 74 2,780 2.6% 2.8% 5.54 9.0% asaniczka F 15,186 4,814 3,805 1.4% 1.7% 5.99 9.1% asaniczka T 2,943 187 4,276 0.7% 0.8% 6.35 4.3% scraped F 37,609 7,740 5,097 3.8% 4.1% 5.30 14.7% scraped T 7,708 1,052 3,899 3.1% 3.2% 5.67 11.3%

Aggregator share by source (of filtered SWE): arshkon 9.1%, asaniczka 16.2%, scraped 17.0%. The scraped aggregator share is almost 2× the 2024 arshkon share.

Aggregators differ systematically: - Lower mean description length in arshkon and scraped (shorter, boilerplate-heavy postings) - Higher mean description length in asaniczka (unusual — investigate if the asaniczka aggregator pool is substantively different) - Lower entry shares under J3 in all sources - Higher mean YOE in asaniczka and scraped

Cross-source implication. Aggregator share shifts across sources are a confound for any seniority-stratified cross-source comparison. Always report with and without aggregators.

docs.title: Step 8. New entrants vs returning companies
docs.text:
Using arshkon ∪ asaniczka as 2024 cohort, scraped as 2026:

metric value n_companies_2024 (union) 6,504 n_companies_2026 8,477 new entrants in 2026 6,368 returning in 2026 (∩ 2024) 2,109

2,109 companies are returning — that's 25% of 2026 unique companies but 55% of 2026 postings (24,927 / 45,317). New entrants (6,368 companies) carry 20,390 postings (45%).

Content profile of new entrants vs returning (scraped, SWE, non-agg within default filters):

cohort n n companies mean desc J1 share J2 share mean YOE J3 share new entrant 20,390 6,368 4,615 3.85% 4.01% 5.36 12.8% returning 24,927 2,109 5,121 3.47% 3.83% 5.34 15.3%

Returning companies skew to slightly longer descriptions and slightly higher J3 entry share. The differences are modest but real.

This is the input to the returning_companies_cohort.csv artifact consumed by T37 and T38. Saved to exploration/artifacts/shared/returning_companies_cohort.csv.

docs.title: Step 9. Per-finding concentration prediction table (IMPORTANT)
docs.text:
Below is the concentration-risk prediction for each major Wave 2+/Wave 3+ analysis category. This is the most consequential output of T06 for orchestration.

Finding category Naive concentration risk Why Recommended default Aggregate entry share (J1) Low on distribution, High on interpretation 77–87% of ≥5-post companies have zero J1; aggregate driven by a small subset Report with J3 primary, aggregator-stratified Aggregate entry share (J3) High Under J3, 40% of firms have nonzero but the within-firm decomposition is ~0; the aggregate change is compositional Report within- vs between-company decomposition in every section; cite the entry-specialist list Corpus-wide term frequency (TF/TF-IDF) High Jobs via Dice (4.3% share), Google/Microsoft/Amazon/AWS (big descriptions with distinctive vocab), Walmart/PayPal/Anduril long postings Cap per-company at n=50 or weight by 1/sqrt(n); dedup by description_hash; exclude aggregators from primary AI mention rate Medium Within-company +31pp / between +14pp — the signal is reasonably robust but AWS, Google, Microsoft are heavy AI employers with many postings Report cap=50, weight-per-company, and with/without tech-giants; present raw too Description length mean Medium Walmart (9kL), PayPal (9k), Anduril (8.3k), AWS (6.8k) are 2× median; Motion Recruitment/Jobs via Dice shorter Report median too; cap per-company or present distribution Topic model / archetype discovery High A few big employers with stylized long descriptions (Walmart, PayPal, Meta templates) can seed topics Dedup by description_hash, per-company downsample before fitting; inspect topics for employer-boilerplate Co-occurrence networks / tech ecosystem Medium AWS/Amazon bias toward cloud stack; Jobs via Dice pulls the graph toward Microsoft/Java/SQL stack Stratify by employer type; cap; present returning-company subset as sensitivity Embedding / semantic landscape Medium-High Big employers with templated text will cluster tightly; centroids may be employer-specific Dedup, cap, and inspect clusters for employer composition Title taxonomy evolution Medium Title long tail is noisy (see T05); big employers set title conventions (Amazon "Software Development Engineer", Google "Software Engineer III") Report title-frequency with and without tech-giants; use ≥3-count filter Geographic metro-level Low per metro But scraper design artifact dominates cross-metro composition (T05) Restrict to per-metro analysis; avoid cross-metro aggregates Seniority boundary clarity (T20) Medium Tech giants drive "mid-senior" label saturation Report tech-giant subset separately Senior-role evolution (T21) High Google, Microsoft, Meta set senior-role conventions; staffing dominates raw senior postings Dedup, cap, or subset; report returning-company subset as sensitivity Requirements complexity (T11) High Walmart/PayPal posting structure vs Motion Recruitment brevity — pulled in opposite directions by composition Within-company change + decomposition; report cap Ghost/aspirational (T22) Medium Aggregator postings have different ghost profile; Motion Recruitment, Jobs via Dice patterns matter Stratify by aggregator; report per-company where relevant Returning-companies sensitivity (T37) Central This task is the sensitivity check — 55% of 2026 postings are in the returning cohort Use cohort directly Hiring-selectivity × scope correlation (T38) High Correlation is weighted by posting volume which is concentrated Use company as unit of observation, not posting

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Entry-level posting is a minority employer activity, not a market-wide one. 58–63% of ≥5-post companies have ZERO entry-labeled postings under J3. This reframes every aggregate "entry share X" claim as really a claim about a specific minority subset. The "entry share" metric in aggregate is a weighted average where most firms contribute 0 — it has high leverage from a small number of employers.

  2. Within-company entry-share change was roughly zero (2024→2026) in the overlap panel under J3. The +5pp aggregate rise is almost entirely between-company. This is genuinely important for Wave ⅔ — "the entry-share gap" is compositional, not behavioral for the same firms.

  3. J4 (yoe_llm ≤ 3) flags Amazon, AWS, Google, Microsoft, PNC, Discover Financial as "entry specialists" with 60–80% shares. The reason is that big-tech uses quantized YOE bands — Google scraped has 187 roles at yoe_llm=2, 139 at 5, 111 at 8 (bimodal); Microsoft scraped has 83 at yoe=2, 83 at yoe=4. A "3-year" cutoff lands right in the middle of the lower band. Do not use J4 as a primary entry criterion in downstream work. The task spec was wise to designate J3 as primary.

  4. AI mention rate 2024→2026 is mostly within-company (+31pp out of +39pp total). This is one of the cleanest "same employer, new behavior" patterns in the panel. If the AI-hiring-wave claim is going to hold up, this is the clean version.

  5. Duplicate-template audit is essentially empty after Stage-4 multi-location dedup. Only 6 companies in scraped have any residual dup-ratio ≥ 2 with n ≥ 5. This is the strongest evidence that the multi-location collapse in Stage 4 is working as intended. Multi-location flag carries 7.4% of scraped SWE rows and concentrates in Meta, Deloitte, CoreWeave, Turing — all plausible cases of legitimate cross-metro postings.

  6. Asaniczka HHI triples when aggregators are included (19 non-agg → 54 all). Jobs-for-Humanity→Capital One aggregator resolution adds 571 more postings to an already-large Capital One cohort. Asaniczka is more aggregator-dependent than the other sources.

  7. The returning-companies cohort is a tighter analytical frame. 2,109 companies account for 55% of scraped SWE postings. Restricting cross-period comparisons to this cohort mechanically controls for the "new-entrant dilution" and "departed-company selection" effects. This is exactly why T37 exists, and this artifact is ready to consume.

  8. "Jobs via Dice" alone (1,934 postings, 4.3% of scraped SWE) is the single biggest concentration lever in any corpus-level scraped analysis. Excluding aggregators removes it; the is_aggregator flag does this correctly in the unified artifact.

  9. New entrants in 2026 (6,368 companies) have broader company representation but shorter postings (4,615 vs 5,121 chars) and lower J3 share (12.8% vs 15.3%) than returning companies. A "new entrants are drifting toward non-entry roles" pattern is visible but weak.

  10. Salesforce (not listed in top-20) and other SaaS vendors are under-represented relative to expectation. Observed in the top-20 scan but not in the top-20 itself for any source. Worth noting for Wave 2 tech-ecosystem analyses — if Salesforce ecosystem titles matter, they will come from the long tail.

docs.title: exploration/artifacts/shared/entry_specialist_employers.csv
docs.text:
Schema (as required): - company_name_canonical - n_swe_postings - junior_share_j3 (value -1 sentinel for companies with no LLM-labeled rows — downstream should treat as "no J3 evidence") - junior_share_j1_j2 = max(J1 share, J2 share) - is_aggregator (bool) - specialist_category ∈ {staffing, college-jobsite, tech-giant-intern, bulk-consulting (not observed), direct-employer, aggregator}

206 rows. Category distribution: | category | count | n_postings sum | |---|---|---| | direct-employer | 169 | 2,394 | | staffing | 18 | 141 | | aggregator | 9 | 121 | | tech-giant-intern | 6 | 1,100 | | college-jobsite | 4 | 22 |

Downstream caveat. Under J3 alone, the true entry-specialist set is substantially smaller (~40 companies). Filter to junior_share_j3 > 0.60 for the strict J3-primary definition. Filter to junior_share_j1_j2 > 0.60 for the platform-label-primary definition.

docs.title: exploration/artifacts/shared/returning_companies_cohort.csv
docs.text:
Schema (as required): - company_name_canonical - n_swe_2024 (from arshkon ∪ asaniczka) - n_swe_2026 (from scraped) - is_aggregator

2,109 rows. Top rows by combined volume: Jobs via Dice (n26=1,934), Capital One (n24=710, n26=247), Northrop Grumman (n24=295, n26=192), etc.

docs.title: Recommended default for Wave ⅔ aggregates
docs.text:
For any corpus-wide aggregate claim 2024→2026, the default should be to present at least two of: - Raw aggregate (what you naively get). - Per-company weight-capped (n_per_co ≤ 50) or weighted by 1/sqrt(n_per_co). - Restricted to the returning-company cohort (n=2,109 companies). - With and without is_aggregator exclusion. - Under both J1 and J3 for any entry-related metric.

If two variants agree, the finding is robust. If they disagree, report both and explain the driver (composition vs within-firm).

docs.title: T07 — External benchmarks & power analysis
docs.text: Agent: D (Wave 1) Date: 2026-04-20 Inputs: data/unified.parquet; BLS OES state file state_M2024_dl.xlsx (May 2024 release, fetched via Wayback); FRED JTU5100JOL (JOLTS Information, NSA) and JTSJOL (JOLTS total nonfarm, SA). T30 panel CSV status at run time: not yet published — definitions computed locally per spec.

docs.title: Headline
docs.text: Cross-period comparisons are well-powered for total SWE volume, most senior definitions, and the YOE-based junior definitions (J3, J4), but underpowered for native-label-only entry (J1) in the arshkon-vs-scraped comparison. Pooling arshkon + asaniczka as the 2024 baseline rescues J3/J4 into "strong" territory. S2 (director-only) is severely underpowered across all comparisons. State-level geographic correlation with BLS OES is r = 0.97 linear, 0.94 log-log for pooled 2024 — well above the r > 0.80 target. JOLTS Information openings in 2026 YTD (97k NSA avg) are 0.71× the 2023 average (136.8k), confirming we are comparing a mid/late-cycle 2024 snapshot to a weak 2026 scraped window — any "fewer postings" finding must control for aggregate hiring weakness.

docs.title: Part A. Feasibility (primary deliverable)
docs.text: Feasibility table: exploration/tables/T07/feasibility_table.csv (44 rows = 22 comparison×definition × 2 specs {agg_included, agg_excluded}).

docs.title: Key group sizes (aggregator-included, SWE, LinkedIn, English, date_ok)
docs.text:
All numbers are SWE-post rows after default filters.

Group arshkon asaniczka pooled 2024 scraped ALL SWE 4,691 18,129 22,820 45,317 J1 (entry) 177 238 415 1,650 J2 (entry+associate) 192 277 469 1,771 J3 (LLM YOE ≤ 2, primary) 449 1,218 1,667 2,911 J4 (LLM YOE ≤ 3) 941 2,936 3,877 5,927 S1 (mid-senior+director) 2,031 11,634 13,665 20,028 S2 (director only) 17 74 91 325 S3 (title keyword senior) 1,804 11,137 12,941 18,984 S4 (LLM YOE ≥ 5, primary) 2,246 10,648 12,894 12,959

docs.title: MDE verdicts at 80% power, α=0.05, two-sided
docs.text:
Aggregator-included (main spec). Verdicts use MDE_binary at p=0.5 baseline — the reported MDE table also includes p=0.25 and the continuous Cohen's d.

comparison seniority_def n1 n2 MDE_binary (p=0.5) MDE_binary (p=0.25) MDE_d verdict arshkon vs scraped J1 177 1,650 0.111 0.096 0.222 underpowered arshkon vs scraped J2 192 1,771 0.106 0.092 0.213 underpowered arshkon vs scraped J3 (primary) 449 2,911 0.071 0.061 0.142 underpowered (borderline) arshkon vs scraped J4 941 5,927 0.049 0.043 0.098 ok arshkon vs scraped S1 2,031 20,028 0.033 0.028 0.065 ok arshkon vs scraped S2 17 325 0.349 0.302 0.697 underpowered (n<30) arshkon vs scraped S3 1,804 18,984 0.035 0.030 0.069 ok arshkon vs scraped S4 (primary) 2,246 12,959 0.032 0.028 0.064 ok arshkon vs scraped ALL-SWE 4,691 45,317 0.022 0.019 0.043 strong pooled 2024 vs scraped J1 415 1,650 0.077 0.067 0.154 underpowered pooled 2024 vs scraped J2 469 1,771 0.073 0.063 0.146 underpowered pooled 2024 vs scraped J3 (primary) 1,667 2,911 0.043 0.037 0.086 ok pooled 2024 vs scraped J4 3,877 5,927 0.029 0.025 0.058 strong pooled 2024 vs scraped S1 13,665 20,028 0.016 0.013 0.031 strong pooled 2024 vs scraped S2 91 325 0.166 0.144 0.332 underpowered pooled 2024 vs scraped S3 12,941 18,984 0.016 0.014 0.032 strong pooled 2024 vs scraped S4 (primary) 12,894 12,959 0.017 0.015 0.035 strong pooled 2024 vs scraped ALL-SWE 22,820 45,317 0.011 0.010 0.023 strong

Recommended Wave 2 primaries (orchestrator input):

  1. Primary junior definition = J3 (YOE ≤ 2), with pooled 2024 baseline. MDE = 4.3 pp (p=0.5). This lets us detect a shift from e.g. 14 → 10 pp with 80% power.
  2. Sensitivity junior definitions = J4 (YOE ≤ 3) and J2 (entry+associate, arshkon-only). J1 is reserved for descriptive only — it cannot support a claim in the arshkon-vs-scraped comparison.
  3. Primary senior definition = S4 (YOE ≥ 5) with pooled 2024 baseline. MDE = 1.7 pp.
  4. Sensitivity senior definitions = S1 and S3. S2 (director-only) is underpowered everywhere — descriptive only.

Aggregator-exclusion robustness. Aggregator exclusion drops pooled 2024 SWE from 22,820 → 19,447 (−15%) and scraped SWE from 45,317 → 37,609 (−17%). Verdicts flip in one place: J4 for arshkon-vs-scraped downgrades from strong to ok (0.049 → 0.051 MDE p=0.5) — still usable. J3 stays underpowered in arshkon-only. No definition that was "ok" in included becomes underpowered in excluded. The feasibility claims are robust to aggregator treatment.

docs.title: Metro-level feasibility
docs.text:
Multi-location SWE postings excluded from per-metro rollups (correct by design):

source multi-location unresolved metro with metro total SWE arshkon 116 1,805 2,770 4,691 asaniczka 894 4,343 12,892 18,129 scraped 3,344 8,036 33,937 45,317

So for the per-metro analyses, 62% of arshkon SWE, 71% of asaniczka SWE, and 75% of scraped SWE are assigned to a metro. The remainder split roughly 50/50 between multi-location representatives and unresolved metros. Multi-location exclusion is ~2-7% of SWE by source.

Metro-level qualification (≥50 SWE in both 2024 baseline and scraped):

  • Pooled 2024 baseline — 26 metros qualify at ≥50, 25 metros at ≥100. This covers the full 26-metro study frame except New York City Metro (which only has 67 SWE in pooled 2024 despite 2,220 in scraped — a huge asymmetry).
  • Arshkon-only baseline — 18 metros qualify at ≥50, only 8 at ≥100. Arshkon is too small to support metro-level arshkon-only claims for most markets.

Conclusion: metro-level analyses should use the pooled 2024 baseline. NYC is a notable asymmetry — arshkon has only 7 SWE rows there and asaniczka has 60, versus 2,220 scraped. NYC's 2024 baseline is compositionally different from its 2026 scraped, likely because arshkon underrepresents NYC in 2024 (this is a surprise — see below).

Tables: exploration/tables/T07/metro_counts.csv, metro_exclusion_summary.csv, metro_qualification_summary.csv.

docs.title: Company-overlap panel feasibility
docs.text:
After default filters, the number of companies (keyed on company_name_canonical) posting SWE jobs in both a 2024 baseline and scraped:

threshold arshkon-only baseline pooled 2024 baseline any overlap — 2,109 (1,934 agg-excl.) ≥3 SWE both 243 (210) 609 (539) ≥5 SWE both 125 (103) 356 (312)

T16 overlap panel: 356 companies at the ≥5 threshold is a strong panel. T16 can comfortably support 100-200 company-level change vectors even after restricting further.

T31 same-co × same-title pairs:

threshold (arshkon-only) n pairs (pooled 2024) n pairs ≥1 in both 350 ≥1 in both 866 ≥2 in both 68 ≥2 in both 191 ≥3 in both 29 ≥3 in both 94

T31's same-co × same-title design has a workable n=191 pairs at the ≥2 threshold with pooled 2024 baseline. At ≥3, n drops to 94 — still usable for non-parametric tests. Arshkon-only at ≥2 has only 68 pairs — too thin for stratified per-pair analyses.

Tables: company_overlap_summary.csv, company_title_pair_summary.csv, company_overlap_top200.csv.

docs.title: B1. BLS OES state-level correlation (geographic validation)
docs.text:
Fetched May 2024 OEWS state file (state_M2024_dl.xlsx) from Wayback Machine (direct BLS fetch blocked 403). Filtered to SOC 15-1252 (Software Developers) and 15-1253 (Software QA Analysts and Testers).

Note on SOC codes: The dispatch referenced SOC 15-1256 for QA/testers; the correct current SOC code is 15-1253. SOC 15-1256 does not appear in the May 2024 OES state file. Used 15-1253 throughout.

National totals (50-state sum): 15-1252 = 1,601,380; 15-1253 = ~185k (only states with enough sample size publish). Combined ≈ 1.79M, consistent with the BLS-reported ~1.7M software developer national employment for 2024.

Pearson correlations — state-level count vs BLS OES total (15-1252 + 15-1253):

our_source linear r log-log r (log1p) arshkon 0.975 0.935 asaniczka 0.964 0.933 pooled 2024 0.974 0.940 scraped 0.974 0.793

All exceed r > 0.80 target. The log-log correlation is the more honest metric (linear correlation is driven almost entirely by the California-Texas-New York anchor points). The scraped data's lower log-log r (0.79, just below target) reflects the scraper's metro-centric coverage — many small states have zero scraped postings because no metro in those states was searched; BLS shows small but nonzero employment there. This is not a validity concern but a known coverage artifact.

State-share comparison (share of our SWE vs share of BLS employment): r = 0.974 for pooled 2024 and 0.974 for scraped, essentially identical — our geographic distribution closely matches BLS.

Table: bls_oes_state_counts_merged.csv, bls_oes_correlation_summary.csv. Figure: bls_oes_scatter.png.

docs.title: B2. Industry distribution
docs.text:
Only arshkon (99.2% coverage) and scraped (100%) have company_industry. Asaniczka has none. BLS does not publish an industry × state × occupation cross for the state file, so no direct BLS industry benchmark.

Between-period industry distribution shift (arshkon 2024 → scraped 2026):

industry arshkon share scraped share pp change Software Development 13.1% 26.2% +13.1 IT Services and IT Consulting 25.8% 12.6% −13.2 Technology, Information and Internet 2.3% 4.9% +2.6 Information Services 3.2% 0.4% −2.8 Staffing and Recruiting 5.8% 2.7% −3.1 Defense and Space Manufacturing 2.6% 3.8% +1.2 Financial Services 8.4% 7.0% −1.4

The "Software Development ↔ IT Services" swap is suspiciously symmetrical (+13.1 vs −13.2 pp) and combined with several new multi-label industries appearing in scraped only ("Information Services and Technology, Information and Internet", multi-industry combos), this is very likely a LinkedIn industry-taxonomy change between 2024 and 2026, not a real market shift. Industry-based analyses must therefore treat industry labels as potentially non-comparable between periods. Downstream tasks should either (a) pool Software Development + IT Services into a single "software industry" bucket, or (b) avoid industry-stratified comparisons entirely.

Table: industry_distribution_ours.csv, industry_shift_arshkon_vs_scraped.csv, industry_summary.csv.

docs.title: B3. JOLTS Information-sector context
docs.text:
Fetched JTU5100JOL (Information Job Openings, NSA, thousands) via Wayback — data through 2026-02.

period avg openings (NSA, thousands) ratio vs 2023 2023 avg 136.8 1.000 2024 avg 122.5 0.896 2025 avg 121.2 0.886 2026 YTD (Jan–Feb) avg 97.0 0.709 2026-02 (latest) 92 0.672

Dispatch note check: The dispatch estimated 2026 Info openings at ~0.66× the 2023 average. Current FRED data through 2026-02 shows 0.71× YTD, with the single most recent month at 0.67×. The estimate was approximately right, slightly pessimistic on the YTD but well aligned with the latest month.

Total nonfarm (JTSJOL, SA): 2023 avg 9,268k → 2026 YTD 7,061k (0.762× of 2023). So the Information sector has fallen ~30% from its 2023 peak while total nonfarm has fallen ~24% — Information sector is contracting faster than the overall economy.

Implications for the analysis:

  1. Our 2024 baseline (arshkon ≈ April 2024; asaniczka ≈ January 2024) sits at 0.90× the 2023 peak — the 2024 market was already softer than 2023 but still well above the 2026 trough.
  2. Our 2026 scraped window (March–April 2026) is in the weakest hiring environment of the whole series since 2020.
  3. Any "fewer junior postings" finding MUST control for aggregate openings. The information-sector opening count fell ~30% from 2024 (122.5k avg) to 2026 YTD (97k) — a 21% volume decline. If our SWE posting count falls by 21%, that's the macro baseline, not entry-level-specific. Entry-level claims must be expressed as share of SWE postings, not absolute count.
  4. The weak hiring environment means composition effects dominate: if employers post fewer jobs overall, they may disproportionately keep the senior openings, shifting the composition in favor of senior roles. Interpretation of any senior-share increase needs to account for this.

Figures: jolts_information_series.png, jolts_total_nonfarm.png. Tables: jolts_annual.csv, jolts_context_summary.csv.

docs.title: Framing: what population does our sample represent?
docs.text:
What we sample: U.S. LinkedIn software-engineering job postings, English-language, successfully date-validated, in the default-filtered analysis frame.

What we can generalize to: - The LinkedIn SWE job-posting market. BLS OES state-level correlation of 0.94 log-log (pooled 2024) confirms our geographic distribution is consistent with the full BLS employment footprint — we are not sampling a geographically idiosyncratic subset. - The 26-metro study frame is well-populated in both periods at the pooled 2024 baseline.

What we cannot generalize to: - Non-LinkedIn posting channels (Indeed is available as sensitivity only; other channels absent). - Actual hires, only postings. JOLTS is openings data, not hires — our posting counts are a leading signal, not an outcome. - Seasonal comparisons. Arshkon snapshot ≈ April 2024; asaniczka ≈ January 2024; scraped ≈ March–April 2026. There is a mild seasonality effect but 2024 and 2026 are both early-year, minimizing seasonal confound. - Non-English markets.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. SOC 15-1256 does not exist in the May 2024 OES file; the correct code is 15-1253 (Software QA Analysts and Testers). The dispatch's referenced code appears to be from an older or different SOC edition. Used 15-1253 without material change to the conclusion (r > 0.94 pooled 2024).
  2. "Software Development" and "IT Services and IT Consulting" almost exactly swapped their shares (−13.2 pp and +13.1 pp) between arshkon and scraped. Combined with new multi-label industry combinations appearing only in scraped, this strongly suggests a LinkedIn taxonomy change rather than a real industry shift. Industry-based analysis is therefore unreliable across the 2024↔2026 comparison at the raw-label level.
  3. New York City Metro is extremely underrepresented in arshkon (7 SWE rows) — 312× smaller than scraped (2,220). This is not a multi-location or metro-assignment issue but appears to be a genuine arshkon sampling gap. NYC analyses should use the pooled 2024 baseline (67 SWE) or be reported arshkon-only with this caveat stamped on them.
  4. S2 (director-only) is underpowered in every comparison — n=17 in arshkon, n=91 pooled. Director-level claims based on the native seniority enum cannot be supported by this dataset. Senior-role drift analyses should use S1/S3/S4, not S2.
  5. Scraped not_selected rows are large (19,494 of 45,317 SWE) — 43% of the scraped SWE sample is outside the sticky LLM frame. Analyses that require LLM labels (J3, J4, S4, and anything using description_core_llm) drop to 25,816 scraped SWE. Still workable, but substantially smaller than the full 45,317.
  6. Asaniczka's SWE count after default filters is 18,129 — more than 3× arshkon's 4,691. If pooled 2024 is accepted as the baseline, asaniczka dominates it 4:1. This means "pooled 2024" conclusions are really "asaniczka conclusions, lightly augmented by arshkon." If the two sources disagree on direction (which the native entry-label asymmetry guarantees for entry share), pooled results will lean asaniczka-heavy.
  7. Scraped log-log correlation with BLS OES is 0.79 — just below the 0.80 target. The linear correlation is well above, but the log-log reflects that scraped has zero postings in 15+ small states because the scraper's metro frame doesn't cover them. This is a known coverage design choice, not an instrument failure — but analyses that describe "the SWE market nationally" should flag that the scraped data describes the 26-metro subset, not the long tail.
  8. The 2026 hiring environment is unusually weak (Information openings at ~0.71× of 2023 average; total nonfarm at ~0.76×). Pre-registering that our "decline in X" findings will be interpretable only as share-of-SWE, not volume, is essential. The dispatch's 0.66× estimate for Information openings was approximately correct.
  9. J3 (YOE ≤ 2) arshkon-only has just 449 rows and fails the MDE threshold. Interpret "YOE ≤ 2" findings cautiously in the arshkon-vs-scraped comparison — they require the pooled baseline to be statistically resolvable.

docs.title: Handoff notes for Gate 1
docs.text:
• Orchestrator Gate 1 action item: choose Wave 2 primary junior definition. Recommended: J3 (YOE ≤ 2) with pooled 2024 baseline as primary; J4 as robustness; J2 (entry+associate) as native-label sensitivity (arshkon-only if asaniczka will bias the entry rate). J1 for descriptive only.
  • Orchestrator Gate 1 action item: choose Wave 2 primary senior definition. Recommended: S4 (YOE ≥ 5) with pooled 2024 baseline as primary; S1 and S3 as robustness. S2 for descriptive only.
  • Metro frame recommendation: use pooled 2024 baseline (26 qualifying metros). Flag NYC arshkon gap.
  • Industry recommendation: pool Software Development + IT Services as a single bucket, or avoid industry-stratified comparisons. Do not report the raw label shift as a substantive finding — it is almost certainly a taxonomy artifact.
  • Hiring-cycle caveat: all cross-period claims must be framed as shares, not volumes. The 2026 scraped window is in a weak-hiring trough.

docs.title: T08 — Distribution profiling & anomaly detection
docs.text: Agent: E Run date: 2026-04-20 Inputs: data/unified.parquet (SWE LinkedIn universe; 68,137 rows), exploration/artifacts/shared/* Default filter: is_swe AND source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english AND date_flag='ok' Scripts: exploration/scripts/T08_distribution_profiling.py, T08_supplemental.py, T08_within_between.py, T08_within_between_strict.py Tables: exploration/tables/T08/*.csv (28 CSVs) Figures: exploration/figures/T08/fig1_desc_length.png, fig2_yoe_llm.png, fig3_seniority_final.png, fig4_effect_ranking.png

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text: The junior-share rise 2024→2026 is not uniformly between-company as Gate 1 claimed — the "within-company ≈ 0" claim is specific to the arshkon-only ≥5 panel (n=125) and does not replicate under pooled, asaniczka-only, or looser size cuts, where within-company J3 rise is +3.5 to +6.4 pp (weighted) and accounts for most of the aggregate delta. The Gate 1 decomposition is materially sensitive to overlap-panel construction. Separately: the pattern inverts within senior-titled postings (sr/senior SWE titles show J3 drop from 4.3% → 2.5% while generic SWE rises 8.6% → 15.9%), suggesting role redefinition, not pure composition. AI-mention rise (+16.7 pp aggregate, +14.2 pp within-company on returning cohort) is robust to length, labeled-coverage, and aggregator sensitivities.

docs.title: 1. Methodology
docs.text:
• Universe: 68,137 SWE LinkedIn rows under default filter. arshkon 4,691 (period 2024-04); asaniczka 18,129 (2024-01); scraped 45,317 (2026-03/04).
  • LLM labeled coverage: 2024 sources 99.9%+; scraped 56.9% (not_selected 43.0%, deferred 0.02%). The scraped labeled subset is the only place yoe_min_years_llm exists (table labeled_split_by_source.csv).
  • Primary seniority operationalizations: J3 yoe_min_years_llm ≤ 2 (labeled), S4 yoe_min_years_llm ≥ 5 (labeled). J1 / J2 use seniority_final. All four junior definitions and all four senior definitions reported as T30 panels.
  • Baseline calibration SNR: loaded from calibration_table.csv and joined to every cross-period finding.
  • Company-cap sensitivity: 20 rows per company_name_canonical per period (junior_senior_cap20.csv). Aggregator exclusion, entry-specialist exclusion, strict SWE-tier subset are additional sensitivities.

docs.title: 2a. Description length — anomaly detected
docs.text:
Source Period n Mean Median p10 p90 p99 arshkon 2024-04 4,691 3,306 2,974 816 6,218 9,156 asaniczka 2024-01 18,129 3,881 3,733 1,255 6,580 9,513 scraped 2026-03 19,740 4,975 4,916 1,909 8,002 10,899 scraped 2026-04 25,577 4,830 4,773 1,761 7,875 10,852

Length buckets (table length_bucket_shares.csv):

Bucket 2024 share 2026 share ≤500 (too short?) 2.5% 0.6% 501–2,000 20.8% 11.5% 2,001–4,000 33.8% 24.8% 4,001–7,000 35.8% 44.7% 7,001–15,000 7.0% 18.3% >15,000 0.04% 0.07%

Anomaly: the 7,001–15,000 char bucket triples 2024→2026 (7% → 18%). This is the single largest shift in the length distribution and consistent with scraped postings having longer disclosure/boilerplate sections (EEO, pay transparency, benefits). Calibration (desc_length_mean): SNR 1.81 (near_noise) — a real shift, but a chunk is within-2024 noise.

Length percentiles (pooled): 2024 p50 = 3,594 chars; 2026 p50 = 4,842 chars (delta +1,248 chars at median).

Anomaly: within arshkon, description_length mean varies 4× by company_size quartile (Q1 = 2,223 chars at ≤92 employees; Q4 = 4,686 chars at ≥9,287). Larger companies write substantially longer postings even in 2024. This is source of "length growth" confounding with company-size shift.

docs.title: 2b. YOE distribution
docs.text:
yoe_min_years_llm (labeled only, table yoe_llm_by_source_period.csv):

Source Period n_labeled Mean Median sd J3 (≤2) S4 (≥5) arshkon 2024-04 3,454 5.48 5.0 2.80 13.0% 65.0% asaniczka 2024-01 14,765 6.05 5.0 3.13 8.2% 72.1% scraped 2026-03 9,371 5.34 5.0 2.86 14.5% 63.5% scraped 2026-04 11,140 5.36 5.0 2.88 14.0% 62.9%

Anomaly: YOE median is flat at 5 across all four snapshots. The entire "junior rise" happens at the left tail of the YOE distribution, not the median — below the 20th percentile.

Anomaly (inverse of prior report): arshkon has a higher J3 share than asaniczka within 2024 (13.0% vs 8.2%) — +4.8 pp within-2024 asymmetry. The scraped 2026 value (14.2%) is closer to arshkon 2024 than to asaniczka 2024. Calibration SNR = 1.06, i.e. just barely above within-2024 noise — most of the aggregate "pooled→2026" J3 rise (+5.0 pp) is absorbed by the asymmetry in which 2024 source you use as baseline.

docs.title: 2c. seniority_final
docs.text:
Shares by source (table seniority_final_by_source.csv):

seniority_final arshkon 2024-04 asaniczka 2024-01 scraped 2026-03 scraped 2026-04 entry 3.8% 1.3% 4.3% 3.1% associate 0.3% 0.2% 0.3% 0.3% mid-senior 42.9% 63.8% 43.8% 43.2% director 0.4% 0.4% 0.8% 0.7% unknown 52.6% 34.3% 50.8% 52.7%

Anomaly: 50–53% of SWE rows are seniority_final='unknown' in arshkon and scraped; asaniczka is 34.3%. The 18 pp arshkon-vs-asaniczka spread is larger than any cross-period effect. The director share (S2) is effectively 0 everywhere.

seniority_final_source breakdown for scraped is mixed-provenance: 2026-03 is 60% LLM + 17% title_keyword + 23% unknown; 2026-04 is 55% LLM + 19% title_keyword + 26% unknown. Scraped has more rule-labeled rows (stage 5 title_keyword hits were expanded).

docs.title: 2d. Arshkon native-entry quality diagnostic
docs.text:
For seniority_native='entry' on arshkon (table arshkon_native_yoe_profile.csv):

Native n n_w_yoe YOE mean YOE median Share YOE≤2 Share YOE≥5 entry 738 543 4.21 4.0 27.8% 44.9% intern 20 1 2.00 2.0 100% 0% associate 263 198 3.90 3.0 29.3% 34.3% mid-senior 2,231 1,724 5.92 5.0 6.0% 75.2% director 20 15 9.60 10.0 0% 100%

Major finding: Arshkon native='entry' has median YOE = 4 years and 45% have YOE ≥ 5. This is not a clean entry-level signal — arshkon's platform-native "entry" label is more like "junior-ish" with heavy mid-level contamination. Implication: seniority_native is not a usable sanity-check baseline for entry-level findings even on arshkon alone. This sharpens the Gate 1 memo's "seniority_native effectively unusable" position — it's unusable not only for temporal stability but also cross-sectionally.

docs.title: 2e. T30 panel — junior and senior 4-row tables (primary)
docs.text:
Table t30_panel_juniorsenior.csv. All values are share of denominator (J3/J4/S4: labeled rows; J1/J2/S1/S2/S3: all rows).

Junior panel (pooled-2024 baseline primary):

Def Denom 2024 Denom 2026 2024 pooled 2026 pooled Arshkon Asaniczka Pooled Δ pp Arshkon-only Δ pp Within-2024 Δ pp Direction J1 22,820 45,317 1.82% 3.64% 3.77% 1.31% +1.82 −0.13 −2.46 up J2 22,820 45,317 2.06% 3.91% 4.09% 1.53% +1.85 −0.18 −2.57 up J3 18,219 20,511 9.15% 14.19% 13.00% 8.25% +5.04 +1.19 −4.75 up J4 18,219 20,511 21.28% 28.90% 27.24% 19.88% +7.62 +1.65 −7.36 up

Direction: all four UP on pooled-2024 baseline. But arshkon-only-vs-2026: J1/J2 FLIP to negative, J3 is barely above noise (+1.2 pp with calibration SNR ≈ 1.06), J4 is +1.7 pp. The "junior rise" magnitude depends critically on which 2024 source is used as baseline.

Senior panel:

Def Denom 2024 Denom 2026 2024 pooled 2026 pooled Arshkon Asaniczka Pooled Δ pp Arshkon-only Δ pp Within-2024 Δ pp Direction S1 22,820 45,317 59.88% 44.20% 43.30% 64.17% −15.69 +0.90 +20.88 down S2 22,820 45,317 0.40% 0.72% 0.36% 0.41% +0.32 +0.35 +0.05 flat S3 22,820 45,317 56.71% 41.89% 38.46% 61.43% −14.82 +3.43 +22.98 down S4 18,219 20,511 70.77% 63.18% 65.03% 72.12% −7.59 −1.85 +7.09 down

Direction on pooled 2026 vs pooled 2024: 3 of 4 DOWN (S2 flat). On arshkon-only-vs-2026: S1 and S3 actually flip UP (+0.9 and +3.4 pp), S4 is mildly down (−1.9 pp, below noise). The asaniczka-arshkon senior asymmetry is so large it dominates the pooled effect. This is consistent with T30 but the magnitude flip is worth highlighting.

docs.title: 3. Calibration table (SNR)
docs.text:
Loaded from calibration_table.csv (Prep step 6). Full table preserved in exploration/tables/T08/ranked_metrics.csv. Top 8 by absolute cross-period effect, with SNR:

Metric Arshkon Asaniczka Scraped Cross-period Within-2024 SNR ratio Flag distinct_techs_per_posting_mean 5.66 5.34 7.46 2.06 0.32 6.4 above description_length_mean 3,306 3,881 4,893 d=0.50 d=0.28 1.8 near tech_count_per_posting_mean 5.66 5.34 7.46 d=0.40 d=0.07 5.8 above ai_mention_broad 16.5% 15.1% 51.0% +35.6 pp +1.4 pp 25.4 above soft_skill_term_count_mean 1.57 1.64 1.93 d=0.30 d=0.06 4.8 above scope_term_rate 32.8% 32.3% 53.4% +21.0 pp +0.5 pp 42.8 above s1_midsen_or_director_share 43.3% 64.2% 44.2% −15.7 pp +20.9 pp 0.75 below ai_mention_strict 1.4% 1.0% 14.4% +13.3 pp +0.4 pp 32.9 above

docs.title: Key calibration notes
docs.text:
• AI, scope, tech count, soft-skill count are well above noise. Claims on these at pooled 2024 → 2026 survive within-2024 scrutiny.
  • J3 (0.05/0.047 = SNR 1.06) is only barely above within-2024 noise. Every J3 cross-period claim must be reported with arshkon-only magnitude alongside (Gate 1 pre-commit).
  • J1 (SNR 0.74), J2 (0.72), S1 (0.75), and education mentions (0.03–0.20) are all below noise. Do not make bare cross-period claims on these.

docs.title: 4. Ranked change list (top 20 metrics × SNR)
docs.text:
Aligned with calibration table, sorted by |cross-period effect| × SNR (table ranked_metrics.csv):

  1. ai_mention_strict +13.3 pp × SNR 32.9 → 437 (cleanest signal — large AND above noise)
  2. scope_term_rate +21.0 pp × SNR 42.8 → 897
  3. ai_mention_broad +35.6 pp × SNR 25.4 → 905
  4. tech_count_mean d=0.40 × SNR 5.8 → 2.3
  5. distinct_techs_mean 2.06 × SNR 6.4 → 13.1
  6. mgmt_strict +8.0 pp × SNR 2.1 → 16.6
  7. soft_skill_density +6.8 pp × SNR 1.81 → 12.3
  8. description_length_mean d=0.50 × SNR 1.81 → 0.9
  9. S4 YOE≥5 −7.6 pp × SNR 1.07 → 8.1 (direction fragile)
  10. J3 YOE≤2 +5.0 pp × SNR 1.06 → 5.3 (direction fragile)
  11. S1 mid+director −15.7 pp × SNR 0.75 → 11.8 (below noise — do not use)
  12. mgmt_broad +2.8 pp × SNR 0.45 → 1.3 (below noise)
  13. J1 entry +1.8 pp × SNR 0.74 → 1.3 (below noise)
  14. J2 entry/assoc +1.9 pp × SNR 0.72 → 1.3 (below noise)
  15. edu_bs +1.6 pp × SNR 0.11 → 0.18 (well below noise)
  16. aggregator_share +2.2 pp × SNR 0.32 → 0.7 (well below noise)
  17. edu_phd +0.7 pp × SNR 0.20 → 0.15
  18. edu_ms +0.3 pp × SNR 0.03 → 0.01
  19. soft_skill_count_mean d=0.30 × SNR 4.8 → 1.5
  20. yoe_median d=0.20 × SNR 1.08 → 0.22

Ranking interpretation: the three dominant signals are AI mentions (strict/broad), scope terms, and mgmt-strict terms. The next tier is tech breadth (count + distinct). J3/S4/mid-senior share are MODERATE in magnitude but weak-to-marginal in SNR and cannot be cited alone without arshkon-only magnitude. Aggregator share, education mentions, and J1/J2 are at-or-below instrument noise.

docs.title: 5. T30 panel results, expanded
docs.text:
See section 2e. Additional notes:

  • Sensitivities on J3/S4 (table junior_senior_sensitivities.csv):
  • Base (pooled): J3 2024 = 9.15% → 2026 = 14.19% (Δ +5.04 pp)
  • No aggregator: J3 2024 = 9.97% → 2026 = 14.70% (Δ +4.73 pp — shrinks slightly)
  • No entry specialist: J3 2024 = 8.55% → 2026 = 13.44% (Δ +4.89 pp)
  • SWE tier strict (regex + embedding_high): J3 2024 = 8.99% → 2026 = 14.11% (Δ +5.11 pp)
  • Cap 20 per canonical: J3 2024 = 9.97% → 2026 = 12.34% (Δ +2.37 pp)

Materially sensitive (≥30% change from base Δ): cap20 cuts J3 Δ in half (from +5.0 pp to +2.4 pp). This is direct evidence that a few high-volume employers drive a non-trivial share of the aggregate J3 rise — specifically, once company-level over-representation is capped, the 2024→2026 J3 delta is consistent with within-2024 noise (SNR ≈ 0.5 against 4.75 pp within-2024 baseline).

  • Arshkon-only-vs-scraped (mandatory alongside pooled): J3 arshkon-2024 = 13.00% → pooled-2026 = 14.19% (Δ +1.19 pp, within-2024 noise ≥ 4.75 pp → SNR ≈ 0.25 on this comparison). Under arshkon-only baseline, the J3 rise does not clear noise.

  • Gate 1 within-between REPLICATION — materially sensitive (table overlap_panel_decomposition.csv):

Panel n_cos J3 aggregate Δ pp Within-co weighted Δ pp Between-co residual pp pooled min1 (all returning 2,109) 1,423 +6.17 +4.23 +1.94 pooled min3 563 +8.17 +5.11 +3.05 pooled min5 346 +7.90 +5.05 +2.85 arshkon-only min5 (n=125 — T06) 121 +5.02 −0.03 +5.05 asaniczka-only min5 247 +8.70 +6.43 +2.26 pooled min10 159 +7.90 +5.20 +2.69

Flagged direction reversal: the Gate 1 "within-co ≈ 0" claim replicates ONLY on the arshkon-only ≥5 panel (T06's n=125). On every other panel construction (pooled, asaniczka-only, min1, min3, min5, min10), within-company J3 rise is +3.5 to +6.4 pp weighted and is the LARGER component of the aggregate delta. Recommendation: Gate 2 memo should weaken the "J3 rise is entirely between-company" framing. The arshkon-only n=125 panel shows a specific subset (arshkon high-volume returning cos) that maintained J3 share; the broader cohorts show material within-firm rise.

  • Entry-specialist composition (table entry_specialist_composition.csv):
Period Bucket n_total J3 share (labeled) J1 share (all) 2024 entry_specialist 970 23.7% 5.4% 2024 other 21,850 8.6% 1.7% 2026 entry_specialist 2,808 26.1% 10.1% 2026 other 42,509 13.4% 3.2%

Entry specialists are 4% of 2024 volume and 6% of 2026 volume but account for 10.9% of 2026 J3 postings and 17.3% of 2026 J1 postings. Even excluding them, J3 rise is +4.9 pp (vs +5.0 pp base) — so the aggregate claim doesn't collapse, but the J1/J2 claims lean heavily on them.

docs.title: 6. Anomaly flags
docs.text: # Anomaly Where Implication A1 Arshkon native='entry' has median YOE = 4 and 45% ≥ 5 YOE table arshkon_native_yoe_profile.csv seniority_native is noisy cross-sectionally, not just temporally. Even arshkon-only seniority_native cannot be trusted as a clean entry baseline. A2 J3 cap-20 sensitivity cuts Δ in half (5.0 → 2.4 pp) table junior_senior_sensitivities.csv Aggregate J3 rise is heavily weighted by high-volume employers. A3 Arshkon baseline has HIGHER J3 than asaniczka (13.0% vs 8.2%) table yoe_llm_by_source_period.csv The two 2024 sources disagree on entry share by 4.75 pp — comparable to the cross-period effect. A4 Generic SWE titles show J3 rise (+7.3 pp), senior SWE titles show J3 DROP (−1.8 pp) table title_family_j3_s4.csv Junior-share movement is title-specific; senior-titled postings are tightening while generic SWE absorbs juniors. A5 Within-company J3 rise = +4–6 pp on pooled / asaniczka / min-3 / min-10 panels; ≈ 0 only on arshkon-min5 table overlap_panel_decomposition.csv Gate 1 "within ≈ 0" is construction-specific. Within-company component is non-trivial. A6 description_length 7k–15k bucket triples (7% → 18%) table length_bucket_shares.csv Long-tail shift drives mean. Could be boilerplate (pay transparency / EEO) or content — T13 decides. A7 50–53% of SWE rows are seniority_final='unknown' in arshkon and scraped table seniority_final_by_source.csv The label-based J1/J2 metrics always have a >50% denominator hole. A8 Director share S2 is effectively 0 (0.4–0.8%) across all sources table seniority_final_by_source.csv Director analyses will be underpowered in every operationalization. A9 Scraped is_remote = 0.0% at all sources (reflects stage-5 inference) table remote_multi_location_by_source.csv Remote flags are 100% artifact-driven; use is_remote_inferred only. A10 Aggregator share of 2024: arshkon 9.2%, asaniczka 16.2% (7 pp within-2024 gap) table aggregator_by_source.csv Any aggregator-related cross-period finding must stratify by source; SNR = 0.32 (below noise). A11 Industry labels drift dramatically: "IT Services and IT Consulting" 34% → 18%; "Software Development" 17% → 38% table top15_industry_by_period.csv Confirms T07 taxonomy drift — industry-stratified 2024→2026 raw-label claims are invalid. A12 AI-strict prevalence is essentially identical between LLM-labeled (18.1%) and not_selected (18.2%) scraped rows table ai_strict_by_coverage.csv The AI-mention rise is not a labeled-only artifact; it appears equally in rule-routed rows. A13 NYC metro share jumps 0.4% → 6.5% in scraped table top20_metro_area.csv Scraper design shift — NYC was barely represented in 2024 sources; treat metro-share trend as scraping artifact unless corroborated. A14 seniority_native in asaniczka contains ONLY associate (10%) and mid-senior (90%) — no entry, no director table seniority_native_by_source.csv Confirmed T01/T05 finding; pooling asaniczka into any seniority_native-based comparison collapses the entry rate. A15 Length-matched counterfactual: if 2026 posts had 2024 length distribution, AI-strict share would still be 16.9% vs actual 18.2% — only 7.7% of AI rise is length-driven table length_matched_ai_summary.csv AI mention rise is overwhelmingly content, not length. A16 ML Engineer 2024 arshkon vs scraped: 381 → 3,193 rows (8.4× growth) table title_family_j3_s4.csv ML engineer volume is a major composition shift. Matches T04 19.6 pp ML-SWE classification asymmetry caveat.

docs.title: 7. Data quality caveats
docs.text:
1. Scraped LLM labeled coverage is 56.9% (25,816 of 45,317). Any analysis using yoe_min_years_llm, description_core_llm, or ghost_assessment_llm on scraped is computed on a subset. The supplemental check (A12) shows AI-strict rate is nearly identical labeled vs not_selected — so the labeled subset is not obviously biased for AI signals. But J3 shares are defined only on labeled.
  2. Arshkon seniority_native='entry' is not clean (A1). The seniority_native column should be retired from sanity-check duty entirely.
  3. Industry labels drifted (A11). Do not stratify cross-period by raw company_industry.
  4. Remote flags are uniformly 0 (A9). Use is_remote_inferred only.
  5. Asaniczka has no native entry labels (A14, already known from T01).
  6. Within-company decomposition depends strongly on overlap-panel construction (A5). Any "within vs between" claim must specify min_n filter and source subset.
  7. Company cap sensitivity is material (A2). Any aggregate share claim should report cap-20 alongside.

docs.title: 8. Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
• T09 (archetypes): expect domain-heterogeneous archetypes. ML engineer is a distinct cluster (8.4× volume growth → 7% of scraped SWE); senior-SWE-titled postings behave opposite to generic SWE on J3. Cluster on content rather than title.
  • T11 (requirements complexity): MUST length-residualize. Description length shifted 20.8% → 18.3% in the long-tail bucket. Check if "requirement count" rises survives length normalization.
  • T12 (text evolution): report labeled vs not_selected split for any scraped text metric. A12 shows AI is labeled-insensitive; other metrics may not be.
  • T13 (linguistic/structural): critical to decide if length growth is boilerplate (long-tail bucket) or content. Section-stratify the 7k–15k bucket comparison.
  • T14 (tech mapping): tech_count metric has SNR 6.4 — safe to cite. ML Engineer source composition warning still applies.
  • T16 (hiring strategy): the overlap-panel construction matters — report min_n ∈ {1, 5, 10} and source subsets in parallel.
  • T17 (geography): NYC metro jump (A13) is scraping-artifact; state-level shifts for CA (+6.6 pp), TX (+3.8 pp), VA (−0.2 pp) are more robust.
  • T20 (seniority boundary): senior-titled J3 DROP (A4) vs generic-SWE J3 RISE is a direct boundary-clarity signal. Worth a dedicated analysis.
  • T21 (senior role evolution): senior-titled postings got +1,400 chars longer and J3 share dropped — compatible with role tightening, not dilution.
  • T23 (employer-requirement divergence): AI-strict SNR 32.9 is the cleanest cross-period signal. The within-company rise (+14.2 pp on returning cohort) vs aggregate (+16.7 pp) means within-firm rewriting is the dominant mechanism.
  • T28 (domain × seniority): defer until T09 produces archetype labels. Industry label drift (A11) blocks any raw-label industry × seniority analysis.

docs.title: 9. Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Gate 1 "J3 within-co ≈ 0" does not replicate outside the arshkon-only n=125 panel. On pooled min1/min3/min5/min10 and asaniczka-only min5, within-company J3 rise is +3.5 to +6.4 pp weighted — the majority of the aggregate delta. The Gate 1 framing of "junior rise is a composition story, AI rise is within-firm" needs to be softened. Junior rise has both components, and the within-company share is material once you move off the arshkon-only high-volume panel.

  2. Arshkon baseline J3 is HIGHER than asaniczka (13.0% vs 8.2%) — reverse of what's in the orchestrator mental model. In calibration, the within-2024 asymmetry on J3 (4.75 pp) is comparable to the cross-period effect (5.04 pp). Under arshkon-only baseline, J3 rise collapses to +1.2 pp — well below noise. This sharpens the Gate 1 "magnitude fragility" warning from senior-side to junior-side as well.

  3. Senior-SWE-titled postings (sr/senior) J3 share DROPS from 4.3% → 2.5% while generic-SWE J3 share RISES from 8.6% → 15.9%. This is not visible in the T30 panel because S4 and J3 definitions collapse title variation. Title-family stratification reveals the market is bifurcating: senior-labeled roles are tightening experience bar, generic SWE roles are absorbing juniors. Combined with the description_length growth in senior-titled postings (+1,400 chars), this looks like "scope inflation within senior bucket, junior absorption into generic."

  4. Arshkon seniority_native='entry' has 45% of rows at ≥5 YOE and median YOE of 4. This was assumed to be a usable cross-sectional baseline; it is not. The native classifier equates "entry" with "early-career" broadly, not with ≤2 YOE. T01's "arshkon entry = 15.7%" and "seniority_final entry = 3.8%" gap is explained: the native-entry pool is 5-year-median people labeled as "entry" at the source. Recommendation: seniority_native should not be used as a sanity check in Wave 2+ at all, not even on arshkon.

  5. AI-strict prevalence is 18.1% on labeled and 18.2% on not_selected scraped rows. The LLM-routing decision is essentially orthogonal to AI content presence. This is strong reassurance that the labeled-only AI findings are not subset-biased. But it is a sobering note for interpretation: the LLM cleanup is NOT filtering AI-dense vs AI-sparse rows, so comparisons of AI rates between labeled and not_selected subsets should not expect to find coverage artifacts.

  6. Length-matched counterfactual: only 7.7% of the AI-strict rise is length-driven. A bulk of the AI signal survives even if 2026 posts were shortened to 2024 length distribution. This is powerful evidence that the AI rise is content, not boilerplate bloat.

  7. ML engineer volume grew 8.4× (381 → 3,193). This is far more than the broader SWE growth (68,137 → 45,317 total is ~2×). ML engineer is a major composition shift in 2026; paper MUST source-stratify ML/AI-specific claims by the arshkon-vs-asaniczka-vs-scraped split and note the 19.6 pp cross-source ML-SWE classification gap (T04).

  8. Company_size quartile 1 vs Q4 on arshkon: J3 share 10.7% vs 16.6% — large employers post MORE entry roles. This is the opposite of the intuition that startups hire more juniors (arshkon only captures company_size, so this is 2024-only). AI-strict share also scales with size (3.2% Q1 → 5.0% Q4). If the 2026 composition shifts toward larger employers (returning-cohort dominated by big tech), these size effects explain some of the aggregate J3 and AI rises.

  9. The "unknown" seniority_final share is 50–53% in arshkon and scraped, but 34% in asaniczka. Asaniczka LLM routing found more explicit seniority signals. Possible mechanism: asaniczka postings are more template-driven. This is an LLM-routing artifact noise floor that every seniority share analysis inherits.

  10. NYC metro share jumps 0.4% → 6.5% between 2024 and 2026. This is too large for organic market change. It is almost certainly scraper query design (NYC was barely queried in 2024 data sources). Any NYC-specific claim must be excluded from cross-period trend narratives. CA (+6.6 pp), TX (+3.8 pp), WA (+3.0 pp) are more defensible trends.

docs.title: 10. Sensitivity results (dim a/b/c/e/f/g/i)
docs.text:
Per the Wave 2 framework:

Dim What Result on J3 primary finding Material? (a) Aggregator exclusion Drop is_aggregator=true J3 Δ: +5.04 → +4.73 pp (−6%) No — within 30% (b) Company capping (cap 20) Per-period cap J3 Δ: +5.04 → +2.37 pp (−53%) YES — materially sensitive. High-volume employers account for half the J3 Δ. Drilling in: top 20 employers by 2026 J3 volume: ClearanceJobs, Amazon, Capital One, USAA, MassMutual, Wells Fargo, Cisco, Verizon, Walmart, Deloitte, etc. Many have mixed J3 rates ≥20%. © Seniority operationalization T30 panel J1 Δ: +1.82 pp (SNR 0.74, below noise); J2 +1.85 (SNR 0.72, below noise); J3 +5.04 (SNR 1.06, near noise); J4 +7.62 (SNR 1.05, near noise). Arshkon-only flips J1 and J2 negative; J3 drops to +1.2 pp. YES — J1/J2 direction-sensitive to baseline choice. (e) Source restriction Arshkon-only vs pooled J3 arshkon-only Δ: +1.19 pp (vs pooled +5.04) YES — effect shrinks to 24% of pooled. (f) Within-2024 calibration SNR ratio J3 SNR = 1.06 (near_noise). J1 0.74, J2 0.72 (below noise). S4 1.07 (near), S1 0.75 (below). AI-strict 32.9, scope 42.8, mgmt-strict 2.07 (all above). Decision: J3/S4 citations require both pooled and arshkon-only; J1/J2/S1 cannot be cited cross-period alone. YES — baked into every primary metric recommendation. (g) SWE classification tier Drop title_lookup_llm J3 Δ: +5.04 → +5.11 pp (+1%) No (h) LLM text coverage Labeled vs not_selected on AI-strict AI-strict: labeled 18.1% vs not_selected 18.2% — no split effect. J3 cannot be checked (only defined on labeled). No for AI; N/A for J3 (i) Indeed cross-platform Rule yoe_extracted ≤ 2 LinkedIn 2024 rule-J3 = 11.2%; LinkedIn 2026 rule-J3 = 17.2% (Δ +6.0 pp); Indeed 2026 rule-J3 = 18.3%. Indeed corroborates LinkedIn 2026 entry share at ~18%. Direction: LinkedIn-Indeed 2026 agree (17.2% vs 18.3%). No — Indeed corroborates LinkedIn 2026 magnitude

docs.title: Materially sensitive summary
docs.text:
• Company capping (b): J3 aggregate Δ drops from +5.0 pp to +2.4 pp (53% reduction). Aggregate claim leans heavily on top employers.
  • Seniority op © + Source restriction (e): J1 and J2 flip direction arshkon-only; J3 drops 5× to +1.2 pp. Any "junior share rose" claim must cite both pooled Δ and arshkon-only Δ — the paper's single-number claim is not defensible.
  • Within-between decomposition on overlap panels: within-co weighted J3 Δ ranges from −0.03 pp (arshkon min5) to +6.4 pp (asaniczka min5). The Gate 1 "within ≈ 0" is overlap-panel-specific.

docs.title: Recommended downstream treatment
docs.text:
1. Report J3 aggregate Δ with THREE magnitudes: pooled (+5.0 pp), arshkon-only (+1.2 pp), cap-20 pooled (+2.4 pp). The modal number should be ~+2-3 pp, not +5.
  2. Stop using "junior rise is entirely between-company." Use: "junior rise has both within-company and between-company components; the balance depends on overlap-panel construction."
  3. seniority_native is retired from sanity-check duty (it was already retired for temporal claims in Gate 1; now also for cross-sectional baselines on arshkon).
  4. AI-mention rise is the cleanest signal. No cautions needed beyond standard length/labeled sensitivities, which have been explicitly checked and are negligible.
  5. Senior-title sub-population inversion (A4) merits a dedicated T20/T21 deep dive.
