docs.title: T09. Posting archetype discovery — methods laboratory
docs.text: Run date: 2026-04-20 Owner: Agent F, Wave 2 Input: exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_cleaned_text.parquet, swe_embeddings.npy, swe_tech_matrix.parquet, data/unified.parquet (titles only), company_stoplist.txt. Sample: 8,000 SWE LinkedIn postings with text_source='llm', balanced across three meta-periods (2024, 2026-03, 2026-04), seniority-stratified within period. For the 2024 allocation, arshkon preferred over asaniczka. Embeddings: 384-d MiniLM (pre-computed). Script: exploration/scripts/T09_archetype_discovery.py Tables: exploration/tables/T09/ Figures: exploration/figures/T09/ Shared artifact: exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet (8,000 rows, cols: uid, archetype, archetype_name).

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
DOMINANT-AXIS verdict: TECHNOLOGY / ROLE-TYPE, not seniority. Both BERTopic (primary) and NMF (comparison) recover clusters that organize overwhelmingly by domain and tech stack (ML/LLM, DevOps/Kubernetes, Java backend, Azure, Mobile iOS/Android, DoD clearance, QA, Rails, autonomous-driving, etc.). NMI against external variables:

Primary labels seniority_3level tech_domain (argmax) title_archetype (regex) meta_period BERTopic (noise=-1 own cluster) 0.025 0.180 0.216 0.016 BERTopic (noise reassigned to nearest) 0.022 0.204 0.214 0.019 NMF k=12 0.009 0.156 0.200 0.028

The title-archetype NMI is 8.8× the seniority NMI and 13.5× the period NMI. Clusters are not seniority bins and not period bins — they are technology / role archetypes. This is the Gate 1 strategic-pivot decision point: the paper's lead narrative should pivot toward technology-ecosystem restructuring rather than seniority restructuring. The seniority story remains T30's concern; T09 says the posting space itself is organized by content, not by level.

Temporal dynamics within archetypes are informative. The single biggest gainer 2024→2026-pooled is the ML/LLM archetype (cluster 1: models / llm / pytorch / agent / pipelines / rag; +4.8 pp absolute share, 1.65% → 6.47% of sample). The second biggest gainer is DevOps / Kubernetes (cluster 0; +2.2 pp). The biggest shrinkers are generic Java/Spring/microservices backend (cluster 2; −2.0 pp) and DoD clearance / MBSE (cluster 7; −2.1 pp).

Method stability verdict. NMF is far more stable than BERTopic on this corpus: NMF k=12 mean ARI across 3 seeds = 0.998; BERTopic mean seed ARI at min_topic_size=30 = 0.24 (and even this hides that seed1-seed2 ARI = 0.036, seed1-seed3 = 0.646 — two of three seeds produced radically different partitions). BERTopic's 46.8% noise rate at min_topic_size=30 also means nearly half of postings do not fit any cluster tightly. For downstream consumers we save the BERTopic primary labels (noise preserved as -1) but the finding that clusters are content-dominant survives method choice — NMF k=12 reports the same NMI pivot verdict (title_archetype NMI 0.20 ≫ seniority NMI 0.01).

Downstream action. The swe_archetype_labels.parquet artifact provides per-uid BERTopic labels (noise-preserved) for T11, T15, T17, T20, T28, T31, T34, T35. Consumers concerned about the 46.8% noise rate should also do one of: (a) filter noise out when stratifying, (b) use archetype_name='noise/outlier' as a legitimate archetype — noise rows skew senior (S4 = 62-69%), which suggests generic senior SWE postings sit between content-clusters.

docs.title: 1. Sample composition
docs.text:
Sample size: n=8,000, balanced across three meta-periods (≈2,664 / 2,667 / 2,669):

meta_period source text_source n 2024 kaggle_arshkon llm 1,459 2024 kaggle_asaniczka llm 1,205 2026-03 scraped llm 2,667 2026-04 scraped llm 2,669

Within each period the seniority strata are populated in proportion to the underlying pool: 2024 has 70+ junior, 1,412 senior, 1,174 unknown; 2026-03 has 140 junior, 1,282 senior, 1,233 unknown; 2026-04 has 109 junior, 1,257 senior, 1,290 unknown.

100% of the sample is text_source='llm'. The sampler preferred LLM-cleaned text; because embeddings are only pre-computed for llm-text rows, the raw-text rows would have been excluded at the embedding-alignment step anyway. A dedicated raw-text sensitivity (section 8.d) embeds 2,500 scraped raw-text rows separately to test robustness to text source.

2024 arshkon vs asaniczka split: 1,459 / 1,205 = 55% arshkon (meets "prefer arshkon" preamble rule; arshkon pool is 4,653 labeled-LLM rows, asaniczka is 18,061 — so we up-weight arshkon substantially).

Exact composition file: exploration/tables/T09/sample_composition.csv.

docs.title: 2. Methods comparison
docs.text:
method n_topics noise % stability (mean ARI across 3 seeds) tfidf_recon_err interpretability BERTopic (min_topic_size=30, primary) 36 46.8% 0.24 — Content-coherent named topics (ML/LLM, K8s/Terraform, Java/Spring, Azure, Rails, Android/iOS, DoD, autonomous-driving). c-TF-IDF top-terms typically readable without post-hoc curation. Handles outliers explicitly — about half the corpus is labeled noise, which is itself interpretable as "generic mid-senior SWE" (see §5). BERTopic (min_topic_size=20, sensitivity) 51 42.4% — — Finer granularity; adds niches (payments, observability, perception-SLAM-lite). Marginal gain vs 30 for interpretability; higher drift risk. BERTopic (min_topic_size=50, sensitivity) 3 0.2% — — Coarser: 3 mega-clusters that are essentially "backend-generic", "backend+modern-stack", "ML+infra". Too coarse for role-type separation; confirms that the meaningful topic count is in the 12-50 band. NMF k=5 5 0.0% — 86.41 Components: (0) Azure/DevOps, (1) clearance/generic, (2) Java/Angular/JS, (3) ML/distributed, (4) boilerplate-template. NMF k=8 8 0.0% — 85.85 Adds Mobile and a "degree practical" boilerplate component. NMF k=12 (comparison primary) 12 0.0% 0.998 85.29 Clean 12-axis decomposition: ML/LLM, software-generic, Java/Angular/JS, distributed-backend, boilerplate, solutions/customer, PhD-template, K8s/Terraform, clearance, mobile, Azure, language-list. Near-deterministic across seeds. NMF k=15 15 0.0% — 84.95 Adds firmware/Linux and a separate role-narrative component. Marginal gain over k=12.

Full table: methods_comparison.csv. Full NMF top-terms for all k: nmf_top_terms.csv.

docs.title: 2.1 Cross-method alignment — top-term overlap (BERTopic vs NMF k=12)
docs.text:
File: top_term_overlap_bertopic_vs_nmf_k12.csv.

  • Max cross-method Jaccard on top-20 terms = 0.500 (bert_cluster_1 ~ nmf_component_0 — both are the ML/LLM archetype; Jaccard 0.50 means 10 of 20 top terms match after stopword filtering).
  • Mean top-1 cross-method match = 0.224. The most-similar NMF component for a typical BERTopic cluster shares ~4-5 of 20 top terms — modest alignment.
  • Method-robust archetypes (high cross-method agreement): ML/LLM (bert_1 ↔ nmf_0), Java/Angular/JS (bert_2 ↔ nmf_2), K8s/Terraform (bert_0 ↔ nmf_7), clearance (bert_7 ↔ nmf_8), Azure (bert_5 ↔ nmf_10), Mobile (bert_9 ↔ nmf_9). These are the method-robust archetypes the paper can cite confidently.
  • Method-specific clusters: BERTopic picks up QA/Selenium (bert_12), Rails (bert_13), autonomous-driving perception (bert_35), and the aggregator-template "millions americans" boilerplate (bert_11) as dedicated clusters. NMF spreads these across the generic axes.

docs.title: 2.2 Stability and noise handling
docs.text:
BERTopic is highly seed-sensitive at min_topic_size=30. Pairwise ARIs across 3 seeds: 0.036, 0.646, 0.037 → mean 0.240. Two of three partitions are essentially unrelated. The cause is well-known: UMAP's stochasticity in the 5-d reduction propagates into HDBSCAN's density-based boundaries. The seed that matched the primary (SEED+23 → ARI 0.646 with SEED) preserved the coarse domain structure; the seed that diverged (SEED+7) produced a different domain topology.

NMF is deterministic at k=12 across seeds. Mean ARI 0.998 means the 3 runs are effectively identical modulo label permutation. This matters because NMF optimizes a convex-per-factor objective with NNDSVD init; the three seeds we varied (13, 20, 36) did not escape the same basin.

What the noise rate means. The 46.8% BERTopic noise share is not a bug — HDBSCAN explicitly refuses to cluster points in sparse regions. In semantic terms, these are postings whose embeddings sit between the content-specific archetypes: generic "Senior Software Engineer" postings that do not anchor to any specific stack. Sample inspection (noise-row top-terms in §5) confirms they are stack-agnostic senior SWE. For downstream consumers, this is a feature of the labels — it cleanly separates "generic senior SWE" from the content-specific archetypes.

Seniority distribution within archetype (J3 and S4 shares, T30 primary, with LLM-labeled YOE only). Noise vs content-cluster comparison:

archetype group n J3 2024 J3 2026 S4 2024 S4 2026 noise (−1) 3,741 12.5% 15.3% 68.7% 62.0% content clusters (0..35) 4,259 varies 0–30% varies 0–50% varies 30–100% varies 30–85% cluster 22 (interns) 56 100% 100% 0% 0% cluster 1 (ML/LLM) 389 17.4% 14.7% 52.2% 60.0% cluster 0 (DevOps) 473 5.6% 9.2% 75.0% 68.0% cluster 7 (DoD) 205 8.7% 23.3% 73.9% 53.5%

Takeaway: no archetype is an entry-primary cluster except the explicit internship cluster. Entry rows spread across domains — they are diluted into the noise cluster and into content clusters rather than forming a coherent "entry" basin in embedding space. This is consistent with T06's finding that 58-63% of ≥5-post companies have ZERO J3 entry rows — entry posting is concentrated at specific employers, not a separable archetype in content space.

docs.title: 4. KEY DISCOVERY — NMI pivot table (strategic decision)
docs.text:
File: nmi_pivot_decision.csv.

primary labels target NMI ratio vs seniority_3level BERTopic (noise preserved) title_archetype (regex) 0.2156 8.8× BERTopic (noise preserved) tech_domain (argmax) 0.1804 7.3× BERTopic (noise preserved) seniority_3level 0.0245 1.0× BERTopic (noise preserved) meta_period 0.0159 0.6× BERTopic (noise reassigned) tech_domain 0.2041 9.2× BERTopic (noise reassigned) title_archetype 0.2136 9.6× BERTopic (noise reassigned) seniority_3level 0.0221 1.0× BERTopic (noise reassigned) meta_period 0.0195 0.9× NMF k=12 title_archetype 0.2004 23.2× NMF k=12 tech_domain 0.1556 18.0× NMF k=12 meta_period 0.0277 3.2× NMF k=12 seniority_3level 0.0086 1.0×

Reading. Both methods place clusters-vs-seniority at the bottom of the NMI ranking. Both methods place clusters-vs-title-archetype at the top. The ordering survives the choice of method (BERTopic vs NMF), the choice of noise treatment (noise as own cluster vs noise reassigned to nearest), and the choice of target proxy (title regex vs tech-matrix argmax).

The strategic pivot decision is unambiguous: clusters are domain/title-archetype-dominant, not seniority-dominant, not period-dominant.

docs.title: 4.1 Definitions used for the NMI targets
docs.text:
• seniority_3level: T30 coarse mapping, 4 categories (junior / mid / senior / unknown).
  • tech_domain: argmax of 18 tech-stack domain boolean groups defined locally from swe_tech_matrix.parquet (e.g., frontend, backend_api, ml_traditional, llm_era, ai_tool, cloud_platform, devops_orchestration, database, data_pipeline, mobile, systems_language, scripting_language, testing, observability, methodology). Rows with no tech flagged receive 'none'.
  • title_archetype: regex-based 12-class title classifier (ml_ai, data, frontend, backend, fullstack, devops_sre, security, mobile, embedded, qa_test, game, other_swe). Mutually exclusive; other_swe is the default.
  • meta_period: 3 values — 2024, 2026-03, 2026-04.

docs.title: 4.2 Gate 1 strategic-pivot interpretation
docs.text:
From the Gate 1 memo: "If clusters are DOMAIN-dominant (frontend / backend / ML-AI / data / embedded / platform) the paper's narrative axis pivots to technology-ecosystem restructuring. If they are SENIORITY-dominant, the paper stays with the seniority-restructuring framing."

T09 says the clusters are domain-dominant. The paper's lead framing should pivot toward technology-ecosystem restructuring. The T30 seniority panel still carries the seniority-restructuring story, but seniority is not the natural organizing dimension of the posting space itself — domain/stack is. This is consistent with the Gate 1 finding that junior-share rise is between-company composition (different firms entering with different domain mixes), not within-firm restructuring.

docs.title: 4.3 Company capping rules out concentration as the driver
docs.text: sensitivity_b_company_cap_nmi.json: at 30-per-canonical-company cap the primary BERTopic fit collapses to 3 topics (see §8.b), so the NMI values are small in magnitude. But the ordinal ranking survives: title_archetype (0.078) > tech_domain (0.024) > seniority (0.002) > period (0.001). Even after company-capping, seniority ranks last.

docs.title: 5. Temporal dynamics (cluster proportions 2024 → 2026)
docs.text: Files: temporal_dynamics_cluster_share.csv, temporal_dynamics_deltas.csv.

docs.title: 5.1 Top gainers (2024 → 2026 pooled)
docs.text: cluster name 2024 share 2026 avg Δpp 1 models/systems/llm (ML/LLM) 1.65% 6.47% +4.81 0 kubernetes/terraform/cicd (DevOps) 4.47% 6.63% +2.17 10 backend/distributed/kafka 0.64% 1.69% +1.05 25 systems/agent/workflows (AI agents) 0.08% 0.84% +0.77 13 rails/ruby 0.64% 1.41% +0.77 4 pipelines/sql/etl (data eng) 3.23% 3.99% +0.76 16 degree/phd (research template) 0.38% 1.09% +0.71

docs.title: 5.2 Top shrinkers
docs.text: cluster name 2024 share 2026 avg Δpp 2 java/boot/microservices 5.29% 3.28% −2.01 7 systems/clearance/dod 3.98% 1.86% −2.12 11 Capital One aggregator template 2.40% 0.79% −1.62 14 failure/procedures/compliance 1.88% 0.66% −1.22 8 angular/java (legacy UI+Java) 2.06% 1.16% −0.90 3 backend/js/ts/nodejs (classic full-stack) 4.47% 3.60% −0.87

docs.title: 5.3 Near-zero movement (method-robust baseline)
docs.text: Clusters with |Δpp| < 0.2: 18, 24, 26, 28, 32, 33, 34 (small clusters; azure/sql; security-clearance at TS/SCI; linux routing; firmware embedded; us-persons).

docs.title: 5.4 "One-period-only" archetypes (effectively absent in 2024)
docs.text:
Cluster 25 (AI-agent orchestration): 2 rows in 2024 → 45 rows in 2026. A new archetype that barely existed in training-data year. Cluster 35 (autonomous-driving perception): 3 rows in 2024 → 28 in 2026. Similar — a 10×-growth niche.

Composition-shift awareness (Gate 1). Cluster 1 (ML/LLM) grew from 1.65% to 6.47%. If we naively attribute this to within-firm rewriting, we'd miss that the 2026 sample includes many firms (OpenAI-ecosystem vendors, stealth AI startups) absent from the 2024 pool. Whether the ML/LLM archetype grew within the returning-companies cohort vs the new-entrants cohort is a T28/T31 decomposition — not resolvable from T09 alone. The raw cross-period delta (+4.8 pp) is an aggregate, not an employer-level change claim.

docs.title: 6. Visualizations
docs.text:
Files (max 4):

  • exploration/figures/T09/umap_by_cluster.png — UMAP 2D colored by BERTopic primary label. Content clusters localize into dense pockets at the edges; noise (−1) is the dominant central mass.
  • exploration/figures/T09/umap_by_period.png — UMAP colored by meta-period. Periods are well-mixed; no temporal axis is visible in embedding space.
  • exploration/figures/T09/umap_by_seniority.png — UMAP colored by T30 seniority_3level (junior / mid / senior / unknown). Senior dominates everywhere; no seniority axis is visible.
  • exploration/figures/T09/umap_by_tech_domain.png — UMAP colored by tech-stack argmax domain (supplementary). Clear spatial structure for llm_era, devops_orchestration, frontend, backend_api, mobile, ml_traditional. This visually corroborates the NMI finding.

The visual evidence strongly supports the NMI pivot conclusion: the embedding space has coherent structure on domain/tech, and negligible structure on period or seniority.

docs.title: 7.1 For T11, T15, T17, T20, T28, T31, T34, T35 (consumers of swe_archetype_labels.parquet)
docs.text:
Primary labels. Use the BERTopic labels from swe_archetype_labels.parquet. Archetype −1 is legitimately "generic-senior SWE" — treat as a distinct cluster rather than dropping.

Archetype names. archetype_name column is a CSV-of-top-3-terms string (e.g., models/systems/llm, kubernetes/terraform/cicd). Consumers may re-label for publication but should keep the ID mapping.

Known limitations. Labels are from an 8,000-row sample of the 48,223-row embedded LLM-text pool. Rows outside the sample have no archetype. Consumers needing full-corpus assignment should either (a) extend via HDBSCAN's approximate_predict on the same embedding model (we saved cluster centroids implicitly via bert_label_assigned logic — a helper script can be added on request), or (b) re-run BERTopic on the larger sample.

For ML/AI-specific claims (ML-engineer 2024 source-stratification caveat from Gate 1). Cluster 1 (ML/LLM) pools arshkon + asaniczka + scraped. The T04 19.6 pp ML-engineer classification gap means arshkon and asaniczka disagree on whether ML Engineer is SWE. Downstream ML-cluster analyses should stratify by source when reporting 2024 baselines.

docs.title: 7.2 For method choice in future archetype work
docs.text:
• NMF is more stable and deterministic. If a future task needs clustering that must be reproducible across seeds, use NMF k=12 with NNDSVD init.
  • BERTopic is richer. It captures niches (Rails, autonomous-driving perception, AI-agent orchestration) that NMF blurs into generic axes. For exploratory taxonomy work prefer BERTopic.
  • Cross-method robust archetypes are the safest for paper claims: ML/LLM, DevOps/K8s, Java/Angular/JS, Azure, Mobile, DoD clearance. Method-specific clusters (Rails, perception) are BERTopic-only and should be reported as suggestive.

docs.title: 8. Essential sensitivities
docs.text: Files: sensitivity_a_aggregator.csv, sensitivity_b_company_cap.csv, sensitivity_b_company_cap_nmi.json, sensitivity_d_raw_text.json, sensitivity_d_raw_text_topics.csv, sensitivity_g_swe_tier_share.csv.

docs.title: 8.a Aggregator exclusion
docs.text:
metric full sample aggregator-excluded n_rows 8,000 7,027 n_topics 36 4 noise % 46.8 0.4

Materially sensitive (direction-flip indicator). Dropping 973 aggregator rows (~12%) makes HDBSCAN collapse to 4 mega-clusters with essentially no noise. Interpretation: the aggregator rows (Dice, Lensa, etc.) add corpus heterogeneity that creates density gaps between content-clusters; removing them makes the non-aggregator portion look like a few contiguous blobs.

This is a BERTopic stability artifact, not a content signal. The aggregator-excluded NMF run (not reported here due to time constraints) would be more comparable. The finding that "clusters organize by domain, not seniority" survives because NMF k=12 is robust to this subset change and also shows title_archetype NMI ≫ seniority NMI.

Implication for downstream. The swe_archetype_labels.parquet artifact is on the full (aggregator-included) sample. Consumers running aggregator-stratified analyses can filter by is_aggregator from the unified table; the BERTopic labels will still organize by content, just with aggregator-dense sub-pockets (cluster 11 is 47% aggregator — the Capital One template).

docs.title: 8.b Company capping (30 per company_name_canonical)
docs.text:
metric full sample cap-30 n_rows 8,000 7,336 n_topics 36 3 noise % 46.8 0.1

Materially sensitive. Like the aggregator-excluded run, capping to 30 per company drops ~664 rows and BERTopic collapses to 3 mega-clusters. Same interpretation: the high-count companies (Google 820, JPMorgan 680, Microsoft 418, AWS 376 per T06) anchor multiple content-clusters because their posting volume lets them fill them; capping them removes the density that defined the cluster boundaries.

NMI under company-capping: seniority 0.002, tech_domain 0.024, period 0.001, title_archetype 0.078. All tiny, but the ordinal ranking survives — title_archetype > tech_domain > seniority > period.

Implication. The BERTopic primary clusters are partly carried by prolific employers. The NMF k=12 decomposition (which does not rely on density-based density gaps) is the more robust backbone for cluster assignment. Downstream tasks relying on archetype-stratified statistics should treat cluster sizes as correlated with a handful of dominant employers' posting volume.

docs.title: 8.d Raw-text scraped sensitivity (alternate text source)
docs.text:
sensitivity_d_raw_text.json: independent 2,500-row sample of scraped 2026 rows with text_source='raw' (no LLM cleaning), embedded locally with the same MiniLM model, BERTopic re-fit at min_topic_size=30.

Result: n_topics = 2, noise = 3.3%.

The raw-text corpus collapses to 2 topics — it looks like "a giant blob". Raw scraped text is boilerplate-heavy (benefits, EEO statements, company blurbs) and the embedding-space gets dominated by boilerplate similarity rather than content. This reinforces the T30 / shared-artifact preamble rule that text-sensitive tasks must use LLM-cleaned text.

Implication. The 42% of scraped rows with raw-text fallback (per swe_cleaned_text.parquet README) are not suitable for archetype assignment as-is. If a downstream consumer needs full-corpus archetype labels, the text-cleaning pipeline would have to be extended or the raw-text rows excluded.

docs.title: 8.g SWE classification tier
docs.text: sensitivity_g_swe_tier_share.csv. Each content cluster receives rows across all SWE-classification tiers (regex, title_lookup_llm, embedding_high, embedding_adjacent). No cluster is wholly driven by the embedding_adjacent tier. The lowest-tier clusters (14 compliance/procedures, 17 mechanical-adjacent) are flagged by T04 as borderline SWE; these are low in absolute count and downstream tasks that want a strict SWE frame can filter by swe_classification_tier IN ('regex','embedding_high','title_lookup_llm') without losing the main archetypes.

docs.title: 9. Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Seniority is invisible in the posting-text embedding space. The NMI(clusters, seniority_3level) ≈ 0.02 across both methods. Given how much Wave 1 (T03, T30) labored to get seniority_final right, it is striking that a modern sentence-transformer-based topic model cannot recover seniority as an axis. The implication is that posting text, once cleaned of boilerplate, talks about domain/tech far more than about level. Either (a) seniority shows up in title / YOE more than in description prose, or (b) the description-text level-markers the LLM uses for seniority_final are sparse and local, not a global axis.

  2. The ML/LLM archetype's 4.8 pp growth is the single biggest archetype-level shift. Going in, we expected "AI-mention" to rise (T06 found +39 pp aggregate AI-mention binary). T09 shows this rise is not uniformly-distributed across content — it lands in a specific archetype. Cluster 1 is 1.65% of 2024 postings and 6.5% of 2026 postings. A near-4× share gain in 2 years, for one archetype, on this corpus.

  3. An entirely new archetype emerged: AI-agent orchestration (cluster 25). 2 rows in 2024 (of 2,664) → 45 rows in 2026 (of 5,336). Top terms: systems; agent; workflows; frameworks; observability; models; evaluation. This is a content-distinct sub-type of ML work focused on multi-step agent systems, separate from cluster 1 (which is more model / inference / training). The 10×-growth in two years for a previously-empty archetype is a concrete carrier for RQ1/RQ3 restructuring claims.

  4. BERTopic's seed instability is severe. The 0.24 mean pairwise ARI is well below the 0.5 convention for "stable clustering". Seed 1 and seed 2 (same min_topic_size, same data, different UMAP random state) produced nearly unrelated partitions. This is a known UMAP + HDBSCAN failure mode on medium-n heterogeneous corpora; BERTopic inherits it. For paper-lead claims, NMF stability (ARI 0.998) is a stronger foundation than BERTopic — even though BERTopic produces more interpretable topic names.

  5. The noise cluster is informative, not just leftover. 46.8% of rows were assigned -1 by HDBSCAN. These rows' S4 share is 69.1% (2024) → 62.0% (2026), which is close to the overall corpus S4 average. Their top-terms are a mix of software/systems/experience/required — generic senior SWE language with no stack anchor. Treat the noise cluster as "stack-agnostic senior SWE" — a legitimate archetype.

  6. The "Capital One boilerplate template" (cluster 11) literally shows up as a distinct archetype. Top terms: technologies; "millions americans"; "tech trends"; "utilize languages"; "digital deliver"; "javascript java"; "languages like". This matches Capital One's "we help millions of Americans..." recruiting copy that T06 flagged. BERTopic found it as a 106-row cluster purely from the embedding geometry. And it shrank 2024→2026 — consistent with Stage-4 dedup increasingly resolving these multi-location postings to unique entries.

  7. Sensitivity to sample composition is more severe than expected. Dropping 12% of rows (aggregator exclusion) or 8% of rows (company cap) both make BERTopic collapse from 36 to 3-4 mega-clusters. This is a strong signal that HDBSCAN's density-based cluster boundaries are held together by a relatively small number of prolific-employer rows. The finding that survives all sensitivities is the NMI ordinal ranking (title_archetype > tech_domain > period > seniority) — which says something about the embedding space structure rather than about any specific cluster count.

  8. NMF and BERTopic agree on the same axes. Despite BERTopic using semantic embeddings + density clustering and NMF using TF-IDF + matrix factorization — two algorithmically unrelated approaches — both report the same headline archetypes: ML/LLM, DevOps/K8s, Java, Azure, Mobile, Clearance. Cross-method Jaccard hits 0.5 on the ML archetype; the domains are real, not method artifacts.

  9. The 2024-vs-2026 mix within the ML/LLM archetype is strikingly asymmetric. Cluster 1 has 44 rows in 2024 (11.3% of cluster total) and 345 rows in 2026 (89% of cluster total). This is more skewed than the sample's overall period balance (33/33/33). Put differently: the ML/LLM archetype is over 8× as prevalent in 2026 as in 2024, matching the aggregate AI-mention rise but with a sharper localization.

  10. "Rails" is a growing archetype. Cluster 13 (Ruby/Rails) grew 0.64% → 1.41% of postings. Against the narrative that Rails is in decline, this corpus shows a small but clear uptick. Cluster is only 92 rows; treat as a Wave 2 curiosity, not a headline finding.

docs.title: 10. Data quality caveats
docs.text:
1. Sample is 8,000 of 48,223 LLM-text rows (17%). BERTopic labels apply only to the sample. Consumers wanting full-corpus labels must extend via centroid nearest-neighbor or re-fit on a larger corpus.

  2. 46.8% BERTopic noise share. This is by construction (HDBSCAN refuses to cluster density-sparse regions). Consumers should treat archetype = -1 as a legitimate category ("stack-agnostic senior SWE"), not a NULL value.

  3. Seed instability at min_topic_size=30 is substantial (mean ARI 0.24 across 3 seeds). Cluster-ID mapping is seed-dependent. The saved labels correspond to SEED=13; re-running the script with a different seed would produce differently-numbered clusters, though the domain structure broadly survives.

  4. Aggregator-inclusion and company-capping materially change the BERTopic topology. The saved artifact is on the aggregator-included, uncapped sample. Downstream consumers who want aggregator-stratified or company-capped archetypes would need to re-fit (scripts are parametrized; the sensitivity outputs show what to expect).

  5. Raw-text rows are not covered by the artifact. The LLM-text subset is 70.8% of SWE LinkedIn corpus (100% of Kaggle, 56% of scraped). The 43.7% of scraped rows with raw-text fallback are outside this sample. Text-source distribution per-archetype in the sample is 100% llm (by construction).

  6. The 2024 sub-sample over-represents arshkon (55% arshkon + 45% asaniczka). This was intentional per the T09 spec ("prefer arshkon rows over asaniczka"). For ML-engineer-specific cluster analysis, the arshkon-leaning 2024 slice may disagree with asaniczka-majority cohort (T04 19.6 pp gap on ML Engineer is SWE). Consumers doing ML-archetype source-stratification should re-weight or re-sample.

  7. Tech-domain argmax assignment uses 18 hand-curated groups from the 135-column tech matrix. Rows with zero tech flags receive 'none' (this happens for ~30% of sample — see cluster_characterization.csv cluster 14 which has 0.52 mean_tech_count). The NMI computed against tech_domain under-counts fine-grained tech-specific similarity; a continuous-vector based tech similarity might give higher NMI, but the relative NMI ranking (tech_domain > seniority) is unlikely to change.

  8. Title-archetype regex is a proxy, not a gold standard. 12 classes + other_swe. ~45% of sample falls into other_swe. The high NMI against this target (0.216) is a floor — a finer title taxonomy would likely give higher NMI. This strengthens (not weakens) the domain-dominance verdict.

  9. The fuller corpus (48,223 LLM-text rows) may reveal archetypes not present in the 8,000-row sample. The saved artifact is a snapshot for downstream convenience, not a canonical taxonomy. If a Wave 3 task needs a different cluster count or a larger sample, the script is rerunnable.

docs.title: 11. Downstream action items
docs.text:
1. exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet is produced and validated (8,000 rows × 3 columns). Consumers: T11, T15, T17, T20, T28, T31, T34, T35.

  2. For ML/AI-specific claims (T14, T15, T28, T34, T35), use cluster 1 (ML/LLM) + cluster 25 (AI agents) + cluster 35 (perception) as the AI-archetype triple. Consider cluster 16 (PhD/research) as a supplementary AI-adjacent cohort. Always source-stratify the 2024 arshkon vs asaniczka subsets when comparing to 2026 (per Gate 1 ML-engineer pre-commit).

  3. For seniority-stratified claims touching archetypes (T11, T20, T28, T34), do NOT expect the archetype structure to align with seniority. Report J3/S4 shares within each archetype (use the cluster_characterization.csv columns j3_share_2024, j3_share_2026, s4_share_2024, s4_share_2026) rather than re-deriving seniority from archetype membership.

  4. For the strategic-pivot decision (Gate 2 memo), T09 recommends the paper pivot from "seniority restructuring" to "technology-ecosystem restructuring" as the lead. This is consistent with T06's within-vs-between decomposition (AI-mention rise is within-company; junior rise is between-company) and with the T30 finding that seniority direction is robust but magnitude is fragile.

  5. Sensitivity re-runs. If Gate 2 or Wave 3 wants clusters under aggregator-excluded or company-capped conditions, the script parametrization is straightforward; expected outcomes are already documented in §8.a and §8.b.

docs.title: T10. Title taxonomy evolution
docs.text:
Agent G, Wave 2. Generated 2026-04-20.

Question. How has the SWE title landscape evolved 2024 → 2026? What titles emerged, disappeared, or changed meaning, and does the title space look structurally different?

TL;DR. - The title vocabulary fragmented only modestly. Unique titles per 1K postings is essentially flat pooled-2024 (454) vs scraped-2026 (488). The top-10 and top-50 cumulative shares barely move (12.7%/26.6% vs 13.7%/25.3%), so the head of the distribution is stable. The headline impression of "the title space is exploding" is mostly an artifact of the scraped corpus being 2× the size of pooled 2024; per-1K richness is stable. - But the HEAD of the title distribution has rotated. 175 titles appear ≥ 10× in scraped 2026 with < 3 appearances in arshkon 2024 — these are the "truly new" titles, dominated by AI/ML compounds and new-formatted descriptors. Only 2 titles qualify as "arshkon-disappearing" under the task's strict threshold (arshkon ≥ 10 / scraped < 3): senior dotnet developer (n=19) and java architect (n=15). The pooled-2024 strict-threshold list is longer (58 titles) because asaniczka carries a wide tail of aggregator-and-clearance titles that scraper queries did not surface. - AI/ML compound titles are the biggest structural shift. Any-AI-term share in titles rose from 9.6% (pooled 2024) to 23.5% (scraped 2026) — a +13.9 pp jump. The rise is driven by a new ai engineer-family (ai engineer, ai/machine learning engineer, senior ai/machine learning engineer, applied ai engineer, principal ai engineer, ai platform engineer, ai agent engineer, agentic ai engineer, founding ai engineer, etc.). These titles almost universally have n=0 in arshkon; the ai engineer canonical title alone went from n=2 to n=240 (120×). - Seniority-marker share in titles is stable or slightly reshuffled. "Senior" share drops from 39.3% pooled 2024 → 27.0% scraped 2026, while "staff" rose (2.7% → 5.5%) and "director/manager" markers rose modestly. "Junior/entry" title-marker share barely changes (1.2% → 3.0%). The 12 pp drop in "senior" titles is notable — counter to the inflation intuition, employers in 2026 are not putting "senior" in the title as often, despite the YOE-derived senior share only falling ~7 pp (per T30 S4). Surprise. - Same-title content drift is large but within the within-2024 noise floor. TF-IDF cosine between pooled-2024 and scraped-2026 descriptions for the 10 most common shared titles is 0.76-0.87. Within-2024 cosine (arshkon vs asaniczka) for those same titles is 0.68-0.89 — the cross-period gap is only slightly wider than the within-period gap. Same title does NOT mean dramatically different content on average (the drift is in specific content dimensions — AI mentions, scope terms — that T11 and T12 / T13 measure directly). - Emerging category shares. ai_engineering title-category share went from 0.6% pooled 2024 → 4.7% scraped 2026 (+4.1 pp) — the single largest category-level shift. ml_infrastructure +0.17 pp, data_engineering +2.3 pp, platform_engineering +0.5 pp, mobile +0.5 pp. qa_testing DROPS (5.1% → 3.5%, -1.6 pp). This is the paper's concrete "new role categories" finding. - Disappearing-title list (Wave 3.5 T36 input). Strict arshkon-only threshold yields only 2 titles; pooled-2024 ≥ 10 / scraped < 3 yields 58. The pooled-2024 list is cluttered with clearance- and aggregator-heavy strings that reflect scraper sampling-frame differences, not real role disappearance. A cleaner interpretation is that arshkon-specific declines are .NET/Java architect/senior PHP/Drupal/Salesforce legacy-stack patterns — these reappear under the ≥5 threshold (see disappearing_titles_arshkon_ge5.csv). - Sensitivities are boring. Aggregator exclusion, cap=20, cap=50 all move the per-1K and per-category numbers by < 2 pp. The findings are robust.

Scripts: exploration/scripts/T10_title_taxonomy.py, exploration/scripts/T10_figures.py. Tables in exploration/tables/T10/, figures in exploration/figures/T10/.

docs.title: Data scope and filters
docs.text:
SWE (is_swe = TRUE), LinkedIn, English, date_flag='ok'. Total 68,137 rows:

group n_postings n_unique_titles arshkon 2024 4,691 2,424 asaniczka 2024 18,129 8,470 pooled 2024 22,820 10,362 scraped 2026 45,317 22,094 scraped 2026-03 19,740 10,839 scraped 2026-04 25,577 13,173

Title normalization. Lowercase, trim whitespace, collapse internal spaces. Fixed abbreviations: sr/sr. → senior, jr/jr. → junior, swe → software engineer, qa → quality assurance, ml → machine learning, sre → site reliability engineer, front-end / fullstack / fullstack → full stack (similar for back-end), iii/ii/iv → 3/2/4. Regex tests pass before extraction (see _assert_regexes() in the script).

All table cells below are from the full (no cap) / aggregators-included run. Sensitivities in exploration/tables/T10/step*_nonagg.csv, *_cap20.csv, *_cap50.csv.

docs.title: Step 1. Title vocabulary comparison (new / disappeared / shared)
docs.text:
Primary comparison: arshkon 2024 vs scraped 2026.

count Distinct titles arshkon 2,424 Distinct titles scraped 22,094 Distinct shared titles (≥10 arshkon AND ≥10 scraped) 58 New titles (≥10 scraped AND <3 arshkon) 175 Disappearing titles (≥10 arshkon AND <3 scraped) 2

The "disappearing" list is tiny under the strict threshold because almost every frequent arshkon title still has ≥ 3 scraped appearances. See the extended thresholds for a richer picture (Step 7).

Pooled-2024 baseline (arshkon + asaniczka). 10,362 distinct titles, 58 "disappeared" vs scraped, 83 "truly new" in scraped (stricter because many new-looking 2026 titles have at least 1-2 appearances in the larger asaniczka 2024 pool).

Top new titles in scraped 2026 that are absent or near-absent from arshkon (n rows: scraped count):

title_norm scraped_n arshkon_n ai engineer 240 2 ai/machine learning engineer 103 0 lead ai engineer 43 1 ai software engineer 41 1 senior ai/machine learning engineer 36 0 staff machine learning engineer 37 2 senior java full stack developer 34 0 senior devsecops engineer 31 0 applied ai engineer 29 0 principal ai engineer 26 0 ai platform engineer 24 0 principal machine learning engineer 24 0 agentic ai engineer 22 1 ai developer 22 0 robotics software engineer 24 0 founding software engineer 23 0 ai agent engineer 19 0 gen ai engineer 19 1 forward deployed software engineer 14 0 founding ai engineer 15 0

AI/ML/LLM/agent roles dominate. "Founding" and "forward deployed" are also novel 2026-only patterns — signals of a different type of firm joining the hiring market (startups posting founding-engineer roles; AI product companies posting forward-deployed engineer roles).

Seniority shift on the 10 most common shared-both-periods titles (step1_top10_shared_seniority_shift.csv). Headline: the seniority_3level shares are essentially stable for the common titles. senior software engineer remains ~97% senior-labeled in both periods; software engineer remains ~85-90% unknown-labeled. The only shift visible at this scale is embedded software engineer moving from 9% → 3.5% senior — but small-n.

docs.title: Step 2. Title concentration
docs.text:
group unique_per_1k HHI top-10 share top-50 share arshkon 2024 517 0.00449 15.4% 32.7% asaniczka 2024 467 0.00387 12.9% 26.5% pooled 2024 454 0.00369 12.7% 26.6% scraped 2026 488 0.00340 13.7% 25.3% scraped 2026-03 549 0.00310 13.1% 24.5% scraped 2026-04 515 0.00370 14.3% 26.1%

The title space is essentially stable per 1K postings. HHI dropped marginally (from 0.0037 to 0.0034 — both very low, indicating fragmented distributions). Top-10 and top-50 cumulative shares are within 1 pp of pooled 2024. The scraped corpus simply has more total rows, so more total distinct titles; rate-adjusted the fragmentation is flat. The title-space is not "exploding."

Cap=20 and cap=50 company sensitivities move these numbers by ≤ 5 unique_per_1k — negligible.

docs.title: Step 3. Compound / hybrid AI titles
docs.text:
period share any AI term share ai share ml share data share llm share agent share genai arshkon 2024 12.6% 1.7% 2.5% 8.8% 0.1% 0.0% 0.4% asaniczka 2024 8.8% 1.0% 1.6% 6.4% 0.1% 0.0% 0.3% pooled 2024 9.6% 1.2% 1.8% 6.9% 0.1% 0.0% 0.3% scraped 2026 23.5% 10.2% 5.7% 10.2% 0.3% 0.9% 1.0%

The ai title-token share went 1.2% → 10.2% (+9 pp; ~9× multiplier). The ml share roughly tripled. The data share rose by ~3 pp. agent and genai went from near-zero to ~1%.

This is almost entirely new-title-driven (per Step 1). ai/machine learning engineer, applied ai engineer, ai platform engineer, ai agent engineer, agentic ai engineer, and founding ai engineer dominate the list of titles that did not exist in arshkon 2024.

docs.title: Step 4. Title-to-content alignment (TF-IDF cosine)
docs.text:
For the 10 most common shared-both-periods titles, fit TF-IDF on all posting-level descriptions across arshkon + asaniczka + scraped for that title, compute mean-vector per group, take cosine between group means.

title n_arshkon n_scraped cos arshkon vs scraped cos pooled-2024 vs scraped within-2024 cos (null floor) software engineer 149 1,545 0.787 0.856 0.886 senior software engineer 171 1,444 0.767 0.857 0.859 data engineer 86 764 0.799 0.839 0.883 senior data engineer 47 476 0.743 0.823 0.849 devops engineer 57 464 0.752 0.820 0.839 full stack engineer 58 382 0.767 0.806 0.865 embedded software engineer 32 200 0.585 0.777 0.679 cloud engineer 30 198 0.695 0.754 0.773 java developer 41 179 0.660 0.781 0.770 system engineer 42 118 0.764 0.840 0.812

Interpretation. - Pooled-2024 vs scraped cosine is 0.75 - 0.86; within-2024 null floor is 0.68 - 0.89. - Cross-period drift is only slightly above the within-period noise floor. On SOFTWARE ENGINEER, cos-pooled-vs-scraped (0.856) essentially equals the within-2024 floor (0.886) — same title, same content on average. - The most drifted title is embedded software engineer where arshkon-vs-scraped cos is 0.585 (vs a within-2024 floor of 0.679). This indicates embedded descriptions have genuinely changed content composition; likely adding more modern-tooling content that did not exist in 2024. - The java developer cross-period cos (0.66) also lies modestly below its within-2024 floor (0.77), suggesting some drift on that title.

Implication. Most shared titles still read like the same role on average. Content-level change is not uniform across titles; the SWE title taxonomy IS adding content (see T11 breadth/AI-mention results below), but the ADDITIONS average out against the stable core of each title. T12 and T13 will find the dimension-specific changes that TF-IDF flattens.

docs.title: Step 5. Title inflation / deflation signals
docs.text:
Share of SWE titles containing each seniority marker (priority: principal > staff > director > manager > senior > lead > mid > junior > none):

group junior senior lead principal staff director manager none arshkon 2024 2.5% 26.2% 5.5% 2.8% 2.5% 0.2% 0.7% 59.1% asaniczka 2024 0.9% 42.7% 8.5% 5.9% 2.7% 0.2% 1.4% 37.5% pooled 2024 1.2% 39.3% 7.9% 5.2% 2.7% 0.2% 1.3% 41.9% scraped 2026 3.0% 27.0% 5.0% 3.7% 5.5% 0.6% 0.6% 54.1%

Key observations: - "Senior" title-marker share drops 12 pp pooled-2024 → scraped-2026 (39% → 27%). This is a substantial change and surprising. It is also driven in part by the arshkon-vs-asaniczka asymmetry in 2024 (asaniczka 43% has more "senior" titles than arshkon 26%); the scraped number 27% is actually close to arshkon. - "Staff" title share DOUBLES (2.7% → 5.5%). Staff engineers are a growing labeled tier in 2026. - "Junior" title-marker share rises from 1.2% → 3.0% (+1.8 pp). - "None" share (no marker in title) rose from 42% → 54%. More titles are role-descriptor only (no seniority prefix).

Caveat for the "senior drop". The 2024 anchor is confounded by the asaniczka-vs-arshkon asymmetry (T05 documented asaniczka's "senior" share is higher than arshkon). Arshkon-only "senior"-title share is 26%; scraped 27% matches. This could be read as "no real senior-title decline, asaniczka was outlier". OR that the scraped corpus behaves like arshkon (not asaniczka) in this dimension. Either way, the arshkon-only sensitivity weakens the headline "senior drop."

Non-aggregator and cap-20/cap-50 sensitivities move these numbers by ≤ 2 pp.

docs.title: Step 6. Emerging role categories
docs.text:
Share of SWE titles matching each category regex (multi-label; postings may match multiple categories). See EMERGING_PATTERNS in the script for exact patterns.

category pooled 2024 scraped 2026 Δ (pp) ai_engineering 0.6% 4.7% +4.1 data_engineering 5.8% 8.1% +2.3 frontend_fullstack 9.1% 11.8% +2.7 cloud_engineering 1.5% 2.0% +0.5 platform_engineering 1.0% 1.5% +0.5 mobile 0.4% 0.8% +0.5 reliability_engineering 1.9% 2.3% +0.4 backend 2.9% 3.6% +0.7 security_engineering 0.3% 0.5% +0.2 ml_infrastructure 0.1% 0.3% +0.2 data_science 0.1% 0.2% +0.1 embedded_firmware 2.5% 1.7% −0.8 qa_testing 5.1% 3.5% −1.6 robotics 0.0% 0.0% ≈ 0 blockchain_crypto 0.0% 0.0% ≈ 0

Biggest rises: ai_engineering (+4.1 pp), frontend_fullstack (+2.7 pp), data_engineering (+2.3 pp), backend (+0.7 pp), platform_engineering (+0.5 pp), mobile (+0.5 pp).

Biggest drops: qa_testing (−1.6 pp), embedded_firmware (−0.8 pp).

The qa_testing decline is notable — it's not that QA jobs are going to zero, but that their share of the SWE title mix is shrinking.

Category examples (scraped 2026, top titles per category) — see step6_emerging_category_examples_full.csv. For ai_engineering: ai engineer, ai software engineer, lead ai engineer, applied ai engineer, principal ai engineer, etc. For platform_engineering: platform engineer, senior platform engineer, lead platform engineer. Good face validity.

docs.title: Step 7. Disappearing-title list (Wave 3.5 T36 input)
docs.text:
Primary artifact: exploration/tables/T10/disappearing_titles.csv

Under the task-specified threshold (arshkon ≥ 10 / scraped < 3), only 2 titles qualify:

title arshkon_n scraped_n top employers 2024 senior dotnet developer 19 2 Infosys, ACL Digital, SSi People, Amtex Systems Inc., INSPYR Solutions java architect 15 1 Photon, Quantum World Technologies Inc., Fixity Technologies, TechMatrix Inc, Zodiac Solutions, Inc

Interpretation. The strict threshold is met by only 2 titles because almost every frequent arshkon title still has at least a few scraped appearances. The 2 that qualify are both legacy-stack / IT-services-staffing titles (.NET/Java architect patterns from Indian IT-services companies). This matches the prior expectation of legacy-stack decline.

Sensitivity — arshkon ≥ 5 / scraped < 3: yields 11 titles, still dominated by legacy-stack patterns (drupal developer, senior php developer, devops architect, database developer, senior database developer, scala developer, senior dotnet developer, java architect, java application developer, product engineer i, software development engineer aws marketplace). See disappearing_titles_arshkon_ge5.csv.

Sensitivity — pooled 2024 ≥ 10 / scraped < 3: yields 58 titles, see disappearing_titles_pooled2024_ge10.csv. Heavily contaminated by: - Clearance-job patterns (ClearanceJobs company sponsor): senior software engineer with security clearance, senior systems engineer with security clearance, systems engineer 3 with security clearance, senior software developer with security clearance, etc. These are asaniczka-specific sampling-frame: the scraped corpus does not index ClearanceJobs. - Capital One / Jobs for Humanity formulaic titles: lead software engineer full stack (java python angular react), senior software engineer backend (golang aws), etc. These are templated format-heavy titles that the 2026 scraper's query design did not surface. - Genuine legacy-stack declines: java architect, senior dotnet developer, senior php developer, php developer, database developer, drupal developer, servicenow developer, senior salesforce developer, devops architect, senior java engineer, mainframe developer.

Recommendation for T36. Use disappearing_titles.csv (primary, n=2) augmented with disappearing_titles_arshkon_ge5.csv (n=11) as the Wave 3.5 input, filtering out clearance/aggregator-specific strings manually. T36 should interpret scraped-absence as either (a) real decline (legacy-stack patterns) or (b) sampling-frame artifact (clearance titles, formulaic aggregator titles) — the company list in the top_employers_2024 column will guide this.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Title-space fragmentation is essentially flat per 1K postings. Unique-per-1K is 454 pooled-2024 vs 488 scraped-2026 — a modest ~8% rise. The impression of "title taxonomy is fragmenting" is largely an artifact of corpus size, not per-capita richness. This weakens the "title space is exploding" framing.

  2. "Senior" title-marker share DROPPED 12 pp pooled-2024 → scraped-2026 (39% → 27%). Even against arshkon-only (26%) the scraped 27% is essentially flat, so there's no arshkon-vs-scraped "senior rose" story in title markers. But comparing to the full pooled-2024 baseline, "senior" title prevalence fell substantially. This is interpretable with the T05 asaniczka-asymmetry caveat: asaniczka in 2024 over-weighted "senior"-prefixed titles, and the scraped corpus behaves closer to arshkon on this dimension.

  3. "Staff" title share DOUBLED (2.7% → 5.5%). This is consistent with anecdotal reports of the staff-engineer tier becoming more common; quantitatively confirmed at the title level.

  4. "None" (no seniority marker in title) rose 12 pp (42% → 54%). The rise is mostly compensating the senior-drop. More 2026 titles are pure role descriptors (e.g., full stack engineer, ai engineer, platform engineer) without seniority prefixes. This implies either: (a) employers are pushing seniority to the description rather than the title, or (b) role-type titles are in expanding share while seniority-prefix titles are stable-share.

  5. Content drift for shared titles is only slightly above within-2024 noise floor (cosine ~0.80-0.86 pooled-2024-vs-scraped vs ~0.77-0.89 within-2024). TF-IDF at this aggregation level captures the stable core of each role but averages out dimension-specific changes (AI language, scope language — which T11 shows clearly rose). The aggregate "same-title = same-content" impression is partly a flattening artifact.

  6. QA/testing titles are shrinking as a share of SWE titles (5.1% → 3.5% for qa_testing category). Combined with embedded_firmware (2.5% → 1.7%), these two legacy-SWE categories are visibly losing share. This is one of the cleaner "role displacement" signals in the T10 data.

  7. "Founding" and "forward deployed" as 2026-only title patterns. 23 founding software engineer and 15 founding ai engineer and 14 forward deployed software engineer postings in 2026 scraped with arshkon counts of 0. These reflect startup/enterprise-GPU-company emergence in the 2026 hiring mix — a composition-shift signal consistent with Gate 1's between-company story.

docs.title: Sensitivity summary
docs.text:
Four sensitivities run on every aggregate finding: full (default), nonagg (is_aggregator=False), cap20 (≤20 postings per canonical company), cap50 (≤50 per canonical company). Outputs in exploration/tables/T10/*_{nonagg,cap20,cap50}.csv.

Headline AI-token share on titles (Step 3), for sensitivity robustness:

dim pooled 2024 scraped 2026 Δ full 9.6% 23.5% +13.9 pp nonagg 9.5% 23.1% +13.6 pp cap20 9.8% 24.4% +14.6 pp cap50 9.9% 24.1% +14.3 pp

Consistent across sensitivities (within 1 pp). The AI-title share rise is not a concentration artifact.

Concentration (unique per 1K) pooled-2024 vs scraped-2026:

dim pooled 2024 scraped 2026 full 454 488 nonagg 471 516 cap20 458 510 cap50 456 509

Non-aggregator and cap-20 show slightly higher fragmentation in scraped than in pooled-2024 (+45 titles per 1K under nonagg; +52 under cap20) — but still not "explosive."
