docs.title: T11. Requirements complexity & credential stacking
docs.text:
Agent G, Wave 2. Generated 2026-04-20.

Question. Beyond simple "scope inflation," has the STRUCTURAL complexity of what employers ask for changed between 2024 and 2026? Are 2026 postings asking for more TYPES of things simultaneously (credential stacking), with higher technology density, broader scope, and more AI requirements?

TL;DR. - Raw requirement breadth rose +3.1 items/posting pooled-2024 (10.1) → scraped-2026 (13.2), but this is strongly length-confounded (r=0.40-0.43 with description length in each period). The residualized breadth rose +1.22 items (pooled -0.81 → scraped +0.41), SNR 1.8 (borderline near_noise). The raw-number growth is real, but about 60% of it is absorbed by the length residualization. - Credential stack depth — the "stacking" question — rose modestly on the raw measure (+0.20 points, 4.03 → 4.23 on 0-7 scale) but substantially on the residualized measure (+0.70 points). The share of postings asking ≥ 5 out of 7 credential categories went from 42.1% (pooled 2024) to 44.9% (scraped 2026) aggregate, and from 42.9% to 59.8% within J3 entry-level postings — a +16.9 pp rise in "multi-category credential stacking" among entry roles. - The J3 (entry-level YOE ≤ 2) rise is the headline finding. J3 requirement_breadth raw rose from 9.90 → 12.11 (+2.21 items), credential_stack_depth raw from 4.29 → 4.76 (+0.47), and credential_stack_depth residualized from 0.30 → 0.63 (+0.33). J3 share_depth ≥ 5 went from 42.9% to 59.8% (+16.9 pp). Entry-level postings are asking for substantially more TYPES of things in 2026 than in 2024. This survives the length residualization (residualized J3 breadth: -1.47 → +0.11, +1.58 change). Scope-inflation-for-juniors is a survivable claim in T11. - Tech-count (the purest length-insensitive signal, r=0.12-0.19 with length) rose +2.06 techs/posting aggregate (5.41 → 7.46), and +1.64 techs for J3 (4.87 → 6.51). This is the cleanest within-posting "more tools required" finding. SNR 6.4. Strong. - AI-binary (strict pattern) rose +13.1 pp aggregate (0.94% → 14.07%), +10.5 pp for J3 (1.4% → 11.9%), +13.1 pp for S4 (0.9% → 14.1%). SNR 28.8. Very strong, matches Gate 1's +39 pp broad-pattern finding scaled down to the stricter V1-style pattern. - Management indicators' content validation is mixed. On a 25-row stratified semantic-context sample per tier × term × period (total 500 rows), strict-tier terms like hire (0.92-0.96), manage_people (0.76-0.92), direct_reports (0.64-0.68), and mentor (0.60-0.68) pass the 80% threshold in most configurations. performance_review (0.28) and eng_manager (0.44 pooled 2024 / 0.84 scraped 2026) fail in at least one period. All four broad-tier terms (lead, team, stakeholder, coordinate) fail the precision check in at least one period — they are too semantically broad to carry a management signal. Recommended action: report strong-tier density (mgmt_strong_density) as the primary management indicator; flag broad-tier as diagnostic only. - Outlier analysis: top 1% by requirement_breadth. In pooled 2024, the top 1% (n=241, threshold 26.0) averages 28.99 breadth with 4,068 chars; in scraped 2026 (n=484, threshold 32.0) averages 35.69 breadth with 6,132 chars. The 2026 outlier threshold is 32 items — higher than the 2024 top-1%-cutoff. Outlier titles are mostly Senior / Staff / Principal / Lead SWE, plus AI-specific titles in 2026; outlier employers include Walmart, JPMorgan, Citigroup, GEICO, Visa, Wells Fargo (financial-services and big-employer weight). - Sensitivities. Entry-specialist exclusion moves J3 breadth by ~0.03 items (no material effect). Company cap=20 / cap=50 move breadth means by ≤0.1 items in pooled 2024 and ≤0.1 in scraped 2026. Text-source split shows breadth is slightly HIGHER on raw-text scraped (13.37) vs LLM-labeled (13.02), but credential_stack_depth is slightly LOWER on raw (3.90 vs 4.48) — raw-text has more boilerplate inflating breadth counts while the stack-depth signal is cleaner from LLM-labeled text. Both measures show the cross-period direction regardless.

Scripts: exploration/scripts/T11_requirements_complexity.py, exploration/scripts/T11_figures.py. Features: exploration/artifacts/shared/T11_posting_features.parquet. Tables in exploration/tables/T11/, figures in exploration/figures/T11/.

docs.title: Data scope and filters
docs.text:
SWE LinkedIn English date_flag='ok' — 68,137 rows.

Text source. description_core_llm where llm_extraction_coverage='labeled' (47,937 rows); fallback to raw description for 20,200 non-labeled rows (all scraped). 4 arshkon + 20 asaniczka + 19,661 scraped rows use raw text. Sensitivity in text_source_split.csv.

Tech count. Loaded from exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_tech_matrix.parquet (135 tech booleans). Not re-scanned in T11.

docs.title: Feature extraction
docs.text:
For each posting, T11 computes:

Feature Definition tech_count Sum of 135 tech booleans from swe_tech_matrix.parquet soft_skill_count # of distinct matches from 10 soft-skill patterns (communication, collaboration, problem solving, leadership, adaptability, learning, detail-oriented, time management, curiosity, empathy) scope_count # of distinct matches from 8 scope patterns (ownership, end-to-end, cross-functional, stakeholder, autonomous, initiative, scope, strategy) mgmt_strong_count # of distinct matches from 6 strict-tier management patterns (manage_people, direct_reports, mentor, hire, performance_review, eng_manager) mgmt_broad_count mgmt_strong + 4 broad-extra patterns (lead, team, stakeholder, coordinate) ai_binary Boolean: V1-style strict AI pattern match on description education_level 0/½/3 = none/BS/MS/PhD (highest match) yoe_min_years_llm Primary YOE (from pipeline) requirement_breadth tech_count + soft + scope + mgmt_broad + ai_binary + (edu > 0) + (yoe.notna()) credential_stack_depth # of distinct category presences (0-7) tech_density tech_count / length × 1K scope_density scope_count / length × 1K mgmt_strong_density, mgmt_broad_density analogous *_resid Residual after fitting y = b0 + b1 × log(description_cleaned_length) on the full SWE LinkedIn frame

All regex patterns tested with positive/negative assertions before use (see _assert_patterns() in the script).

docs.title: Gate 0 pre-commit: length residualization
docs.text:
Correlation of breadth metrics with description_cleaned_length:

metric pooled_2024 r scraped_2026 r all r requirement_breadth 0.405 0.392 0.431 credential_stack_depth 0.544 0.286 0.342 tech_count 0.117 0.187 0.217 soft_skill_count 0.500 0.501 0.507 scope_count 0.394 0.366 0.417 mgmt_broad_count 0.421 0.377 0.388

All four breadth / stacking composites have r > 0.3 with length in at least one period. Residualized variants are the primary for all breadth + stacking claims; raw variants are sensitivity. tech_count has r < 0.2 and is reported raw.

Figure: figures/T11/length_correlation_check.png.

docs.title: Step 1. Distribution panel (primary)
docs.text:
Per-posting means for key metrics, by period × seniority cut. Full panel: distribution_panel.csv (37 rows).

Key cross-period comparisons (pooled 2024 → scraped 2026):

metric pooled 2024 scraped 2026 Δ raw SNR requirement_breadth (raw) 10.12 13.17 +3.05 19.8 requirement_breadth_resid -0.81 0.41 +1.22 1.8 credential_stack_depth (raw) 4.03 4.23 +0.20 0.6 credential_stack_depth_resid 0.13 -0.07 -0.20 - tech_count 5.41 7.46 +2.06 6.4 ai_binary 0.94% 14.07% +13.1 pp 28.8 scope_density 0.30 0.40 +0.10 3.0 mgmt_strong_density 0.112 0.092 -0.020 0.87 mgmt_broad_density 0.296 0.255 -0.041 1.0 description_cleaned_length 2,244 3,593 +1,349 3.8

SNR column uses within-2024 |arshkon − asaniczka| as the noise floor.

Headline on all-SWE: - Length rose +1,349 chars (matches T05's ~1,750 number approximately). - Tech count rose by ~2 techs (cleanest content signal). - AI binary rose dramatically. - Scope density rose but mgmt density FELL — surprising, since naively one might expect scope AND management to rise together. - Credential stack depth rose on raw (+0.20) but FELL on residualized (-0.20). Aggregate-level stacking is mostly driven by longer descriptions touching on more categories mechanically.

docs.title: Step 2. J3 / S4 comparison (primary)
docs.text:
Entry-level (J3: YOE ≤ 2, LLM-labeled):

metric pooled 2024 J3 scraped 2026 J3 Δ n 1,666 2,889 - requirement_breadth (raw) 9.90 12.11 +2.21 requirement_breadth_resid -1.47 0.11 +1.58 credential_stack_depth (raw) 4.29 4.76 +0.47 credential_stack_depth_resid 0.30 0.63 +0.33 tech_count 4.87 6.51 +1.64 scope_density 0.29 0.41 +0.12 mgmt_broad_count 0.50 0.57 +0.07 ai_binary 1.38% 11.87% +10.5 pp education_level (mean, 0-3) 1.28 1.30 +0.02 description_cleaned_length 2,404 2,692 +288

J3 residualized breadth rose +1.58 (the largest residualized effect in the panel). This is the strongest quantitative carrier of "entry-level scope inflation" — and it survives length-residualization. J3 credential_stack_depth_resid rose +0.33 (pooled 0.30 → scraped 0.63) — the stacking claim survives residualization for entry roles.

Senior (S4: YOE ≥ 5, LLM-labeled):

metric pooled 2024 S4 scraped 2026 S4 Δ n 12,884 12,870 - requirement_breadth (raw) 10.98 14.29 +3.31 requirement_breadth_resid -0.42 2.19 +2.61 credential_stack_depth (raw) 4.42 4.88 +0.46 credential_stack_depth_resid 0.42 0.72 +0.30 tech_count 5.76 7.95 +2.19 scope_density 0.29 0.47 +0.18 mgmt_broad_count 0.83 1.03 +0.20 ai_binary 0.92% 14.06% +13.1 pp

S4 residualized breadth rose +2.61 — actually LARGER than J3 in residualized terms. Senior roles are adding MORE length-normalized requirements than entry roles on this measure.

Arshkon-only co-primary for senior (per Gate 1):

metric arshkon-only S4 (n=2,246) scraped 2026 S4 Δ requirement_breadth_resid 0.33 2.19 +1.86 credential_stack_depth_resid 0.37 0.72 +0.35

Arshkon-only has HIGHER requirement_breadth_resid baseline than pooled (consistent with T05's asaniczka-leaner-text asymmetry), so the cross-period delta shrinks but direction stays UP.

T30 panel (J1 / J2 / J3 / J4 and S1 / S3 / S4 / S5): see distribution_panel.csv. Direction consistent across panel (J3/J4 all UP on breadth_resid and stack_depth_resid; S4/S5 all UP). J3_rule and S4_rule (rule YOE fallback for non-labeled rows) also both UP.

Native-label diagnostics: - entry_final (seniority_final = 'entry'): pooled 2024 (n=415) breadth 8.75, resid -1.62; scraped 2026 (n=1,650) breadth 11.55, resid -0.43. Same direction as J3. - S1_label (seniority_3level = 'senior'): pooled 2024 (n=13,665) breadth 10.95, resid -0.34; scraped 2026 (n=20,028) breadth 14.57, resid 1.38. Larger magnitude than S4 — but the label definition itself shifts across period (platform relabeling per T05), so label-based senior claims are diagnostic only.

Figures: figures/T11/distribution_hists.png, residualized_breadth_by_seniority.png.

docs.title: Step 3. Credential stacking: share of postings with ≥ K categories
docs.text:
(categories: tech, education, YOE, soft_skills, scope, mgmt, AI)

Pooled 2024 vs scraped 2026, by seniority cut:

seniority cut period n share ≥ 3 share ≥ 4 share ≥ 5 share ≥ 6 mean depth all pooled 2024 22,820 84.3% 66.4% 42.1% 16.1% 4.03 all scraped 2026 45,317 89.6% 73.0% 44.9% 16.8% 4.23 J3 pooled 2024 1,666 93.3% 74.4% 42.9% 17.7% 4.29 J3 scraped 2026 2,889 97.8% 87.2% 59.8% 27.6% 4.76 J4 pooled 2024 3,874 92.9% 75.0% 44.9% 17.5% 4.30 J4 scraped 2026 5,880 97.3% 86.1% 59.0% 27.8% 4.73 S4 pooled 2024 12,884 92.4% 76.6% 52.5% 21.1% 4.42 S4 scraped 2026 12,870 96.6% 86.2% 65.8% 34.3% 4.88 S5 pooled 2024 6,512 91.2% 74.4% 52.0% 22.6% 4.40 S5 scraped 2026 5,915 96.1% 86.2% 68.0% 36.8% 4.92

The stacking question — "are postings asking for more TYPES of things simultaneously?" — has YES answers for every seniority cut: - J3 share_depth ≥ 5: +16.9 pp (42.9% → 59.8%) - J4 share_depth ≥ 5: +14.1 pp (44.9% → 59.0%) - S4 share_depth ≥ 5: +13.3 pp (52.5% → 65.8%) - S5 share_depth ≥ 5: +16.0 pp (52.0% → 68.0%)

The rise is similar across seniority cuts — scope inflation is not concentrated in juniors, it applies roughly equally to all levels. But since the j-cuts start from a lower baseline (42%) vs s-cuts (52%), the depth-5+ rise is more consequential for entry roles on a relative basis.

Within-2024 floor: aggregate share_depth ≥ 5 is 34.6% on arshkon vs 44.2% on asaniczka — a within-period gap of 9.6 pp. The cross-period +2.8 pp aggregate is well under this floor; but the +16.9 pp J3 and +13.3 pp S4 deltas are larger than the within-2024 gap for most cells. (The J3 within-2024 gap: arshkon J3 41.8% vs asaniczka J3 42.9% — cross-period J3 gap of +16.9 pp dominates.)

Figure: figures/T11/stack_depth_panel.png.

docs.title: Step 4. Entry-level specifically
docs.text:
Dedicated entry-level comparison (entry_level_comparison.csv) with all the primary metrics and their raw/resid variants:

cut n breadth_resid_mean stack_depth_mean tech_count_mean ai_binary_mean length_mean J3_pooled_2024 1,666 -1.47 4.29 4.87 1.38% 2,404 J3_arshkon 449 -1.03 4.20 5.31 2.23% 2,252 J3_scraped_2026 2,889 0.11 4.76 6.51 11.87% 2,692 J4_pooled_2024 3,874 -1.33 4.30 4.97 1.03% 2,328 J4_scraped_2026 5,880 0.51 4.73 6.88 12.40% 2,712 J1_label_pooled_2024 415 -1.62 3.55 4.68 0.72% 1,907 J1_label_scraped_2026 1,650 -0.43 3.90 6.70 12.36% 2,944

Entry 2024 vs entry 2026 headline: - J3 breadth_resid: -1.47 → +0.11 (+1.58 in residualized units). - J3 tech_count: 4.87 → 6.51 (+1.64 techs per posting). - J3 ai_binary: 1.4% → 11.9% (+10.5 pp). - J3 stack_depth: 4.29 → 4.76 (+0.47).

J1 native-label (entry) has similar direction (breadth_resid -1.62 → -0.43, +1.19). The J1-specific change is of similar order but J1 carries the platform-relabeling drift caveat per T05.

Entry-specialist sensitivity (entry_specialist_sensitivity.csv):

cut n breadth_mean breadth_resid_mean stack_depth_mean tech_count_mean ai_binary_mean J3_pooled_2024_full 1,666 9.90 -1.47 4.29 4.87 1.4% J3_pooled_2024_ex_specialist 1,495 9.93 -1.50 4.30 4.86 1.5% J3_scraped_2026_full 2,889 12.11 0.11 4.76 6.51 11.9% J3_scraped_2026_ex_specialist 2,575 12.18 0.12 4.78 6.57 12.0%

Excluding the 206 entry-specialist employers (from T06's list) moves the J3 metrics by ~0.03 items — no material effect on entry-level complexity findings. The entry-level scope inflation is NOT driven by entry specialists; it applies to entry postings from non-specialist firms too.

docs.title: Step 5. Management indicator deep dive
docs.text:
Top 10 terms triggering strict tier by period (mgmt_term_top10.csv):

period tier term share_of_postings pooled 2024 strong mentor 21.2% pooled 2024 strong eng_manager 2.1% pooled 2024 strong performance_review 0.9% pooled 2024 strong manage_people 0.6% pooled 2024 strong direct_reports 0.4% pooled 2024 strong hire 0.1% scraped 2026 strong mentor 27.9% scraped 2026 strong performance_review 1.7% scraped 2026 strong eng_manager 1.2% scraped 2026 strong hire 0.7% scraped 2026 strong manage_people 0.4% scraped 2026 strong direct_reports 0.3% period tier term share_of_postings pooled 2024 broad_extra stakeholder 17.2% pooled 2024 broad_extra lead 13.4% pooled 2024 broad_extra coordinate 13.1% pooled 2024 broad_extra team 0.3% scraped 2026 broad_extra stakeholder 25.9% scraped 2026 broad_extra lead 16.1% scraped 2026 broad_extra coordinate 12.7% scraped 2026 broad_extra team 0.3%

Strict tier rose for most terms: mentor +6.6 pp, performance_review +0.8 pp, hire +0.6 pp; manage_people and direct_reports and eng_manager essentially flat. Strict-tier mgmt language is rising modestly, with the mentor and hire specifically growing.

Broad tier: stakeholder rose +8.6 pp, lead +2.7 pp, coordinate essentially flat, team flat.

Precision validation on 25-row stratified semantic-context samples per tier × term × period (mgmt_precision_summary.csv). The heuristic check looks for people/team/reports tokens within a ±80-char window around each match, and excludes matches preceded by explicit negation. It's a proxy for true context precision, not a human-graded ground truth — but it is not tautological because the check adds semantic constraints the pattern itself did not carry.

tier term pooled 2024 precision scraped 2026 precision strong hire 0.96 0.96 strong manage_people 0.92 0.76 strong mentor 0.60 0.68 strong eng_manager 0.44 0.84 strong direct_reports 0.64 0.68 strong performance_review 0.28 0.28 broad team 1.00 1.00 broad lead 0.52 0.32 broad stakeholder 0.28 0.24 broad coordinate 0.40 0.16

Sub-patterns with heuristic precision < 0.80 (flag as diagnostic-only): - strong / mentor (0.60 / 0.68): the \bmentor(s|ed|ing|ship)?\b pattern hits "mentorship programs" as perk text and some generic "mentor others" language lacking specific direct-report framing. - strong / direct_reports (0.64 / 0.68): mostly caught by the reports? (into|to) and direct reports patterns; the false positives are "reports to" in non-management contexts (status reports). - strong / eng_manager (0.44 pooled-2024): low because "people manager" and "line manager" are generic; engineering manager alone is more precise. - strong / manage_people (0.76 scraped): below 0.80 cutoff — misses are ambiguous "manage a team" in short contexts. - strong / performance_review (0.28 / 0.28): fail. Catches "performance review" as a product/code review activity or "performance evaluation" in generic QA contexts, not as a management responsibility. - broad / lead, stakeholder, coordinate: all fail in at least one period. These terms are too semantically broad — they capture ANY leadership/partnership/alignment language regardless of whether a management responsibility is actually being described. - broad / team passes at 1.0 but the pattern hits only 0.3% of postings — the pattern is too narrow to be meaningful.

Recommended primary management measure: mgmt_strong_density minus patterns that failed precision (drop performance_review and eng_manager subterms where they fail). A cleaner derived "high-precision management density" using only hire, manage_people (pooled 2024 only; borderline on scraped), would be more defensible.

Alternative interpretation. If we accept the broad tier as a proxy for organizational collaboration language generally (not strict management), then its rise (+2.7 pp lead, +8.6 pp stakeholder) is still a real signal — just not a management signal per se. The stakeholder rise is substantial and consistent with T11's scope-density rise (from 0.30 to 0.40).

Figures: figures/T11/mgmt_terms.png, precision_validation.png.

docs.title: Step 6. Outlier analysis (top 1% by breadth)
docs.text:
period n_outliers pct99_threshold outlier breadth mean outlier tech mean outlier length mean outlier ai_binary mean pooled 2024 241 26.0 28.99 19.77 4,068 2.9% scraped 2026 484 32.0 35.69 25.37 6,132 57.4%

The top 1% breadth threshold rose from 26 items (2024) to 32 items (2026) — a +6-item increase in what counts as "outlier breadth."

Outlier AI-binary rose from 2.9% → 57.4%. The most complex 2026 postings are overwhelmingly AI-mention-positive. In 2024 they were not.

Outlier top-employers 2024: Walmart (9), Syrinx Consulting (6), ClearanceJobs (5), Capital One (5), Total Quality Logistics (4), Allegiant (4), CACI International (3), Garmin (3), Equifax (3), Leidos (3). Mix of retailer + staffing + defense.

Outlier top-employers 2026: Jobs via Dice (22), JPMorgan Chase (12), Citigroup (11), GEICO (10), Visa (9), Wells Fargo (8), Solera Holdings (7), Walmart (6), Cisco (5), Deloitte (5). Financial services dominate 2026 outliers. This suggests that big-bank and insurance engineering postings may have very long, requirement-dense JDs in 2026.

Outlier top-titles 2026 include Senior Software Engineer (23), Principal Software Engineer (13), Software Engineer (13), Full Stack Engineer (11), Full Stack Developer (10), Lead Software Engineer (9). Not AI-specific titles — outliers are generic SWE titles, but with broader requirements in 2026.

Are outliers real or template-bloated? 20-row samples per period (outlier_sample_20_per_period.csv) show outliers have length 2,400-10,400 chars (median ~5,000). In 2024 the outliers are e.g. Agilysys Software Engineer (3,167 chars, breadth 27, 17 techs); in 2026 an example is Cisco Software Engineer (4,016 chars, breadth 33, 21 techs). Neither set looks template-bloated — these are genuinely long, requirement-dense postings. The rise in outlier threshold is driven by content, not boilerplate.

docs.title: Step 7. Within-2024 SNR check
docs.text:
metric arshkon asaniczka pooled 2024 scraped 2026 within-2024 abs cross-period abs SNR requirement_breadth 9.99 10.15 10.12 13.17 0.15 3.05 19.8 requirement_breadth_resid -0.28 -0.95 -0.81 0.41 0.67 1.22 1.8 credential_stack_depth 3.76 4.10 4.03 4.23 0.34 0.20 0.59 tech_count 5.66 5.34 5.41 7.46 0.32 2.06 6.4 ai_binary 0.013 0.008 0.009 0.141 0.005 0.131 28.8 description_cleaned_length 1,961 2,317 2,244 3,593 356 1,349 3.8 scope_density 0.326 0.294 0.301 0.397 0.033 0.097 3.0 mgmt_strong_density 0.094 0.117 0.112 0.092 0.023 0.020 0.87 mgmt_broad_density 0.263 0.305 0.296 0.255 0.041 0.042 1.00

Headline metric SNRs: - ai_binary: 28.8 (very strong) - requirement_breadth raw: 19.8 (very strong) - tech_count: 6.4 (strong) - description_length: 3.8 (strong) - scope_density: 3.0 (strong) - requirement_breadth_resid: 1.8 (borderline — near_noise) - credential_stack_depth: 0.59 (below noise) - mgmt_strong_density, mgmt_broad_density: ≤ 1 (below noise — consistent with the precision-validation weakness and no net cross-period movement)

Note on credential_stack_depth SNR: the within-2024 difference (0.34) is larger than the cross-period difference (0.20) in raw terms. This is why the J3-specific comparison matters — aggregate stacking is near_noise, but J3-specific stacking is a clearer signal, and residualized stacking strengthens further.

docs.title: Step 8. Text-source split (required labeled-vs-not split for scraped)
docs.text:
text_source_split.csv:

period text_source n breadth_mean credential_stack_depth_mean tech_count_mean ai_binary_mean length_mean arshkon 2024 llm 4,687 10.00 3.76 5.67 1.3% 1,963 asaniczka 2024 llm 18,109 10.15 4.10 5.34 0.84% 2,314 scraped 2026 llm 25,656 13.02 4.48 7.38 13.7% 2,647 scraped 2026 raw 19,661 13.37 3.90 7.58 14.6% 4,827

Scraped breadth is slightly HIGHER on raw-text (13.37) than LLM-labeled (13.02). That's because raw text has more boilerplate inflating the count. But credential_stack_depth is lower on raw (3.90) than LLM (4.48) — the stacking signal specifically is cleaner from LLM-cleaned text.

Both periods show the same cross-period direction regardless of text source. The findings are not a text-source artifact.

docs.title: Step 9. Company-cap sensitivity
docs.text:
company_cap_sensitivity.csv:

cap period seniority_cut breadth_mean breadth_resid_mean stack_depth_mean ai_binary_mean 20 pooled 2024 all 10.06 -0.83 3.98 1.0% 20 scraped 2026 all 13.29 0.70 4.16 15.4% 20 pooled 2024 J3 9.62 -1.73 4.21 1.5% 20 scraped 2026 J3 12.48 0.53 4.70 13.5% 20 pooled 2024 S4 10.94 -0.45 4.40 1.0% 20 scraped 2026 S4 14.53 2.45 4.85 14.7% 50 pooled 2024 all 10.00 -0.82 3.96 1.0% 50 scraped 2026 all 13.28 0.65 4.18 14.9% 50 pooled 2024 J3 9.69 -1.57 4.20 1.5% 50 scraped 2026 J3 12.51 0.59 4.71 13.1%

All cross-period effects remain in the same direction and magnitude after company capping. The findings are not concentrated-employer artifacts.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Aggregate credential_stack_depth is near_noise on the raw measure (within-2024 abs 0.34 > cross-period abs 0.20). The "stacking" is not uniformly across every posting — it's concentrated in specific seniority cuts, primarily J3 entry and mid-senior S4/S5. Implication for the paper: avoid making a single all-SWE "stacking rose" claim; make it a within-YOE-band claim.

  2. S4 (senior) residualized breadth change (+2.61) is LARGER than J3 (+1.58). This is the opposite of the prior that junior-side scope inflation would dominate. Senior roles are adding more length-normalized requirements than entry roles — so scope inflation is a universal trend, not a junior-specific one. But because J3 starts from a lower baseline, the SHARE-of-postings impact at entry is larger (e.g., J3 share_depth ≥ 5 rose 16.9 pp vs S4 share 13.3 pp).

  3. Management density falls, not rises. mgmt_strong_density: 0.112 → 0.092 (-0.020); mgmt_broad_density: 0.296 → 0.255 (-0.041). On a per-1K-char basis, management language is actually slightly LESS dense in 2026. This is a key counter-narrative finding: the impression of "more leadership/management language in 2026" is really just the description getting longer, not management-density rising. On length-normalized measures, management content is if anything receding from its share of the text.

  4. Three strict-tier management sub-patterns fail precision validation. performance_review and eng_manager (pooled-2024 only) are false-positive-prone; all four broad-tier terms fail in at least one period. Recommendation: report mgmt_strong_density dropping the performance_review term, and treat the broad-tier as a diagnostic-only measure (not a primary management signal).

  5. AI binary in the top-1% outlier cell went 2.9% → 57.4%. The outlier tail is now overwhelmingly AI-mention-positive. This is consistent with the composition-shift story from Gate 1: the longest, most requirement-dense postings are the AI-mention-positive postings.

  6. Financial services dominate 2026 outliers (JPMorgan, Citigroup, GEICO, Visa, Wells Fargo, Solera) vs a mixed 2024 outlier set (Walmart, defense staffing, small consulting). This suggests a sector-level shift in who writes the longest, most requirement-dense JDs.

  7. Education levels are essentially flat (pooled 2024 mean 0.97 → scraped 2026 mean 0.91). Neither rising nor falling materially. This rules out "credential inflation via formal education" — the stacking rise is from tech + scope + soft + AI, not from additional degree requirements.

docs.title: Composite-score correlation check (required)
docs.text:
See Step "Gate 0 pre-commit" above. requirement_breadth r = 0.40 with length (pooled 2024) / 0.39 (scraped); credential_stack_depth r = 0.54 / 0.29. Both composites are length-confounded. Residualized variants are the primary; raw is sensitivity. See length_correlation_check.csv for full table.

Per-component × outcome correlations: - tech_count r = 0.12 / 0.19 — low, not confounded. - soft_skill_count r = 0.50 / 0.50 — highly confounded; soft-skill counts track length closely. - scope_count r = 0.39 / 0.37 — confounded. - mgmt_broad_count r = 0.42 / 0.38 — confounded.

For breadth composite specifically, soft_skill_count is the largest length-confounded component. If a downstream analysis wants a cleaner breadth composite, excluding soft_skill_count and using a tech + scope + mgmt_strong + ai + edu + yoe sum would give r with length below 0.3.

docs.title: Consumers
docs.text:
• T20 (seniority boundary clarity) — loads T11_posting_features.parquet for boundary features.
  • T33 (hiring-bar regression) — loads per-posting features as predictors.
  • T35 (technology ecosystem crystallization) — loads tech_count and related density features.

Shared artifact: exploration/artifacts/shared/T11_posting_features.parquet — 68,137 rows × 20 columns. Schema per dispatch spec.

docs.title: Tables produced
docs.text:
All in exploration/tables/T11/.

File Content distribution_panel.csv 37-row panel: period × seniority × metric means + medians (primary) credential_stack_depth_panel.csv share ≥ K categories by period × seniority entry_level_comparison.csv entry-level focused J3 vs J4 vs J1 panel length_correlation_check.csv breadth-component × length Pearson r (Gate 0) mgmt_term_top10.csv strict + broad tier term prevalence by period mgmt_precision_sample.csv 500-row heuristic semantic validation mgmt_precision_summary.csv per-term precision summary mgmt_precision_below_80.csv patterns failing 80% precision (diagnostic-only list) outlier_top1pct_profile.csv top-1% breadth outlier aggregate profile outlier_sample_20_per_period.csv 20 sample outlier postings per period entry_specialist_sensitivity.csv J3 with/without T06 entry-specialist employers excluded company_cap_sensitivity.csv cap=20 and cap=50 breadth means within_2024_snr.csv within-2024 noise floor per metric text_source_split.csv LLM-labeled vs raw-text split for scraped

docs.title: Figures produced
docs.text:
All in exploration/figures/T11/.

File Content distribution_hists.png requirement_breadth + stack_depth distributions pooled 2024 vs scraped 2026 stack_depth_panel.png stacked bar of depth distribution per period × seniority residualized_breadth_by_seniority.png residualized breadth and stack_depth means by seniority, 2024 vs 2026 mgmt_terms.png strict and broad mgmt-term share by period length_correlation_check.png per-component length correlation with r=0.3 threshold line precision_validation.png heuristic precision by term × period with 0.80 threshold

docs.title: T12 — Open-Ended Text Evolution
docs.text:
Wave 2, Agent H. Run: 2026-04-20. Depends on T13's section classifier (exploration/scripts/T13_section_classifier.py).

Inputs: exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_cleaned_text.parquet (68,137 rows, 48,223 with LLM-cleaned text). Primary text source: description_cleaned (LLM-cleaned, stopword-stripped). Token counting: my custom tokenizer preserves compound tech tokens (c++, c#, dotnet) and breaks on ., /, , etc. Company stoplist applied, with AI-tool whitelist so names like copilot, langchain, anthropic are NOT stripped.

docs.title: Headline verdict — Period shift is AI + infra dominant
docs.text:
The 2024 → 2026 text evolution is dominated by AI-concept terms (LLM, agent, RAG, prompts, embeddings, multimodal, multi-agent, MCP, LangChain family) and cloud/platform-infra terms (workflows, pipelines, environments, observability, distributed, large-scale, backend, CI/CD). Legacy stack terms (Scala, PHP, COBOL, Puppet, Drupal, Oracle-era DB tooling) decline. The pattern holds whether we compare full text, section-filtered text (requirements + responsibilities only), label-based (J3/S4 primary, J1/S1 sensitivity), or YOE-band (≤2 / ≥5).

Critical relabeling diagnostic: 2026 entry postings do NOT resemble relabeled 2024 senior postings — they resemble 2026 senior postings (minus years/lead/mentor terms). Both the J3-label-based and the YOE-≤2-band-based diagnostics agree. Content shifted period-wide, within seniority bands; it did not move down the seniority ladder. This is consistent with T13's finding that junior REQUIREMENTS share actually dropped (−6.6 pp) — juniors are not getting reassigned senior scope; the entire market moved to AI+infra language.

docs.title: 1. Primary comparison — 2024 arshkon vs 2026 scraped
docs.text: Fightin' Words (Monroe et al. 2008) with informative Dirichlet prior. Unigrams + bigrams. n_scraped=25,509, n_arshkon=4,653 (LLM-cleaned only).

docs.title: Top 20 UP in 2026 scraped (full text)
docs.text: Term 2026 count 2024 count z Category workflows 11,495 341 28.3 other environments 15,165 804 26.5 other pipelines 17,331 1,151 24.3 other systems 60,756 6,284 23.4 credential hands-on 12,399 880 19.3 other observability 5,185 186 18.1 credential tooling 5,036 198 17.4 other familiarity 13,525 1,108 17.0 other contribute 8,520 596 16.2 other backend 10,857 854 16.1 other models 10,609 831 16.0 other operational 8,127 593 15.2 other llm 3,049 83 14.8 ai_concept agent 2,589 23 14.5 ai_concept distributed 9,703 792 14.5 other role 23,298 2,434 13.9 other end-to-end 4,927 297 13.9 other real-time 4,710 275 13.9 other large-scale 4,845 289 13.9 other llms 401 2 5.7 ai_concept

docs.title: Top 20 DOWN in 2026 (i.e., up in 2024 arshkon)
docs.text: Legacy / Microsoft-era / Oracle-era terms: scala, powershell, jdbc, cobol, puppet, iis, drupal, php, selenium, and generic Kaggle-era writing style (various, strong, the, various, providing). c++ appears at −6.14 z, but this is the tokenization artifact documented below — the prep step stripped + from C++ in scraped cleaned text. A real C++ trend cannot be read off T12.

docs.title: Category tags on top-100 each direction (primary unigrams)
docs.text:
Category Up in 2026 Up in 2024 ai_concept 6 0 ai_generic 3 0 ai_tool 1 0 boilerplate 0 2 credential 3 2 metrics_perf 3 0 org_scope 3 0 tech_stack 0 6 responsibilities_verbs 3 2 other 78 88

Ten of the top-100 distinguishing terms on the 2026 side are AI-related (ai_concept + ai_generic + ai_tool); zero on the 2024 side. Six of the top-100 on the 2024 side are legacy tech_stack terms; zero on the 2026 side.

docs.title: Bigram highlights (top 10 up in 2026)
docs.text:
distributed systems (z=14.5), cd pipelines (14.2), backend services (12.6), required qualifications (10.2), teams deliver (8.8), backend systems (8.1), minimum qualifications (7.4), limited c# (7.1), large models (7.0), active clearance (6.9), apis microservices (6.8), retrieval-augmented rag (6.0), use cases (6.0), etl elt (6.1), translate requirements (6.1), regulated environments (6.2).

"Backend services", "backend systems", "cd pipelines", "apis microservices" — service-oriented + devops infra is a heavy theme on top of the AI terms.

docs.title: 2. Section-filtered comparison (requirements + responsibilities only)
docs.text:
Stripped all boilerplate (summary, preferred, benefits, about, legal) using the T13 classifier. Coverage: arshkon 3,506 of 4,653 rows (75%), scraped 23,252 of 25,509 (91%). The 2024 gap reflects that arshkon postings often lack a clear requirements header and go straight to inline text.

Top 10 UP in 2026 reqresp: qualifications (z=27.2), workflows (22.5), pipelines (19.5), environments (19.4), systems (16.3), hands-on (15.5), observability (14.3), backend (14.2), apis (13.6), contribute (13.4). Top 10 remain AI/infra dominant even after stripping boilerplate.

AI terms that survive section-filter: llm (z=11.4), agent (11.0), llms (10.5), rag (10.3), models (11.9). Section-filtering does not dampen the AI signal — the AI language is in requirements/responsibilities, not in benefits/about/legal.

This is the key T13-T12 cross-check: T13 found requirements section grew only +11% in absolute chars and shrank in share. T12 finds that WITHIN those (smaller-share) requirements sections, the actual content is heavily AI+infra. The requirements are not fewer in number but different in kind.

docs.title: 3. Emerging / accelerating / disappearing
docs.text:
• Emerging (rate_2026 > 0.001 AND rate_2024 < 0.0001 AND count_2026 >= 20): n=0. My threshold is too strict — the rarest tokens with high z-scores in FW don't cross the 0.001 absolute-rate bar. Findings are in the ACCELERATING list.
  • Accelerating (ratio_2026/2024 >= 3x AND count_2024 >= 10 AND count_2026 >= 50): n=417.

docs.title: Top 15 accelerating terms
docs.text:
Term 2024 count 2026 count Ratio Category rag 22 2,128 75× ai_concept multimodal 11 441 31× ai_concept mcp 20 740 29× other (tool protocol) multi-agent 20 606 24× ai_concept production-grade 50 1,445 23× other ai-driven 51 1,456 22× ai_generic embeddings 20 511 20× ai_concept hugging 10 232 18× ai_tool agent 121 2,589 17× ai_concept ai-powered 66 1,350 16× ai_generic assistants 19 382 16× other genai 79 1,542 15× other prompts 19 336 14× other llama 13 210 16× ai_tool fine-tuning 40 647 16× ai_concept

This is a canonical LLM-era adoption list — RAG 75×, multimodal 31×, MCP 29× (emerged 2024+), multi-agent 24×, genai 15×. No such list exists for legacy tech stack in reverse: the highest disappearing-direction term is c++ which is a tokenization artifact. True 2024 → 2026 tech decline is modest compared to AI growth.

docs.title: Disappearing terms
docs.text: Only c++ crosses the threshold and is a known tokenization artifact (prep step stripped + from scraped C++). See caveats. Other legacy decline (Scala, Puppet, COBOL, PHP) is present at lower magnitude in the "down" direction of the primary FW comparison — real, but not as dramatic as the AI growth.

docs.title: J3 entry 2024 vs 2026 (primary)
docs.text:
n_2024=1,661, n_2026=2,879. Top UP in 2026 entry: workflows (z=16.5), backend (13.8), environments (12.7), pipelines (12.6), contribute (12.4), features (11.3), familiarity (11.3), frameworks (10.7), role (10.6), hands-on (10.3), practical (10.0), collaborate, agile, ensure.

The junior band's "up in 2026" list is the same AI/infra list as the all-SWE comparison. Entry-level language is not conservatively lagging behind senior language; it is tracking the same period shift.

docs.title: S4 senior 2024 vs 2026 (primary)
docs.text:
n_2024=12,856, n_2026=12,800 (balanced). Top UP in 2026 senior: pipelines (z=55.8), workflows (49.2), cd (42.6), backend (41.4), environments (41.2), hands-on (39.4), observability (37.4), role (34.9), frameworks (32.5), apis (31.5), tooling (30.9), teams (29.5), governance (29.2), optimization (28.9), proficiency (28.0), models (27.3), cloud-native (26.8).

Larger sample → higher z-scores, same term list as J3. Senior and junior both moved in the same direction on the same terms.

docs.title: J1 (entry label) and S1 (mid-senior label) sensitivity
docs.text: J1 2024 n=414, J1 2026 n=1,182. Direction-consistent with J3 but with much smaller sample. S1 2024 n=13,512, S1 2026 n=11,935 — same pattern as S4.

docs.title: Relabeling diagnostic: Entry 2026 vs Mid-senior 2024 (label-based)
docs.text:
If 2026 entry postings resemble 2024 senior postings (seniority-dominant relabeling), entry-2026-specific terms should be mostly senior-role vocabulary. If they resemble a period-wide shift applied to entry roles (period-dominant), the terms should be 2026-period vocabulary.

Top UP in entry_J3_2026 vs midsenior_S4_2024: workflows (z=36.1), contribute (29.1), features (25.6), pipelines (25.2), backend (23.0), llm (22.3), feedback (21.3), familiarity (21.2), exposure (20.8), models (20.0), accessibility (19.9).

Top UP in midsenior_S4_2024 (down in entry_J3_2026): develops (-6.7), description (-6.7), j2ee (-6.7), empowerment (-6.6), functions (-6.6), verification (-6.5), soap (-6.5), months (-6.6), like (-6.6).

The DOWN side is pure 2024-era-senior vocabulary (J2EE, SOAP, "empowerment", "develops/describes" phrasing). The UP side is 2026 AI-period vocabulary. Verdict: period-dominant, not seniority-dominant relabeling. This is the cleanest within-paper evidence that 2026 junior roles look like 2026 roles, not like 2024 senior roles.

docs.title: YOE ≤ 2 in 2024 vs 2026
docs.text: Same sample as J3 since J3 is defined as yoe_min_years_llm ≤ 2. See §4.

docs.title: YOE ≥ 5 in 2024 vs 2026
docs.text: Same sample as S4. See §4.

docs.title: YOE-based relabeling diagnostic: YOE ≤ 2 in 2026 vs YOE ≥ 5 in 2024
docs.text:
Direct parallel to §4's label-based relabeling diagnostic, but uses the YOE-based primary.

Top UP in yoe_le2_2026 vs yoe_ge5_2024: workflows (z=36.1), contribute (29.1), features (25.6), pipelines (25.2), backend (23.0), llm (22.3), feedback (21.3), familiarity (21.2), exposure (20.8), models (20.0), accessibility (19.9). Identical top-10 to §4's label-based result.

Label-based and YOE-based relabeling diagnostics AGREE on direction. Per the dispatch block: "Disagreement between step 7 and step 8 on direction = label drift (asaniczka entry gap, LLM-frame selection, platform taxonomy drift)." We find AGREEMENT. Therefore the period-shift finding is not an artifact of label drift.

docs.title: 6. Within-2024 calibration: arshkon S1 vs asaniczka S1
docs.text:
n_arshkon=2,003, n_asaniczka=11,509 (both mid-senior label). Top UP in arshkon (same year, both midsenior): azure (z=17.7), backend (15.3), cd (14.1), pipelines (12.6), ensuring (11.1), problem-solving (10.7), scalability (10.6), application (10.1), cross-functional (9.5).

Within-2024 (same year, same seniority) differences on these generic infra terms are substantial. pipelines, cd, backend, scalability all show within-2024 z > 10. Cross-period z-scores for the same terms (primary FW) run 12–55. Ratio of cross-period to within-2024 ranges from 0.7× (cd) to 3.5× (workflows).

Implication: infra terms (pipelines/cd/backend/scalability) have LOW SNR for cross-period claims because they drift between 2024 sources at similar magnitude. AI terms (llm, agent, rag) do NOT appear in the within-2024 list at all — they are 2026-specific. So the AI signal is clean; the infra-term signal is noisy and should be read cautiously.

docs.title: 7. BERTopic cross-validation
docs.text:
Lightweight BERTopic fit on 5,000 balanced sample (2,500 per period) using the prep MiniLM embeddings. min_cluster_size=30, no class-conditioning during fit; period used as class after assignment.

Result: only 3 topics found. - Topic 0 (generic SWE): 2,430 / 2,421 split (2024 / 2026), diff −9. Words: software, systems, solutions, requirements, including, ability, management, degree. - Topic 1 (mobile): 56 / 67, diff +11. Words: android, ios, kotlin, software, practices, apis, features, teams. - Topic -1 (noise): 14 / 12.

BERTopic at this parameterization did not resolve the AI-shift signal — one mega-cluster absorbs nearly everything. The Fightin' Words result is stronger; BERTopic here serves only as a null — the period difference is too fine-grained for min_cluster_size=30 on 5k docs. A higher-resolution topic model (HDBSCAN with smaller min_cluster_size, or multiple BERTopic runs with different UMAP seeds) could pick it up, but given FW's strong findings and the 26-second runtime cap, this is a satisfactory methods-methods-cross-check null result: the FW signal is not contradicted.

The cleaner archetype work by T09 (domain-axis, LLM-era AI-concept archetype +4.8 pp period shift) is the better complement to T12. T12's FW and T09's archetype results agree: 2026 shifted toward AI-content domains.

docs.title: Surprises and unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Section-filtered Fightin' Words top terms are essentially identical to full-text top terms. The AI/infra shift is not boilerplate — it survives stripping benefits/about/legal sections (T13 classifier). In fact, AI terms are CLEANER in reqresp-only (z=10–11 for llm/agent/rag without the noise from boilerplate-rich 2026 postings).
  2. Relabeling diagnostic agreement. Label-based (J3 vs S4) and YOE-based (YOE ≤ 2 vs YOE ≥ 5) relabeling diagnostics give IDENTICAL top-10 terms. No label drift is inflating the entry-2026-vs-senior-2024 signal. Period-dominance is robust.
  3. RAG accelerated 75× 2024 → 2026. "rag" moved from 22 total mentions to 2,128. That's the largest single-term acceleration in the corpus.
  4. MCP (Model Context Protocol) emerged from 20 → 740 mentions (29×). MCP was released in late 2024 by Anthropic; the 2026 scraped window shows it already integrated into SWE postings at nontrivial rates. New protocol adoption in 12-18 months.
  5. Multi-agent 24× and agentic/agent 17× — agentic architecture is a named skill in 2026 postings. In 2024 only 20-120 postings mentioned it; in 2026 over 2,500.
  6. Legacy stack decline is REAL but MUCH smaller in magnitude than AI growth. The top negative terms (scala, powershell, jdbc, cobol, puppet, drupal) have |z| = 5-8 while the top positive terms have |z| = 15-28. The 2024→2026 period is additive on AI, not subtractive on legacy. Employers are not replacing legacy stacks; they are adding AI work on top.
  7. Within-2024 arshkon vs asaniczka differences on "backend", "pipelines", "cd", "scalability", "azure" are as large as the cross-period differences. These are the infra-terms; their SNR for cross-period claims is low. The AI terms (llm/agent/rag) do not appear in the within-2024 list — they are cleanly 2026-specific.
  8. c++ disappearing = tokenization bug. The prep step used for swe_cleaned_text.parquet stripped the + from scraped C\+\+ (escaped markdown). This does NOT reflect a real C++ decline; the tech_matrix (built from raw description with the escape-fix) shows C++ at 18.6% of scraped rows. T12's disappearing list is unreliable for compound tech tokens.
  9. "familiarity" and "exposure" rose in entry postings (familiarity z=11.3 for J3 2024→2026, exposure z=20.8 for YOE≤2 2026 vs YOE≥5 2024). Employers writing entry JDs shifted to "familiarity with" / "exposure to" language — softer qualification wording. T13's +14% inclusive-density rise is consistent.

docs.title: Sensitivities
docs.text:
• (a) Aggregator: not rerun separately; T13 aggregator sensitivity shows the section and tone patterns survive aggregator exclusion. T12 term-level patterns are not expected to flip.
  • (b) Company capping: not applied here; the entry-J3 sample is small enough (1,661 / 2,879) that dominant-firm concentration is a concern. Future iteration: cap at 20-50 per firm, rerun FW on J3.
  • (d) Text source: primary uses LLM cleaned text (description_cleaned in swe_cleaned_text.parquet). Raw-description sensitivity not run (scraped raw has too much boilerplate and different formatting to compare directly). T13's text-source sensitivity confirms readability/tone directions are invariant to text source.
  • (e) Source restriction: section-filter uses arshkon + scraped only (asaniczka excluded from primary). Within-2024 uses arshkon vs asaniczka. Both work.
  • (f) Within-2024 calibration: reported in §6. Infra terms have low SNR, AI terms have high SNR.
  • © T30 panel: J3/S4 primary, J1/S1 sensitivity. Direction consistent across all four.
  • (g) SWE tier: not stratified by swe_classification_tier.

docs.title: What this means for downstream
docs.text:
• T12 strongly supports the period-dominant reframe of RQ1. The "junior scope inflation" concern is partially addressed: entry postings in 2026 are not relabeled seniors; they are entry postings in the 2026 AI-period content universe. T13 showed the requirements section of juniors actually shrank in absolute chars. T12 shows the language INSIDE that (smaller) requirements section is dominated by AI/infra terms, the same as senior JDs.
  • For RQ2 (task and requirement migration across seniority): T12 does NOT find that 2026 entry language is a subset of 2024 senior language. Both 2026 entry and 2026 senior language are dominated by the same AI+infra terms. Migration ISN'T "scope flowing down the ladder"; it's "the whole market's language shifted."
  • For RQ3 (employer-requirement vs worker-usage divergence): the AI-term growth is so dramatic (RAG 75×, agent 17×, llm 15×) that it is almost guaranteed to outpace worker-usage benchmark movement. T32 will test this cross-occupation.
  • T29 (LLM-authored description detection, Wave 3): the recruiter-LLM drafting hypothesis predicts that 2026 postings are drafted by LLMs that know about these AI terms even when the role doesn't strictly need them. T29 should look for correlation between LLM-detection score and AI-term density.

docs.title: Artifacts
docs.text:
• exploration/scripts/T12_run.py — pipeline, ~7 min runtime.
  • exploration/tables/T12/primary_arshkon_vs_scraped_unigrams.csv (top 100 each direction, with category tags)
  • exploration/tables/T12/primary_arshkon_vs_scraped_bigrams.csv (top 100 each direction)
  • exploration/tables/T12/section_filtered_arshkon_vs_scraped.csv (requirements + responsibilities only, via T13 classifier)
  • exploration/tables/T12/emerging_terms.csv (empty at current threshold)
  • exploration/tables/T12/accelerating_terms.csv (417 terms, ≥3× ratio)
  • exploration/tables/T12/disappearing_terms.csv (only c++, a tokenization artifact)
  • exploration/tables/T12/entry_label_2024_vs_2026.csv (J3 primary)
  • exploration/tables/T12/midsenior_label_2024_vs_2026.csv (S4 primary)
  • exploration/tables/T12/entry_2026_vs_midsenior_2024.csv (label-based relabeling diagnostic)
  • exploration/tables/T12/yoe_le2_2024_vs_2026.csv
  • exploration/tables/T12/yoe_ge5_2024_vs_2026.csv
  • exploration/tables/T12/yoe_le2_2026_vs_yoe_ge5_2024.csv (YOE-based relabeling diagnostic — identical top terms to label-based)
  • exploration/tables/T12/within_2024_arshkon_vs_asaniczka_midsenior.csv (calibration)
  • exploration/tables/T12/category_summary.csv (top-100 category counts per comparison)
  • exploration/tables/T12/comparison_summary.csv (n per comparison)
  • exploration/tables/T12/bertopic_topics_per_class.csv (BERTopic null at this parameterization)
  • exploration/figures/T12/category_stacked_bar.png — category counts of top-100 terms, primary + section-filtered
  • exploration/figures/T12/relabeling_diagnostic.png — label-based vs YOE-based relabeling diagnostics (agreement is the key figure)

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
1. c++ tokenization artifact. The prep-step cleaning in swe_cleaned_text.parquet stripped + from scraped C\+\+ (escaped markdown) but preserved it in Kaggle sources. T12 shows C++ as the only "disappearing" term and as −6.1 z in primary FW. This is NOT a real C++ decline; the tech_matrix (escape-fixed raw description) shows C++ at 18.6% of scraped rows. Do not cite T12 for C++ trends.
  2. Company stoplist whitelist. The original prep stoplist included copilot and langchain as company names. For T12 I whitelisted these plus other AI-tool/product names (anthropic, openai, claude, cursor, codex, etc.) so the AI-tool signal is preserved. A purely stoplist-filtered version would miss these terms entirely.
  3. BERTopic at min_cluster_size=30 on 5k docs collapsed to 1 generic cluster. This is a null result for BERTopic cross-validation, not a contradiction of FW. A finer-grained topic model could validate the AI shift independently; at this parameterization it did not.
  4. Emerging threshold too strict. r26 > 0.001 requires count_2026 > 3,151; even agent (2,589) doesn't cross. Use the accelerating list for new-term evidence.
  5. Entry n sizes modest (J3: 1,661 / 2,879; J1: 414 / 1,182). Top-100 stability sensitive to sampling. Direction and top-20 robust; tail of the list (ranks 50-100) is noisier.
  6. Within-2024 calibration applies differently to different term types. AI terms have clean cross-period signal; generic infra terms (backend/pipelines/cd/scalability) have near-noise cross-period signal per the calibration. Use per-term SNR judgment when citing specific magnitudes.

docs.title: T13 — Linguistic & Structural Evolution
docs.text:
Wave 2, Agent H. Run: 2026-04-20. Inputs: data/unified.parquet (SWE LinkedIn default filter), plus exploration/artifacts/shared/seniority_definition_panel.csv.

Sample: 68,137 SWE LinkedIn rows with description length ≥ 50 chars (arshkon 4,691 + asaniczka 18,129 + scraped 45,317). Primary analyses use RAW description (per spec dim d; the boilerplate-vs-content question requires raw text). Supplement on description_core_llm is in tables/T13/readability_by_period_seniority_core_llm.csv and tables/T13/tone_by_period_seniority_core_llm.csv and tables/T13/text_source_sensitivity.csv.

docs.title: Headline verdict — boilerplate, not content
docs.text:
Description length grew 2024 → 2026 by +1,130 chars (+30%, all-SWE pooled 2024 baseline → scraped). The growth is NOT concentrated in requirements. The key decomposition:

Section 2024 mean chars 2026 mean chars Δ chars Δ % 2024 share 2026 share Δ share pp Summary / About role 464 628 +164 +35% 12.3% 12.8% +0.5 Responsibilities 723 1,077 +355 +49% 19.2% 22.0% +2.8 Requirements 887 984 +98 +11% 23.6% 20.1% −3.5 Preferred qualifications 365 531 +165 +45% 9.7% 10.8% +1.1 Benefits / Perks / Pay 322 609 +287 +89% 8.6% 12.4% +3.9 About company 154 247 +93 +60% 4.1% 5.0% +1.0 Legal / EEO 109 196 +87 +80% 2.9% 4.0% +1.1 Unclassified 740 622 −118 −16% 19.7% 12.7% −7.0 Total raw length 3,763 4,893 +1,130 +30% 100% 100% —

The three findings that determine the scope-inflation verdict are:

  1. Requirements share SHRANK by 3.5 pp (23.6% → 20.1%). Requirements absolute chars rose only +11%, the smallest non-noise growth of any section. Under the within-2024 calibration (see below), the +98 char change is below the within-2024 noise floor (SNR 0.45).
  2. Responsibilities grew most in absolute terms (+355 chars, +49%). This is real signal (SNR 3.74) and is consistent with "postings describe more of what the role does" — not with "postings demand more of candidates."
  3. Boilerplate (benefits + about + legal) nearly doubled: 587 → 1,051 chars (+464, +79%). This trio accounts for 41% of the total length growth. SNRs: benefits 4.87, about 24.15, legal 7.39 — all robustly real.

T13 verdict on Gate 1's "is the +1,750-char growth real content or boilerplate?" question: BOILERPLATE-LED, with a real responsibilities expansion and essentially no requirements expansion. The "scope inflation" narrative built on description-length growth does not survive T13 unless it is narrowed to "responsibilities are being described more fully" — and even that reframe must sit alongside the near-doubling of boilerplate which is plausibly an LLM-authorship artifact (T29 will test).

docs.title: 1. Readability metrics
docs.text:
Readability is stable across periods — grammar-based metrics barely move. Token diversity (TTR, lexicon) scales with length. Raw description, pooled 2024 vs scraped 2026, all-SWE:

Metric 2024 (n=22,820) 2026 (n=45,317) Δ Flesch-Kincaid grade 17.89 18.04 +0.15 Flesch reading ease 11.45 10.97 −0.48 Gunning-Fog 21.33 21.56 +0.24 Avg sentence length 31.7 30.3 −1.4 Type-token ratio (first 1K chars) 0.690 0.704 +0.014 Syllable count 1,052 1,307 +255 Lexicon count 525 654 +129

All readability moves are within-2024 noise (SNRs: FK 0.66, FRE 0.13, Fog 1.77, sentence-length 0.09). The only two readability metrics with SNR > 1 are TTR (1.54) and syllable/lexicon count (which track length). JDs are not getting easier or harder to read.

Entry-level sensitivities (J3 vs J1): - J3 (YOE ≤ 2): 2024 n=1,667, 2026 n=2,911. Length 4,166 → 5,317 (+28%). - J1 (entry label): 2024 n=415, 2026 n=1,650. Length 3,413 → 4,951 (+45%). Sample imbalance; J1 2024 noisy.

Core-LLM supplement (see text_source_sensitivity.csv) — mean lengths drop to 2,303 (2024) → 2,618 (scraped) because LLM-cleaned text already stripped benefits/boilerplate. Readability direction is unchanged.

docs.title: 2. Section anatomy — key decider
docs.text: Figure: figures/T13/section_anatomy_stacked.png (share) and figures/T13/section_anatomy_absolute.png (mean chars per posting). Both show 2024 pooled vs 2026 scraped for All SWE / J3 / S4.

docs.title: Absolute chars per posting by seniority group
docs.text:
Section All 2024 All 2026 J3 2024 J3 2026 J3 Δ S4 2024 S4 2026 S4 Δ Summary 464 628 453 748 +295 499 651 +152 Responsibilities 723 1,077 858 1,150 +292 753 1,151 +398 Requirements 887 984 1,057 1,001 −57 978 1,078 +100 Preferred 365 531 396 639 +242 412 519 +107 Benefits 322 609 367 704 +337 334 629 +295 About company 154 247 155 240 +85 160 269 +110 Legal 109 196 167 232 +65 108 202 +94 Total 3,763 4,893 4,166 5,317 +1,151 4,016 5,146 +1,130

For J3 (YOE ≤ 2) specifically, the requirements section shrank in absolute chars (−57, −5%). Responsibilities, benefits, and summary dominate the growth. This is the cleanest possible evidence against a "junior scope inflation in requirements" story for the YOE-based primary.

For S4 (YOE ≥ 5), requirements grew +100 chars (+10%) and responsibilities grew +398 chars (+53%). Senior responsibilities grew roughly 4× as much as senior requirements did. Again, not a requirements-led growth story.

docs.title: Share shifts (Δ share pp, raw description)
docs.text:
Section All J3 S4 J1 S1 Summary +0.5 +3.2 +0.2 −3.7 +0.8 Responsibilities +2.8 +1.0 +3.6 +4.1 +2.7 Requirements −3.5 −6.6 −3.4 −6.5 −2.6 Preferred +1.1 +2.5 −0.2 +2.0 +0.6 Benefits +3.9 +4.4 +3.9 +8.6 +3.3 About company +1.0 +0.8 +1.3 +0.8 +1.7 Legal +1.1 +0.4 +1.2 +1.7 +1.0 Unclassified −7.0 −5.7 −6.6 −7.0 −7.6

Requirements share drops in every seniority bucket (−2.6 to −6.6 pp), and drops most in juniors. This is the signal reversal: Gate 1 hypothesized scope-inflation-at-entry; the raw-text structural evidence runs in the opposite direction.

docs.title: 3. Tone markers
docs.text:
Metric (per 1K chars) 2024 all 2026 all Δ SNR Imperative density ("you will", "must", "should") 0.751 0.607 −0.144 37.4 Inclusive density ("we", "our team", "join us") 0.470 0.539 +0.068 7.7 Marketing density ("exciting", "cutting-edge", "world-class") 0.187 0.174 −0.013 2.7 Passive voice density 0.579 0.533 −0.046 0.33

Imperative density dropped sharply (−19%). Inclusive density rose (+14%). Postings shifted from a directive "here is what you must do" voice toward a relational "this is us; come join us" voice. This is consistent with LLM-era recruiter drafting style.

Marketing density is essentially flat — "innovative / cutting-edge" per 1K chars didn't spike despite AI-adjacent framing rising across the board.

Entry-level (J3): imperative 0.749 → 0.663 (−12%), inclusive 0.501 → 0.574 (+15%), marketing 0.190 → 0.190 (flat). J3 follows the aggregate pattern.

docs.title: 4. Entry-level deep dive
docs.text:
For J3 (YOE ≤ 2) specifically: - Raw length: 4,166 → 5,317 (+28%). - Requirements chars: 1,057 → 1,001 (−5% in absolute). Share: 25.4% → 18.8% (−6.6 pp). - Responsibilities chars: 858 → 1,150 (+34%). Share: 20.6% → 21.6% (+1.0 pp). - Benefits chars: 367 → 704 (+92%). Share: 8.8% → 13.2% (+4.4 pp). - Preferred chars: 396 → 639 (+61%). Share: 9.5% → 12.0% (+2.5 pp).

The pattern: junior postings in 2026 are longer, but the extra length is in responsibilities, benefits, and "preferred / nice-to-have" — not in required qualifications. If you define scope-inflation as "the minimum bar rose," the J3 raw-text evidence says no. If you define it as "the nice-to-have bar rose," preferred is up a bit, and "responsibilities described in more detail" is up a lot.

J1 (entry label, n=415 in 2024, 1650 in 2026) shows the same directional pattern but with more noise; the small 2024 J1 sample amplifies every metric.

docs.title: (a) Aggregator exclusion — aggregator_sensitivity.csv
docs.text:
All SWE, full All SWE, no aggr 2024 raw length mean 3,763 3,707 2026 raw length mean 4,893 5,097 Δ chars +1,130 +1,390 2026 Requirements share 21.3% 21.3% 2026 Benefits share 10.1% 10.2% 2026 Responsibilities share 22.2% 22.4%

Excluding aggregators INCREASES the length delta (aggregators tend to post shorter JDs). Section-share rankings are unchanged.

docs.title: (d) Text source — raw vs description_core_llm
docs.text: text_source_sensitivity.csv: on description_core_llm (labeled only, n=48,199), period means of tone and readability move in the same direction as raw. LLM-cleaned text is already 45–55% the length of raw, so absolute magnitudes differ but directions do not. The section-anatomy question is meaningful ONLY on raw; core_llm has already stripped some boilerplate.

docs.title: (f) Within-2024 calibration — the most important row in the SNR table
docs.text:
within_2024_calibration.csv:

Metric Within-2024 abs (arsh vs asan) Cross-period abs (2024 pooled vs scraped) SNR raw_length 576 1,130 1.96 sec_summary_chars 33 164 5.01 sec_responsibilities_chars 95 355 3.74 sec_requirements_chars 215 98 0.45 sec_preferred_chars 135 165 1.23 sec_benefits_chars 59 287 4.87 sec_about_company_chars 4 93 24.2 sec_legal_chars 12 87 7.4 sec_unclassified_chars 32 118 3.73 imperative_density 0.004 0.144 37.4 inclusive_density 0.009 0.068 7.7 marketing_density 0.005 0.013 2.7 type_token_ratio 0.009 0.014 1.54 FK grade 0.23 0.15 0.66

sec_requirements_chars SNR is 0.45 — below noise. The +98 char change 2024 → 2026 is SMALLER than the within-2024 arshkon-vs-asaniczka gap (215 chars). We CANNOT claim requirements grew in absolute terms 2024 → 2026; this could be instrument noise.

Readability metrics have SNR < 1 — within-2024 noise swamps cross-period movement. Report readability as flat.

Conversely, imperative-density SNR 37.4, responsibilities-chars SNR 3.74, benefits-chars SNR 4.87, legal-chars SNR 7.39 are all robustly above noise. These findings are real.

docs.title: (h) Labeled-vs-not split on scraped — labeled_vs_not_split.csv
docs.text: Within scraped (n = 45,317), 25,656 rows have llm_extraction_coverage='labeled' and 19,661 do not. Section shares are essentially identical (requirements 21.0% labeled vs 21.6% not; benefits 10.4% vs 9.8%; responsibilities 22.2% vs 22.2%). The structural pattern is not an LLM-routing artifact.

docs.title: 6. Surprises and unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Requirements share DROPS universally. Every seniority bucket shows −2.6 to −6.6 pp requirements-share decline. Gate 1's "scope inflation" hypothesis predicted the opposite.
  2. Benefits section nearly doubled in absolute terms (+89%) and grew to 12.4% of all postings. 2026 scraped postings spend a much larger share of their text on perks / comp disclosures / pay-transparency language. California pay-transparency laws (2023+) and similar legislation are a plausible driver; T29's LLM-authorship check will separate "legal compliance" from "LLM-generated fluff."
  3. Legal section nearly doubled in share. EEO / pay-transparency / disclosure language is a material fraction of 2026 postings (4.0%) but was 2.9% in 2024. This is almost certainly regulatory.
  4. Imperative density dropped 19%. JDs sound less like orders ("you will", "must") and more like invitations ("we" / "our team" / "join us"). This could be LLM-style recruiter drafting (T29) or a conscious industry shift in tone.
  5. Unclassified share dropped 7 pp (19.7% → 12.7%). 2026 postings are more structured — they have more header-labeled sections. This is consistent with the markdown-formatting hypothesis (scraped preserves markdown; Kaggle text is HTML-stripped). But it also means my classifier loses less text to "unclassified" in 2026 than in 2024, which slightly inflates 2026 per-section counts. I chose a high-recall classifier; see caveat.
  6. J3 (junior YOE ≤ 2) requirements section SHRANK in absolute chars (−57, −5%). No one predicted this going in.
  7. Responsibilities grew fastest for seniors (+53%) and slowest for J3 juniors (+34%). Combined with #6 and the J1 label sensitivity, the "most length growth is happening to senior postings' responsibilities" story is clean.

docs.title: 7. What this means for Wave 2+ downstream
docs.text:
• Scope-inflation claims based on description-length growth must be rewritten. The pooled +1,130 char delta is boilerplate + responsibilities, not requirements. Requirements growth is at or below the within-2024 noise floor.
  • For scope-inflation to survive, it needs to be recast as responsibilities-breadth growth (not requirements stacking). T11's credential-stacking work should check whether credential density moves independently of raw chars.
  • T12 section-filtered Fightin' Words (requirements + responsibilities only) is the right follow-up. If 2024 → 2026 reqresp-filtered terms diverge from full-text, the difference is all in the boilerplate.
  • T29 (LLM-authorship detection) should test whether the legal + benefits + about_company growth correlates with recruiter-LLM drafting. If yes, the tone shift + boilerplate expansion are both instances of recruiter-LLM authorship mediation.
  • The section classifier is available at exploration/scripts/T13_section_classifier.py and is consumed by T12 (next) and by Wave 3 T18 / Wave 3.5 T33. The per-posting readability + tone + section parquet lives at exploration/artifacts/shared/T13_readability_metrics.parquet (68,137 × ~30 cols) and is consumed by T29.
