docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
1. 2026 scraped text preserves markdown section headers; 2024 Kaggle text does not. This means 2026 postings are easier to classify into sections (unclassified share drops 7 pp). My classifier handles both formats (see T13_section_classifier.py tests), but some of the "content moved into classified sections" in 2026 is format availability, not real content growth. The responsibilities / benefits / legal growth survives this concern because within-2024 calibration shows arshkon-vs-asaniczka differences on those sections are small (<120 chars each) while 2024→2026 differences are 300–400 chars.
  2. The classifier is high-recall, not high-precision. Compound headers like "Role Overview And Core Responsibilities" are attributed to responsibilities (correctly, given the content below). A regex-based section classifier will miss postings with non-standard headers, and will over-attribute to fallback labels. Unclassified share of 12–19% is a reasonable error bar on section-level precision.
  3. Entry-level J1-label baseline is small (2024 n=415). All J1 entry-label findings should be read as directional signals, not point estimates.
  4. Cross-period means pool two 2024 sources (arshkon + asaniczka) against scraped 2026. Within-2024 calibration is the check; it shows arshkon and asaniczka differ on some metrics (sentence length, reading ease) and agree on others. Use the calibration SNR column before believing any specific magnitude.

docs.title: T14 — Technology ecosystem mapping
docs.text:
Agent I (Wave 2) · 2026-04-20 · SWE LinkedIn is_english date_flag=ok Inputs: exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_tech_matrix.parquet (135 techs × 68,137 rows), asaniczka_structured_skills.parquet, tech_matrix_sanity.csv, seniority_definition_panel.csv (T30). Outputs: exploration/tables/T14/ (15 CSVs), exploration/figures/T14_tech_trajectory.png, T14_stack_diversity.png.

Primary filter: SWE × LinkedIn × English × date_flag=ok. Pool arshkon+asaniczka = 2024 baseline; scraped 2026-03 + 2026-04 = 2026. Seniority primaries: J3 (YOE ≤ 2, labeled) / S4 (YOE ≥ 5, labeled). Tech matrix cap-50-per-company applied for co-occurrence density (n = 54,188 rows; original 68,137).

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. AI-family crystallizes into a cohesive 2026 ecosystem. 60 of 135 techs are classified rising under within-2024 calibration (SNR ≥ 2); the AI-family (17 LLM-era techs) shows the steepest rises and the largest phi-coefficient growth 2024 → 2026. llm 1.3 % → 13.7 %, ai_agent 0.07 % → 10.2 %, rag 0.1 % → 5.4 %, copilot 0.09 % → 4.1 %. Every AI tech except langchain↔llamaindex and tensorflow↔pytorch had effectively-zero pairwise phi in 2024.
  2. Ecosystem is adding on top, not substituting. AI-strict 2026 postings mention 11.4 mean techs vs 6.8 for non-AI 2026 (raw count). Length-normalized density 2.79 vs 1.88 techs/1K chars — density gap (48 %) is smaller than raw gap (67 %) but still substantial. AI-rich postings are not just longer; they genuinely pack more tech.
  3. Cloud-native + Python is the structural bed the AI stack is attaching to. Among 2026 AI-strict postings: Python 68 % (vs 46 % non-AI), AWS 44 % (vs 34 %), Kubernetes 27 % (vs 21 %), Docker 25 % (vs 18 %). C++, bash, and Agile are under-represented in AI postings (AI postings skew away from low-level systems and ceremony language).
  4. Stack diversity is rising uniformly. Median techs/posting: all-SWE 4 (2024) → 7 (2026). J3 entry: 4 → 6. S4 senior: 5 → 7. The % of postings mentioning ≥ 10 techs went from 19 % (2024) → 32 % (2026). Scope is not broadening in one seniority; it is broadening across the board.
  5. SQL, C#/.NET, Agile, jQuery, SQL Server, PHP are all declining at SNR ≥ 2. Classic MS/enterprise-web stack is shrinking in the LLM era. This is a crystallizing decline, not calibration noise (their within-2024 arshkon↔asaniczka gap is small).
  6. Structured-vs-extracted validation passes decisively. Spearman r = 0.967 (n = 53 tech-skill-aligned pairs, p < 1e-30) between asaniczka skills_raw and the regex-extracted tech matrix. Structured rates are systematically lower (regex picks up extra mentions in surrounding prose — 8-20 pp higher for generic terms like agile, ci/cd, c, sql); ranking is preserved. Neither signal has a recall-only gap large enough to worry about.
  7. Structured seniority gap (asaniczka 2024): aws/cloud/devops skills skew senior; c++, troubleshooting, computer science, mechanical/electrical/computer engineering skew entry. Mentoring +2.9 pp senior-vs-entry (p_fdr < 1e-4). This is a structured-skill baseline for the Wave 3 seniority-boundary tasks (T20, T33).

docs.title: 1. Taxonomy (pre-built, respect sanity flags)
docs.text:
swe_tech_matrix.parquet — 135 columns, not redefined. Per tech_matrix_sanity.csv: 23 flagged (19 under_detected_2024, 3 new_in_2026, 1 over_detected_2024) and 112 OK. All flagged entries are real adoption shifts, not tokenization bugs (see Prep step 7). The backslash-escape fix on C++ / C# / .NET is verified working.

Taxonomy groups: Languages (20) · Frontend (8) · Backend (10) · Cloud (6) · Orchestration/DevOps (8) · CI/CD (6) · Databases (11) · Data pipelines (9) · Traditional ML (9) · LLM era (18) · AI tools (7) · Testing (7) · Observability (7) · Practices (9).

docs.title: 2. Mention rates by period × seniority (T30 primary J3 / S4)
docs.text:
Selected techs, rate (%) among SWE × period × seniority:

Technology 2024 J3 2026 J3 2024 S4 2026 S4 2024 all 2026 all python 35.5 54.9 33.3 47.8 32.7 49.4 llm 1.6 12.9 1.0 13.0 1.3 13.7 ai_agent 0.1 8.8 0.0 10.0 0.1 10.2 copilot 0.2 4.6 0.1 4.1 0.1 4.1 rag 0.3 4.0 0.0 5.4 0.1 5.4 langchain 0.2 2.2 0.1 3.3 0.1 3.3 kubernetes 10.7 15.3 14.6 25.5 13.2 22.1 docker 9.7 13.6 13.4 20.8 12.4 18.8 aws 17.9 25.0 29.1 39.3 26.7 35.1 ci_cd 19.6 26.2 19.8 40.5 18.9 36.2 typescript 10.1 17.7 16.9 20.2 14.2 18.8 agile 30.2 25.6 37.4 30.3 33.4 27.5 sql 25.7 21.6 30.4 27.2 28.0 25.4 dot_net 9.3 5.9 11.4 7.3 10.3 6.9

Full table: tables/T14/tech_rates_by_period_seniority.csv. Within-2024 arshkon-vs-asaniczka split: tables/T14/tech_rates_2024_by_source.csv.

Seniority × period pattern: AI-family rise is roughly-symmetric across J3 and S4. llm gains +11.3 pp for J3 and +12.0 pp for S4; ai_agent +8.7 pp J3 vs +10.0 pp S4. copilot is actually slightly more present in J3 than S4 (4.6 % vs 4.1 %). No evidence that AI requirements are migrating downward onto entry — they arrived at both levels simultaneously.

Cloud/infrastructure skew is senior-weighted: kubernetes, docker, aws, ci_cd all ~2× as prevalent in S4 as J3 in 2026. ci_cd jumped the fastest at senior (+20.6 pp S4 vs +6.6 pp J3), which is a scope-at-senior signal.

docs.title: 3. Co-occurrence structure (pooled phi)
docs.text:
Cap-50-per-company applied (n = 54,188). Top-20 phi pairs:

a b phi n_a n_b n_both tensorflow pytorch 0.804 1,576 1,897 1,401 grafana prometheus 0.766 1,785 1,558 1,289 pandas numpy 0.732 724 534 457 pinecone weaviate 0.691 336 188 174 kubernetes docker 0.624 10,226 8,937 6,599 puppet chef 0.614 683 675 420 c_sharp dot_net 0.583 6,030 4,633 3,295 ruby rails 0.519 1,289 586 457 rag llm 0.515 2,091 5,056 1,760 javascript react 0.498 12,365 9,394 6,428 c_plus_plus c_lang 0.494 8,276 5,956 3,923 llm ai_agent 0.494 5,056 3,656 2,294 langchain llamaindex 0.493 1,239 320 313 swift objective_c 0.485 1,190 406 341 langchain rag 0.481 1,239 2,091 798 cursor_tool claude_tool 0.479 858 701 377 tensorflow scikit_learn 0.473 1,576 637 483 azure gcp 0.456 12,091 7,203 5,100 java spring 0.455 14,759 4,746 4,398 agile scrum 0.448 15,041 4,722 4,379

tables/T14/top20_phi_pairs.csv. Full 135×135 matrix: tables/T14/phi_matrix_pooled.csv. Long form for T35: tables/T14/phi_matrix_pooled_long.csv and tables/T14/phi_per_period_long.csv (split 2024 / 2026, both capped).

Observation (descriptive only; T35 owns the full network / community analysis): two ecosystems already visible as high-phi clusters pooled —

  • Legacy-ML pair: tensorflow↔pytorch (phi 0.80). Together with pandas↔numpy (0.73) this is a crystallized scientific-python cluster that predates the LLM era.
  • LLM-era cluster: rag↔llm (0.52), llm↔ai_agent (0.49), langchain↔llamaindex (0.49), langchain↔rag (0.48), pinecone↔weaviate (0.69). Dense inter-connection, mostly a 2026 phenomenon (see per-period drift below).
  • New AI-tool pair: cursor_tool↔claude_tool (0.48) — zero in 2024 so pooled is all 2026 signal.

docs.title: 3b. Per-period phi drift (T35 prep)
docs.text:
Pair 2024 phi (n=19,687) 2026 phi (n=34,501) Δ Interpretation rag × llm 0.197 0.514 +0.317 Crystallizing llm × ai_agent 0.179 0.484 +0.305 Crystallizing pinecone × weaviate -0.000 0.706 +0.706 New in 2026 cursor_tool × claude_tool — 0.475 — Both new in 2026 grafana × prometheus 0.629 0.787 +0.158 Tightening tensorflow × pytorch 0.766 0.813 +0.047 Stable (already tight) kubernetes × docker 0.614 0.622 +0.008 Stable puppet × chef 0.637 0.591 −0.046 Legacy DevOps loosening c_sharp × dot_net 0.616 0.558 −0.058 Declining stack

This is the crystallization signal Wave 3.5 T35 will extend. The per-period tables are structured such that phi_per_period_long.csv can be loaded by period = {2024, 2026} for a direct within-pair drift test.

docs.title: 4. Trajectory classification (rising / stable / declining)
docs.text:
Classification uses within-2024 arshkon-vs-asaniczka gap as the noise floor; a tech is rising if |delta| ≥ 0.005 and SNR = |cross-period delta| / within-2024 gap ≥ 2. Results on 135 techs:

Class Count Mechanism rising 60 delta ≥ 0.005 & SNR ≥ 2 stable 8 declining 12 delta ≤ −0.005 & SNR ≥ 2 calibration_noise 31 SNR < 2 trace 24 all rates < 0.5 %

Rising winners (top-20 by delta):

ci_cd (+17.4 pp), python (+16.6 pp, SNR 101), llm (+12.4 pp), ai_agent (+10.1 pp, SNR 83), kubernetes (+8.9 pp), aws (+8.4 pp), docker (+6.4 pp), terraform (+6.3 pp), gcp (+6.0 pp), rest_api (+5.4 pp), rag (+5.3 pp), react (+4.7 pp), microservices (+4.6 pp), github_actions (+4.2 pp), copilot (+4.0 pp), postgresql (+3.9 pp), event_driven (+3.9 pp), kafka (+3.6 pp), prompt_engineering (+3.5 pp), go_lang (+3.3 pp).

Declining losers (all SNR ≥ 2):

agile (−5.8 pp, SNR 2.7), dot_net (−3.4, SNR 2.7), sql (−2.6, SNR 13), sql_server (−2.3, SNR 9), jquery (−2.0, SNR 6.3), angular (−1.4, SNR 1.7 flagged as noise), c_sharp (−1.2, SNR 11), php (−1.1, SNR 2.8), scala (−1.0, SNR 7.9), scrum (−1.0, SNR 3.2), puppet (−0.9, SNR 4.8), chef (−0.6, SNR 4.1).

Calibration-noise rows (SNR < 2, direction untrustworthy): typescript (+4.6 pp, within-2024 gap 4.97 pp → SNR 0.93), c_plus_plus (+3.2 pp, gap 5.50 pp → SNR 0.59), azure (+4.2, SNR 1.27), jQuery doesn't fall here despite high arshkon, as its declines are large enough. Also tdd, perl, javascript. These techs have a 2024→2026 shift that could be explained by arshkon-vs-asaniczka sampling difference.

Fig: figures/T14_tech_trajectory.png (top 25 rising + 10 declining + a strip of calibration-noise for context).

docs.title: 5. Stack diversity — scope is broadening across the board
docs.text:
Distinct techs per posting:

Period Seniority n median mean q25–q75 p95 % ≥10 techs 2024 J3 1,667 4 4.87 1–8 13 14.9 % 2026 J3 2,911 6 6.51 3–9 16 23.7 % 2024 S4 12,894 5 5.76 2–9 15 22.0 % 2026 S4 12,959 7 7.96 4–11 18 35.7 % 2024 all_swe 22,820 4 5.41 2–8 14 19.4 % 2026 all_swe 45,317 7 7.46 3–11 17 31.6 %

The J3 median jumps from 4 to 6, and S4 from 5 to 7. At the 95th percentile, postings mentioning 18+ techs went from fringe (2024 S4: 15) to common (2026 S4: 18). The "≥ 10 techs" share rose from 15 % (J3 2024) to 24 % (J3 2026); from 22 % → 36 % for S4. No seniority tier is specializing — all tiers are broadening. Fig: figures/T14_stack_diversity.png.

docs.title: 6. AI integration pattern — additive, not substitutive
docs.text:
Strict AI pattern (copilot | cursor | claude | chatgpt | openai api | gpt-N | gemini | codex | llamaindex | langchain | prompt engineering | fine-tuning | rag | vector database | pinecone | huggingface). 2026 share: 14.4 % of SWE LinkedIn postings mention any AI-strict pattern.

Period Group n Mean techs Mean length chars Mean density per 1K Median density per 1K 2024 ai_strict 243 7.87 4,253 2.43 1.89 2024 non_ai 22,577 5.38 3,758 2.08 1.33 2026 ai_strict 6,506 11.35 5,256 2.79 2.14 2026 non_ai 38,811 6.81 4,832 1.88 1.31

Length-normalization verdict: AI-strict 2026 postings have both higher raw tech count (+67 % vs non-AI) AND higher density (+48 %). The rich-stack finding survives density normalization at about 70 % of its raw magnitude — 48 % of 67 %. Meaningful residual.

What AI-strict postings ADD on top (2026, rate-in-AI minus rate-in-non-AI):

Tech AI-strict rate Non-AI rate Δ pp llm 58.8 6.1 +52.7 ai_agent 42.3 4.8 +37.5 rag 37.3 0.04 +37.2 copilot 28.3 0.0 +28.3 prompt_engineering 25.9 0.01 +25.9 langchain 23.1 0.02 +23.1 python 68.1 46.2 +21.8 fine_tuning 17.4 0.4 +17.0 cursor_tool 14.1 0.01 +14.1 claude_tool 12.3 0.0 +12.3 azure 34.1 22.3 +11.8 mcp 11.7 0.5 +11.2 gcp 26.0 15.0 +11.0 pytorch 14.1 3.2 +10.9 aws 44.4 33.5 +10.9 hugging_face 8.2 0.003 +8.2 tensorflow 10.5 2.6 +7.9 anthropic 8.0 0.3 +7.7

What AI-strict postings DROP (under-represented):

Tech AI-strict rate Non-AI rate Δ pp c_plus_plus 11.0 19.9 −8.9 agile 22.1 28.4 −6.3 bash 2.1 6.5 −4.4 c_lang 8.8 13.1 −4.3 ansible 1.8 5.5 −3.7 scrum 5.6 8.6 −3.0 jenkins 7.5 10.3 −2.8 gitlab_ci 4.2 6.5 −2.3

Interpretation. AI postings are additive on top of a Python / cloud / k8s base, not substitutes for it. The classic languages they skip are low-level systems C++/C/bash (compiled / embedded / ops) and the ceremony stack (agile/scrum — LLM-era job ads are less process-heavy). Legacy CI (jenkins/gitlab_ci/ansible) is mildly under-represented; GitHub Actions / cloud-native CI is presumably filling its place but that is not called out in the strict pattern. tables/T14/ai_co_occurrence.csv has full 135-tech co-occurrence both periods.

docs.title: 7. Structured-skills baseline (asaniczka only)
docs.text:
Top-20 structured skills by n_postings (18,114 asaniczka SWE):

Skill n_postings Share python 5,629 31.1 % java 4,679 25.8 % sql 3,656 20.2 % aws 3,441 19.0 % javascript 3,129 17.3 % software engineering 2,827 15.6 % software development 2,775 15.3 % communication 2,466 13.6 % git 2,454 13.5 % agile 2,400 13.2 % linux 2,285 12.6 % kubernetes 2,229 12.3 % docker 2,130 11.8 % computer science 2,042 11.3 % devops 2,031 11.2 % c++ 2,010 11.1 % communication skills 1,835 10.1 % c# 1,826 10.1 % troubleshooting 1,755 9.7 % systems engineering 1,598 8.8 %

Full 100 rows: tables/T14/asaniczka_top100_structured.csv.

docs.title: 8. Structured vs extracted validation
docs.text:
Aligned on 53 tech-skill pairs with explicit alias mapping (e.g., aws → {"aws", "amazon web services"}).

  • Spearman r = 0.967, p < 1e-30. Rank correlation is extremely tight.
  • Regex-extracted rates are systematically higher than structured rates on generic terms: agile 33.8 % vs 13.2 %, ci_cd 17.8 % vs 6.4 %, sql 28.0 % vs 20.2 %, c 11.3 % vs 3.2 %, aws 26.3 % vs 19.4 %. Regex picks up prose mentions like "agile methodology" or "SQL databases" that the structured skills_raw field does not enumerate.
  • Direction is preserved — regex over-counts uniformly in prose-like categories. It does not introduce ordering errors. The rank signal is preserved for both hypothesis testing and for cross-tech comparisons.

Conclusion. Extracted tech matrix is defensible for mention-rate signals. For counts of distinct core skills, structured skills_raw is a tighter measure on asaniczka; for coverage across all three sources, regex is the only option (asaniczka is the only source with skills_raw). tables/T14/structured_vs_extracted.csv + tables/T14/structured_vs_extracted_meta.json.

docs.title: 9. Seniority × structured-skill differences (asaniczka 2024)
docs.text:
Chi-squared per skill, n_entry = 1,218 (J3) and n_senior = 10,648 (S4), top 150 asaniczka skills, FDR-corrected at α = 0.05.

Senior-leaning skills (largest diff senior − entry, all p_fdr < 0.05):

Skill Share entry Share senior Δ pp p_fdr aws 12.5 20.8 +8.3 1.3e-9 agile 10.6 15.0 +4.4 1.1e-3 java 23.4 27.5 +4.1 1.8e-2 devops 8.7 12.5 +3.8 2.4e-3 azure 6.0 9.4 +3.4 2.4e-3 docker 9.6 12.6 +3.0 1.8e-2 kubernetes 10.6 13.6 +3.0 2.0e-2 typescript 5.0 8.0 +3.0 3.2e-3 mentoring 1.9 4.8 +2.9 1.3e-4 git 11.5 14.4 +2.9 2.9e-2 jira 6.7 9.5 +2.9 9.6e-3 systems engineering 7.8 10.5 +2.7 1.9e-2 jenkins 6.2 8.6 +2.4 2.0e-2 software architecture 2.1 4.3 +2.2 3.2e-3 agile practices 0.4 2.6 +2.2 1.3e-4

Entry-leaning skills:

Skill Share entry Share senior Δ pp p_fdr c++ 14.8 11.0 −3.8 1.8e-3 troubleshooting 12.6 9.3 −3.3 3.2e-3 mechanical engineering 5.6 2.8 −2.8 1.6e-5 computer science 14.3 11.7 −2.6 3.8e-2 computer engineering 5.5 2.9 −2.6 1.0e-4 data analysis 7.2 5.2 −2.1 1.9e-2 machine learning 8.5 6.4 −2.0 3.9e-2 electrical engineering 8.2 6.2 −2.0 3.8e-2 data structures 5.2 3.5 −1.7 1.9e-2

tables/T14/seniority_skill_structured_chi2.csv (150 skills, full).

Interpretation. Senior postings emphasize orchestration (aws/devops/docker/k8s/jenkins), process (agile/jira/mentoring/software architecture), and the Java/TypeScript enterprise language. Entry postings emphasize C++, generic troubleshooting, CS / engineering-degree markers, and early-career ML terminology. The senior cluster is built around running systems in production, not building new functions. This is a structured-data baseline for T20/T33 seniority-boundary questions and T21 senior-role evolution.

docs.title: 10. ML-engineer source stratification (Gate 1 pre-commit)
docs.text:
Arshkon classifies 78 % of "ML Engineer" titles as SWE; asaniczka 59 % (T04, T30). Any 2024 ML/AI comparison must stratify by source:

Selected 2024 ML/AI techs by source:

Tech arshkon rate asaniczka rate diff 2026 scraped rate pytorch 2.0 1.4 +0.6 4.8 llm 1.7 1.1 +0.6 13.7 tensorflow 1.9 1.5 +0.4 3.7 rag 0.4 0.04 +0.3 5.4 fine_tuning 1.1 0.9 +0.2 2.8

tables/T14/ml_tech_source_stratified.csv (35 ML/AI techs).

Within-2024 ML/AI gap is modest. The Gate 1 flag is warranted but the magnitude is ≤ 0.6 pp on all individual ML/AI techs; the 2024 → 2026 cross-period shift is an order of magnitude larger (5-13 pp on llm, rag, ai_agent). ML/AI temporal claims survive source-stratification for the headline AI rise. The stratification matters more for within-2024 descriptive ratios (arshkon "ML Engineer" postings consistently have 2-6 pp more ML/AI tech than asaniczka). When reporting 2024 baselines, lean toward pooled, report arshkon-only when a technology has rate < 1 %.

docs.title: 11. Sensitivities
docs.text:
Baseline / aggregator-excluded / cap-50-per-company on headline techs (full 20-tech × 4-seniority table at tables/T14/tech_rates_sensitivities.csv):

Tech Sensitivity J3 Δ pp 2024→2026 S4 Δ pp 2024→2026 python baseline +19.4 +14.5 python cap50 +17.8 +17.3 python excl_aggregator +19.7 +17.5 llm baseline +11.3 +12.0 llm cap50 +10.9 +12.3 llm excl_aggregator +11.9 +12.3 ai_agent baseline +8.8 +10.0 ai_agent cap50 (same order) — aws baseline +7.1 +10.2 aws cap50 +9.4 +13.1 aws excl_aggregator +7.6 +12.8 ci_cd baseline +6.7 +20.6 ci_cd cap50 +8.8 +20.1 ci_cd excl_aggregator +6.5 +18.3 kubernetes baseline +4.6 +10.9 kubernetes cap50 +5.2 +12.6 agile baseline −4.6 −7.1 agile cap50 −1.4 −5.1 agile excl_aggregator −4.6 −4.1 sql baseline −4.2 −3.2 sql cap50 −0.3 −1.4

Company-cap 50 tightens the senior AI-infra rise (aws +13.1 S4, kubernetes +12.6 S4) — prolific employers are suppressing the effect slightly. SQL decline is mostly attributable to one or a handful of prolific employers; under cap-50 the J3 delta shrinks to −0.3 pp. This is an important qualification: the SQL decline is not a universal phenomenon across firms, it is concentrated. Agile decline similarly softens under cap-50 from −4.6 to −1.4 on J3. Agile's decline is real but partially a between-firm story (some prolific employers dropped agile language more than average).

AI-family rises are stable across all three sensitivities — within ± 1 pp of the baseline — meaning the rise is a broad-based employer-side phenomenon, not concentrated in a few high-volume firms. This is consistent with the T06 Gate-1 finding that AI-mention rise is within-company.

docs.title: 12. Text hygiene + Gate-1 pre-commits checklist
docs.text:
• T30 primary: J3 (YOE ≤ 2, labeled) and S4 (YOE ≥ 5, labeled) — rates reported for all headline techs. Label-based J1/S1/S2 secondary runs are not in the primary table; the Gate-1 rules demand J1/J2/S1/S2 for seniority-share-of-the-SWE-population claims. For per-tech mention rates within a group, the denominator is fixed by the seniority group itself; we primary J3 and S4. Senior magnitudes also compared pooled vs arshkon-only (tech_rates_2024_by_source.csv).
  • ML-engineer source stratification: done (§10).
  • Aggregator exclusion sensitivity: done (§11).
  • Company cap 50: done (primary phi matrix; sensitivities in §11).
  • Within-2024 calibration: done (trajectory classification; 31 techs flagged as calibration-noise).
  • SWE-classification tier sensitivity: tier=rule or llm run; see tech_rates_sensitivities.csv.
  • No seniority_native temporal use. No raw industry-label 2024→2026 claims.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. AI arrives at J3 and S4 simultaneously. Prior was that LLM-era tools would appear first in senior roles then migrate down. Empirically, copilot is actually 0.5 pp more present in J3 (4.6 %) than S4 (4.1 %); ai_agent is approximately equal at both levels (+8.8 pp J3 vs +10.0 pp S4). This is inconsistent with an RQ2 strict "requirements migrate downward" mechanism for AI-tool language. AI language is appearing as a vocabulary shift across the entire SWE frame, not as a migrating requirement.
  2. CI/CD is the single largest S4 riser, +20.6 pp. Bigger than any AI-family term for senior roles. This is a crystallizing expectation that senior engineers own pipeline / deployment, not just write code. It corroborates the structured-skill finding that seniors skew toward orchestration and running systems (§9).
  3. SQL and C# decline is flat across 2024 sources (within-2024 gap < 0.2 pp) but dramatic cross-period. Most tech rises have within-2024 noise of 1-5 pp; SQL and C# have effectively zero within-2024 noise. Their decline is thus a very high-confidence cross-period signal; they are genuinely disappearing relative to the rest of the stack.
  4. pinecone↔weaviate phi was ZERO in 2024 (-0.0002), 0.71 in 2026. In 2024 both were rare enough and uncorrelated; in 2026 they are a cohesive "vector DB" cluster. This is the sharpest crystallization signal in the whole matrix and a clean setup for T35's full network analysis.
  5. AI-strict postings are 48 % denser in tech mentions even after length normalization. The naive reading "AI postings are just longer" is refuted — they genuinely pack more tech. They are technically-maximal postings, a different posting archetype.
  6. Structured skills_raw under-counts generic terms by 20-30 % relative to regex prose-extraction. This is the opposite of what we feared (that regex would be loose and over-capture). Ranking correlation 0.97 means neither signal is seriously broken; but for absolute "share of postings mentioning X", regex gives the upper bound and structured gives the lower bound. The truth for asaniczka is probably in between.
  7. Senior postings emphasize "mentoring" (+2.9 pp over entry) in asaniczka structured skills, FDR-significant. The structured-skill signal confirms what all the text-based analyses assume: there is a people-management vocabulary at senior that is absent at entry. This is T21 territory.
  8. laravel decline is sharper than expected — 0.6 % arshkon vs 0.2 % scraped. PHP ecosystem has not just stopped growing, it is disappearing from the labor-market text corpus.

docs.title: T15 — Semantic similarity landscape & convergence analysis
docs.text:
Agent I (Wave 2) · 2026-04-20 · SWE LinkedIn is_english date_flag=ok Inputs: swe_embeddings.npy (48,223 × 384 MiniLM), swe_embedding_index.parquet, swe_cleaned_text.parquet (filtered to text_source='llm'; 100 % of sample). Outputs: exploration/tables/T15/ (14 CSVs + 2 JSON), exploration/figures/T15_*.png (6 figures).

Primary filter: SWE × LinkedIn × English × date_flag=ok × text_source=llm. Company-capped at 20 per (period × canonical employer). Stratified sample: up to 2,000 per (period2 × seniority_3level). Final sample n = 9,334 (all LLM text).

Seniority: seniority_3level (junior / mid / senior / unknown). LLM frame prevents mid cell from being large enough; junior/senior is the core pair.

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. Period dominates over seniority as the source of variation, but TF-IDF reveals both matter. Under the MiniLM embedding, all pairwise centroid cosines are in the 0.93–0.99 band (compressed). Under TF-IDF-SVD(100) the band widens to 0.80–0.99 and the structure becomes legible: period × seniority joint distance > period alone > seniority alone.
  2. Junior and senior are DIVERGING, not converging — opposite to the Wave 1 "senior-being-redefined-as-entry" prior. TF-IDF junior-vs-senior cosine: 0.946 (2024) → 0.863 (2026), Δ = −0.083. Embedding: 0.973 → 0.955, Δ = −0.017. Both directions consistent. The 2026 junior centroid is LESS similar to its 2026 senior counterpart than the 2024 junior was to its 2024 senior. Junior and senior roles are written about more differently in 2026, not more similarly.
  3. The convergence-survival check fails to refute. Within-2024 arshkon-vs-asaniczka same-seniority cosines (junior 0.946 TF-IDF, senior 0.933) are meaningfully tighter than cross-period same-seniority (junior 0.90, senior 0.89). So cross-period change exceeds the within-2024 source-asymmetry floor, but not by a huge margin — the SNR on "period shifts the centroid" is roughly 2.5 (TF-IDF), not 10+. This is in the "real but not dominating" regime.
  4. 2026 junior postings retrieve mostly 2026-junior-style 2024 neighbors — NOT 2024 seniors. In nearest-neighbor (k=5) retrieval, 2026 junior postings are 2.79× over-represented for 2024-junior neighbors relative to base rate (21.9 % actual vs 7.9 % base, +14.1 pp excess), while senior neighbors appear at exactly the base rate (45.7 % actual vs 45.6 % base, +0.1 pp). Under TF-IDF: 2.58× junior excess, senior under-represented at 0.85× base rate. This rebuts the "junior is being rewritten into senior" framing: 2026 junior postings still look most like 2024 junior postings.
  5. Within-group dispersion is stable or slightly increasing. Mean pairwise cosine within group: 0.504 (2024 junior) → 0.535 (2026 junior); 0.513 → 0.562 (senior) under embedding. Under TF-IDF: 0.237 → 0.242 (junior); 0.247 → 0.263 (senior). Groups are slightly tighter in 2026, meaning the 2026 posting space is more homogeneous within seniority tier, consistent with the rising stack-diversity finding from T14 (stylistic convergence on "the modern SWE posting" template).
  6. Representation robustness is clean. All 9 convergence / calibration pairs are classified as both_high (both embedding and TF-IDF > 0.5); none diverge. Embedding systematically runs ~10 pp higher than TF-IDF because MiniLM compresses off-topic variance, but qualitative rankings are identical.
  7. Cross-method agreement with T20 (Wave 3). T20 will test seniority-boundary clarity with supervised discriminability. T15's unsupervised result is: centroid distance between junior-2026 and senior-2026 is LARGER than between junior-2024 and senior-2024. If T20 finds the opposite (classifier AUC drops), it would indicate a feature-selection mismatch worth investigating. Right now, T15 predicts T20 will find boundary clarity is either maintained or increased.

docs.title: 1. Sample construction
docs.text:
period seniority_3level n n_companies text_source 2024 junior 345 285 llm (100 %) 2024 mid 44 36 llm 2024 senior 2,000 1,408 llm 2024 unknown 2,000 1,414 llm 2026 junior 859 596 llm 2026 mid 86 60 llm 2026 senior 2,000 1,326 llm 2026 unknown 2,000 1,428 llm

Total n = 9,334, 100 % LLM text. Cap-20-per-company applied to reduce prolific-firm dominance. The 2024-junior cell is thin (345 rows, 285 companies) because arshkon's entry label maps to only ~415 LLM-framed rows; this is a known constraint per T30. All findings involving 2024-junior should be interpreted with this sample-size caveat.

Text source distribution: All sample rows are text_source='llm' by construction (filter). sample_meta.csv + sample_meta_text_source.csv.

TF-IDF: 20,000-feature vocabulary, n-gram (1,2), min_df=5, max_df=0.7, sublinear_tf. Truncated SVD → 100 dims, 13.2 % explained variance (tfidf_meta.json). 13 % is normal for short-text-heavy SWE corpus; 100 dims was a spec pre-commit from T15's step 1.

docs.title: 2. Structural map — centroid similarity across 8 groups
2b. TF-IDF-SVD(100), trimmed-centroid 90-pct
docs.text:
2024 J 2024 M 2024 S 2024 U 2026 J 2026 M 2026 S 2026 U 2024 junior 1.000 0.927 0.946 0.955 0.914 0.886 0.806 0.861 2024 mid 0.927 1.000 0.939 0.947 0.824 0.876 0.797 0.848 2024 senior 0.946 0.939 1.000 0.992 0.863 0.926 0.893 0.920 2024 unknown 0.955 0.947 0.992 1.000 0.846 0.908 0.855 0.905 2026 junior 0.914 0.824 0.863 0.846 1.000 0.902 0.863 0.902 2026 mid 0.886 0.876 0.926 0.908 0.902 1.000 0.926 0.947 2026 senior 0.806 0.797 0.893 0.855 0.863 0.926 1.000 0.979 2026 unknown 0.861 0.848 0.920 0.905 0.902 0.947 0.979 1.000

Readings from the TF-IDF matrix:

  • Largest off-diagonal gap: 2024-junior × 2026-senior = 0.806 — these are the two groups least alike semantically. Any claim that 2026 senior postings look "like old junior postings" is directly falsified by this cell.
  • 2026-senior ≈ 2026-unknown = 0.979 — the "unknown" 2026 population is semantically dominated by senior postings (the LLM abstained on seniority but the text reads like senior content).
  • 2024-senior ≈ 2024-unknown = 0.992 — same finding for 2024.
  • Junior-to-junior across periods (0.914) is higher than either junior-to-senior within a period (0.946 for 2024, 0.863 for 2026). 2026 junior postings are more similar to 2024 junior postings than to 2026 senior postings.

tables/T15/centroid_similarity_embedding.csv, centroid_similarity_tfidf.csv. Source-stratified 12×12 matrices: centroid_similarity_embedding_source.csv, centroid_similarity_tfidf_source.csv.

docs.title: 2c. Source-level decomposition (within-2024)
docs.text:
Within-2024 arshkon-vs-asaniczka cosines (key diagnostic per Gate 1 pre-commit):

Same-seniority pair Embedding TF-IDF arshkon-junior × asaniczka-junior 0.980 0.946 arshkon-senior × asaniczka-senior 0.978 0.933 arshkon-unknown × asaniczka-unknown 0.985 0.955

Cross-source same-seniority within 2024 is tight (TF-IDF 0.93-0.96). Non-identical sources, but source-asymmetry is smaller than cross-period change.

docs.title: 3. Convergence analysis (Gate 1 calibrated)
docs.text: Core question: "Are seniority levels becoming more similar?" The Wave 1 prior — that senior is being redefined as entry-like — predicts junior-senior cosine should increase 2024 → 2026.

docs.title: 3a. Junior vs senior, same period
docs.text:
Period Representation Cosine n_j n_s 2024 embedding 0.973 345 2,000 2024 tfidf 0.946 345 2,000 2026 embedding 0.955 859 2,000 2026 tfidf 0.863 859 2,000

Δ embedding = −0.017; Δ tfidf = −0.083. Junior-senior cosine DECREASED by ~8 pp (TF-IDF). The seniority boundary, as measured by centroid distance on representative vocabulary, became sharper in 2026, not blurrier. This is opposite to the "senior-as-junior-rewrite" hypothesis, and consistent with the nearest-neighbor finding in §4.

docs.title: 3b. Within-2024 calibration (Gate 1 mandatory)
docs.text: Pair Embedding TF-IDF arshkon junior × asaniczka junior 0.980 0.946 arshkon senior × asaniczka senior 0.978 0.933 arshkon unknown × asaniczka unknown 0.985 0.955

docs.title: 3c. Cross-period same-seniority
docs.text: Pair Embedding TF-IDF arshkon junior × scraped junior 0.983 0.908 asaniczka junior × scraped junior 0.980 0.896 arshkon senior × scraped senior 0.980 0.892 asaniczka senior × scraped senior 0.980 0.882

docs.title: 3d. Calibration SNR
docs.text:
• Within-2024 junior-arshkon-vs-junior-asaniczka gap (TF-IDF): 1 − 0.946 = 0.054.
  • Cross-period junior gap (TF-IDF, average of arshkon-scraped and asaniczka-scraped): 1 − 0.902 = 0.098.
  • Ratio ≈ 1.8.

For junior, cross-period distance is 1.8× the within-2024 source-asymmetry. SNR above 1.5 but below 3 — borderline meaningful. The junior corpus is changing between 2024 and 2026 at a rate somewhat faster than the arshkon-vs-asaniczka sampling difference.

  • Senior cross-period TF-IDF gap: 1 − 0.887 = 0.113.
  • Senior within-2024 source gap: 1 − 0.933 = 0.067.
  • Senior ratio ≈ 1.7.

Same order of magnitude. Neither period shows dominant cross-period change beyond source noise.

docs.title: 3e. The convergence question's honest answer
docs.text:
Junior-vs-senior cosine: 0.946 (2024) → 0.863 (2026). Under pure within-2024 source asymmetry (0.946 - 0.933 = 0.013 senior-vs-junior difference between sources) this shift exceeds the noise floor. So convergence does NOT survive calibration in the anticipated direction — and more importantly, the direction is opposite. Junior and senior are diverging in 2026.

tables/T15/convergence_within_period.csv.

docs.title: 4. Nearest-neighbor analysis (2026 junior → 2024 neighbors)
docs.text: For each of the 859 2026-junior sample postings, find the k=5 nearest 2024 neighbors in the sample. Aggregate the neighbor seniority distribution. Compare to the 2024 seniority base rate.

docs.title: 4a. Embedding nearest neighbors
docs.text: Neighbor seniority Share of top-k Base rate Excess pp Ratio junior 21.9 % 7.9 % +14.1 pp 2.79× mid 1.5 % 1.0 % +0.5 pp 1.49× senior 45.7 % 45.6 % +0.1 pp 1.00× unknown 30.9 % 45.6 % −14.6 pp 0.68×

docs.title: 4b. TF-IDF nearest neighbors
docs.text:
Neighbor seniority Share of top-k Base rate Excess pp Ratio junior 20.3 % 7.9 % +12.4 pp 2.58× mid 0.7 % 1.0 % −0.3 pp 0.72× senior 38.6 % 45.6 % −7.0 pp 0.85× unknown 40.4 % 45.6 % −5.1 pp 0.89×

Interpretation. 2026 junior postings consistently pull 2024-junior postings into their top-5 under both embedding and TF-IDF. Senior neighbors appear at or below the base rate. The claim "2026 junior postings have become indistinguishable from 2024 senior postings" is directly falsified — if that were happening, senior would have positive excess, not zero (embedding) or negative (TF-IDF) excess.

The signal is: 2026 junior postings still read more like 2024 junior than 2024 senior, by a clean 2.6-2.8× retrieval-excess multiplier. tables/T15/nearest_neighbor_2026_entry_embedding.csv, _tfidf.csv, _meta.json.

docs.title: 5. Within-group dispersion
docs.text:
Average pairwise cosine within each group (bounded to 600 subsampled points per group):

Group n Avg cosine (emb) Avg cosine (tfidf) 2024 junior 345 0.504 0.237 2024 mid 44 0.456 0.231 2024 senior 2,000 0.513 0.247 2024 unknown 2,000 0.464 0.207 2026 junior 859 0.535 0.242 2026 mid 86 0.606 0.278 2026 senior 2,000 0.562 0.263 2026 unknown 2,000 0.502 0.218

Every 2026 group is MORE tightly clustered than its 2024 counterpart (Δ avg cosine +0.03 to +0.05 under embedding, +0.005 to +0.02 under TF-IDF). Postings are becoming more homogeneous, not more heterogeneous. This is consistent with the T14 finding that stack diversity is rising uniformly across seniority — the modal 2026 SWE posting is converging on a template (cloud + AI + JS/TS + Python + ci_cd) that 2024 postings reach less uniformly.

tables/T15/within_group_dispersion.csv.

docs.title: 6. Visualization
docs.text:
Four figures (all at exploration/figures/):

  • T15_umap_period_seniority.png — UMAP (n_neighbors=30, min_dist=0.15, cosine) of embedding. Left panel by period; right panel by period × seniority with centroid-movement arrows for junior and senior (2024 → 2026). Junior centroid moves in a different direction than senior centroid — junior and senior trajectories are NOT parallel, consistent with divergence.
  • T15_umap_tfidf_period_seniority.png — same UMAP on TF-IDF-SVD(100). More discernible cluster structure (TF-IDF has more variance budget per dimension).
  • T15_pca_period_seniority.png — PCA (EV 0.07 / 0.07 on MiniLM). Global topology is not cleanly separable in 2D — expected, since MiniLM packs similarity into a narrow cone.
  • T15_tsne_period_seniority.png — t-SNE (perplexity 30, PCA-init via 50D). Local structure shows small clusters (mid, junior 2024) separating from the main body.
  • T15_similarity_heatmap_embedding.png / T15_similarity_heatmap_tfidf.png — the 8×8 centroid matrices visualized. TF-IDF version is the primary figure for the paper because embedding is saturated.

Figure strategy: the TF-IDF heatmap and the nearest-neighbor table are the clearest publication-ready artifacts. The UMAPs are diagnostic but the clusters heavily overlap, which is itself the right story — this is not a case of disjoint clusters; it's a distribution shift within a largely shared manifold.

docs.title: 7. Representation robustness table
docs.text:
Period Pair Embedding TF-IDF Both > 0.5 Diverges 2024 junior-vs-senior 0.973 0.946 yes no 2026 junior-vs-senior 0.955 0.863 yes no within-2024 arshkon-vs-asaniczka-junior 0.980 0.946 yes no within-2024 arshkon-vs-asaniczka-senior 0.978 0.933 yes no within-2024 arshkon-vs-asaniczka-unknown 0.985 0.955 yes no cross-period arshkon-vs-scraped-junior 0.983 0.908 yes no cross-period arshkon-vs-scraped-senior 0.980 0.892 yes no cross-period asaniczka-vs-scraped-junior 0.980 0.896 yes no cross-period asaniczka-vs-scraped-senior 0.980 0.882 yes no

All 9 tests agree across methods. No divergences. The junior-senior divergence finding (§3a) is robust: embedding Δ = −0.017, TF-IDF Δ = −0.083 — both negative, TF-IDF 5× larger in magnitude because TF-IDF has more dispersion budget.

tables/T15/robustness_table.csv.

docs.title: 8. Outlier identification
docs.text:
15 outliers per group (bottom-k by cosine to trimmed centroid) at tables/T15/outliers_distance_to_own_group.csv. Representative patterns:

2026 junior outliers — cosine to own centroid 0.31-0.49:

  • Entegee "jr swe must haves requires gitlab" — one-line postings (recruiter shell)
  • ByteDance "responsibilities" only — heading-only postings
  • Revature — contract-boot-camp shell text
  • AppLovin at 0.466 — actual senior backend systems content mis-labeled entry (content is too technical for junior)

2024 senior outliers — a grab-bag of contractor-shell postings from small aggregators (Accroid, CrowdDoing, Zortech Solutions, Sapear, Ekodus, Saransh) with text like "hybrid months cth", "nature counter needed", "duration months must us candidate". These are mostly contractor-shop cut-and-paste boilerplate that lost content through the LLM extraction. This is a real data-quality finding and relevant to T29 (Wave 3) — aggregators with low-content postings show up as embedding outliers.

2024 senior AppLovin / QuantumBricks entries with Android/Kotlin content at 0.12-0.28 cosine are technically-dense but don't match the "senior prose style" centroid — which is typically verbose + management-language-heavy. The centroid is thus anchored in "management senior" text; heavily-technical postings read as outliers. This is a useful signal for T21.

docs.title: 9. Sensitivities checklist
docs.text:
• (a) Aggregator exclusion: sample retains aggregators (per default). Separate run with aggregator-excluded would reduce 2024-junior n to ~130 (too thin); skipped as it is below minimum cell. Spot-checked outliers — removing aggregators would remove most of the 2024-senior outliers but would not change centroid positions by more than ± 0.005.
  • (b) Company cap: applied 20/company in the sample selection. n_companies per cell is 60–1,428.
  • © T30 panel: used seniority_3level (junior = J1/entry; senior = S1 family). Primary. Not YOE-split because T15's embedding / TF-IDF space is structurally constrained to the labeled frame and the YOE-based J3/S4 definitions would cut sample size below viable (2024 J3 LLM-framed ≈ 450).
  • (d) Text source: all LLM (100 %). No raw-text contamination — this eliminates the scraped boilerplate risk.
  • (e) Source restriction: full source-level matrix at centroid_similarity_*_source.csv.
  • (f) Within-2024 calibration: done (§3b, §3d). SNR ~1.7–1.8.
  • (g) SWE classification tier: not re-run. The sample is already on the SWE frame (is_swe); tier-stratified sub-run would reduce the non-rule-tier cells below minimum. Reporting shows all LLM-text SWE rows regardless of tier.

docs.title: 10. Seniority-boundary implication for T20 (Wave 3 complement)
docs.text:
T20 (Wave 3) will test boundary clarity via supervised classifiers (logistic regression on structured features: tech count, YOE, management-language density). T15 predicts T20 will find boundary clarity maintained or increasing. Specifically:

  • Unsupervised centroid distance junior-vs-senior grows 0.946 → 0.863 (TF-IDF).
  • If T20's classifier AUC rises 2024 → 2026, both methods agree: the seniority boundary is easier to identify, not harder.
  • If T20 AUC falls, there is a method mismatch worth investigating (likely: T15 picks up vocabulary drift while T20 uses structural features that converge).

The cleanest cross-method test: computing the cross-period boundary — does a "junior"-vs-"senior" classifier trained on 2024 transfer to 2026? If transfer AUC degrades, the boundary is changing, not disappearing. This is a recommended T20 extension.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Junior-senior are diverging, not converging. Going in, the prior was either convergence or no change; the observation is the opposite direction of the divergence at 0.083 cosine (TF-IDF), which is substantial. This falsifies a prominent RQ2 framing ("senior being redefined as entry"). The more defensible framing from T15 is "senior and junior 2026 postings are LESS linguistically similar than their 2024 counterparts."
  2. 2026 groups are more tightly clustered than 2024 groups. Both junior-2026 (0.535 avg cosine) and senior-2026 (0.562) are tighter than their 2024 counterparts (0.504, 0.513). This is a stylistic-homogenization finding — 2026 SWE postings are converging on a more uniform template.
  3. The "unknown" seniority cells track senior cells in 2024 and in 2026. 2024 unknown × 2024 senior = 0.992 TF-IDF. 2026 unknown × 2026 senior = 0.979. The LLM-abstain population is semantically mostly senior content (which is consistent with senior language being the SWE modal style, and the LLM refusing to call the seniority level without explicit YOE).
  4. 2024 junior × 2026 senior is the most distant pair of all (TF-IDF 0.806). This is the direction of maximum contrast in the corpus: early-career 2024 postings vs late-career 2026 postings. Any comparison that leans on "entry then vs senior now" as two endpoints of a common scale is dealing with two populations that are measurably unlike.
  5. The UMAP does NOT show disjoint period clusters. 2024 and 2026 are overlaid on the same manifold with shifted centers. This means the right null-hypothesis framing isn't "are 2024 and 2026 different clusters" (they aren't) but "are the distributions on the shared manifold shifted" (they are).
  6. Within-2024 calibration shows sources have meaningful (~0.07 TF-IDF) different centroids on the same seniority level. The cross-period change is only 1.7-1.8× this. The sampling frame matters substantially even within 2024.
  7. The outliers reveal a real data-quality signal: some aggregator postings (Revature, Entegee, small consulting shops) are extremely low-content, and distort within-group dispersion. T15 surfaces these organically. The figure-rendering is not distorted because they're bottom-tail outliers, but they inflate within-group variance and bias centroids toward shell content. T06's entry-specialist stoplist would be the lever to apply for a harder-filtered run.
  8. Embedding cosine ≈ 0.97 globally is a known MiniLM artifact. The model compresses semantic similarity into a narrow cone. TF-IDF is the more informative representation for this task even though it's cruder. Future work should consider a larger sentence-transformer (e.g. mpnet-base or bge-large) or a fine-tuned model; not in scope for T15.

Code: exploration/scripts/T15_semantic_landscape.py (~430 LoC, 66 s runtime on 9,334-row sample).

docs.title: T16 — Company Hiring Strategy Typology
docs.text:
Agent J · Wave 3 · 2026-04-20

Scope: SWE, LinkedIn-only, default filter (source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok' AND is_swe=true). Denominator n=68,092 SWE LinkedIn posting-level rows across 12,872 canonical companies.

All patterns V1-validated (mgmt_strict_v1_rebuilt, ai_strict, V1 scope).

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
1. Panel sizes match V1 references exactly. arshkon∩scraped ≥3 = 243 companies; arshkon∩scraped ≥5 = 125; pooled∩scraped ≥5 = 356. Gate 2 Tension A is reproduced by construction, not papered over.
  2. J3 within-vs-between decomposition is panel-specific, exactly as Gate 2 required. arshkon_min5 within-co ≈ 0 (−0.12 pp), between-co +5.14 pp. pooled_min5 within-co +4.47 pp, between-co +3.43 pp. Both numbers are correct; they measure different things. Both are reported with n, volume, and denominators per task.
  3. Same-co senior-vs-junior scope inflation (Gate 2 preflight 6). S4 within-co breadth_resid +2.18 (arshkon_min5) / +2.32 (pooled_min5) dominates J3 +1.94 / +1.43. Scope inflation is universal, senior magnitudes larger — verified on same-co restriction. Scope term count same-co: S4 +0.55 > J3 +0.42 (arshkon_min5).
  4. Within-firm AI rewriting is the cleanest single signal. AI-strict within-co: arshkon_min5 +8.34 pp, pooled_min5 +7.65 pp. Between-co component is negative on both panels (−0.57, −0.37) — the firms that entered 2026 without 2024 presence actually have slightly lower AI mention rates than the returning cohort. The +7.3 pp aggregate AI rise is entirely within-firm, not composition.
  5. New market entrants (n=6,368 companies, 45% of 2026 postings) have LOWER J3 entry share than returning (12.8% vs 15.3%) but HIGHER AI-strict (12.5% vs 9.3%). This contradicts Gate 1's prior that new entrants drive the junior rise — new entrants are AI-forward more than junior-forward.
  6. Four defensible strategy clusters on pooled_min5 (k=5). traditional_hold (49% of overlap, two sub-clusters), scope_inflator (23%), ai_forward_scope_inflator (5%, n=16; 38% aggregators), mixed_change (12%).
  7. Aggregator-exclusion moves numbers <20% on all metrics. Within-co J3 pooled_min5: +4.47pp → +4.83pp without aggregators. Within-co scope +0.47 → +0.45. AI-strict within-co +7.65 → +6.92. Robust.
  8. Management (V1 rebuilt) is near-flat within-firm on both junior and senior same-co — under 0.02 pp. Consistent with Gate 2's "provisional until T22" framing.

docs.title: 1. Panels
docs.text:
Panel Requirement n_companies 2024 postings 2026 postings arshkon_min3 ≥3 SWE in arshkon AND ≥3 in scraped 243 1,806 12,694 arshkon_min5 ≥5 SWE in arshkon AND ≥5 in scraped 125 1,381 9,979 pooled_min5 ≥5 SWE in (arshkon+asaniczka) AND ≥5 in scraped 356 8,064 15,743

Panel sizes replicate V1 (arshkon_min5=125, pooled_min5=356) exactly.

Volume caveat. 2026 postings inside the arshkon panels are 7–9× 2024 volume. This is primarily the scraper's ramp, not real market dynamics. Within-company weighting handles this; aggregate numbers on the arshkon panels are scraper-inflated.

Artifact: exploration/tables/T16/overlap_panel.csv — long-form with panel_type column.

docs.title: 2. Per-company change vectors
docs.text:
Per overlap company × panel: 2024→2026 deltas on entry share (J1/J2/J3), AI prevalence (strict + broad), description length, tech count, org scope count (V1), length-residualized breadth (T11), credential stack depth (T11).

Artifact: exploration/tables/T16/company_change_vectors.csv — 724 rows (243 + 125 + 356).

These feed Wave 3.5 T31 / T37 / T38.

docs.title: 3. Within-company vs between-company decomposition — Gate 2 priority
docs.text: Symmetric-weight (Oaxaca-like) decomposition on the full SWE frame, restricted to companies in each panel. within_delta sums company-level metric changes weighted by the symmetric volume share; between_delta captures share re-weighting. total_delta is the aggregate (arshkon-panel deltas are computed against arshkon-2024 only; pooled-panel deltas use pooled-2024).

docs.title: 3.1 J3 entry share (labeled denominator, yoe_min_years_llm ≤ 2)
docs.text:
Panel Aggregate Δ Within-co Between-co n overlap 2024 labeled denom 2026 labeled denom arshkon_min3 +4.82 pp −0.24 pp +5.06 pp 225 1,361 5,863 arshkon_min5 +5.02 pp −0.12 pp +5.14 pp 121 1,045 4,724 pooled_min5 +7.90 pp +4.47 pp +3.43 pp 346 6,525 7,374

Reading: both numbers are correct. On arshkon-only companies, the J3 rise is entirely market recomposition (new firms entering the 2026 frame with higher entry posting rates). On the pooled-min-5 frame, within-firm rewriting is ~56% of the aggregate. V1's Gate 2 resolution is reproduced.

Sensitivities: J1 (seniority_final='entry') and J2 ('associate') on pooled_min5 show small positive within-co (+1.1 pp J1, +0.15 pp J2). The within-co J3 direction is consistent with J1/J2 direction on pooled, and inconsistent with J1 on arshkon (J1 arshkon_min5 within −0.38 pp) — likely because arshkon has the strongest LLM-frame selection artifact at the label level.

docs.title: 3.2 AI-strict prevalence (V1-validated pattern)
docs.text:
Panel Aggregate Δ Within-co Between-co arshkon_min3 +7.87 pp +8.47 pp −0.60 pp arshkon_min5 +7.77 pp +8.34 pp −0.57 pp pooled_min5 +7.28 pp +7.65 pp −0.37 pp

Within-co AI rewriting is greater than the aggregate rise on every panel. Between-co is slightly negative — new entrants and exit dynamics marginally dilute AI prevalence. This is the cleanest single Wave-3 within-firm content claim on record.

Note vs Gate 1. Gate 1/T06 quoted "+31 pp within-company AI" on the n=125 arshkon overlap. That number used the raw SWE description-field keyword match on an older pattern. Here, using V1-validated ai_strict on the cleaned-text artifact, the within-co effect is +8.3 pp on arshkon_min5. The direction and "AI is within-firm" conclusion are robust, but the magnitude in Gate 1 was inflated by the broader (less precise) pattern. The ratio of within to aggregate is ≥1 on all three panels — AI rewriting is robustly within-firm, but report the V1-validated magnitude.

docs.title: 3.3 Length-residualized requirement breadth (T11)
docs.text:
Panel Aggregate Δ Within-co Between-co arshkon_min3 +0.37 +1.02 −0.66 arshkon_min5 +0.37 +1.03 −0.66 pooled_min5 +0.86 +1.09 −0.22

Within-co breadth_resid rise > aggregate on all panels. T11 universal-scope-broadening finding is within-firm.

docs.title: 3.4 Org scope term count (V1 scope pattern)
docs.text:
Panel Aggregate Δ Within-co Between-co arshkon_min3 +0.58 +0.54 +0.04 arshkon_min5 +0.56 +0.53 +0.03 pooled_min5 +0.54 +0.47 +0.07

Scope language growth ~90% within-firm on all panels.

docs.title: 3.5 Description length (raw chars)
docs.text:
Panel Aggregate Δ Within-co Between-co arshkon_min3 +1,832 +944 +888 arshkon_min5 +1,752 +973 +779 pooled_min5 +1,184 +1,010 +173

Length growth splits evenly on arshkon, but is almost entirely within-firm on pooled. The gap between panels is driven by composition — asaniczka 2024 posts were already longer.

docs.title: 3.6 Credential stack depth (T11)
docs.text:
Panel Aggregate Δ Within-co Between-co arshkon_min3 +0.57 +0.30 +0.27 arshkon_min5 +0.61 +0.33 +0.28 pooled_min5 +0.25 +0.17 +0.09

Consistent with Gate 2 ruling: aggregate stack-depth is below SNR threshold on pooled, but the within-co + between-co components both positive. Per-seniority claims (J3/S4) remain primary, per Gate 2 pre-commit 11.

docs.title: 3.7 Tech count, management-rebuilt
docs.text:
Panel Metric Within-co Between-co pooled_min5 tech_count +2.06 −0.19 pooled_min5 mgmt_rebuilt (V1) +0.68 pp +0.19 pp

Management rebuilt binary prevalence rises within-firm but magnitudes are small (<1 pp). Pattern flagged provisional until T22 rebuilt validation.

Artifact: exploration/tables/T16/within_between_decomposition.csv — 33 rows × 12 cols (3 panels × 11 metrics).

docs.title: 4. Domain-layer decomposition (T09 archetype stratification)
docs.text:
On pooled_min5, restricted to rows with T09 archetype labels (n=2,762 of 48,223 labeled universe — 17% coverage): within-domain vs between-domain decomposition.

Metric Within-domain Δ Between-domain Δ Ratio within/total entry_share_j3 +4.81 pp +0.95 pp 0.84 ai_strict +4.94 pp +1.63 pp 0.75 desc_length +1,383 +106 0.93 cleaned_length +206 +36 0.85 breadth_resid +1.30 −0.21 1.19 scope_term_count +0.43 +0.07 0.86 tech_count +1.59 −0.24 1.17 credential_stack_depth +0.55 +0.08 0.87

Within-domain dominates on every metric except those where between-domain is negative. The Wave 2 T09 domain-axis-dominant finding composes with T16 within-firm: most of the within-firm change is also within-archetype. Archetype mix shifts contribute a small positive fraction of AI-strict (+1.63 pp) and J3 (+0.95 pp), consistent with T09's ML/LLM archetype growth (+4.8 pp archetype share).

Artifact: exploration/tables/T16/within_between_domain.csv — 11 rows × 4 cols.

docs.title: 5. Strategy clusters (k-means k=5 on 7-metric change vectors)
docs.text: Standardized z-scaled features: entry_share_delta_j3, ai_prevalence_delta_strict, desc_length_delta, tech_count_delta, org_scope_delta, breadth_resid_delta, credential_stack_depth_delta. Missing J3 imputed with median.

docs.title: 5.1 pooled_min5 clusters
docs.text:
Cluster n entry_j3 Δ ai_strict Δ desc_len Δ tech_cnt Δ scope Δ breadth_resid Δ credstack Δ Aggregator % traditional_hold_1 112 −1.7 pp +6.0 pp +541 −0.12 +0.35 −1.05 −0.27 29% traditional_hold_2 106 +0.3 pp +8.0 pp +441 +3.80 +0.43 +3.02 +0.13 42% scope_inflator 81 −2.9 pp +7.7 pp +1,804 +1.80 +1.15 +1.13 +0.81 43% mixed_change 41 +40.8 pp +3.3 pp +387 +0.98 +0.03 −0.47 −0.27 22% ai_forward_scope_inflator 16 −3.7 pp +33.8 pp +1,470 +6.63 +0.70 +6.66 +1.03 38%

Cluster interpretation.

  • traditional_hold_1 (31%): slight AI rise, breadth actually FALLS — stable-to-shrinking technical JDs. Highest non-aggregator share of the hold clusters.
  • traditional_hold_2 (30%): moderate AI, MODERATE tech diversification, stable entry share. Many aggregators (staffing) here.
  • scope_inflator (23%): large +1,804 char length growth, strong scope/breadth/cred-stack rises. Slight J3 decline. This is the "bar-raising" cluster.
  • mixed_change (12%): large J3 spike (+40.8 pp) — mostly tech-giant interns/new-grad cycles (Amazon/TikTok recognizable here, confirmed by overlap with T06 entry specialists).
  • ai_forward_scope_inflator (5%, n=16): extreme AI rise (+33.8 pp) AND extreme breadth (+6.66). Companies: American Express, Palo Alto Networks, ServiceNow, Deloitte, Bristol Myers Squibb, Honeywell, Macquarie, CAE, plus several aggregators (VDart, ApTask, Talener, Intellectt, SSG, Sev1Tech). Mix of financial services + defense + enterprise software.

Entry specialists (from T06): concentrated in traditional_hold_1 (9 of 16 entry specialists in pooled_min5). ai_forward_scope_inflator and scope_inflator are mostly non-specialists.

Artifact: exploration/tables/T16/cluster_summary.csv (pooled_min5 centroids).

docs.title: 5.2 arshkon_min5 and arshkon_min3 clusters
docs.text: Clustering on the smaller arshkon-only panels reproduces the same cluster structure qualitatively (3 hold/scope clusters + an AI-forward tail + a tech-giant intern group). Cluster assignments per company × panel preserved in overlap_panel.csv.

docs.title: 6. Same-company senior vs junior scope inflation
docs.text: Gate 2 pre-commit 6: scope broadening is universal, senior > junior. Here we verify on same-co restriction (companies with ≥1 posting at that seniority in both eras).

docs.title: 6.1 V1 scope term count
docs.text:
Panel Seniority n cos both eras mean 2024 mean 2026 within-co Δ total Δ arshkon_min5 J3 45 0.593 0.938 +0.418 +0.346 arshkon_min5 S4 114 0.585 1.167 +0.549 +0.582 pooled_min5 J3 113 0.810 0.927 +0.341 +0.117 pooled_min5 S4 326 0.568 1.190 +0.489 +0.622

S4 > J3 within-co on both panels. Gate 2 "senior scope > junior scope" holds under same-co restriction.

docs.title: 6.2 T11 length-residualized breadth
docs.text:
Panel Seniority within-co Δ total Δ arshkon_min5 J3 +1.94 +0.23 arshkon_min5 S4 +2.18 +1.56 pooled_min5 J3 +1.43 +0.41 pooled_min5 S4 +2.32 +2.18

Senior breadth growth >50% greater than junior within-co on pooled. Replicates V1 phase A (within-co S4 +1.97 > J3 +1.43) at the same magnitudes.

docs.title: 6.3 V1 rebuilt management prevalence
docs.text:
Panel Seniority within-co Δ total Δ arshkon_min5 J3 +0.66 pp −0.19 pp arshkon_min5 S4 −1.28 pp −0.18 pp pooled_min5 J3 +0.50 pp −0.50 pp pooled_min5 S4 +0.59 pp +1.45 pp

Management (V1 rebuilt) is effectively flat within same-co on both seniority levels. arshkon_min5 S4 is slightly negative within-co, pooled_min5 S4 slightly positive — the ~2.0 pp span is noise. Claim held provisional until T22.

Artifact: exploration/tables/T16/scope_same_co_senior_vs_junior.csv — 12 rows × 7 cols.

docs.title: 7. New market entrants vs returning cohort
docs.text:
Companies in 2026 with no 2024 match (new entrants) = 6,368; returning = 2,109; 2024-only = 4,395.

Cohort n companies n 2026 postings J3 share (labeled) S4 share (labeled) AI-strict Scope terms Desc len Tech count Breadth resid Credstack Aggregator share returning 2,109 24,927 15.3% 62.4% 9.3% 1.16 5,121 7.43 +0.25 4.29 14.3% new_entrant 6,368 20,390 12.8% 64.2% 12.5% 1.29 4,615 7.51 +0.61 4.16 20.3%

Surprise. Gate 1 posited new entrants drive the junior-share rise. This is false on 2026 snapshot: new entrants have lower J3 share (12.8% vs 15.3%) and higher AI-strict (12.5% vs 9.3%). Gate 2 pre-commit aligns: the +5 pp between-firm J3 movement on arshkon-min5 is driven not by new entrants being MORE junior-heavy than returning, but by the exit of 4,395 2024-only companies that had lower-than-average junior share, leaving a remaining 2026 mix that is junior-heavier than the arshkon 2024 mix. Between-co components report is correct; the interpretation changes from "new entrants bring juniors" to "firms that exited the 2024 panel were lower-J3 than firms that stayed."

docs.title: 7.1 Industry profile of new entrants
docs.text:
Top 2026-only industries (raw labels; taxonomy drift caveats from Gate 1 apply):

Industry n new-entrant companies Software Development 1,555 IT Services and IT Consulting 451 Technology, Information and Internet 339 Financial Services 228 Staffing and Recruiting 142 Hospitals and Health Care 142 Technology, Information and Media 106 Internet Publishing 95 Defense and Space Manufacturing 94 Business Consulting and Services 85

Financial services + defense + health-care are overrepresented vs the 2024 panel. Consistent with T11's top-1% AI-saturated outliers (JPM/Citi/GEICO/Visa/Wells/Solera) being finance-heavy.

Artifact: exploration/tables/T16/new_entrants_vs_returning.csv, new_entrant_industries.csv.

docs.title: 8. Aggregator vs direct employer dynamics (pooled_min5)
docs.text:
Cohort Era n postings n companies J3 share AI-strict Scope terms Desc len Tech count Breadth resid aggregator 2024 1,694 54 5.4% 1.8% 0.34 3,530 7.36 +1.28 aggregator 2026 2,807 119 10.9% 9.0% 1.00 4,069 7.21 −0.08 direct 2024 6,370 332 10.0% 0.9% 0.61 4,257 4.97 −1.41 direct 2026 12,936 322 17.9% 8.2% 1.12 5,552 7.38 +0.03

Aggregator J3 share roughly doubled; direct-employer J3 share rose 7.9 pp. AI prevalence rose ~7× for aggregators and ~9× for direct employers (from lower base). Aggregators' breadth residual actually DROPPED (+1.28 → −0.08) — their JDs became less distinctive-per-length than the average.

Aggregator-exclusion sensitivity (pooled_min5, 322 non-aggregator companies). Within-co components change <20% on every metric:

Metric Pooled_min5 within-co No-aggregator within-co entry_share_j3 +4.47 pp +4.83 pp ai_strict +7.65 pp +6.92 pp scope_term_count +0.47 +0.45 breadth_resid +1.09 +1.12 desc_length +1,010 chars +898 chars

Conclusion: aggregators slightly amplify the AI-strict within-co signal but the direction and magnitude hold without them. Aggregator exclusion is not material.

Artifacts: aggregator_vs_direct.csv, sensitivity_aggregator_exclusion.csv.

docs.title: 9. Sensitivities summary
docs.text:
Sensitivity Affected claim Direction Magnitude change (a) aggregator exclusion All within-co metrics pooled_min5 same <20% (b) company capping Not re-run — aggregate SWE numbers already company-weighted by panel design — — © T30 J1/J2 panel J3 within-co direction same on pooled, mixed on arshkon J1 arshkon_min5 within −0.38 pp (below MDE); J2 within +0.15 pp T09 domain stratification Within-firm effects same — within-domain dominant Between-domain 15–30% of within-domain for most metrics arshkon-only vs pooled J3 within-co flips sign −0.12 pp → +4.47 pp

arshkon-only vs pooled is the materially sensitive dimension. Both are reported as primaries per Gate 2 Tension A resolution.

docs.title: 10. Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. New entrants are less junior-heavy than the returning cohort (J3 12.8% vs 15.3%), reversing the Gate 1 narrative. The +5 pp between-firm J3 movement on arshkon panels is driven by exit, not entry. This reframes the RQ1-revised composition story: it's not "new firms arriving as entry specialists," it's "firms with lower-than-typical J3 share dropped out of the 2026 LinkedIn panel." Whether this is real exit (firms stopped hiring SWEs) or scraping-coverage change is Wave 3.5 T37's job.
  2. AI-forward-scope-inflator cluster (n=16) is 38% aggregators (VDart, ApTask, Talener, Intellectt, SSG, Sev1Tech). The extreme JD-broadening cluster is partly a staffing-firm writing-style artifact, not pure demand-side restructuring. Three of the 16 are defense/health-care enterprises (Bristol Myers Squibb, Honeywell, CAE) — NOT big tech, NOT consumer internet.
  3. Within-firm AI magnitude (+7.7 to +8.3 pp) is 4× smaller than T06's +31 pp. V1-validated ai_strict gives cleaner but more conservative numbers. The direction ("AI is within-firm") holds on every panel, but the absolute magnitude claim of "+31 pp within-firm" from Gate 1 was an artifact of the pre-V1 broader pattern. Gate 3 synthesis should cite the +7.7–8.3 pp range.
  4. Management language (V1 rebuilt) is flat within-firm. Even on the sample where T11 reported a −0.02 to −0.04 decline, the rebuilt pattern shows essentially zero within-firm movement on both J3 and S4. T22 validation is the gate, but the "senior management → orchestration" narrative has no within-firm trace on the rebuilt pattern.
  5. Scope inflator cluster has slight J3 DECLINE (−2.9 pp). The firms broadening their JDs are simultaneously narrowing junior posting rates. The "scope inflation = junior bar raised" reading collapses: scope is inflating at the SENIOR level, and the firms doing it are actually posting FEWER J3 roles. Cluster evidence is another nail in the "junior-specific scope inflation" framing.
  6. Mixed_change cluster (n=41) has +40.8 pp J3 jump. Tech-giant intern/new-grad cycle companies. Amazon, AWS, TikTok, JPMorgan Chase, Walmart, and the returning Google all return to 2026 with heavy new-grad hiring. This is not universal market reshaping — it's large-employer campus cycle timing.
  7. Direct employers' 2024 breadth resid was −1.41 (deeply below average) → +0.03 in 2026. The direct-employer 2024 baseline was much narrower than mean; the rise is a "catch-up to aggregator verbosity" phenomenon. Interpretive caveat: length growth in direct employers is partially a convergence on a staffing-firm writing style.

docs.title: To Wave 3.5 T31 (same-co × same-title drift, H_M)
docs.text: Primary panel: pooled_min5 (n=356). company_change_vectors.csv has per-company deltas needed for pair-level drift analyses. same_co_senior_vs_junior.csv is the seniority-slice baseline. Directional expectation: within-co title-controlled entry share Δ should remain non-zero on pooled (Gate 2 Tension A), decay toward zero on arshkon_min5 (V1 finding).

docs.title: To Wave 3.5 T37 (sampling-frame returning-cohort sensitivity, H_H)
docs.text: Overlap panel + new-entrants analysis is the primary input. Key question: does restricting to arshkon_min5 vs pooled_min5 flip any Wave 2 headlines? T16 shows it flips J3 within-co sign (0 → +4.5 pp), leaves AI within-co direction intact (+8.3 → +7.7), and preserves scope/breadth within-co magnitudes. T37 must decide which is the analysis-plan-registered primary.

docs.title: To Wave 3.5 T38 (hiring-selectivity × scope correlation, H_N)
docs.text: company_change_vectors.csv provides the scope delta (breadth_resid_delta, org_scope_delta) and the volume proxy (2026/2024 post-count ratio derivable from overlap_panel). The scope_inflator cluster (n=81) is the "elevated selectivity candidates"; mixed_change (n=41) is the "volume-up with entry focus" candidates.

docs.title: To Gate 3 synthesis
docs.text:
• Gate 2 Tension A reproduced cleanly. Use pooled_min5 as primary with arshkon_min5 as co-primary (V1 recommendation).
  • Senior-scope-inflation-greater-than-junior claim verified on same-co restriction.
  • Scope inflator cluster composition is not junior-specialist-dominated.
  • Within-firm AI magnitude: report +7.7 to +8.3 pp not +31 pp.
  • New-entrants interpretation is exit-driven, not entry-driven — reframe composition story.

docs.title: T17 — Geographic Market Structure
docs.text:
Agent J · Wave 3 · 2026-04-20

Scope: SWE, LinkedIn-only, default filter. 68,137 SWE LinkedIn rows. Multi-location collapse (n=4,354) and null-metro-non-multi (n=14,184) excluded from metro rollups as specified. Total null-metro rows: 18,538 (27% of SWE frame).

AI-strict = V1-validated. Scope = V1-validated. J3 = llm_classification_coverage='labeled' AND yoe_min_years_llm ≤ 2.

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
1. 26 metros have ≥50 SWE in both eras. Tech-hub vs non-tech-hub (5 vs 21) shows modest hub premium on J3 rise (+6.6 pp vs +4.0 pp) and AI-strict rise (+9.9 pp vs +8.6 pp) — both directions hub > non-hub, but gaps <2 pp.
  2. AI-strict metro deltas are highly above-noise (median SNR ≈ 10, max >250 for Chicago). Cross-period AI rise is not a tech-hub phenomenon — it's universal and uniform across metros (SD across metros = 3.1 pp; range 4.7–14.5 pp).
  3. J3 metro deltas are near-noise for ~half the panel. Within-2024 arshkon-vs-asaniczka median metro J3 abs-diff = 4.8 pp. Cross-period J3 rises above this bar: SF (+4.9), Seattle (+13.6), LA (+10.9), Portland (+11.6), Philadelphia (+11.5), San Diego (+14.9), Phoenix (+7.9), Nashville (+8.6). Below-noise or flat: Austin (+0.7), Atlanta (+0.4), Chicago (+1.3), Dallas (−0.3), Houston (−1.4), Charlotte (−2.7), Detroit (−5.1).
  4. Metro-level correlation AI Δ × J3 Δ is mildly NEGATIVE (Pearson −0.22, Spearman −0.15, n=26). Metros with bigger AI surges do NOT have bigger junior-share surges. The two phenomena are largely independent geographically — they don't co-concentrate.
  5. Metro-level correlation AI Δ × scope Δ is mildly positive (Pearson +0.29, Spearman +0.27). Weak coupling — AI rise and scope broadening partially co-move at metro level, but not tightly.
  6. Remote is a 2026 category, not a metro feature. 19.9% of 2026 scraped postings are is_remote=true with NULL metro_area (3,983 2026-03 + 5,024 2026-04). 2024 remote is ~0% (Gate 1 data artifact). Remote postings are AI-richer (11.1% vs 10.5% metro) and slightly less junior (13.1% vs 14.2% metro J3).
  7. Multi-location (n=2,373 2026) has the HIGHEST AI-strict (13.7%), HIGHEST J3 share (17.7%), HIGHEST scope terms (1.51) — multi-location postings are the "aggregator of aggregators" with distinct content profile. Since they're excluded from metro rollups by design, this is a side-channel finding.
  8. ML/LLM archetype geographic concentration rose in tech hubs (+8.8 pp Seattle, +4.4 pp SF — though SF already had 1.8% baseline) but also in unexpected places: Minneapolis +13.0 pp, Tampa Bay +11.4 pp, Portland +9.1 pp, Raleigh-Durham +8.6 pp. ML/LLM is spreading, not concentrating in hubs.
  9. Archetype HHI rose in most metros (archetype mix concentrated). Top HHI risers: Philadelphia (+0.164), Chicago (+0.153), Austin (+0.084), Minneapolis (+0.080), LA (+0.072). Suggests metro-level technology specialization. Seattle/SF HHI roughly flat.
  10. Aggregator-exclusion sensitivity: metro-level AI and J3 deltas are robust (<2 pp median shift). Company-capping at 20 postings per metro × era also robust. Findings are not driven by single large employers.

docs.title: 1. Metro-level metrics (26 metros, ≥50 SWE per era)
docs.text:
Metro n_2024 n_2026 J3 share Δ AI-strict Δ Scope-terms Δ Desc-len(med) Δ Tech-count(med) Δ San Francisco Bay Area 1,730 5,483 +4.9 pp +11.2 pp +0.60 +950 +1 Washington DC Metro 2,108 3,389 +4.2 pp +6.0 pp +0.41 +1,057 +3 Seattle Metro 762 2,428 +13.6 pp +6.9 pp +0.57 +1,472 +3 Dallas-Fort Worth Metro 852 2,315 −0.3 pp +11.0 pp +0.43 +928 +2 New York City Metro 67 2,220 +7.5 pp +11.6 pp +1.00 +884 +3 Salt Lake City Metro 329 1,898 +4.1 pp +12.4 pp +0.72 −100 +3 Los Angeles Metro 1,008 1,818 +10.9 pp +6.6 pp +0.47 +960 +1 Boston Metro 859 1,736 +6.4 pp +10.4 pp +0.55 +931 +2 Austin Metro 358 1,269 +0.7 pp +9.5 pp +0.60 +945 +2 Charlotte Metro 305 1,231 −2.7 pp +9.6 pp +0.74 +893 +3 Atlanta Metro 585 1,189 +0.4 pp +14.1 pp +0.69 +1,021 +3 Chicago Metro 728 1,157 +1.3 pp +9.3 pp +0.82 +637 +1 Philadelphia Metro 497 929 +11.5 pp +8.1 pp +0.45 +1,422 +1 Denver Metro 557 814 +3.8 pp +5.7 pp +0.47 +440 +2 Phoenix Metro 353 803 +7.9 pp +7.3 pp +0.49 +1,478 +3 Detroit Metro 360 723 −5.1 pp +5.3 pp +0.67 +1,326 +4 Minneapolis-Saint Paul 260 622 +4.6 pp +9.9 pp +0.36 +1,320 +1 Raleigh-Durham-Chapel Hill 243 617 +1.2 pp +11.5 pp +0.53 +140 +1 Houston Metro 233 589 −1.4 pp +6.1 pp +0.73 +1,043 +3 San Diego Metro 268 535 +14.9 pp +6.0 pp +0.58 +1,403 +1 Pittsburgh Metro 206 521 +1.7 pp +4.7 pp +0.58 +377 +1 Columbus Metro 140 557 +2.5 pp +5.4 pp +0.53 +940 +2 Miami Metro 218 369 +4.0 pp +12.9 pp +0.58 +313 +1 Tampa Bay Metro 214 317 +0.6 pp +14.5 pp +0.60 +834 +3 Nashville Metro 102 241 +8.6 pp +7.5 pp +0.47 +1,264 +2 Portland Metro 116 167 +11.6 pp +6.9 pp +0.55 +1,321 0
