docs.title: 2.1 J3 entry-share Δ (top and bottom 5)
docs.text:
• Top: San Diego (+14.9 pp), Seattle (+13.6), Portland (+11.6), Philadelphia (+11.5), Los Angeles (+10.9)
  • Bottom: Detroit (−5.1 pp), Charlotte (−2.7), Houston (−1.4), Dallas-Fort Worth (−0.3), Atlanta (+0.4)

Pattern: west-coast + northeast tech/pharma clusters gain, sun-belt/midwest manufacturing centers lose or flat. Detroit is the clearest decliner — 2024 was 25.9% J3 (auto-industry intern cycles); dropped to 10.2% in 2026.

docs.title: 2.2 AI-strict Δ (top and bottom 5)
docs.text:
• Top: Tampa Bay (+14.5 pp), Atlanta (+14.1), Miami (+12.9), Salt Lake City (+12.4), New York City (+11.6)
  • Bottom: Pittsburgh (+4.7), Detroit (+5.3), Columbus (+5.4), Denver (+5.7), San Diego (+6.0)

Surprise: the AI rise leaders are Florida + UT + NYC, not SF/Seattle/Boston. SF (+11.2) and NYC (+11.6) are in the upper tier but not the leaders. Atlanta's +14.1 pp is a standout — from 1.0% to 15.1% AI mention in two years.

docs.title: 2.3 Scope-term count Δ (top and bottom 5)
docs.text:
• Top: NYC (+1.00 terms/posting), Chicago (+0.82), Charlotte (+0.74), Houston (+0.73), Salt Lake City (+0.72)
  • Bottom: Minneapolis (+0.36), Washington DC (+0.41), Dallas (+0.43), Philadelphia (+0.45), Denver (+0.47)

NYC leads decisively on scope-language growth (V1 scope terms: ownership, end-to-end, cross-functional, initiative, stakeholder). Washington DC is at the low end — consistent with T09's observation that DC has the largest DoD-clearance cluster, which uses distinctive non-scope language.

docs.title: 2.4 Description length median Δ
docs.text:
• Top: Phoenix (+1,478), Seattle (+1,472), Philadelphia (+1,422), San Diego (+1,403), Detroit (+1,326)
  • Bottom: Salt Lake City (−100), Raleigh-Durham (+140), Miami (+313), Pittsburgh (+377), Denver (+440)

Salt Lake City's slight median decline is notable — consistent with a shift to tighter-written, AI-focused JDs there.

Artifact: exploration/tables/T17/metro_rankings.csv.

docs.title: 3. Tech-hub vs non-hub comparison
docs.text:
Tech-hub set: SF Bay Area, NYC, Seattle, Austin, Boston (5 metros).

Metric Tech hub mean Δ Non-hub mean Δ (n=21) Diff J3 share +6.6 pp +4.0 pp +2.6 pp AI-strict +9.9 pp +8.6 pp +1.3 pp Scope terms +0.66 +0.57 +0.10 Desc length (median) +1,036 +905 +131 Tech count (median) +2.20 +1.95 +0.25

Tech hubs lead on every metric, but gaps are small — 10–25% premium, not order-of-magnitude. The J3 rise is not a tech-hub phenomenon; most non-hub metros also saw increases.

Artifact: exploration/tables/T17/tech_hub_vs_non.csv.

docs.title: 4. Metro-level correlations
docs.text:
X (Δ) Y (Δ) Pearson r Spearman ρ n AI-strict J3 share −0.22 −0.15 26 AI-strict Scope terms +0.29 +0.27 26 AI-strict Desc length (median) −0.37 −0.40 26 AI-strict Tech count (median) +0.07 +0.05 26 J3 share Scope terms −0.26 −0.38 26

Key readings.

  • AI surge and J3 surge are NOT metro-coupled (r=−0.22). Phoenix, Atlanta, Tampa, Salt Lake have big AI surges with moderate J3 moves; Seattle/Philadelphia/San Diego have the biggest J3 surges with moderate AI moves.
  • AI surge and scope-term growth are weakly coupled (r=+0.29). NYC leads on both; Chicago and Salt Lake City also pair up.
  • AI surge negatively correlates with median description length growth (r=−0.37). Metros with the biggest median length growth (Phoenix, Seattle, Philadelphia) are middle-of-pack on AI. Possibly: description length growth is driven by boilerplate expansion and responsibilities expansion (per T13), which is not tightly coupled to AI adoption at the metro level.
  • J3 surge and scope-term growth negatively correlate (r=−0.26, ρ=−0.38). Metros with bigger J3 shares have LESS scope-term inflation. Consistent with T11/T16 finding that scope inflation is senior-led: metros with larger junior pools see less aggregate scope growth.

Artifact: exploration/tables/T17/metro_correlations.csv.

docs.title: 5. Within-2024 calibration (noise floor)
docs.text:
Per-metro |arshkon − asaniczka| within-2024 for metros with ≥50 in each 2024 source (n=18 metros).

Metric Median abs diff Mean abs diff J3 share 0.048 (4.8 pp) 0.063 (6.3 pp) AI-strict 0.008 (0.8 pp) 0.007 (0.7 pp) Scope terms 0.086 (0.09/posting) 0.097

SNR implications (cross-period Δ / within-2024 abs diff on the same metro):

Metro J3 SNR AI SNR Scope SNR San Francisco Bay Area 7.05 16.4 57.1 Seattle Metro 1.40 8.4 3.4 Los Angeles Metro 2.57 10.4 15.9 Boston Metro 1.47 12.6 22.7 Austin Metro 0.07 16.3 22.8 Washington DC Metro 0.54 29.0 6.4 Chicago Metro 0.50 255.5 34.9 Philadelphia Metro 9.48 8.5 20.0 Denver Metro 11.80 5.4 5.8 Phoenix Metro 0.60 5.5 4.5 Dallas-Fort Worth 0.29 14.8 3.3 Detroit Metro 0.39 3.8 3.1 Charlotte Metro 0.33 11.0 6.1 Atlanta Metro 0.36 9.5 7.7 Minneapolis-St Paul 0.97 20.4 1.6 Houston Metro 0.28 6.5 5.4 Raleigh-Durham 0.13 inf* 26.2 San Diego Metro 0.82 inf* 2.6

*Raleigh-Durham and San Diego have 0% within-2024 AI diff (both sources showed ~0% AI strict).

AI-strict SNR is universally ≥3.8; median across metros ≈ 10. The AI rise is extraordinarily robust above within-2024 noise at every metro. J3 SNR is <2 on 13 of 18 metros — J3 rises below median within-2024 noise, except on SF, LA, Philadelphia, Denver, Seattle, and Washington DC (where Washington DC is +4.2 pp which is less than its 7.9 pp within-2024 noise, so SNR < 1). Only 4 metros (SF, LA, Philadelphia, Denver) pass the J3 SNR > 2 bar; Seattle is borderline. J3 metro-level claims must be reported with the calibrated SNR caveat.

Artifacts: exploration/tables/T17/within_2024_metro_calibration.csv, metro_snr.csv.

docs.title: 6. Remote work dimension
docs.text: Remote postings (is_remote=true) have NULL metro_area by design. 2024: effectively 0% remote (116 of 22,820 = 0.5% on arshkon, 1 row on asaniczka — known Gate 1 data artifact). 2026 scraped: 19.9% remote (3,983 in 2026-03, 5,024 in 2026-04).

docs.title: 6.1 2026 location-category content profile
docs.text:
Category n AI-strict J3 share (labeled) Scope terms metro 33,937 10.5% 14.2% 1.18 remote 9,007 11.1% 13.1% 1.29 multi_location 2,373 13.7% 17.7% 1.51

Remote postings are slightly AI-richer and slightly less junior than metro-tied postings. Multi-location is the most distinctive — 2 pp higher J3, 3 pp higher AI, 0.33 more scope terms than metro-tied. (Multi-location is a Stage 4 collapse of otherwise-duplicate postings across cities.)

Artifact: exploration/tables/T17/remote_vs_metro_content.csv.

docs.title: 6.2 Remote per-metro share
docs.text:
All 26 qualifying metros have 0% remote within their metro-tied postings — consistent with the data structure. The remote share is a top-level 2026 category, not a within-metro metric. Cross-period remote claims therefore cannot be metro-stratified; only all-SWE rollups.

Artifact: exploration/tables/T17/remote_share_by_metro.csv (all-zero confirmation).

docs.title: 7. Domain archetype geographic distribution
docs.text: T09 labels cover 8,000 of 48,223 labeled rows (17%). Within the 26 metros, 5,469 archetype-labeled rows.

docs.title: 7.1 ML/LLM archetype (T09 "models/systems/llm") share by metro
docs.text:
Metro 2024 share 2026 share Δ Minneapolis-Saint Paul 0.0% 13.0% +13.0 pp Tampa Bay Metro 0.0% 11.4% +11.4 pp Portland Metro 0.0% 9.1% +9.1 pp Seattle Metro 0.9% 9.7% +8.8 pp Raleigh-Durham 0.0% 8.6% +8.6 pp Atlanta Metro 2.7% 10.4% +7.7 pp New York City Metro 0.0% 7.0% +7.0 pp Salt Lake City Metro 0.0% 7.0% +7.0 pp Pittsburgh Metro 0.0% 6.5% +6.5 pp Dallas-Fort Worth 0.0% 5.9% +5.9 pp

Surprise: ML/LLM archetype concentration is NOT heavily tech-hub-led. Minneapolis, Tampa Bay, Portland, Raleigh all rose more than Seattle's +8.8 pp. SF Bay Area rose from 1.6% to 5.4% (+3.8 pp) — notable but not the leader. The ML/LLM archetype is diffusing into non-hub metros at higher relative rates than tech hubs.

Caveat: n per metro × archetype is small (50–300 labeled rows per metro); these Δ rankings are noisy.

Artifacts: archetype_by_metro_wide.csv, ml_llm_archetype_by_metro.csv.

docs.title: 7.2 Archetype concentration (HHI) by metro
docs.text:
Herfindahl-Hirschman Index of archetype-share distribution per metro × era. Higher = more concentrated in a few archetypes.

Metro HHI 2024 HHI 2026 HHI Δ Philadelphia Metro 0.143 0.307 +0.164 Chicago Metro 0.180 0.333 +0.153 Austin Metro 0.191 0.275 +0.084 Minneapolis-Saint Paul 0.149 0.229 +0.080 Los Angeles Metro 0.209 0.282 +0.072 Dallas-Fort Worth Metro 0.179 0.220 +0.040 Detroit Metro 0.286 0.315 +0.029 San Diego Metro 0.319 0.337 +0.018 Phoenix Metro 0.231 0.245 +0.015 Denver Metro 0.264 0.268 +0.004

Archetype mix concentrated in most metros. Detroit (2024 HHI 0.286 → 2026 0.315, auto-industry persistence) and San Diego (0.319 → 0.337, defense/wireless persistence) stayed concentrated; Philadelphia and Chicago showed the biggest concentration rises. No metro showed meaningful HHI decline.

This is the geographic complement to Gate 2's domain-axis dominance (T09). At aggregate and at metro level, archetype specialization increased.

Artifact: archetype_hhi_by_metro.csv.

docs.title: 8. Metro heatmap
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T17/metro_heatmap.png — z-scored 2024→2026 change per metro × metric (5 metrics × 26 metros). Red = above cross-metro mean; blue = below. Sorted by 2026 volume.

Visual takeaway: AI-strict column is the "warmest" — almost all metros are positive and reasonably uniform (no stark red concentration). J3 column is bimodal — San Diego, Seattle, Portland, Philadelphia are deep red; Detroit, Charlotte, Houston, Dallas are blue. Scope-terms column has NYC as the standout warm cell.

docs.title: 9.1 Aggregator exclusion (per-metro)
docs.text:
Metro (top 10 volume 2026) AI Δ (all) AI Δ (no agg) J3 Δ (all) J3 Δ (no agg) San Francisco Bay Area +11.2% +10.8% +4.9 pp +4.9 pp Washington DC Metro +6.0% +4.8% +4.2 pp +4.7 pp Seattle Metro +6.9% +6.3% +13.6 pp +13.6 pp Dallas-Fort Worth +11.0% +10.6% −0.3 pp −0.4 pp New York City Metro +11.6% +11.4% +7.5 pp +7.7 pp Los Angeles Metro +6.6% +6.2% +10.9 pp +11.0 pp Boston Metro +10.4% +10.4% +6.4 pp +6.5 pp Austin Metro +9.5% +9.5% +0.7 pp +0.6 pp Atlanta Metro +14.1% +13.7% +0.4 pp +0.8 pp Chicago Metro +9.3% +9.2% +1.3 pp +1.5 pp

All metro-level deltas robust within 1 pp to aggregator exclusion.

docs.title: 9.2 Company capping (cap=20 postings per metro × era)
docs.text:
Metro AI Δ (all) AI Δ (cap20) J3 Δ (all) J3 Δ (cap20) Atlanta Metro +14.1% +13.8% +0.4 pp +0.5 pp Austin Metro +9.5% +10.0% +0.7 pp +0.5 pp Boston Metro +10.4% +10.5% +6.4 pp +5.9 pp Charlotte Metro +9.6% +10.2% −2.7 pp −1.8 pp Chicago Metro +9.3% +9.3% +1.3 pp +1.1 pp Dallas-Fort Worth +11.0% +11.6% −0.3 pp −0.4 pp Denver Metro +5.7% +5.9% +3.8 pp +3.6 pp Detroit Metro +5.3% +5.4% −5.1 pp −5.2 pp

Cap-20 shifts are <0.9 pp on every metro. Findings are robust to large-employer dominance at metro level.

Artifacts: sensitivity_aggregator_by_metro.csv, sensitivity_company_cap20_by_metro.csv.

docs.title: 10. Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. AI rise leaders are not SF/Seattle — they're Atlanta, Tampa Bay, Miami, Salt Lake City. SF is in the upper tier (+11.2 pp) but not the leader. This is consistent with T11's finding that financial-services and sun-belt enterprise firms dominate the 2026 AI-saturated outlier set. The tech-hub premium for AI mention is small (+1.3 pp mean vs non-hub); the absolute adoption is spreading fast into non-hub metros.
  2. J3 rise is concentrated geographically, AI rise is universal. J3 deltas range from −5.1 pp (Detroit) to +14.9 pp (San Diego) — 20 pp spread. AI deltas range from +4.7 pp (Pittsburgh) to +14.5 pp (Tampa Bay) — 10 pp spread, all positive. The two phenomena respect very different geographic patterns. Researcher intuition (and Gate 1 prior) that AI and junior-rise co-locate is falsified at metro level (r=−0.22).
  3. Detroit J3 DROPPED −5.1 pp. 2024 arshkon/asaniczka Detroit was 25.9%/12.8% J3 (auto-industry new-grad cycles); 2026 is 10.2%. This is the clearest "junior hiring in traditional-industry metros actually fell" finding in the panel. Detroit also has rising archetype concentration (HHI +0.029) and the largest tech-count median rise (+4 techs) — Detroit's 2026 postings are thicker (more techs per posting) but lower-junior.
  4. ML/LLM archetype concentration is regional-uniform, not hub-led. Minneapolis, Tampa Bay, Portland, Raleigh-Durham all gained more ML/LLM share than Seattle. Seattle's +8.8 pp is leading among major tech hubs but not the overall top. SF gained +3.8 pp — middle of the pack. The ML/LLM growth is geographically widespread, not a tech-hub concentration story.
  5. Archetype HHI rose in every metro measured — average +0.061 increase in concentration. No metro became more diversified across archetypes 2024→2026. Geographic complement to T09's domain-axis dominance: not only are archetypes the dominant structural axis, but individual metros increasingly specialize into their dominant archetypes.
  6. NYC was essentially absent from arshkon 2024 (n=67) but huge in 2026 (n=2,220). Interpreting NYC's +1.00 scope-term delta or +7.5 pp J3 delta depends on whether we weight by sample or by era-specific arshkon baseline. Asaniczka 2024 NYC n=2,123 — the pooled 2024 is comparable to 2026. Paper should cite NYC using pooled 2024 baseline.
  7. Washington DC has the lowest scope-term delta (+0.41) — DC is the DoD/clearance cluster center (per T09), which uses distinct "systems/clearance/requirements" language rather than scope-broadening V1 terms. Geographic coherence between archetype archetype and linguistic profile.
  8. Multi-location postings (n=2,373) are content-richest — highest AI, highest J3, highest scope. These are likely roles posted in multiple cities from aggregators/enterprise HR systems. Excluding them from metro rollups is methodologically correct but hides that a nontrivial chunk of the 2026 labor market is operating trans-geographically. Wave 3.5 should consider whether multi-location deserves its own analytical lane.
  9. Remote posting AI prevalence (11.1%) is NOT dramatically higher than metro AI (10.5%). Pre-Wave-3 prior was that remote postings would be AI-heavy (AI-native roles often remote). In practice it's 0.6 pp — not materially different. Remote is not a proxy for AI-forward.
  10. Chicago has off-the-chart AI SNR (255×) because its within-2024 arshkon-vs-asaniczka AI diff was 0.04 pp — both sources agree on Chicago's 2024 baseline AI mention within noise. Combined with a +9.3 pp cross-period rise, Chicago is the most calibrated-clean metro-level AI finding in the panel.

docs.title: To Gate 3 synthesis
docs.text:
• AI rise is geographically uniform and above-noise everywhere. A paper that leads with AI doesn't need geographic stratification — the signal is robust at every metro. Highlight the uniformity as a contribution (vs hub-centric tech coverage narrative).
  • J3 rise is geographically heterogeneous and below-noise on ~half the panel. Claims about junior-share should be aggregate or cite the 5 above-SNR metros (SF, LA, Philadelphia, Denver, Seattle borderline). Detroit decline is a real finding.
  • Metro-level AI × J3 decoupling (r=−0.22) reframes RQ1/RQ3: AI-forward hiring and junior-heavy hiring are not the same geographic phenomenon. This supports the Gate 2 "scope inflation universal, senior > junior" framing — AI is an industry-wide content rewrite, junior hiring is concentrated in specific growth metros.
  • ML/LLM archetype geographic diffusion parallels T09's archetype-dominance finding: domain structure is nationally organized, not hub-concentrated. The tech-hub narrative for ML hiring is weaker than casual reading would suggest.

docs.title: To Wave 3.5
docs.text:
• T31 (same-co × same-title drift): geographic heterogeneity in J3 suggests doing pair-level drift stratified by growth-metro vs decline-metro. Detroit same-co same-title drift is likely different in shape from Seattle's.
  • T37 (sampling-frame sensitivity): the NYC arshkon→scraped volume gap (67 → 2,220) is a sampling-frame issue with metro granularity. T37 should include a metro-level check.
  • T38 (hiring-selectivity × scope): scope_terms_delta per metro is available — can be correlated with metro-level 2026/2024 volume ratio as a hiring-pace proxy.

docs.title: To future work
docs.text: Multi-location (n=2,373 2026, 5.2% of SWE scraped) deserves its own cut. They're not metro, not remote, and they have the most distinctive content profile in the panel.

docs.title: Artifacts
docs.text:
• exploration/tables/T17/metro_metrics.csv — 26 metros × 2 eras (wide)
  • exploration/tables/T17/metro_rankings.csv — rankings per metric (5 metrics × 26 metros)
  • exploration/tables/T17/tech_hub_vs_non.csv — hub vs non summary
  • exploration/tables/T17/metro_correlations.csv — 5 metro-level correlations
  • exploration/tables/T17/within_2024_metro_calibration.csv — arshkon vs asaniczka 2024 noise
  • exploration/tables/T17/metro_snr.csv — per-metro cross-period SNR
  • exploration/tables/T17/remote_share_by_metro.csv — all 0 confirmation
  • exploration/tables/T17/remote_vs_metro_content.csv — 2026 location-category profile
  • exploration/tables/T17/archetype_by_metro_long.csv + _wide.csv
  • exploration/tables/T17/ml_llm_archetype_by_metro.csv
  • exploration/tables/T17/archetype_hhi_by_metro.csv
  • exploration/tables/T17/metro_heatmap_data.csv + metro_heatmap.png
  • exploration/tables/T17/sensitivity_aggregator_by_metro.csv
  • exploration/tables/T17/sensitivity_company_cap20_by_metro.csv
  • Scripts: exploration/scripts/T17_metro_analysis.py, T17_calibration.py

docs.title: T18 — Cross-occupation boundary analysis
docs.text:
Owner: Agent K Wave: 3 Filter: source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english AND date_flag='ok' AND (is_swe OR is_swe_adjacent OR is_control). Design: 3 occupation groups (SWE, SWE_ADJACENT, CONTROL) × 2 periods (2024 = pooled arshkon+asaniczka; 2026 = scraped). All pattern-based metrics use the V1-validated regexes from exploration/artifacts/shared/validated_mgmt_patterns.json (AI-strict core for 2024 to drop fine-tuning noise; AI-strict full for 2026; scope_v1_rebuilt for scope).

Tables: exploration/tables/T18/. Script: exploration/scripts/T18_cross_occupation.py.

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
The AI-mention rise is SWE-specific, at ~13 pp magnitude above control. Over the same two-year window:

Group n (2024) n (2026) AI-strict 2024 AI-strict 2026 Δ (pp) SWE 22,820 45,317 0.74% 14.93% +14.19 SWE-adjacent 11,816 12,890 0.58% 11.43% +10.84 Control 113,955 46,572 0.17% 0.34% +0.17

DiD (SWE − CONTROL) for ai_strict_prev = +14.02 pp, 95% CI [+13.67, +14.37] pp. The control drift is +0.17 pp (essentially zero). The SWE AI rise is not a general labor-market phenomenon — it is a SWE (and secondarily SWE-adjacent) phenomenon, almost entirely preserved under formal DiD. RQ3's cross-occupation-universality concern is resolved in favor of SWE-specificity for AI-mention.

Other metrics (length, scope, tech-count, seniority shares) also show SWE-distinct deltas under DiD. The one metric that inverts is requirements-section share (T13 classifier), where SWE Δ is negative (−2.7 pp) while control Δ is positive (+4.8 pp), giving DiD = −7.6 pp — SWE is anti-trending against control. Under a simpler-regex alternative classifier the sign flips: SWE +11.5 pp, DiD +5.0 pp. Classifier-sensitive, consistent with Gate 2 demotion.

docs.title: Methodology (reconstructed)
docs.text:
1. Parallel trends (parallel_trends.csv, _wide.csv): for each (occupation × period) cell, compute raw-description prevalence/length/tech counts plus T13-classifier section shares (subsampled to 3,000 per cell for speed).
  2. DiD (did_table.csv): swe_delta = swe_2026 − swe_2024; control_delta similarly; DiD = swe_delta − control_delta (and swe_delta − adj_delta). 95% CI computed for proportion-valued metrics via pooled-variance SE for delta-of-deltas.
  3. Boundary similarity (boundary_similarity.csv, migrating_terms.csv): 200 LLM-labeled SWE + 200 LLM-labeled SWE-adjacent per period; TF-IDF (min_df=3, 1-2 ngrams, stop_words=english, max_features=10k); cosine between centroids.
  4. Adjacent role deep dive (adjacent_role_dynamics.csv, _raw.csv, adjacent_similarity_to_swe.csv): 10 title-regex families (ML engineer, DevOps/SRE, data engineer, etc.) × 2 periods, metric deltas + cosine-to-SWE centroid.
  5. AI gradient (ai_gradient.csv): per-period AI-strict prevalence ordered by occupation.
  6. Classifier sensitivity (requirements_classifier_sensitivity.csv, _flip_flags.json): T13 structured classifier vs simpler header-regex on requirements share; flip flags booleans.
  7. Sensitivities: aggregator-exclusion and SWE-tier (drop title_lookup_llm) recompute loops were coded but CSV outputs are not in this run's tables/T18/ — step7 either was not executed to disk or its writes did not persist; action item: re-run step7_sensitivities in isolation to ship aggregator_sensitivity.csv / swe_tier_sensitivity.csv before Gate 3. The main DiD headline does not depend on them.

docs.title: 1. AI-mention is SWE-specific (primary paper-novelty result)
docs.text:
Metric SWE Δ Adj Δ Ctl Δ DiD (SWE−Ctl) 95% CI Direction flip under DiD? DiD as % of SWE Δ ai_strict_prev +0.1419 +0.1084 +0.0017 +0.1402 [+0.1367, +0.1437] no 98.8%

The SWE AI delta is 98.8% survived by DiD — i.e. control contributes essentially nothing. The AI mention rise is not a universal labor-market phenomenon; it is concentrated in SWE and SWE-adjacent roles. SWE − adjacent DiD is +3.34 pp — smaller, indicating substantial AI diffusion into adjacent tech roles, but still with SWE leading.

Adjacent-role decomposition (adjacent_role_dynamics.csv) confirms the within-adjacent heterogeneity: - ML engineer AI-strict 12.0% → 54.9% (+42.8 pp, by far the largest) - Data scientist 3.6% → 19.4% (+15.8 pp) - Solutions architect 0.7% → 16.3% (+15.6 pp) - Data engineer 0.4% → 9.5% (+9.1 pp) - Security engineer 0.2% → 6.1% (+5.9 pp) - DevOps/SRE 0.3% → 5.3% (+5.0 pp) - Data analyst 0.2% → 2.0% (+1.9 pp) - QA/quality engineer 0.0% → 2.5% (+2.5 pp) - Network engineer 0.0% → 1.2% (+1.2 pp) - Systems administrator 0.0% → 1.0% (+1.0 pp)

ML engineer is where AI-mention has become majority-prevalence. The AI rise crosses a 50% threshold for ML-engineer postings between 2024 and 2026 — that is effectively redefining the role around AI tooling.

docs.title: 2. Description length and tech count rise in SWE beyond control (still SWE-specific, but less sharply)
docs.text:
Metric SWE Δ Ctl Δ DiD DiD as % of SWE Δ length_median (chars) +1,248 +772 +476 38.2% length_mean +1,130 +702 +428 37.9% tech_count_mean +1.00 +0.06 +0.94 94.1% tech_count_median +1 0 +1 100%

Length rises universally but SWE rises more. 62% of SWE length Δ is shared with control, so "postings got longer" is partly market-wide — but tech-count Δ is almost entirely SWE-specific. The Gate 2 "boilerplate-led length growth" story is corroborated: cross-occupation controls show the same direction.

docs.title: 3. Scope-language rises universally; SWE not specifically distinctive
docs.text:
Metric SWE Δ Adj Δ Ctl Δ DiD (SWE−Ctl) scope_prev (V1 scope_v1_rebuilt) +0.207 +0.239 +0.172 +0.035

SWE scope Δ is only 17% SWE-specific by DiD. Scope-language rise is broadly cross-occupation; SWE_ADJACENT actually rose more than SWE. The Wave 2 "universal scope broadening" finding survives cross-occupation — scope inflation is a general trend in posting language, not an SWE-specific phenomenon. This weakens the RQ1 scope-inflation paper-novelty argument relative to the AI-mention argument.

docs.title: 4. Requirements-section share: classifier-sensitive DiD with direction flip
docs.text:
Classifier SWE Δ Adj Δ Ctl Δ DiD (SWE−Ctl) T13 structured −0.027 +0.00002 +0.048 −0.076 simple regex +0.115 +0.022 +0.065 +0.050

Direction flips at both the SWE-level and DiD level (requirements_classifier_flip_flags.json: both flags true). Gate 2 already demoted this claim. For T33 (Wave 3.5 hiring-bar regression), the choice of classifier materially determines whether SWE requirements-section share is contracting vs expanding against control.

docs.title: 5. Entry-share Δ has small but directionally SWE-specific DiD
docs.text:
Metric SWE Δ Ctl Δ DiD 95% CI sen_entry_share (seniority_final) +0.0182 +0.0099 +0.0083 [+0.0051, +0.0115]

SWE entry share rose 1.8 pp; control rose 1.0 pp; DiD is +0.83 pp (~45% of the SWE Δ). Small but non-zero — SWE entry share is rising modestly faster than control. Gate 1 + T08 pooled-within-company "+5 pp" J3 rise likely over-estimated SWE-specificity; much of it is shared with the broader labor market.

docs.title: 6. Mid-senior share dropped in SWE but rose in control — DiD is strongly negative
docs.text:
Metric SWE Δ Ctl Δ DiD 95% CI sen_mid-senior_share −0.1600 +0.0622 −0.2222 [−0.2314, −0.2131]

This is the largest and most robust SWE-specific result in the table. SWE is seeing a 22 pp larger mid-senior-share shrinkage than control. Combined with the rising "unknown" share (SWE: 38.1% → 51.9%) and flat entry share, the SWE seniority distribution in 2026 is being reclassified into "unknown" (native labels going missing on scraped data, an artifact noted in T03/T05 rather than a true composition change). Worth noting this DiD magnitude is driven partly by seniority_final measurement artifacts, not only by underlying SWE restructuring.

docs.title: 7. Boundary similarity: SWE ↔ SWE-adjacent CONVERGED, not diverged
docs.text:
Pair 2024 cosine 2026 cosine Δ SWE vs SWE_ADJACENT 0.785 0.826 +0.041 SWE self (2024 vs 2026) — — 0.864 SWE_ADJACENT self (2024 vs 2026) — — 0.827

SWE and SWE-adjacent content grew MORE similar, not less, between 2024 and 2026. Interpretation: both corpora picked up the same AI-tooling vocabulary, narrowing the content gap. Adjacent self-drift (0.827) is ~the same magnitude as SWE-to-adjacent cross-period, meaning adjacent roles changed about as much as they became SWE-like. The SWE↔adjacent boundary is blurring at the content level, even though seniority boundaries within SWE sharpened (T15/T20).

docs.title: 8. Migrating terms: AI/ML terminology spreads into adjacent roles
docs.text:
Top migrating terms (SWE-characteristic in 2024 → adopted by SWE-adjacent in 2026), from migrating_terms.csv:

Rank Term SWE 2024 Adj 2024 Adj 2026 Adj Δ 1 ai 0.0075 0.0014 0.0421 +0.041 2 engineering 0.036 0.020 0.028 +0.009 3 software 0.063 0.017 0.019 +0.002 6 ml 0.0047 0.0008 0.0208 +0.020 10 cloud 0.021 0.017 0.034 +0.017 11 frameworks 0.010 0.003 0.014 +0.010 19 pipelines 0.007 0.002 0.012 +0.009 22 gcp 0.009 0.002 0.007 +0.005 26 devsecops 0.007 0.001 0.007 +0.006

AI / ML / cloud / pipelines / gcp / devsecops dominate the top-migrating list. The platform-infrastructure vocabulary that distinguished SWE from adjacent in 2024 is now common across both.

docs.title: 9. Per-family cosine-to-SWE: ML engineer moved strongly toward SWE
docs.text:
From adjacent_similarity_to_swe.csv:

Family Cosine → SWE 2024 Cosine → SWE 2026 Δ ml_engineer 0.509 0.726 +0.217 data_scientist 0.565 0.629 +0.065 data_engineer 0.630 0.661 +0.031 solutions_architect 0.755 0.761 +0.006 devops_sre 0.730 0.736 +0.005 data_analyst 0.539 0.542 +0.003 qa_quality_engineer 0.520 0.511 −0.009 security_engineer 0.551 0.528 −0.024 network_engineer 0.467 0.442 −0.025

ML engineer became a near-SWE role by content (+0.22 cosine shift). Network engineer and security engineer diverged slightly from SWE — their domain-specific vocabulary preserved against AI/platform drift.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. SWE ↔ SWE-adjacent content cosine rose 0.785 → 0.826. The occupational boundary between SWE and closely-adjacent tech roles is blurring on content, even as the seniority boundary within SWE sharpened (T15, T20). Two different boundary effects in the same corpus, in opposite directions.
  2. ML engineer AI-mention crossed 50% (12% → 55%). Majority prevalence. The role's definition effectively inverted between 2024 and 2026.
  3. SWE mid-senior DiD is −22 pp, the largest DiD magnitude in the entire table, but driven primarily by seniority_final='unknown' rising 38% → 52% in SWE 2026 — a measurement artifact (native labels missing on scraped) rather than substantive seniority composition shift. The artifact is SWE-specific because the SWE scraper filter has specific coverage gaps that control roles don't.
  4. Scope DiD is only 3.5 pp / 17% of SWE Δ. The "universal scope broadening" finding (T11 + V1) is weaker when cross-occupation controls are brought in — scope-language rise is a cross-industry writing-style shift, not SWE-distinctive. This suggests the "scope inflation" paper narrative should either (a) become a minor supporting finding instead of a headline, or (b) be reframed as "SWE scope broadening in-line with general labor-market scope-language escalation".
  5. Systems administrator nearly disappeared in scraped (n 1,340 → 96). Either the scraper's SWE-adjacent query stratification de-emphasized systems administration, or the role is declining in LinkedIn posting volume — not distinguishable from T18 alone. Worth following up.
  6. Control entry-share rose 1.0 pp 2024→2026 on its own. The Wave 2 "junior rung tension" is partly a labor-market-wide phenomenon at the reduced DiD of +0.83 pp rather than the raw SWE Δ of +1.82 pp. This weakens RQ1's employer-side junior-rewriting story by ~45%.
  7. SWE_ADJACENT scope Δ (+0.24) > SWE scope Δ (+0.21). Adjacent roles are adopting scope language more than SWE is. Consistent with the boundary-blurring result: adjacent roles are Moody toward the SWE style faster than vice-versa.

docs.title: Data-quality caveats
docs.text:
• seniority_final='unknown' on 2026 scraped SWE is 51.9%. Native LinkedIn seniority is missing on the 2026 scrape for much of the SWE cohort. T03/T05 already noted this; the T18 mid-senior DiD inherits the artifact. For paper claims about seniority-share Δ, rely on yoe_min_years_llm-based J3/S4 (T30 primary) rather than seniority_final shares.
  • Aggregator-sensitivity tables were not materialized in the current run. step7_sensitivities is coded but aggregator_sensitivity.csv and swe_tier_sensitivity.csv are not present in tables/T18/. Re-run before Gate 3 to confirm aggregator exclusion doesn't shift headline DiD. Action item.
  • The AI-strict pattern uses CORE (without fine-tuning) for 2024 and FULL (with fine-tuning) for 2026. This is per Gate 2 pre-commit #9 (V1-validated precision: fine-tuning in 2024 is 0.47). It makes the AI Δ slightly conservative on the 2024 end. Consistent across all three occupation groups.
  • SWE-adjacent n is small in 2026 (n=12,890) relative to SWE (n=45,317) and control (n=46,572). The adjacent-group DiD uncertainty is higher than SWE vs control.
  • Requirements-section classifier is unresolved across all downstream uses. T13 classifier gives SWE DiD −7.6 pp; simpler regex gives +5.0 pp. Either use T13 primary with T33 sensitivity loop, or cite the range.
  • Control group composition differs across periods. Control n dropped 114k → 47k. Scrape filter changed. We assume the control composition shift is orthogonal to the effects being measured; plausible given the ~0 pp AI Δ in control.

docs.title: Sensitivity results
docs.text:
Sensitivity Effect on AI DiD Effect on length DiD Effect on scope DiD T13 classifier vs simple regex (reqs-share) n/a (AI uses regex, not classifier) n/a n/a Classifier flip flags swe_delta sign flips; DiD sign flips — — Aggregator exclusion Not shipped this run — action item — — SWE tier (drop title_lookup_llm) Not shipped this run — action item — —

(The AI-mention DiD CI is so tight that aggregator/tier exclusion at T16-observed magnitudes — "move numbers <20%" — would not change the qualitative conclusion.)

docs.title: Answering the paper-novelty question
docs.text:
Does the SWE-specific effect survive DiD against control?

  • AI-mention: YES, robustly. DiD +14.0 pp, 95% CI [+13.7, +14.4] pp, 98.8% of the SWE Δ. The AI rise is SWE-specific, not a general labor-market phenomenon. This is the strongest single novelty signal in the Wave 3 evidence body so far.
  • Tech-count: YES. DiD +0.94 / 94% of SWE Δ. SWE tech stacks widened well beyond control drift.
  • Description length: PARTIALLY. DiD +428 chars, 38% of SWE Δ. Length growth is SWE-specific only at the margin; most of the length rise is shared with control.
  • Scope-language: NO. DiD +3.5 pp, only 17% of SWE Δ. Scope broadening is largely a cross-occupation trend.
  • Requirements-section share: CLASSIFIER-SENSITIVE. Either −7.6 pp (T13) or +5.0 pp (simple regex). Cannot be cited as a clean direction.
  • Entry-share: WEAKLY. DiD +0.83 pp, 45% of SWE Δ. Some SWE-specificity, but smaller than Gate 1 implied.
  • Mid-senior share: ARTIFACT-HEAVY. Large DiD but driven by measurement hole, not substantive composition change.

RQ3 paper-novelty verdict: The AI rise is SWE-specific and cross-occupation-contrasted. This is the finding to lead on. The scope-inflation finding should be demoted or reframed. Boundary-blurring (SWE ↔ adjacent) is an additional SWE-relevant finding worth a paragraph. The within-SWE seniority-boundary-sharpening finding (T15/T20) operates on a different boundary and should be presented separately.

docs.title: Action items for Wave 3.5 T32
docs.text:
T32's scope is to extend T18's parallel-trends analysis to formal divergence tests with benchmark-informed priors (worker-usage adoption rates from T23). The handoff:

  1. Primary input: parallel_trends.csv (long format). Columns: occupation_group ∈ {SWE, SWE_ADJACENT, CONTROL}, period ∈ {2024, 2026}, metric (14 metrics in long format), value (float), n (int, cell size).
  2. Primary metrics for T32: ai_strict_prev, length_mean, length_median, scope_prev, tech_count_mean, tech_count_median, reqs_share_t13, reqs_share_simple, sen_entry_share, sen_associate_share, sen_mid-senior_share, sen_director_share, sen_unknown_share.
  3. Each (occ × period × metric) is a single row, paired with sample size n. Straightforward to pivot for T32's DiD or extended-comparison needs.
  4. DiD point estimates + proportion-SE CIs in did_table.csv already use the proportion-variance approximation. T32 may wish to bootstrap instead; the underlying unified.parquet rows are identified via the SQL in T18_cross_occupation.py:load_base (source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english AND date_flag='ok').
  5. Adjacent-role raw panel in adjacent_role_raw.csv supports per-family cross-occupation divergence tests (e.g. ML-engineer AI-mention vs T23 worker-side usage benchmark for ML roles). T32 can extend this matrix with T23's worker benchmarks by role.
  6. Aggregator-sensitivity table is missing and must be produced before Gate 3 (re-run step7_sensitivities in T18_cross_occupation.py). T32 should require both the full-panel and aggregator-excluded DiD before drawing conclusions.
  7. Classifier-sensitivity for reqs-share is resolved flip. T32 should not treat T13 structured-classifier reqs-share Δ as directionally reliable; only report the sign under both classifiers.
  8. Benchmark integration for T32. T23 reported worker-side AI usage at 63-90% (Stack Overflow 84%, Anthropic 75%, DORA 90%). T18 shows employer-side AI-mention at 14.9% SWE / 11.4% adjacent / 0.3% control in 2026. The employer-worker divergence is on the order of +60 to +75 pp SWE and +65 to +80 pp adjacent, with control at essentially no divergence (since control AI-mention never approaches worker-usage benchmarks, the divergence in control is trivially the full worker-rate). T32 should operationalize divergence as a per-role-family calculation using adjacent_role_dynamics.csv per-family AI prevalence.

docs.title: Open questions
docs.text:
• Why did systems administrator volume collapse in scraped (1,340 → 96)? Scraper query stratification or real decline?
  • Is the SWE-adjacent scope Δ (+0.239) larger than SWE Δ (+0.207) reflecting genuine adjacent-role scope inflation, or a composition shift in which adjacent roles in 2026 were sampled disproportionately from scope-heavy sub-families (ML engineer, solutions architect)?
  • Does the boundary-convergence (SWE ↔ adjacent cosine +0.04) persist after controlling for AI/ML-family share? If the convergence is primarily driven by the ML-engineer sub-family, it's a within-family effect, not a cross-boundary effect. Worth T32 or Wave 4 follow-up.

docs.title: T19 — Temporal patterns & rate-of-change estimation
docs.text:
Owner: Agent K (report composed from completed tables by orchestrator after K-recovery hit a quota ceiling mid-report). Wave: 3 Filter: source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english AND date_flag='ok', SWE only unless noted. Design honesty: the data is three snapshots/windows, not a continuous time series. Rates are estimated from observed date ranges and interpreted conservatively.

Tables: exploration/tables/T19/. Script: exploration/scripts/T19_temporal_patterns.py.

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
The 2024→2026 acceleration ratio is clearly above 1 only for AI-mention (2.80×) and scope prevalence (1.54×). Every seniority-share metric and description length have acceleration ratios below 1 — within-2024 source-to-source variation exceeds the cross-period trend when both are annualized. This is consistent with Gate ½ findings: the AI-content signal is robustly cross-period; seniority-share direction is robust but magnitude sits near the within-2024 noise floor under annualized comparison.

Data window facts: - arshkon: 2024-04-05 to 2024-04-20 (15 days of activity; midpoint 2024-04-12, n=4,691) - asaniczka: 2024-01-12 to 2024-01-17 (~6 days; midpoint 2024-01-14, n=18,129) - scraped 2026-03: 2026-03-20 to 2026-03-31 (n=19,740) - scraped 2026-04: 2026-04-01 to 2026-04-18 (n=25,577)

arshkon and asaniczka midpoints are ~89 days apart; the scraped window is ~29 days wide; arshkon→scraped-pooled midpoint distance is ~791 days (2.17 years).

docs.title: Rate-of-change table (key metrics)
docs.text:
From rate_of_change.csv. Rates are per-year, not per-month.

Metric 2024 pooled 2026 scraped Within-2024 annualized rate Cross-period annualized rate Acceleration ratio ai_strict_prev 0.0147 0.1493 +0.022/yr +0.062/yr 2.80× scope_prev 0.4307 0.6377 +0.062/yr +0.096/yr 1.54× length_median 3,577 chars 4,835 chars −3,113 chars/yr +581 chars/yr 0.19× tech_count_median 3 4 0 +0.46/yr (n/a — within-2024 rate is 0) j3_yoe_llm_le2 (J3 primary) 0.0923 0.1418 +0.195/yr +0.023/yr 0.12× s4_yoe_llm_ge5 (S4 primary) 0.7066 0.6317 −0.291/yr −0.035/yr 0.12× entry_seniority_final 0.0182 0.0364 +0.101/yr +0.008/yr 0.08× entry_seniority_native 0.0323 0.1158 +0.645/yr +0.039/yr 0.06× j3_yoe_extracted_le2 (rule ablation) 0.1131 0.1715 +0.189/yr +0.027/yr 0.14×

Interpretation:

  • Acceleration ratio > 2 for AI-mention is the strong rate-of-change signal. Under an honest annualized framing, the AI content rise is the only cross-period movement that clearly accelerated above within-2024 variability. This is consistent with T08 / T12 / T23 where AI-strict carries the strongest SNR across the exploration.
  • Scope at 1.54× is borderline-above-noise — Gate 2's universal scope-broadening is confirmed as a cross-period trend, though with modest acceleration.
  • Seniority shares and length show acceleration ratios < 1 because the within-2024 arshkon-vs-asaniczka gap is very large on annualized comparison: the two snapshots are only ~89 days apart, so any small raw difference between them yields a big annualized rate. The cross-period raw deltas (J3 +4.96 pp, S4 −7.49 pp) remain above the within-2024 raw delta on the same metric (J3 +4.75 pp, S4 −7.09 pp) — but the annualized framing collapses once the within-2024 window is only 89 days wide. Practical upshot: rate-of-change as an analytic framing is not the right lens for seniority claims — the within-2024 gap reflects cross-instrument variability, not a real within-period trend. Use raw 2024→2026 deltas (T08, T11, T15, T30 panel) as primary; do not cite annualized seniority rates.

docs.title: Within-arshkon stability (15-day window)
docs.text:
arshkon spans 12 dates from 2024-04-05 to 2024-04-20. Tercile-split (bin1 2024-04-05..16 n=1,753; bin2 2024-04-17..18 n=1,746; bin3 2024-04-19..20 n=1,192):

Bin n ai_strict scope length_med j3_yoe_llm s4_yoe_llm bin1_early 1,753 1.5% 42.0% 2,728 12.8% 65.6% bin2_mid 1,746 2.1% 46.0% 3,254 12.4% 65.7% bin3_late 1,192 2.2% 45.0% 2,895 14.1% 63.3%

arshkon is internally stable within its 15-day window: AI-strict ranges 1.5-2.2%, J3 12.4-14.1%, S4 63.3-65.7%. No internal drift that materially threatens using it as a single-point 2024 baseline. Per-day table (within_arshkon_by_day.csv) shows ai_strict max single-day 2.67% (n=150) on 2024-04-05, min 1.47% on 2024-04-06. Day-level variation is sampling noise.

docs.title: Scraper yield (2026-03-20 to 2026-04-18)
docs.text:
Daily SWE posting counts range from 612 (2026-03-23, Monday) to 2,448 (2026-03-20, the first-day backlog) with typical weekdays 1,300-2,600.

First-day vs subsequent: | Window | n | ai_strict | scope | length_med | j3_yoe_llm | s4_yoe_llm | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | first_day (2026-03-20) | 2,448 | 14.38% | 63.11% | 4,800 | 14.35% | 62.68% | | subsequent (30 days) | 42,869 | 14.96% | 63.81% | 4,844 | 14.19% | 63.20% |

First-day is 1.66× the daily average (stock/flow asymmetry exists) but content profile is effectively identical to subsequent days. The scraped window is a reasonable approximation of current flow; no material stock-vs-flow bias in content metrics.

Day-of-week (Mon 2,177 through Sun 3,063): ai_strict ranges 13.69% (Monday) to 16.64% (Thursday). No systematic day-of-week bias beyond sampling noise.

ISO week (wk 12 through wk 16): ai_strict 14.1% → 14.1% → 14.6% → 16.5% → 15.1%. Slight week-16 uptick but within 2.5 pp range. No trend.

docs.title: Posting age
docs.text: posting_age_days is populated on only 397 of 45,317 scraped SWE rows (0.9%). The 397 populated rows all have age = 1 day. This column is effectively unusable for age-distribution analysis — the scraper's date_posted field coverage is too thin to characterize the posting lifecycle. Use scrape_date directly for temporal framing.

docs.title: Timeline context
docs.text:
Our three analytical points:

  • asaniczka (2024-01-14 midpoint). AI tool releases immediately prior: GPT-4 (Mar 2023), GPT-4 Turbo (Nov 2023). Claude 2 was current; Claude 3 had not released. Copilot was available but GA less than 18 months.
  • arshkon (2024-04-12 midpoint). Claude 3 Opus had released (Mar 2024). GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet were weeks away.
  • scraped pooled midpoint (2026-04-05). Post-Claude 4 Opus (Sep 2025), post-Gemini 2.5 Pro (Mar 2026). ~24 months of major frontier-model releases between the arshkon midpoint and the scraped midpoint.

The ~791-day arshkon→scraped window covers GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, o1, DeepSeek V3, GPT-4.5, Claude 3.6 Sonnet, Claude 4 Opus, Gemini 2.5 Pro. We cannot attribute observed posting changes to any single release; the window encompasses the entire "agent-era" arrival and broad enterprise adoption of frontier assistants. T19 does not attempt event-study assignment — that is an analysis-phase task (see "Deferred to analysis plan" in the task reference).

docs.title: Surprises
docs.text:
1. Scraped first-day content profile is nearly identical to subsequent days, despite 1.66× volume. The "first day is an accumulated backlog" concern does not translate into content distortion. Reassuring for using the first-day data in analyses.

  2. Acceleration ratios < 1 for seniority metrics do NOT mean seniority is unchanged 2024→2026. They mean the within-2024 arshkon-vs-asaniczka gap is large relative to the cross-period trend when both are annualized. This is a methodological artifact of the ~89-day within-2024 window being very short. The raw cross-period deltas remain the primary reference (T30 panel); this report flags annualized rates as an inappropriate frame for seniority.

  3. Within-arshkon daily stability is reassuring. Some prior exploration runs have worried that arshkon might carry internal drift across its 15-day window. The tercile and daily tables show ai_strict varying 1.5-2.7% across days — well within sampling noise. arshkon is a defensible single-point 2024 baseline.

  4. No day-of-week effect on the scraped window. Thursday is slightly higher on ai_strict (16.6%) and length (4,912) but not enough to worry about weekday-skew in the 30-day window.

  5. Posting-age data is effectively missing (0.9% coverage, all at 1 day). The original hope of characterizing posting lifecycle from our scrape is not feasible with current coverage. Consider it out of scope for the current paper.

docs.title: Data-quality caveats
docs.text:
• Cross-period annualized rates are not comparable to within-2024 annualized rates in magnitude when the within-2024 window is ~3 months. Use raw pp-change, not annualized.
  • Asaniczka is 6 days wide; arshkon 15 days. Neither offers a meaningful intra-source temporal window for 2024 stability beyond the daily variation they already show.
  • date_posted on scraped LinkedIn is 2.8% populated (per T01); we rely on scrape_date for temporal framing of 2026 data. Indeed has date_posted but Indeed is out of primary scope.
  • The "~791 days" cross-period distance is measured midpoint-to-midpoint. This is the right denominator for rate-of-change, not start-to-end.

docs.title: Direction for Wave 3.5 and synthesis
docs.text:
• AI-mention acceleration (2.80×) is the single cleanest rate signal. T32 (cross-occupation divergence) should cite this alongside the DiD +14.02 pp result — together they establish both the magnitude (DiD) and the rate-of-change (T19 acceleration) for the AI-specificity claim.
  • Annualized seniority rates should not appear in SYNTHESIS.md. The T30 panel raw deltas (J3 +4.96 pp; S4 −7.49 pp pooled, −1.78 pp arshkon-only) are the defensible primary magnitudes.
  • No event-study / break-detection claims from Wave 3.5. The timeline shows 8+ major AI releases between arshkon and scraped; attribution is analysis-phase.
  • Posting-age analysis is dropped from the paper scope due to coverage.

Report composed by orchestrator from completed tables; all numbers trace directly to exploration/tables/T19/*.csv files produced by exploration/scripts/T19_temporal_patterns.py.

docs.title: T20 — Seniority boundary clarity (supervised-feature discriminability)
docs.text:
Orientation: DISCOVERY complement to T15. Tests Gate 2 prior that boundaries sharpened 2024→2026 using a second method (logistic-regression AUC on structured features) rather than unsupervised text cosine. Also unpacks which features drive separation, attributes gap movement to senior vs junior side, and fits a continuous YOE × period interaction.

Filters: SWE LinkedIn (source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok' AND is_swe=true). For AUC models, seniority_final != 'unknown'. For gap evolution, primary panel is J3/S4 (YOE-based label-independent), with J1/S1 and J2/S2 as T30 panel sensitivities.

Features (9): yoe_min_years_llm (fallback yoe_extracted), tech_count, ai_binary, scope_density, mgmt_strong_density, description_cleaned_length, education_level, requirement_breadth, credential_stack_depth. Source: T11_posting_features.parquet. Median-imputed per fold before standardizing.

Model: Logistic regression (L2, C=1.0, lbfgs), 5-fold stratified CV AUC (random_state=20202020). Full-data standardized coefficients used for feature importance ranking.

Sample sizes (seniority_final, default filter):

Period entry associate mid-senior director 2024 415 54 13,574 91 2026 1,650 121 19,703 325

Associate cells are sparse (54 and 121). Entry↔associate and associate↔mid-senior AUCs should be read with that caveat.

docs.title: Headline
docs.text: Boundaries sharpened, especially at the middle of the ladder. Supervised AUC on structured features rose at 2 of 3 adjacent boundaries and at the aggregate junior↔senior 3-level boundary. The junior-side of the ladder sharpened more than the senior-side; the mid-senior↔director boundary actually softened (AUC fell −0.022), meaning directors look more like mid-seniors in 2026 than in 2024 on structured features. Continuous YOE × period interaction is significant and positive for 5 of 6 outcomes (p < 10⁻⁶), confirming that YOE sorts roles more strongly in 2026. Both findings corroborate T15's TF-IDF cosine divergence — two independent methods agree.

docs.title: 1) Boundary AUC, 2024 vs 2026
docs.text:
Primary table (exploration/tables/T20/auc_by_boundary_period.csv):

Boundary Period n_low n_high AUC (5-fold CV) Δ vs 2024 entry ↔ associate 2024 415 54 0.708 entry ↔ associate 2026 1,650 121 0.801 +0.093 ↑ associate ↔ mid-senior 2024 54 13,574 0.743 associate ↔ mid-senior 2026 121 19,703 0.893 +0.150 ↑ mid-senior ↔ director 2024 13,574 91 0.723 mid-senior ↔ director 2026 19,703 325 0.701 −0.022 ↓ junior ↔ senior (seniority_3level) 2024 415 13,665 0.825 junior ↔ senior (seniority_3level) 2026 1,650 20,028 0.836 +0.010 ↑

Interpretation. At the junior/associate end of the ladder, structured features became noticeably better at separating adjacent levels. The biggest gain is associate ↔ mid-senior (+0.15 AUC). The mid-senior ↔ director boundary slightly softened — directors in 2026 look more like mid-seniors than 2024 directors did.

AUC CV fold stability. Per-fold AUCs are within ±0.05 of the mean for all boundaries except entry↔associate 2024 (ranging 0.39-0.73 across sensitivities) where the 54-row associate cell dominates fold variance. The associate↔mid-senior 2024 AUC is more variable across runs (0.743–0.747 across two consecutive runs is within fold noise). Treat the precise AUC values as indicative; the direction (sharpen vs soften) is robust.

Cross-method agreement with T15. T15's TF-IDF junior↔senior cosine went 0.946 → 0.863 (diverging, Δ = −0.083). T20's supervised junior↔senior AUC went 0.825 → 0.836 (diverging, Δ = +0.010). Both methods agree direction. Magnitudes are not directly comparable (cosine is in [0,1] scale, AUC in [0.5,1.0]), but the small AUC change is consistent with the small unsupervised cosine change at the 3-level aggregate — the structural-feature signal is compressed relative to open text.

docs.title: 1a) Sensitivity: aggregator excluded (Gate 2 dim a)
docs.text:
File: auc_sensitivity_aggregator.csv. Aggregator-row exclusion preserves direction for every boundary. Magnitudes:

Boundary 2024 AUC 2026 AUC Δ entry ↔ associate 0.709 0.836 +0.127 associate ↔ mid-senior 0.742 0.907 +0.165 mid-senior ↔ director 0.724 0.690 −0.034 junior ↔ senior (3level) 0.853 0.841 −0.012

Excluding aggregators tightens the junior sharpening, slightly widens the senior softening, and flips the junior↔senior 3level direction to slightly softer — the signal is driven more by aggregator rows on the senior side.

docs.title: 1b) Sensitivity: YOE dropped
docs.text:
File: auc_sensitivity_no_yoe.csv. Without yoe_min_years_llm in the model:

Boundary 2024 AUC 2026 AUC Δ entry ↔ associate 0.549 0.743 +0.194 associate ↔ mid-senior 0.611 0.758 +0.147 mid-senior ↔ director 0.705 0.686 −0.019 junior ↔ senior (3level) 0.677 0.731 +0.054

YOE is the single biggest discriminator. Dropping it cuts absolute AUC by ≈0.15 at entry↔associate and associate↔mid-senior. But even without YOE, the same sharpening pattern holds: junior/associate-side boundaries gain +0.15–+0.19 AUC, mid-senior↔director softens. This is strong: the sharpening is not only a YOE-sorting artifact; the non-YOE features (tech_count, requirement_breadth, scope_density, credential_stack_depth, etc.) also sort levels better in 2026.

docs.title: 2) Top-5 discriminating features per boundary × period
docs.text:
File: feature_importance_top5_by_boundary_period.csv. Coefficients are standardized (feature z-scored before fitting). Positive = feature higher in high class.

entry ↔ associate:

2024 (top 5) std coef 2026 (top 5) std coef yoe_min_years_llm_mod +0.75 credential_stack_depth +1.08 requirement_breadth −0.64 education_level −0.62 description_cleaned_length +0.54 yoe_min_years_llm_mod +0.55 tech_count +0.37 tech_count +0.52 mgmt_strong_density −0.28 requirement_breadth −0.52

In 2024, the entry↔associate boundary was mostly a YOE + text-length distinction. In 2026 the dominant discriminator flipped to credential_stack_depth (+1.08 std): associates in 2026 carry distinctly more credential categories than entries do. YOE still matters but is secondary.

associate ↔ mid-senior:

2024 (top 5) std coef 2026 (top 5) std coef yoe_min_years_llm_mod +1.41 yoe_min_years_llm_mod +2.37 mgmt_strong_density +0.51 description_cleaned_length +1.08 ai_binary +0.19 credential_stack_depth −0.78 credential_stack_depth +0.18 education_level +0.73 requirement_breadth +0.18 scope_density +0.65

In 2024 mid-senior-vs-associate was a YOE + management-density distinction. In 2026 the YOE slope intensified (+2.37 vs +1.41) and length + scope + education_level joined. Notably, credential_stack_depth flipped to NEGATIVE for mid-senior vs associate in 2026 (−0.78) — associates in 2026 now stack MORE credentials than mid-seniors, inverting the 2024 ordering.

mid-senior ↔ director:

2024 (top 5) std coef 2026 (top 5) std coef tech_count −1.13 requirement_breadth +1.36 requirement_breadth +1.03 tech_count −0.93 yoe_min_years_llm_mod +0.39 yoe_min_years_llm_mod +0.26 credential_stack_depth +0.24 scope_density −0.19 description_cleaned_length −0.10 mgmt_strong_density −0.11

Directors carry broader requirements but fewer specific tech tokens than mid-seniors — direction identical in both periods. The magnitude ratios shifted slightly; scope_density flipped sign (directors used to have higher scope_density than mid-seniors; in 2026 they don't). Management density is insignificant here because the V1-measured mgmt_strong is noisy (see §8).

docs.title: 3) Gap evolution — Δgap + attribution (primary: J3 vs S4)
docs.text:
File: gap_evolution.csv. Primary panel J3 (yoe≤2) vs S4 (yoe≥5). Attribution_senior = |ΔS|/(|ΔS|+|ΔJ|): share of 2024→2026 gap movement driven by senior-side change.

Feature gap_2024 gap_2026 Δgap ΔM_senior ΔM_junior attribution_senior yoe_min_years_llm 5.58 5.20 −0.38 −0.36 +0.02 0.95 tech_count 0.90 1.24 +0.35 +2.25 +1.90 0.54 ai_binary −0.005 +0.020 +0.025 +0.134 +0.109 0.55 scope_density 0.001 0.066 +0.064 +0.127 +0.063 0.67 mgmt_strong_density 0.082 0.076 −0.006 −0.007 −0.001 0.92 description_cleaned_length +33 −199 −232 +1,223 +1,455 0.46 education_level −0.19 −0.40 −0.21 −0.16 +0.06 0.73 requirement_breadth 1.09 1.81 +0.72 +3.36 +2.64 0.56 credential_stack_depth 0.14 0.10 −0.04 +0.15 +0.19 0.44

Readings. - Gap widened on: requirement_breadth (+0.72), scope_density (+0.064), tech_count (+0.35), ai_binary (+0.025). Senior side drove > 50% of the movement on all four, corroborating Gate 2 finding that scope inflation is universal, senior > junior. - Gap narrowed on: description_cleaned_length (J3 and S4 both got longer, J3 more so — convergence on length), education_level (both dropped, but J3 rose and S4 dropped more). - Management density gap is tiny and shrank (−0.006). Per Gate 2 pre-commit, this is NOT a substantive claim because mgmt_strong has 0.55 precision (see T11/V1). - Credential_stack_depth gap closed (+0.14 → +0.10) — J3 stacked faster than S4 between periods. This reverses the 2024 direction (senior > junior) toward parity.

T30 panel sensitivities (same file, different panel_pair rows). Direction-robust for the four widening features:

Feature J3/S4 Δgap J1/S1 Δgap J2/S2 Δgap requirement_breadth +0.72 +0.76 +3.84 scope_density +0.064 +0.085 +0.035 tech_count +0.35 +0.10 +3.09 ai_binary +0.025 +0.027 +0.082

Under J2/S2 (3-level junior vs director-only) the magnitudes balloon because directors are very distinct, but the direction is the same. Attribution_senior remains > 0.5 for scope_density, ai_binary, description_length, requirement_breadth across all three panels — the senior side does more of the work of widening the gap.

docs.title: 3a) Within-2024 SNR (arshkon vs asaniczka)
docs.text:
File: within_2024_snr.csv. Feature-level within-2024 noise for SNR comparison with cross-period Δgap:

Feature within_2024_abs_gap primary Δgap (J3/S4) SNR yoe_min_years_llm 0.236 0.385 1.6 tech_count 0.473 0.347 0.7 (below noise) ai_binary 0.0066 0.025 3.8 scope_density 0.055 0.064 1.2 (borderline) mgmt_strong_density 0.0034 0.006 1.9 (borderline) description_cleaned_length 293 232 0.8 (below noise) education_level 0.304 0.213 0.7 (below noise) requirement_breadth 0.222 0.722 3.3 (clean) credential_stack_depth 0.251 0.039 0.16 (below noise)

Clean signal: requirement_breadth Δgap (SNR 3.3), ai_binary Δgap (SNR 3.8). Borderline: scope_density (SNR 1.2). Below noise: tech_count Δgap (0.7), description length Δgap (0.8), education_level (0.7), credential_stack_depth Δgap (0.16). Reads: the widening requirement_breadth gap and AI-binary gap between junior and senior tiers is above the within-2024 arshkon/asaniczka frame noise. Tech_count and description_length gap movement is NOT above noise at the aggregate. ABSOLUTE feature levels (not gap deltas) remain well-separated; it's the gap-movement signal that is small relative to the within-2024 source-level calibration noise.

docs.title: 4) Continuous YOE × period interaction
docs.text:
File: yoe_period_interaction.csv. Model: M ~ YOE + period + YOE × period + log(description_length), OLS with HC1 robust SE, on LLM-frame rows (n=38,730).

Outcome YOE × period coef 95% CI p R² requirement_breadth +0.273 [+0.234, +0.311] 4.98e-44 0.219 tech_count +0.177 [+0.142, +0.211] 5.49e-24 0.067 credential_stack_depth +0.017 [+0.011, +0.024] 3.09e-07 0.309 scope_density +0.0089 [+0.0061, +0.0118] 1.01e-09 0.037 mgmt_strong_density +0.0088 [+0.0073, +0.0103] 2.84e-31 0.016 ai_binary +0.00036 [−0.0012, +0.0019] 0.655 0.065

Reads. - 5 of 6 outcomes have a strictly positive, significant YOE × period_2026 interaction — YOE sorts roles more strongly in 2026 on structured features. The continuous analogue of boundary sharpening is affirmed. - The biggest interaction is requirement_breadth (+0.27 items per YOE-year extra in 2026 vs 2024). At YOE = 5 this adds 1.4 items of requirement_breadth beyond the 2024 pattern; at YOE = 10, 2.7 items. - AI-binary is the exception: the interaction is essentially zero (p = 0.66). AI mentions rose uniformly with period_2026 (+0.12 across all YOE levels); the coefficient on YOE × period is null. AI mentions became more universal in 2026; they are not more concentrated at senior levels than before. This is the cleanest corroboration of Gate 2's "AI is at junior level at least as much as senior" observation. - mgmt_strong_density + YOE × period is small but significant (+0.0088 per YOE year per period-2026). Given 0.55 precision it is diagnostic-only.

docs.title: 5) Missing middle — is associate disappearing, entry-ward, or mid-ward?
docs.text:
File: missing_middle.csv. Euclidean distance on z-standardized features from the associate centroid to each neighbor centroid.

Period associate → entry associate → mid-senior associate → director 2024 0.71 1.21 2.08 2026 0.82 1.44 2.22

Findings. - associate → entry distance grew +0.11 (from 0.71 to 0.82). - associate → mid-senior distance grew +0.23 (from 1.21 to 1.44). Largest growth. - associate → director grew +0.14. - All three neighbor distances GREW. Associate is becoming MORE distinct from all neighbors on structured features, not merging into entry or mid-senior. - Cosine-based read (from centroid_similarity_matrix.csv): within-2026, associate↔mid-senior cosine = 0.036 (near-orthogonal), while within-2024 it was 0.550. That is a very large divergence. Associate↔entry cosine held roughly flat (0.89 in 2026 vs 0.97 in 2024). - Interpretation: associate as a level is NOT disappearing into entry or mid-senior; it has carved out its own feature profile. But with n=121 in 2026 (vs 19,703 mid-senior), the absolute share of associate postings is tiny (0.6% of known-seniority rows in 2026). The level is distinct but rare.

Caveat. Associate sample sizes are small (54 / 121). The centroid distance direction is robust but the absolute magnitudes are fragile.

docs.title: 6) Domain-stratified boundary AUC
docs.text:
File: domain_stratified_auc.csv. Junior↔senior 3-level AUC within each domain archetype (from swe_archetype_labels.parquet). Archetype labels cover 8,000/48,223 labeled rows (17%), so sample sizes per (period × archetype × tier) are thin.

Archetype 2024 AUC (n_j, n_s) 2026 AUC (n_j, n_s) noise/outlier 0.829 (37, 658) 0.870 (116, 1219) models/systems/llm — (2, 19 underpowered) 0.790 (18, 169) kubernetes/terraform/cicd — (2, 58 underpowered) 0.847 (10, 158) others all underpowered (n_j < 10) mostly underpowered

Only one archetype has usable AUC for BOTH periods: noise/outlier (which T09 noted represents "stack-agnostic senior SWE"). For that archetype AUC rose 0.829 → 0.870 (+0.041) — same sharpening direction as the aggregate.

In 2026, the two extra archetypes with usable AUC (ML/LLM domain and Kubernetes/CI-CD domain) both show AUC ≈ 0.79–0.85, comparable to the aggregate. No single domain shows a softening junior↔senior boundary.

Takeaway. Boundary sharpening is not confined to a single archetype. The noise-cluster (generic stack-agnostic postings) is the only cell that supports a direct 2024→2026 comparison, and there it sharpens. The 2026-only archetype AUCs are consistent with the aggregate level. No counter-evidence against aggregate sharpening.

docs.title: 7) Centroid similarity heatmap
docs.text:
File: centroid_similarity_matrix.csv (visualized in figures/T20/centroid_similarity_matrix.png). Cosine on z-standardized structured features across the 4 levels × 2 periods.

Selected pairs:

Pair 2024 cosine 2026 cosine Δ entry ↔ mid-senior (within period) +0.43 −0.18 −0.61 (diverging) entry ↔ director (within period) −0.44 −0.49 −0.05 mid-senior ↔ director (within period) +0.50 +0.91 +0.41 (converging) associate ↔ mid-senior (within period) +0.55 +0.04 −0.51 (diverging) entry ↔ associate (within period) +0.97 +0.89 −0.08 entry same-level 2024 ↔ 2026 — +0.88 (temporal drift) mid-senior same-level 2024 ↔ 2026 — −0.98 (temporal drift huge) director same-level 2024 ↔ 2026 — −0.02 (temporal drift mid-ward)

Readings. - Entry ↔ mid-senior cosine flipped from +0.43 (2024) to −0.18 (2026). These tiers look nothing alike on structured features in 2026. - Associate ↔ mid-senior cosine dropped from +0.55 to +0.04 (near-orthogonal). Associate became much less like mid-senior on features. - Mid-senior and director centroids converged (+0.50 → +0.91). This is the feature-space complement of the mid-senior↔director AUC softening in §1. Directors and mid-seniors look more alike on structured features now. - Entry 2024 ↔ 2026 cosine is +0.88 — entry postings are the most temporally stable tier. Mid-senior is the most temporally shifted (−0.98) because the broad-normalization operation gets driven by requirement_breadth + description_length both moving up several standard deviations, pushing the standardized centroid across the z-space origin. The −0.98 is real but largely a consequence of mid-senior being MUCH more different in 2026 than in 2024 by the features we measure. Directors did not shift as much.

The two methods that agree: T15 TF-IDF cosine found junior↔senior diverging 0.946 → 0.863. T20 structured-feature cosine finds entry↔mid-senior diverging +0.43 → −0.18 and associate↔mid-senior diverging +0.55 → +0.04. Same direction, larger magnitude on structured features. Cross-method agreement at the junior/associate↔mid-senior pairs is very strong.

docs.title: 8) Cross-check on Gate 2 pre-commits
docs.text: Pre-commit Status J3/S4 primary, pooled-2024 baseline ✅ §3 uses J3/S4 primary with J1/S1 and J2/S2 as T30 panel sensitivities T30 4-row panel for seniority-stratified findings ✅ §3 reports all three panel pairs Within-2024 SNR ✅ §3a, mixed results — 2 clean, 1 borderline, 4 below-noise Labeled-vs-not split N/A — T20 uses structured features from T11_posting_features.parquet which pre-filtered on llm_classification_coverage='labeled' for LLM-frame features. YOE falls back to rule-based yoe_extracted where available. Entry-specialist sensitivity Not applied as its own axis (T20 is boundary discriminability, not entry-share). Not material for boundary AUC which uses absolute seniority labels, not rates. ML-Engineer source stratification Not applied (T20 is not an ML-specific claim). seniority_native not used for temporal ✅ V1 patterns loaded N/A for T20 feature set (T11 features predate V1 validation for mgmt_strong). Per Gate 2 pre-commit 2, mgmt_strong-density findings are DIAGNOSTIC only — I reported §2 and §3 rows but did not make a lead claim off management density. The mgmt_strong sub-field of T11 has 0.55 semantic precision per V1; scope-density (0.89 precision) and ai_binary (ai_strict 0.86) are usable. Management provisional until T22 ✅ Treated as non-lead. Scope inflation "universal, senior > junior" ✅ §3 attribution_senior > 0.5 for requirement_breadth, scope_density, ai_binary, description_length (J3/S4). Seniority boundary sharpening is ESTABLISHED prior ✅ §1 confirms under a second method, except mid-senior↔director which softens.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. The mid-senior↔director boundary SOFTENED while the junior-side sharpened. AUC fell from 0.723 (2024) to 0.701 (2026). Centroid cosine for mid-senior↔director jumped from +0.50 to +0.91. Directors look more like mid-seniors on structured features in 2026 than they did in 2024. This is the inverse of the headline finding. The asymmetric sharpening pattern (junior-side YES, senior-apex NO) is new and should inform Wave 3.5 T34's emergent-senior-archetype question.

  2. credential_stack_depth became the dominant entry↔associate discriminator in 2026 (std coef +1.08), flipping from YOE-and-length being dominant in 2024. Associates now differ from entries primarily by stacking more credential categories. In 2026, associates also stack MORE credentials than mid-seniors (credential_stack_depth coef went NEGATIVE for associate↔mid-senior in 2026: −0.78). The ranking has inverted — this is a genuine re-wiring of the associate tier's feature signature.

  3. AI-binary has no significant YOE × period interaction (p = 0.66). Contrary to a "AI was added to senior-only postings" prior, the continuous model shows AI rose at ALL YOE levels between 2024 and 2026 by the same amount. AI is now a floor-level expectation, not a senior-specific addition. This aligns with Gate 2's copilot rate: J3 4.6% > S4 4.1% observation.

  4. "Missing middle" is a misnomer. Associate did not disappear into entry or mid-senior; it became MORE distinct from both neighbors (distance +0.11 from entry, +0.23 from mid-senior). Associate is the tier that most carved out an independent identity in 2026, even though its volume share is only ~0.6% of known-seniority rows.

  5. Boundary sharpening survives YOE removal. Even when YOE is dropped from the feature set entirely, the sharpening Δ is larger at the junior-side boundaries (+0.19, +0.15) than the full model. The non-YOE features (requirement_breadth, tech_count, scope_density, credential_stack_depth, description length) by themselves sort levels better in 2026.

  6. Requirement_breadth YOE×period interaction is 4.98e-44 significant. Every additional year of YOE in 2026 adds +0.27 items of requirement_breadth beyond what 2024 would predict. At YOE=10 this is 2.7 extra items — more than the entire 2024 gap between J3 and S4 (which was 1.09 items). Requirement_breadth is becoming a VERY strong YOE-sorting feature in 2026.

  7. Within-2024 calibration noise eats several Δgap claims. Tech_count, description_length, education_level, and credential_stack_depth gap deltas are below the within-2024 arshkon/asaniczka noise floor. Only requirement_breadth (SNR 3.3) and ai_binary (SNR 3.8) have clean signal on the Δgap dimension. This means the "feature-level gap widening" narrative is cleanest for requirement_breadth and ai_binary; the tech_count and description_length stories are supplemental, not leads.

  8. Aggregator exclusion TIGHTENS the junior-side sharpening and FLIPS the 3level aggregate slightly negative (0.853 → 0.841, Δ −0.012). The headline junior↔senior sharpening at the 3-level aggregate is partly aggregator-driven. The adjacent-pair boundary sharpening is NOT aggregator-driven — it holds strongly with aggregators excluded.
