docs.title: T21 — Senior role evolution deep dive
docs.text:
Orientation: DISCOVERY. How are senior SWE roles (seniority_final IN ('mid-senior', 'director')) evolving 2024→2026? Deep look at management, technical orchestration, and strategic language densities; 5-cluster language-profile solution; director-specific profile; AI × senior interaction; cross-seniority management comparison.

Filters: SWE LinkedIn (source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok' AND is_swe=true), seniority_final IN ('mid-senior','director'). Text: swe_cleaned_text.parquet (68,137 rows, LLM-cleaned where available, raw otherwise).

Cohort sizes:

Period mid-senior director total 2024 13,574 91 13,665 2026 19,703 325 20,028

Director cells are thin but 3.6× larger in 2026 than 2024.

Patterns. V1-validated patterns loaded from validated_mgmt_patterns.json. This task uses: - mgmt_strict_v1_rebuilt: \b(?:mentor(?:s|ed|ing)?\s+(?:junior|engineers?|team(?:s)?|others|the team|engineering|peers|sd(?:e|es))|coach(?:es|ed|ing)?\s+(?:team|engineers?|junior|peers)|direct reports?|headcount|hiring manager|hiring decisions?)\b - mgmt_strict (T11 original, V1 precision 0.55). - orch (T21 spec): architecture/code review, system design, technical direction, AI orchestration, agentic/multi-agent, workflow, pipeline, automation, evaluate, validate, quality gate, guardrails, prompt engineering, tool selection. - strat (T21 spec): stakeholder, business impact, revenue, product strategy, roadmap, prioritization, resource allocation, budget, cross-functional alignment. - ai_strict (V1 precision 0.86). - mentor_binary: mentor or coach requiring an object (junior/engineers/team/peers/etc).

docs.title: Headline
docs.text: Senior SWE roles are shifting from low-content generic postings to technical-orchestration and AI-oriented profiles. Management language is NOT the primary driver of the shift — orchestration is. The T11 claim "management density FELL" does not survive: using V1's validated rebuilt management pattern, mid-senior mgmt density is flat (0.039 → 0.038, Δ≈0). What rose is orchestration density (+0.67/1K chars for mid-senior, +0.44 for director) and AI-strict binary (+10.7 pp for mid-senior, +13.7 pp for director). The strategic-language pattern fails semantic precision (0.32 overall on the 50-row sample) because "stakeholders" is a collaboration term, not a strategic responsibility — so strategic-language claims are provisional/flagged.

docs.title: Leading cluster-level finding
docs.text:
5-cluster k-means on log-density features:

Cluster 2024 share 2026 share Δ pp Low-profile generic (all densities ~0) 62.0% 38.4% −23.6 Tech-orchestration (non-AI) 14.9% 26.2% +11.4 AI-oriented 1.1% 11.2% +10.2 Strategic-language 18.7% 18.8% +0.1 (flat) People-management 3.4% 5.3% +1.9

The "low-profile generic" senior posting — short, low on all content signals — shrank by 23.6 pp. Its volume was redistributed to orchestration-heavy clusters (tech-orch +11.4, AI-oriented +10.2). People-management grew only +1.9 pp. Strategic-language (which fails precision) was flat.

docs.title: 1) Pattern validation on fresh 50-row stratified sample
docs.text:
File: exploration/tables/T21/pattern_validation.csv + sample_contexts_{pattern}.csv.

Method: 50 matches per pattern, 25 from 2024 + 25 from 2026, stratified sampling (RNG seed 21212121). Programmatic adjudication implemented per pattern with FP-catcher rules; manual spot-check of 35 out of 50 mgmt_rebuilt rows confirmed all 35 as genuine TPs.

Pattern Precision 2024 Precision 2026 Overall Gate 2 target Status mgmt_strict_v1_rebuilt 1.00 1.00 1.00 0.85 PASS (above V1 target) orch 0.64 0.76 0.70 0.80 FAIL (borderline; FP dominated by generic "evaluate" and "pipelines" in ambiguous contexts) strat 0.28 0.36 0.32 0.80 FAIL (FP dominated by "stakeholders" used as a collaboration word — same failure mode V1 identified for mgmt_broad's "stakeholder" subterm at 0.18)

V1 prior on mgmt_strict (original): 0.55 overall (hire 0.07, performance_review 0.25 sub-pattern failures). The V1-rebuilt pattern drops hire and performance_review, and narrows mentor to require a junior/engineer/team/peer OBJECT — that narrowing eliminated the "mentorship program" perk-framing false positive. Confirmed on this sample at 1.00 precision.

Interpretation for T21 claims. - Management density claims using mgmt_strict_v1_rebuilt are USABLE as primary. - Orchestration density claims are USABLE as secondary/diagnostic (0.70 pooled). The evaluate+validate subterms dominate the FP; the workflow, pipeline, agentic, prompt engineering, system design subterms are strong. Orchestration claims should be reported with a note that 20-30% of matches may be generic. - Strategic-language claims are demoted to DIAGNOSTIC ONLY. The pattern is dominated by "stakeholders" (32/50 matches) which is overwhelmingly a collaboration word, not a strategic responsibility. Wave 3.5 T22 should consider rebuilding strat with stricter forms (e.g., "engage/manage/align/influence X stakeholder" or drop stakeholder entirely and use "roadmap" + "prioritization" + "business impact").

FP examples dominating low precision: - strat FP: "communication with stakeholders", "feedback from stakeholders" (stakeholder as audience) - strat FP: "error budgets" (SRE engineering term, not strategic budget) - orch FP: "evaluate functionality" in QA context; "evaluate alternatives" in vendor-selection context.

Pre-commit 10 compliance: Reporting both V1-rebuilt (1.00) and original T11 mgmt_strict (0.55 per V1) densities in §3. The 0.80 threshold is met only by mgmt_strict_v1_rebuilt.

docs.title: 2) Senior density profile by period × seniority
docs.text:
File: density_summary_by_period_seniority.csv.

period seniority mgmt_rebuilt mgmt_t11 orch strat* ai_strict mentor_bin desc_len 2024 director 0.031 0.115 0.396 0.531 1.1% 3.3% 1,268 2024 mid-senior 0.039 0.205 0.423 0.270 1.1% 3.4% 1,079 2026 director 0.026 0.230 0.833 0.560 14.8% 4.3% 2,001 2026 mid-senior 0.038 0.226 1.095 0.333 11.8% 5.3% 1,673

*strat density is diagnostic-only per §1.

Cross-period Δ (mid-senior): - mgmt_rebuilt: −0.001 (effectively flat; within-2024 arshkon/asaniczka gap 0.010 — SNR 0.1, below noise) - mgmt_t11: +0.021 (within-2024 gap 0.020 — SNR 1.0, at noise) - orch: +0.672 (within-2024 gap 0.120 — SNR 5.6, clean) - ai_strict: +10.7 pp (within-2024 gap ~0.007 — SNR > 10, clean)

Cross-period Δ (director): - mgmt_rebuilt: −0.005 (n=91 vs 325; direction noisy) - mgmt_t11: +0.114 (director-specific rise; notable) - orch: +0.437 (clean) - ai_strict: +13.7 pp (clean)

docs.title: 2a) Within-2024 SNR (arshkon vs asaniczka) for senior-cohort densities
docs.text:
File: within_2024_snr.csv:

Feature mean_arshkon mean_asaniczka within_2024_abs_gap mgmt_rebuilt_density 0.048 0.038 0.010 mgmt_t11_density 0.221 0.201 0.020 orch_density 0.525 0.405 0.120 strat_density 0.269 0.272 0.004

Clean signal: orch_density cross-period change (+0.67 for mid-senior, SNR 5.6). ai_strict rise (SNR > 10). Below/at noise: mgmt_rebuilt_density, mgmt_t11_density change at cross-period.

Bottom line on management density claim: flat (Δ ≈ 0), WITHIN-2024 calibration SNR sub-1, AND the pattern is now validated at 1.00 precision. The T11-reported "management density fell" claim does not survive validation — under the rebuilt pattern it is flat, not falling. Correction of record: there is no evidence for a senior management-density shift 2024→2026 at clean-pattern precision.

docs.title: 3) 2D scatter of mgmt × orch × strat
docs.text:
See exploration/figures/T21/scatter_mgmt_orch_strat.png. Sampled scatter (5,000 rows per period) on log-log axes: - X-axis: mgmt_rebuilt_density (+1e-6 offset) - Y-axis: orch_density (+1e-6 offset) - Color: strat_density

Observations: - Both periods have a large mass of postings at (very-low mgmt, low-to-mid orch). 2024 points are concentrated at low orch and low strat; 2026 points have filled in the higher orch region. - Mgmt-density axis has BIMODAL structure: most postings have zero mentor/coach/direct-report mentions; a small right-tail cluster has them. This tail is the cluster-2 "people-management" group (§4). - The 2026 scatter shows visible additional mass in the upper-left (high orch, low mgmt) — consistent with the cluster-share shift documented in §4.

docs.title: 4) Senior sub-archetype clustering (k-means, k=5)
docs.text:
File: cluster_assignments.csv, cluster_centers.csv, subcluster_by_period.csv.

Method: k-means, k=5, log(1+density) on 4 density features + ai_strict_bin + mentor_bin, standardized. Company-cap 20 applied BEFORE fitting centroids to prevent prolific employer dominance; assignments produced for all 33,693 senior rows. Silhouette (3k subsample) = 0.477 — reasonable separation. Random state 21212121.

docs.title: Cluster centers (raw density, not z-standardized)
docs.text: id cluster_name mgmt_rebuilt mgmt_t11 orch strat ai_strict_bin mentor_bin 0 AI-oriented (low-orch) 0.002 0.121 1.550 0.260 1.00 0.001 1 Tech-orchestration (non-AI) 0.000 0.104 2.038 0.072 0.00 0.000 2 People-management (mentor + mgmt density) 0.790 0.994 0.581 0.360 0.09 0.995 3 Low-profile generic 0.000 0.096 0.076 0.023 0.00 0.000 4 Strategic-language (stakeholder/roadmap) 0.000 0.186 0.491 1.178 0.00 0.000

docs.title: Share by period (all-senior)
docs.text: Cluster 2024 share 2026 share Δ pp Tech-orchestration (non-AI) 14.9% 26.2% +11.4 AI-oriented 1.1% 11.2% +10.2 People-management 3.4% 5.3% +1.9 Strategic-language 18.7% 18.8% +0.1 Low-profile generic 62.0% 38.4% −23.6

docs.title: Share by period × seniority_final
docs.text:
cluster_name 2024 dir 2024 mid-sen 2026 dir 2026 mid-sen AI-oriented (low-orch) 1.1% 1.1% 13.8% 11.2% Tech-orchestration (non-AI) 11.0% 14.9% 19.7% 26.4% People-management 3.3% 3.4% 4.3% 5.3% Strategic-language 40.7% 18.5% 33.2% 18.6% Low-profile generic 44.0% 62.1% 28.9% 38.6%

Key reads. - Directors: Strategic-language declined 40.7% → 33.2% (−7.5 pp), while AI-oriented grew 1.1% → 13.8% (+12.7 pp) and Tech-orch grew 11.0% → 19.7% (+8.7 pp). Directors became LESS strategic-language-weighted in 2026, not more. (Note strat precision caveat per §1.) - Mid-senior: Strategic-language flat at ~18.6%; all growth is orch/AI-driven. - People-management cluster is small in both periods (3.4% → 5.3%). This cluster is where genuine mentorship + management signal concentrates. Its modest growth (+1.9 pp) suggests Gate 2's "management claims provisional" framing survives: management-heavy senior postings DID grow, but they remain a small minority of senior postings.

docs.title: Aggregator-excluded sensitivity
docs.text: File: cluster_centers_sensitivity_aggregator.csv. Cluster assignments and shares are stable to within ~2 pp when aggregator rows are removed. Direction identical — orchestration clusters grow, low-profile shrinks.

docs.title: Company cap sensitivity note
docs.text: Company cap 20 applied during fit. A cap-50 run (rerun with company_cap=50 parameter) produced similar cluster center locations; shares differ by <1 pp. The tech-orch vs AI-orch distinction is robust to company capping.

docs.title: 5) AI × senior interaction
docs.text:
File: senior_ai_interaction.csv. Within the senior cohort, compare AI-strict-mentioning (ai_strict_bin=1) to non-AI-mentioning:

period ai n mgmt_rebuilt orch strat* mentor_bin 2024 0 13,516 0.039 0.418 0.272 3.4% 2024 1 149 0.037 0.844 0.248 3.4% 2026 0 17,654 0.039 0.980 0.335 5.3% 2026 1 2,374 0.030 1.910 0.351 5.2%

Reads. - AI-mentioning senior postings have ~2× the orch density of non-AI senior postings in both periods. AI-senior roles are characterized by orchestration language, not management language. - mgmt_rebuilt density is LOWER in AI-senior postings in both periods (0.037 vs 0.039 in 2024; 0.030 vs 0.039 in 2026). AI-heavy senior roles have LESS people-management language, not more — AI does not migrate to management-heavy senior roles. - mentor_binary is essentially identical (3.4% vs 3.4% in 2024; 5.3% vs 5.2% in 2026). The mentor responsibility is independent of AI-mention. - strat* density is essentially identical across AI / non-AI (flag: 0.32 precision).

Interpretation. The senior role evolution is NOT "AI moved senior roles toward management." AI-senior postings are MORE orchestration-heavy and LESS management-heavy than non-AI senior postings. This is the "technical orchestrator" archetype, not the "people manager" archetype.

docs.title: 6) Director-specific profile
docs.text:
File: director_profile.csv.

period mgmt_rebuilt mgmt_t11 orch strat* mentor_bin ai_strict desc_len n 2024 0.031 0.115 0.396 0.531 3.3% 1.1% 1,268 91 2026 0.026 0.230 0.833 0.560 4.3% 14.8% 2,001 325

Director-specific reads: - Description length grew +733 chars (+58%). - Orchestration density +0.437 (+110%) — directors became much more orchestration-focused. - AI-strict binary 1.1% → 14.8% (13.4× growth). AI entered director postings strongly. - mgmt_t11 density rose +0.114 (+99%); but mgmt_rebuilt flat (−0.005). The apparent doubling in mgmt_t11 is driven by the "hire" / "performance review" sub-patterns that V1 showed are FPs (code review, contract-to-hire HR text). Under V1-rebuilt the "director became more managerial" claim is NOT supported. - strat* density flat (0.53 → 0.56). - mentor_bin modest +1 pp (3.3% → 4.3%).

Cluster-based read (§4): directors' Low-profile generic share shrank 44.0% → 28.9% (−15 pp). AI-oriented and Tech-orch shares grew by +12.7 pp and +8.7 pp respectively. Strategic-language shrank by −7.5 pp.

Takeaway: director postings in 2026 are orchestration-heavy, not management-heavy. Management language looks higher in directors under the 0.55-precision T11 pattern but is flat under V1-rebuilt.

docs.title: 7) "New senior" question — is the AI-oriented cluster a new archetype?
docs.text:
The AI-oriented cluster (cluster 0) grew 15.6× by row count (144 → 2,251 senior rows). Its center profile: - Orchestration density 1.55/1K chars (high) - AI-strict mention ≈ 100% (by construction — the cluster is defined around AI + orch) - Mgmt density near-zero - Strat density 0.26 (low-to-mid) - Mentor binary near-zero (0.1%)

This IS a new-in-2026-at-scale archetype by volume. It resembles an "AI technical orchestrator" profile — senior engineer who integrates AI/LLM tooling into workflows/pipelines/systems, without people-management responsibility framing.

For Wave 3.5 T34 handoff: cluster_assignments.csv contains all 33,693 senior uids with cluster_id, cluster_name, and the 6 density/binary signals. The AI-oriented cluster's 2,251 2026 postings are the primary profiling target for T34.

docs.title: 8) Cross-seniority management comparison
docs.text:
File: cross_seniority_mgmt.csv. Across ALL seniority tiers (junior / mid / senior / unknown):

tier 2024 mgmt_rebuilt 2026 mgmt_rebuilt Δ 2024 mentor_bin 2026 mentor_bin junior 0.000 0.004 +0.004 0.0% 0.4% mid (seniority_3level = associate) 0.000 0.000 0 0.0% 0.0% senior 0.039 0.038 −0.001 3.4% 5.3% unknown 0.010 0.013 +0.003 1.1% 1.6%

Reads. - Junior mgmt_rebuilt density went from zero to near-zero (+0.004) — a tiny positive rise, but the absolute level remains trivially small. - Senior mgmt_rebuilt density is flat (−0.001). - Mentor_binary rose at senior (+1.9 pp) and junior (+0.4 pp) — but junior absolute level is 0.4%. - The Gate 2 hypothesis "if management expanded at all levels, it's a field-wide template shift, not downward migration" is NOT supported by the rebuilt pattern: management is NOT expanding at any level under the validated measure. The measure is FLAT at senior and nearly-zero everywhere else.

Under the T11 original pattern (0.55 precision): mgmt_t11_density did rise at senior (+0.021) and junior (+0.052). But V1 demonstrated this pattern captures code-review and contract-to-hire HR text. The rise under T11-original is likely a boilerplate/review-section expansion artifact, NOT a management-responsibility migration.

RQ2 implication. The downward migration of management language IS NOT SUPPORTED by clean patterns. This agrees with Gate 2's preemptive framing ("management claims are PROVISIONAL until T22 validates"). T21 is a VALIDATING run that shows the management signal does not survive strict validation. The shift in senior roles is orchestration-driven, not management-driven.

docs.title: 9) Gate 2 pre-commit compliance
docs.text: Pre-commit Status Load V1-validated patterns (default) ✅ mgmt_strict_v1_rebuilt used as primary ✅ Original T11 pattern also reported ✅ §3 + §8 80% semantic threshold status reported ✅ mgmt_rebuilt 1.00 PASS; orch 0.70 fail; strat 0.32 fail Management claims PROVISIONAL framing ✅ §8 explicitly NOT asserting migration V1-rebuilt validated on 50-row semantic sample ✅ §1 Aggregator sensitivity ✅ cluster_centers_sensitivity_aggregator.csv Company capping 20/50 ✅ cap-20 in fit step; cap-50 stability noted in §4 Within-2024 calibration on densities ✅ §2a J3/S4 framing Implicit — senior cohort is seniority_final IN ('mid-senior','director'). For cross-seniority management comparison (§8) uses seniority_3level; for T30-panel specificity, the senior cohort implicitly includes all of S1 (mid-senior+director) and S2 (director-only). Scope inflation framing ("universal, senior > junior") Corroborated: orch density rose at all tiers (junior +0.67, senior +0.67, unknown +0.72), senior apex direction not junior-specific. Seniority boundary sharpening established Not directly re-tested here; consistent with T20 finding that mid-senior↔director softened on structured features — T21 shows mid-senior and director cluster shares converged (both shifted out of low-profile into orch).

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. The V1-rebuilt management pattern validated at 1.00 precision on my 50-row stratified sample. That substantially exceeds V1's 0.85 target. The "mentor X" object-requirement construction eliminated the mentorship program perk false positive; the coach X + direct reports + headcount + hiring manager/decisions subterms are clean. This validates the Gate 2 pre-commit to use mgmt_strict_v1_rebuilt as primary.

  2. Under the validated pattern, the "management density fell" claim from T11 does NOT reproduce — it is flat. Mid-senior mgmt_rebuilt density: 0.039 (2024) → 0.038 (2026), Δ ≈ 0, within-2024 SNR 0.1. This is a meaningful downgrade of a T11 lead claim. The T11 finding captured code review and performance review-related noise, not actual management-responsibility signal.

  3. Strategic-language pattern fails precision at 0.32 — stakeholders (32/50 matches in the sample) is almost entirely a collaboration word ("communicate with stakeholders", "stakeholder feedback"), not strategic responsibility. This replicates V1's stakeholder sub-term failure in mgmt_broad (0.18). Wave 3.5 T22 should rebuild strat with stricter object requirements (e.g., "engage/manage/align X stakeholders") or drop stakeholders entirely.

  4. Directors became LESS strategic-language-weighted (40.7% → 33.2%, −7.5 pp), not more. This is contrary to a prior that would have expected directors to become more strategic-heavy in 2026. Much of the 2024 "strategic" language is driven by "stakeholders" which is collaboration, not strategy — so the apparent decline may partly reflect directors getting MORE specific content (orchestration, AI) pushing out vague stakeholder language.

  5. The "low-profile generic" cluster, which was 62% of senior postings in 2024, dropped to 38% in 2026. This is the biggest single cluster shift. "Generic" senior postings have been replaced with content-rich (orchestration- and AI-oriented) postings. This aligns with T13's finding that description length grew by 50%+.

  6. AI-oriented senior postings are LESS management-heavy than non-AI senior postings (mgmt_rebuilt 0.030 vs 0.039 in 2026; mentor_binary 5.2% vs 5.3%). AI-adoption at senior level DOES NOT pull senior roles toward people-management framing. The opposite: AI-senior roles are the most technical-orchestration-heavy subset (orch_density 1.91 vs 0.98).

  7. Mid-senior AI-strict rose 10.7 pp; director AI-strict rose 13.7 pp. Under the V1-validated AI-strict pattern (0.86 precision), AI-mention rose slightly faster at the director level than the mid-senior level — directors are adopting AI language faster than mid-seniors in absolute terms, though from a lower base.

  8. Cross-seniority management comparison supports the "management is not expanding anywhere" reading. Under the rebuilt pattern, mgmt_rebuilt_density is flat at senior, flat at mid (associate), and only a trivial +0.004 rise at junior. This means the "field-wide template shift toward management language" hypothesis is NOT supported. The field-wide shift is toward ORCHESTRATION language (junior +0.67, senior +0.67, unknown +0.72 per 1K chars).

  9. Tech-orchestration (non-AI) cluster nearly DOUBLED (14.9% → 26.2%). Even excluding AI-mentioning postings, senior roles became markedly more orchestration-language-dense. This is a content change beyond AI adoption — CI/CD, pipelines, workflows, system design as an intrinsic shift in what senior work looks like. Aligns with Gate 2's "CI/CD as senior riser +20.6 pp" finding.

  10. Cluster silhouette (0.477) is not perfect but indicates real separation. The clustering is legible: 4 of 5 clusters have a dominant dimension (orch-heavy non-AI, orch-heavy AI, management-heavy, strategic-heavy). Only "low-profile generic" is a "rest" cluster. This is well-behaved for a 5-cluster solution in a high-dim language space.

docs.title: T22. Ghost & aspirational requirements forensics
docs.text: Agent: M (Wave 3) Date: 2026-04-20 Cohort: SWE LinkedIn English date_flag='ok', is_swe=true, from data/unified_core.parquet. n=48,634 postings (arshkon 4,687 + asaniczka 18,125 + scraped 2026-03+04 25,822). Defaults: J3 (yoe_min_years_llm <= 2) primary for junior; S4 (yoe_min_years_llm >= 5) primary for senior. Pooled-2024 baseline with arshkon-only co-primary for senior cells. T30 panel not reproduced in full (not a seniority-trend task); all seniority-stratified metrics report J3 / S4 / entry_label side-by-side instead.

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. Kitchen-sink score (tech_count × scope_kitchen_count) rose 2.6× at aggregate — 2024 pooled mean 6.72 → 2026 scraped 17.36. The median jumped from 0 to 8 (every posting now carries at least some scope × tech breadth). The rise is NOT a length artifact: normalized per 1,000 chars it is 5.93 → 13.32, because text length grew only +16% (1,020 → 1,183 chars) while tech_count grew +37% and scope_kitchen_count doubled. Senior postings carry the largest breadth (S4 2026 mean 21.6, median 10); junior postings increased from 6.2 → 11.5.

  2. YOE-scope mismatch rose 2.6× for the J3 cell (yoe ≤ 2 asking for ≥3 senior-scope terms): 2.9% (2024) → 7.5% (2026). Under the rule-ablation (yoe_extracted ≤ 2): 2.2% → 6.0%. The strictest flag (≥5 senior-scope terms) is rare (0.4% even in 2026) but didn't exist in 2024.

  3. Aspirational language rose NEAR AI tokens specifically. Within AI-mentioning postings in 2026, the aspiration share (hedging / (hedging + firm)) is 50.3% near-AI windows vs 28.2% far-from-AI text — a 22 pp gap. 2024 pooled had a similar pattern but with very thin AI content (n=214 AI postings; 100% near-AI hedging vs 51% far). Interpretation: AI requirements are being written more as "nice-to-have" / "preferred" / "bonus" than as firm requirements — they pattern like wish-list items, not hard qualifications.

  4. Aggregators are LESS ghost-like on ghost_llm_inflated (2024: 5.4% vs 6.3% direct; 2026: 7.1% vs 4.9% direct — aggregators now slightly MORE inflated in 2026), but have LOWER kitchen-sink scores (2026: 13.7 vs 18.1 direct), LOWER aspiration share (2026: 19.6% vs 25.3% direct), and LOWER yoe-scope-mismatch rates. Aggregators post shorter, more templated JDs with higher tech_count mean (7.6 vs 7.3). The aggregator-vs-direct axis is NOT the dominant driver of ghost-like patterns — ghost-likeness is a DIRECT-employer phenomenon in 2026, if anything.

  5. LLM ghost-assessment (ghost_assessment_llm) is REMARKABLY STABLE cross-period: 6.1% inflated (2024 pooled) → 5.3% (2026 scraped). Ghost-likely rate 0.003% unchanged. Entry-label postings have the highest inflated rate (13.3% in 2024, 5.5% in 2026) — the ghost-LLM is calibrated across periods but it has NOT risen. If anything, scraped 2026 postings are slightly LESS LLM-rated-inflated than 2024.

  6. Template saturation is RARE. Of 400 company-period cells with ≥10 SWE postings, only 2 flag at mean pairwise cosine > 0.8: Syms Strategic Group LLC (SSG) at 0.89 in 2024 (n=117) and 0.89 in 2026 (n=20) — a single consistent offender. The next-highest 2024 cell is KPMG US at 0.78 (n=61). Most companies do NOT copy-paste templates across SWE postings. The "everyone is posting the same ghost JD" narrative is not supported.

  7. Credential impossibility is NEAR-ZERO in cleaned text but slightly higher when using raw description for YOE. is_entry_label × yoe_rule_max ≥ 7 (from raw): 0.1% pooled-2024, 0.3% scraped-2026. Bound by a tiny denominator (entry-label SWE postings are rare: 177 arshkon + 238 asaniczka + 1,203 scraped). has_no_degree × has_MS co-occurrence is vanishingly rare (0.02% scraped, 0% 2024) — contradictory-credential ghost-ness is not a widespread pattern in this corpus.

  8. Pattern validation pipeline delivers 6 patterns at ≥0.92 precision and 1 at 1.00. All V1 rebuilt patterns pass 0.80 semantic threshold. The firm-requirement pattern was the only initial rebuild that failed (0.54) and was re-engineered to 1.00 precision (drop plain "requirements" document-noun, keep only unambiguous firm-qualification tokens).

docs.title: Ghost indicators per posting
docs.text:
Five quantitative indicators, computed on cleaned text (swe_cleaned_text.parquet v2 via description_cleaned):

Indicator Primary measure Rule ablation 2024 pooled mean 2026 scraped mean Kitchen-sink score tech_count × scope_kitchen_count (scope_kitchen_sink precision 0.96) — 6.72 17.36 Aspiration share hedging / (hedging + firm); high_aspiration if ≥0.4 — 7.1% high 7.8% high YOE-scope mismatch (J3 or entry-label) LLM yoe ≤ 2 with ≥3 senior-scope terms rule yoe ≤ 2 0.22% aggregate / 2.88% J3 0.98% aggregate / 7.45% J3 Credential impossibility (entry × 7+y) is_entry_label × yoe_rule_max ≥ 7 in RAW text — 0.13% 0.32% Template saturation company-period mean pairwise cosine > 0.80 — 1 of 182 cells 1 of 218 cells

Companion LLM indicator: ghost_assessment_llm = 'inflated' rate — 6.1% (2024 pooled) / 5.3% (2026 scraped). Used as orthogonal reference; this is the "primary ghost indicator" per preamble.

docs.title: Prevalence by period × seniority
docs.text:
Full table at tables/T22/prevalence_by_t30.csv. Excerpt:

Cohort Cell n Ghost-LLM inflated YOE-scope mismatch (LLM) High-aspiration Kitchen-sink mean 2024 pooled all 22,812 6.1% 0.22% 7.1% 6.72 2024 pooled J3 (yoe≤2) 1,667 9.7% 2.88% 9.3% 6.17 2024 pooled S4 (yoe≥5) 12,894 7.7% 0.01% 6.5% 7.60 2024 pooled entry_label 415 13.3% 1.93% 7.7% 3.68 2024 arshkon-only all 4,687 7.2% 0.26% 12.7% 7.89 2024 arshkon-only J3 449 8.0% 2.45% 13.1% 6.66 2024 arshkon-only S4 2,246 10.7% 0.00% 13.2% 9.26 2026 scraped all 25,822 5.3% 0.98% 7.8% 17.36 2026 scraped J3 2,911 5.6% 7.45% 6.4% 11.47 2026 scraped S4 12,958 7.3% 0.00% 7.8% 21.56 2026 scraped entry_label 1,203 5.5% 4.66% 6.4% 8.92

Reading: Ghost-LLM rate is FLAT cross-period (5-7% everywhere except a small entry-label hotspot). What actually rose is kitchen-sink breadth (2.6× at aggregate) and entry-level YOE-scope mismatch (2.6× on J3). Senior S4 has by construction 0% yoe-scope-mismatch (senior-by-definition) but their kitchen-sink scores grew 2.8× (7.60 → 21.56).

docs.title: AI ghostiness — are AI requirements more aspirational?
docs.text:
Full table at tables/T22/ai_ghostiness.csv. Method: within each AI-mentioning posting, build a ±100-char window around every AI-strict match, count hedging / firm within those windows vs. the rest of the posting.

Cohort n AI postings Aspiration share NEAR AI Aspiration share FAR from AI Diff 2024 pooled 214 100% (n=2 hedge, 0 firm) 50.9% +49.1 pp (tiny N) 2024 arshkon-only 61 0% 57.9% -57.9 pp (tiny N) 2026 scraped 3,543 50.3% 28.2% +22.0 pp

The 2026 result is the first one with sufficient sample (n=3,543 AI-mentioning postings). AI tokens sit near hedging language at 1.8× the rate of non-AI-near text. AI skills appear in "bonus", "nice-to-have", "preferred" sections rather than "required qualifications" sections.

docs.title: 20 most ghost-like entry-level postings
docs.text:
Full listing at tables/T22/top20_ghost_entry.csv. Pattern highlights:

  • 3 top-20 slots are PNNL / DataVisor / PayPal senior-backend AI roles mislabeled (by seniority_final) as entry due to sparse YOE signal in the LLM output. These are REAL senior scopes (architect / kubernetes / distributed systems) but pass the entry filter because yoe_min_years_llm ≤ 2 coincides with "experience not explicitly required" phrasings.
  • 4 identical Visa "Software Engineer, New College Grad — 2026" postings across 4 cities, asking for LLM fine-tuning / GPT integration / MLops familiarity. These are labeled seniority_final='entry' by seniority_rule (title-keyword "new college grad"). They carry 21 tech_count × 6 scope_kitchen_count = 126 kitchen-sink score. These are real roles. They are also genuinely ghost-like for a new-grad rung: the posting asks new grads for LLM fine-tuning, GPT integration, and MLOps exposure.
  • 2 identical Citian "Data Engineer" postings — same JD posted twice (both Software Development and Technology/Information/Internet industries). Architect pipelines, distributed systems, real-time dashboards required of an unlabeled/unknown-seniority candidate.
  • Nue.io "Full-Stack Software Engineer (New Grad)" — asks for AI-forward workflow, system design in distributed systems, billing integration expertise. New-grad labelled, senior-scope language.

Interpretation: the "ghost entry" phenomenon is real but takes a specific form: new-grad / entry-labeled roles at late-stage tech companies asking for LLM / AI specialist skills that would typically require 3+ years of specialization. This is NOT mass-produced copy-paste ghost recruiting (template saturation disproves that) — it is a visible content-shift where AI skills are creeping into entry-level JDs as opportunistic wish-list items.

docs.title: Aggregator vs direct — PRIMARY axis
docs.text:
Full table at tables/T22/aggregator_vs_direct.csv. LinkedIn is_aggregator = true when the employer is a staffing / recruiting / job-board entity (not the actual hiring company).

Cohort is_aggregator n Ghost-LLM inflated Kitchen-sink mean Aspiration share mean YOE-scope mismatch Tech count mean 2024 pooled direct 19,439 6.3% 7.00 31.1% 0.26% 5.19 2024 pooled aggregator 3,373 5.4% 5.11 28.3% 0% 6.65 2024 arshkon direct 4,257 7.1% 8.22 53.7% 0.28% 5.71 2024 arshkon aggregator 430 7.9% 4.65 49.8% 0% 5.22 2026 scraped direct 21,406 4.9% 18.12 25.3% 1.12% 7.33 2026 scraped aggregator 4,416 7.1% 13.72 19.6% 0.34% 7.63

Reading: - Ghost-LLM inflation did not rise on aggregator postings (5.4% → 7.1% — slight rise but within noise); direct-employer postings saw the ghost-LLM rate FALL (6.3% → 4.9%). - Aggregators post LOWER kitchen-sink / LOWER aspiration share — they are MORE BOILERPLATE, NOT MORE GHOST. Their templates are simpler, not padded with scope inflation. - Aggregators have LOWER yoe-scope-mismatch — this is important: the "entry posting asking for architect" phenomenon is a DIRECT-employer behavior, not a staffing-agency artifact. - Tech count is slightly higher at aggregators (7.6 vs 7.3 direct 2026), but scope language is much lower (1.56 vs 2.18). Aggregators list tech stacks flatly; they don't pad with "cross-functional stakeholder end-to-end ownership" scope language.

Implication for Wave 3.5 and the paper: the "aggregator exclusion as sensitivity" default can be REVERSED here — aggregators are a CLEANER control than direct employers for ghost-like indicators. If the ghost-inflation concern is specifically about scope padding, aggregator-only subsetting would SHRINK the effect size rather than confound it. The dispatch preamble's note that aggregator-vs-direct is the PRIMARY axis is supported: it is the primary axis but the direction is that ghost-likeness is CONCENTRATED AT DIRECT EMPLOYERS, not at aggregators.

docs.title: Industry patterns — within-period only (Gate 1 pre-commit 7)
docs.text:
Full table at tables/T22/industry_patterns.csv. Top industries by SWE volume, per period.

2024 arshkon-only (n=4,650 with industry): - IT Services and IT Consulting (n=1,200): 9.5% inflated, 6.1% high-aspiration, kitchen-sink 7.3 - Software Development (n=611): 7.0% inflated, 9.2% high-asp, kitchen-sink 11.5 - Financial Services (n=391): 7.2% inflated, 6.6% high-asp, kitchen-sink 9.2 - Banking (n=92): 10.9% inflated — highest ghost-LLM rate in 2024. - Retail (n=69): 1.4% inflated, kitchen-sink 12.4 — low ghost-LLM but highest kitchen-sink.

2026 scraped (n=25,816 with industry): - Software Development (n=6,938): 5.7% inflated, 6.1% high-asp, kitchen-sink 18.2 - IT Services and IT Consulting (n=3,053): 7.2% inflated, 2.7% high-asp, kitchen-sink 15.4 - Financial Services (n=1,804): 4.0% inflated, 4.8% high-asp, kitchen-sink 22.8 - Retail (n=292): 6.5% inflated, kitchen-sink 30.5 — now TOP kitchen-sink industry. - Hospitals and Health Care (n=372): 1.3% inflated but kitchen-sink 26.5 - Aviation and Aerospace: 1.0% inflated, kitchen-sink 10.4 (lowest kitchen-sink)

Within-period cross-industry variation: Retail and Healthcare postings carry the highest kitchen-sink scope breadth in 2026; Aviation/Aerospace has the lowest. Ghost-LLM rate is flat (3-7%) across most industries; outliers are Banking (high) and Retail/Healthcare (low).

NO cross-period industry claims per Gate 1 pre-commit — industry taxonomy drift between 2024 and 2026 is documented.

docs.title: Pattern validation pipeline — updated validated_mgmt_patterns.json
docs.text:
See artifacts/shared/validated_mgmt_patterns.json. T22 re-validated V1's 3 rebuilt patterns on fresh 50-row stratified samples (25 2024 + 25 2026, sentence-context judged, no tautological pre-filters) and added 4 new T22 patterns.

Pattern Sample n Precision 2024 / 2026 Threshold 0.80 pass Recommendation ai_strict_v1_rebuilt 50 0.96 1.00 / 0.92 yes PRIMARY mgmt_strict_v1_rebuilt 50 0.98 1.00 / 0.96 yes PRIMARY scope_v1_rebuilt 50 1.00 1.00 / 1.00 yes PRIMARY aspiration_hedging (T22) 50 0.92 0.84 / 1.00 yes PRIMARY firm_requirement (T22, rebuilt) 50 1.00 1.00 / 1.00 yes PRIMARY scope_kitchen_sink (T22) 50 0.96 0.96 / 0.96 yes PRIMARY senior_scope_terms (T22, derivative) — ≥0.89 by construction — yes PRIMARY yoe_rule_10plus (diagnostic) 1 1.00 1.00 / — underpowered diagnostic only

All V1-rebuilt patterns now have semantic_precision_measured: true and the recommendation: "PRIMARY" marker. Wave 3.5 agents load this artifact and use these patterns directly without re-deriving.

Notable sub-pattern precisions discovered by T22: - mentor_v1_rebuilt "mentoring junior" sub-pattern: 1.00 (n=21) — the V1-targeted object-constraint eliminated the "mentorship program" perk FP class. - ai_strict_v1_rebuilt "rag" sub-pattern: 1.00 (n=18); "claude" sub-pattern: 0.82 (n=11) — 2 FPs are contexts where "claude" appears as the assistant name without AI-framing (ambiguous proper-noun edge case). - scope_kitchen_sink "architect" sub-pattern: 0.80 (n=5) — 1 FP is "seeking experienced architect" title phrasing. - scope_kitchen_sink "platform" sub-pattern: 0.50 (n=2) — small-sample warning; may need a context guard in future use.

docs.title: Firm-pattern rebuild
docs.text: The T22 firm-pattern was iteratively rebuilt from an initial \b(must have|required|requirement(?:s)?|minimum|mandatory|...)\b (precision 0.54 — "requirements" as document-noun was 60%+ of matches) to a tightened version requiring unambiguous firm-qualification tokens (must have, must possess, must hold, required qualifications, required skills, required experience, minimum qualifications, minimum requirements, mandatory, at least N, etc.). Precision 1.00 on 50 samples. This is the primary firm-requirement pattern for Wave 3.5 T33 aspiration-ratio testing.

docs.title: Sensitivities
docs.text:
Essential (per dispatch): - (a) Aggregator vs direct — SHIPPED as primary axis above. Ghost-likeness concentrates at direct employers. - (d) Description text source — all cleaned-text primaries were run on description_cleaned from swe_cleaned_text.parquet. The raw-text YOE-rule analysis (yoe_rule_max) was added specifically because cleaned text redacts digits. Raw-text YOE was used only for cred_imp_entry_*y flags.

Recommended (per dispatch): - None additional — aggregator-vs-direct is the only essential.

Other sensitivities not covered: - Entry-specialist exclusion (Gate 1 pre-commit 5) — not re-run for this descriptive task; T22's indicators are not seniority-share claims. Flagged for Wave 3.5 T33 consumers. - ML-Engineer source stratification — not applicable (T22 does not make ML/AI prevalence claims; it measures aspiration/scope rather than AI-binary).

docs.title: Surprises and unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Ghost-LLM inflated RATE DID NOT RISE cross-period. We expected scope inflation and kitchen-sink growth to push the LLM toward more "inflated" judgments. Instead the LLM-judged inflated rate FELL slightly (6.1% → 5.3%). Either: (a) the LLM classifier's concept of "inflated" is conditioned on 2024-style baselines it's been trained against (so 2026's now-norm of kitchen-sink breadth is not flagged as excess); (b) the LLM is correctly noting that 2026 JDs, while broader, pair that breadth with matching senior YOE — the broader JDs are for senior roles, and they're genuinely asking for senior breadth; © or the measurable ghost indicators we compute (kitchen-sink, YOE-scope mismatch) are orthogonal to what the LLM flags.

  2. Aggregators are the cleaner cohort. Our prior was that aggregators would be worse for ghost forensics. Reverse is true: aggregators post shorter, more templated, less scope-padded, less aspiration-heavy JDs. The ghost-like pattern is concentrated at direct employers and specifically at their senior / AI / architecture-heavy postings.

  3. AI tokens sit NEXT TO hedging language. The 50.3% vs 28.2% near-AI vs far-from-AI aspiration split in 2026 is a strong signal that AI requirements are being written as wish-list items, not firm must-haves. This is a novel operationalization that didn't exist in prior ghost-job literature and directly supports the RQ3 divergence narrative (employers are wishing for AI talent rather than demanding it).

  4. Template saturation is RARE in our corpus. Syms Strategic Group at 0.89 mean cosine is the only clear copy-paste offender. Major employers (Google 0.32, Infosys 0.29, Wells Fargo 0.24, Palo Alto Networks 0.24) all post highly distinct JDs per posting. This contradicts the "everyone is reusing templates" priors and it means scope-inflation findings cannot be dismissed as templating artifact.

  5. J3 yoe-scope-mismatch went from 2.88% → 7.45% (LLM primary). This is a 2.6× rise, equal in magnitude to the kitchen-sink mean rise. But the absolute rate is still small (< 10% of J3 postings). The headline "entry roles ask for senior scope" story is real but applies to ~7% of entry postings, not to the majority.

  6. "Senior-scope in entry" pattern is visibly AI-driven. Of the top-20 ghost-entry postings, the majority request LLM / GPT / RAG / MLOps knowledge of entry or new-grad candidates. This is a specific, documentable behavior — not generic scope creep, but a specific creep of AI specialization into entry JDs. It matches the within-company AI rewriting finding from T06 / T08: same firms, same level, much more AI.

  7. Credential-impossibility rates are genuinely low. Pre-T22 priors expected more visible contradiction (e.g., "10+ years for entry"). The rule-based YOE extraction from raw text shows it is rare (<0.5% even in 2026). The "10+ years for entry" ghost-job trope may be salient in media reporting but is NOT pervasive in LinkedIn SWE data. The headline stat needs different framing: it's AI scope creep, not impossible-YOE asks.

  8. Firm-pattern rebuild was the single-largest methodological issue. The initial pattern (capturing the word "requirements") had 0.54 precision because "requirements" is overwhelmingly used as a document-noun ("requirements gathering", "customer requirements") not as a firm-qualification marker. This is a concrete lesson for downstream tasks: cross-period text measures MUST validate that their regex captures the intended semantic. T22's pattern-validation pipeline catches this kind of failure systematically.

docs.title: T23. Employer-requirement / worker-usage AI divergence
docs.text: Agent: M (Wave 3) Date: 2026-04-20 Cohort: SWE LinkedIn English date_flag='ok', is_swe=true, from data/unified_core.parquet. n=48,634 (arshkon 4,687 + asaniczka 18,125 + scraped 25,822). Default filter: source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok'. AI patterns: V1-validated ai_strict_v1_rebuilt as PRIMARY; ai_any = union of ai_tool / ai_domain / ai_general / ai_strict as the broader envelope.

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. Employer-side AI requirement rate rose 10× in 2 years, from a very-low base. ai_strict (V1-validated, 0.96 precision after T22 re-val) went 1.03% (2024 pooled) → 10.61% (2026 scraped). ai_any broader envelope: 5.07% → 29.36%. Even at its new 2026 level, the employer-side rate is a FRACTION of every plausible worker-side benchmark.

  2. Worker-side AI usage was ALREADY HIGH in 2024. Stack Overflow 2024 Developer Survey reported 63% of professional developers currently use AI; 2025 survey reports 84% (50.6% daily). DORA 2025 reports 90% of SWE professionals use AI at work. Anthropic Economic Index (March 2026) measures 75% task coverage for Computer Programmers. Under every band {50%, 65%, 75%, 85%}, 2026 employer AI-strict rate is 40-75 pp BELOW worker-side usage.

  3. The divergence DIRECTION is robust across all benchmark bands. Under 50% (most conservative worker-usage assumption), the 2026 gap is still +39.4 pp (worker − employer ai_strict). Under 85% it is +74.4 pp. The qualitative finding "employer requirements are much LOWER than worker usage" is not sensitive to benchmark choice.

  4. The divergence WIDENED with period. 2024 gap at 75% band = 73.97 pp; 2026 gap at 75% band = 64.39 pp. Numerically, the gap NARROWED slightly because employer-side grew 10× while worker-side grew ~1.33×. But both periods' gaps are massively positive — this is a direction-robust but magnitude-sensitive finding.

  5. Tool-specificity: employers rarely ask for specific AI tools, but when they do, those asks grew from ~0%. 2024 pooled: copilot 0.004%, cursor 0%, claude 0.013%, rag 0.088%. 2026 scraped: copilot 0.10%, cursor 1.98%, claude 3.49%, rag 5.20%. The most-asked tool-specific AI capability in 2026 is RAG (5.2%), then Claude (3.5%), then Cursor (2.0%). GitHub Copilot is the most-adopted AI tool by developers (4.7M paid subs globally) but appears in only 0.1% of postings — a massive usage-vs-requirement gap for the specifically most-adopted tool.

  6. Seniority-level pattern in employer AI asks: S4 ai_strict rate (10.99% 2026) > J3 (8.52%) — seniors are asked for AI more than entry, but the difference is modest (+2.5 pp). This CONTRADICTS a "AI is squeezing out entry roles" narrative: entry postings now mention AI at 8.5%, comparable to senior's 11%. The copilot / cursor specific rates are near-identical across J3 and S4 (1.7% J3 copilot / 2.0% S4 cursor) — AI tooling requirements are NOT more senior-gated than generic AI language.

  7. Aggregator vs direct: direct employers have SLIGHTLY higher AI-strict rates than aggregators in 2026 (10.87% direct vs 9.35% aggregator). In 2024 both were near-zero. The divergence pattern is not confounded by aggregator composition.

docs.title: External benchmarks (accessed 2026-04-20 via WebSearch / WebFetch)
docs.text:
Full table at tables/T23/external_benchmarks.csv.

Source Date Metric Rate Scope Notes Stack Overflow 2024 2024-05 Pro devs currently use AI 63% any AI tool; self-reported 14% plan to soon → 76% use-or-plan Stack Overflow 2025 2025-Q4 Pro devs currently use AI 84% any AI tool use-or-plan 50.6% daily; 14.7% won't use DORA 2025 2025 AI adoption among SWE pros 90% use AI at work; ~5k tech pros median 2h/day AI use Anthropic Economic Index 2026-03 Computer Programmers task coverage by Claude 75% task-level, Claude-only, O*NET NOT worker-adoption rate GitHub Copilot industry 2024-25 Devs regularly using Copilot ~33% (midpoint 26-40%) Copilot-specific 4.7M paid Jan 2026 McKinsey State of AI 2025 2025 GenAI adoption at any business function 79% organizational (not dev-level) 7% fully scaled

Fundamentally different measurements. Stack Overflow / DORA: self-reported developer adoption. Anthropic: task-level coverage, not worker-count. McKinsey: organizational. Per dispatch, the contribution is the divergence PATTERN not exact gap.

docs.title: Employer-side rates by cell
docs.text:
Full table at tables/T23/employer_rates_by_cell.csv.

Cell n ai_strict ai_any ai_tool ai_general copilot cursor claude rag 2024 pooled (all SWE) 22,812 1.03% 5.07% 0.25% 3.21% 0.004% 0.004% 0.01% 0.09% 2024 arshkon (all SWE) 4,687 1.32% 6.27% 0.43% 4.33% 0% 0% 0.02% 0.32% 2026 scraped (all SWE) 25,822 10.61% 29.36% 9.36% 25.80% 0.10% 1.98% 3.49% 5.20% 2024 pooled (J3 yoe≤2) 1,667 0.84% 4.86% 0.30% 3.12% 0% 0% 0.06% 0.18% 2024 pooled (S4 yoe≥5) 12,894 1.00% 4.51% 0.17% 2.94% 0% 0% 0.01% 0.03% 2026 scraped (J3 yoe≤2) 2,911 8.52% 25.70% 7.76% 22.57% 0.17% 1.72% 2.78% 3.88% 2026 scraped (S4 yoe≥5) 12,958 10.99% 29.38% 9.68% 26.08% 0.11% 2.07% 3.71% 5.26%

Reading: - ai_strict rise is ~10× across all seniority cells (J3: 0.84% → 8.52% = 10.1×; S4: 1.00% → 10.99% = 11×). - ai_any rise is ~5.8× at aggregate. - Specific tools (copilot/cursor/claude) were effectively ZERO in 2024 and are now 1-4%. This is near-from-scratch creation of an AI-tool requirement category. - RAG at 5.2% is remarkable — RAG is an architectural pattern, and it appears in 1 of every 19 SWE postings in 2026, up from 1 in 1,100 in 2024.

docs.title: Divergence computation and benchmark sensitivity
docs.text:
Full table at tables/T23/benchmark_sensitivity.csv. Approach: report gap = worker_band − employer_rate, under each of 50% / 65% / 75% / 85% worker-side assumption bands.

Gap (worker band − employer ai_strict):

Cohort n Employer ai_strict 50% band gap 65% band gap 75% band gap 85% band gap 2024 pooled 22,812 1.03% +48.97 pp +63.97 pp +73.97 pp +83.97 pp 2024 arshkon-only 4,687 1.32% +48.68 pp +63.68 pp +73.68 pp +83.68 pp 2026 scraped 25,822 10.61% +39.39 pp +54.39 pp +64.39 pp +74.39 pp

Under ai_any (broader):

Cohort n Employer ai_any 50% band gap 65% band gap 75% band gap 85% band gap 2024 pooled 22,812 5.07% +44.93 pp +59.93 pp +69.93 pp +79.93 pp 2026 scraped 25,822 29.36% +20.64 pp +35.64 pp +45.64 pp +55.64 pp

Robust across all bands: direction is ALWAYS "worker > employer" (positive gap). The magnitude varies by band but qualitative finding "employer requirements are far below worker usage" holds everywhere.

Sensitivity to pattern choice: ai_any reduces the 2026 gap under 50% band from 39.4 pp to 20.6 pp. This is the tightest lower-bound on the divergence. Even under the most charitable employer-count (ai_any mentions even "machine learning" or "ai" anywhere) and most conservative worker-count (50%), the gap is 20+ pp.

docs.title: Temporal divergence — how fast is each side growing?
docs.text:
Full table at tables/T23/temporal_divergence.csv.

Measure 2024 2025-2026 Change (pp) Ratio Employer ai_strict (ours) 1.03% 10.61% +9.58 pp 10.30× Worker SO currently use AI 63.0% 84.0% +21.0 pp 1.33×

Both sides grew, but the ratios are starkly different: employer-side multiplied 10× from a tiny base while worker-side grew 1.33× from a large base. In absolute pp, worker-side grew MORE (21 pp vs 9.6 pp), but from a much higher starting point. The gap in absolute terms narrowed modestly (73.97 pp → 64.39 pp under 75% band), but remains multiplicatively massive — employers are ~6× slower to codify AI into formal requirements than developers are to adopt AI in practice.

docs.title: Divergence chart
docs.text: exploration/figures/T23_divergence_chart.png (also SVG). Paper-quality figure showing: - Employer series (red/orange): ai_strict, ai_any, copilot — all near 0-5% in 2024, 10-30% in 2026. - Worker series (blue): StackOverflow 63% → 84%, daily 50.6%, Anthropic task coverage 75%, DORA 90%, McKinsey 79%. - Benchmark bands (green horizontal shading) at 50%, 65%, 75%, 85%. - Gap annotation showing ~73 pp difference in 2026.

docs.title: Specificity — are employers asking for specific AI tools at rates exceeding usage?
docs.text:
Full table at tables/T23/tool_specificity_rates.csv.

Tool Employer 2026 Industry benchmark 2024-2025 Gap copilot 0.10% ~33% regular use (GitHub Copilot industry) +33 pp (worker >> employer) cursor 1.98% No public usage benchmark; fast-growing 2024-2025 unknown claude 3.49% No SWE-specific public benchmark unknown RAG (architecture) 5.20% N/A (not a tool; pattern question) — ai_general (AI/ML/LLM) 25.80% SO 84% use-or-plan +58 pp

Clear pattern: employers are NOT asking for specific AI tools at rates exceeding likely adoption. GitHub Copilot is the single most-adopted AI coding tool (4.7M paid subs globally, ~33% regular-use in industry surveys) and appears in only 0.10% of postings. The divergence is not "employers asking for everything"; it is "employers under-codifying even the most-adopted tools." The mention of ai_general (AI / ML / LLM) at 26% is the only category approaching a fifth of usage-benchmark rates — and this is the most vague category, precisely where hedging language concentrates (T22 near-AI aspiration 50%).

docs.title: Aggregator vs direct
docs.text:
Full table at tables/T23/ai_rate_by_aggregator.csv.

Cohort is_aggregator n ai_strict ai_any cursor claude 2024 pooled direct 19,439 1.00% 5.12% 0.01% 0.02% 2024 pooled aggregator 3,373 1.19% 4.80% 0% 0% 2026 scraped direct 21,406 10.87% 30.26% 2.19% 3.67% 2026 scraped aggregator 4,416 9.35% 25.00% 0.95% 2.65%

Direct employers have slightly higher AI requirement rates than aggregators (+1.5 pp on ai_strict in 2026). The divergence pattern is consistent across both cohorts. The sensitivity (a) per dispatch is passed: the aggregator subset does not drive the finding; excluding aggregators would tighten (not flip) the divergence.

docs.title: Seniority operationalization sensitivity (per dispatch)
docs.text: T30 panel levels (J3 / S4 / entry-label) all show the same ~10× employer-side growth. The finding does not depend on seniority operationalization.

docs.title: Sensitivities
docs.text: Essential (per dispatch): (a) aggregator vs direct — passed, see above. Recommended (per dispatch): © seniority operationalization — passed, see Employer-side rates by cell above.

docs.title: Surprises and unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. 2024 employer ai_strict was 1.03%. Given 2024 Stack Overflow data says 63% of developers were already using AI, employer postings in 2024 essentially IGNORED AI as a formal requirement. The divergence was already massive in 2024 — 63 pp / 60× multiplicative. This means the divergence story is NOT a 2026 phenomenon; it has been present since at least 2024.

  2. The multiplicative growth gap is ~10×. Employer-side grew 10.3× (1% → 10.6%) vs worker-side 1.33× (63% → 84%). Employer requirements are MULTIPLYING faster but STARTED SO LOW they're still far behind in absolute terms. Framing: employer formalization of AI is playing catch-up to developer adoption, not driving it.

  3. Copilot at 0.10% is astonishing. GitHub Copilot has been the #1 AI coding tool since 2023. Microsoft reports 4.7M paid subs and enterprise penetration at 90% of Fortune 100. That only 0.10% of SWE postings mention "copilot" means employers are NOT formalizing AI tool requirements even for the tool most of their employees actually use. The "copilot as entry-level requirement" narrative is false in the data.

  4. RAG at 5.20% is leading the tool-specific rise. RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) is an ARCHITECTURAL pattern, not a discrete product. Its presence in 5% of 2026 SWE postings (up from 0.09% in 2024) indicates employers are asking for AI-pipeline / vector-db competencies more than tool-specific skills. This matches the "AI-as-domain" expansion found in T09 (ML/LLM archetype emergence).

  5. Entry-level copilot rate (0.17% in 2026) is HIGHER than senior copilot rate (0.11%) — consistent with T11's finding that "copilot rate J3 4.6% > S4 4.1%" under broader patterns. AI tools are not being gated to senior levels.

  6. Anthropic's 75% task coverage is NOT a worker-adoption rate. It measures task-level usage if a programmer uses Claude. It's methodologically different from SO's "percent of developers currently use AI." The chart caveats both series to avoid false equivalence.

  7. DORA 2025's 90% is likely an upper-envelope bias (their sample is 5k tech-involved professionals, self-selected via Google-networked channels). 50% SO-daily is a more conservative worker-side point estimate. Using it still gives a 39+ pp gap in 2026.

  8. Aggregator-direct gap is small in 2026 for AI rates but in opposite direction than for ghost-indicators (T22). Aggregators post LESS AI (9.35% vs 10.87% direct on ai_strict) but MORE tech_count (7.63 vs 7.33). Aggregators seem to strip scope/aspiration language but preserve (and possibly over-emphasize) technology lists. They are a separate posting style.

docs.title: What RQ3 should carry forward
docs.text:
The divergence is definitively direction-consistent across all reasonable worker-side benchmarks (50-85% band), cross-period robust (positive in both 2024 and 2026), and magnitude-large (20+ pp even under most charitable pairing). The qualitative finding "employers under-codify AI skills relative to worker adoption" is not sensitive to methodological choices.

The NOVEL contribution of T23 is a DOCUMENTED DIVERGENCE PATTERN rather than an exact gap. Combined with T22's finding that 50% of near-AI-token windows contain hedging language, the emerging mechanism is: employers formally require AI in only 10% of SWE postings but WHEN they mention AI, they frame it as "preferred / bonus / nice-to-have" half the time. This aligns with RQ3: employers are expressing AI skill preferences, not codifying them as firm requirements, even though the underlying workforce already uses AI at 63-90% rates.

For Wave 3.5 T32 (cross-occupation AI divergence), this T23 pattern is the SWE-specific baseline. If SWE-adjacent and control occupations show similar divergence patterns, RQ3 becomes a generic labor-market finding. If SWE is uniquely divergent, RQ3 becomes SWE-specific and may strengthen into a paper-lead finding.

docs.title: T24 — Hypothesis Consolidation and Analysis-Phase Priorities
docs.text:
Owner: Agent N (Wave 4) Date: 2026-04-20 Primary inputs: exploration/memos/gate_3.md, exploration/reports/V2_verification.md, exploration/reports/V1_verification.md, task reports T01-T38.

This report consolidates the exploration's hypothesis landscape at the close of Wave 3.5 / V2. Eight hypotheses (H_A..H_N) were directly tested in Wave 3.5; verdicts are recorded with effect size, CI, and downstream implications. Six hypotheses from the T24 planned list (H_D, H_E, H_F, H_G, H_I, H_J) remain deferred; priorities and data/method needs are specified. Two new post-3.5 hypotheses (H_O, H_P) are formalized. The report closes with surprise and method-suitability inventories, top-5 tensions for the analysis phase, and a data-gap list distinguishing exploration-induced from pre-existing gaps.

docs.title: RQ1 — Employer-side restructuring (junior rung narrowing, scope inflation, senior redefinition)
docs.text: Subclaim Verdict Primary evidence V1/V2 status Junior share reduces Contradicted J3 pooled +5.04 pp, 7/7 T30 junior definitions UP (T30) V1 confirmed panel consistency; V2 none required Junior scope inflates Partial — supported but not junior-specific T11 J3 breadth_resid +1.58 < S4 +2.61; T28 within-domain +1.27 = 61% of total V1 confirmed S4>J3 within-company; V2 confirmed scope SNR 42.8 Junior roles relabeled as senior Contradicted T15 TF-IDF junior↔senior cosine 0.946→0.863 (diverging); T12 label-based AND YOE-based relabeling diagnostics agree V1 confirmed H2 exactly; V2 confirmed T15 sharpening via T20 AUC Senior redefinition toward AI orchestration Supported + emergent-role-specific T21 cluster 0 +10.2 pp + cluster 1 +11.4 pp; T34 cluster 0 15.6× growth V1 validated rebuilt mgmt pattern @ 1.00 precision; V2 Cluster 0 composition coherent 14/20 Senior shift AWAY from management Contradicted under V1-validated pattern T21 mgmt_rebuilt mid-senior 0.039→0.038 (flat, SNR 0.1) V1 exposed mgmt_broad 0.28 precision; V2 re-confirmed flatness Seniority boundaries sharpened Strongly supported (falsifies pre-exploration prior) T15 TF-IDF diverging; T20 AUC +0.150 associate↔mid-senior; T12 relabeling diagnostic V1 confirmed T15 direction; V2 confirmed T20 middle boundary, flagged extremes as sample-thin

docs.title: RQ2 — Task and requirement migration
docs.text: Subclaim Verdict Primary evidence V1/V2 status Requirements migrate downward across seniority Contradicted — AI is at all levels, not migrated T11 J3 copilot 4.6% ≈ S4 4.1%; T20 AI-binary × YOE × period interaction p=0.66 (null) V1 confirmed; V2 confirmed T20 null Migration into YOE≤2 postings Supported with qualification T28 J3 within-domain +6.84 pp; but S4 scope within-domain also +9.10 pp V1 confirmed within-dominant decomposition; V2 not re-run Requirements-section shrinking Demoted — classifier-sensitive T13 −3.5 pp (T13 classifier) vs +5.3 pp (simple regex); T33 confirmed aggregate direction-flip V1 flagged H3; V2 confirmed T33 flip AI-tool asks → senior Contradicted T11 + T23 show J3 and S4 near-identical copilot/cursor/claude rates V1 confirmed; V2 not re-run

docs.title: RQ3 — Employer-requirement / worker-usage AI divergence
docs.text: Subclaim Verdict Primary evidence V1/V2 status Employer AI rate is below worker rate Strongly supported, universal 16/16 subgroups T23 employer 10.61% vs worker 63-90%; T32 16/16 subgroups positive gap V1 none; V2 confirmed T32 universality exact SWE-specific AI rise magnitude Strongly supported T18 DiD SWE−CTL +14.02 pp [+13.67, +14.37] V2 confirmed across 4 alt control defs Rank-correlation between worker adoption and employer rate Strongly supported T32 Spearman(worker_mid, employer_2026) = +0.92 V2 confirmed from saved output Divergence widened with period Partial — narrowed slightly in absolute pp T23 2024 gap 74 pp → 2026 gap 64 pp under 75% band V2 confirmed direction robust across bands

docs.title: RQ4 — Mechanisms (interviews)
docs.text:
RQ4 is scoped for qualitative interview research post-exploration. Wave 3.5 pre-cleared three leading alternative explanations:

Alternative mechanism Verdict Primary evidence V1/V2 status LLM-authorship mediation (recruiter-tool writing) Rejected as dominant mediator T29 low-LLM quartile content effects 80-130% of full-corpus; length growth 52% LLM-mediated V2 not re-run Hidden hiring-bar lowering Rejected T33 classifier-sensitive direction; 0/50 loosening language; ρ Hiring-selectivity response (volume-down → raise bar) Rejected T38 r

docs.title: 2. Wave 3.5 verdict table
docs.text:
Task Hypothesis Verdict Headline numeric result Change to ranked findings T31 H_M: same-co × same-title drift exceeds co-level SUPPORTED (direction robust; magnitude range-dependent) Pair-level AI Δ +10 to +13.4 pp > T16 co-level +7.7-8.3 pp; mgmt_rebuilt Δ +12-16 pp; breadth pair-level −0.02 to +0.08 (90% attenuation vs co-level) Promoted to Tier A (A2); range-reported per V2 flag T32 H_A: cross-occupation employer-worker AI divergence universal STRONGLY SUPPORTED 16/16 subgroups positive gap 2024 AND 2026; Spearman(worker_mid, employer_2026) = +0.92; SWE magnitude 35-58 pp adjacent / 15-50 pp control Promoted to Tier A (A1); paper-lead candidate T33 H_B: requirements-contraction as hiring-bar signal REJECTED T13 period coef −0.019 vs simple +0.030 (both p<1e-13); 0/50 loosening language; ρ T34 H_C: emergent senior archetype STRONGLY SUPPORTED — TWO archetypes Cluster 0 (Applied-AI/LLM Engineer) 15.6× growth, 94% 2026, 28%+ AI-titled; Cluster 1 (Data-Platform/DevOps) 2.6× growth, +11.4 pp share Promoted to Tier A (A5); becomes paper narrative anchor T35 H_K: ecosystem crystallization PARTIALLY SUPPORTED Modularity +0.029 (0.632→0.662); LLM/AI cluster pre-existed 2024; AI dev-tools sub-cluster SPLIT OFF (⅘ members new-in-2026) Kept as Tier B (B7); modularity modest but sub-cluster novelty is cleaner story T36 H_L: legacy → AI substitution REJECTED 6 of 11 titles match; 2026 neighbors mean ai_strict 3.6% < market 14.4%; content drift is legacy-stack → modern-stack (Postgres + CI/CD + microservices + Terraform), not AI Kept as Tier C supporting (C5); negates one leading alt explanation T37 H_H: sampling-frame artifact REJECTED 13 of 15 headlines ratio ≥0.80 on returning cohort (V2 table says 14/15); J3/S4 directions INTENSIFY (J3 +5.05→+6.17; S4 −7.62→−8.29); within-co AI +7.91 pp matches T16 range Kept as Tier B (B4); robustness-appendix centerpiece T38 H_N: hiring-selectivity × scope REJECTED r

Net Wave 3.5 impact on ranked findings: three Tier A promotions (T32→A1, T31→A2 pair-level, T34→A5 two archetypes); three Tier B rejections of alternative explanations (T33, T36, T38); two Tier B affirmations (T37 robustness, T35 modularity + sub-cluster); zero demotions of prior-Wave Tier A claims under Wave 3.5 evidence.

docs.title: Cross-wave surprises (all waves)
docs.text:
1. Dominant axis is technology/domain, not seniority (T09 NMI 8.88× title-archetype vs seniority). Pre-exploration prior was seniority-dominant. The pivot drove Gate 2's positioning change.
  2. Seniority boundaries sharpened, not blurred (T15 + T20). Pre-exploration RQ1 subclaim was "blurring"; the opposite result survives across unsupervised and supervised methods.
  3. Scope inflation is universal and senior > junior (T11 + T28). Pre-exploration assumption was junior-specific. Senior-side magnitudes larger in absolute terms.
  4. Within-firm AI rewriting exceeds between-firm AI rise (T06 Gate 1 +31 pp; V1/T16 corrected to +7.7-8.3 pp under V1 pattern). Magnitude revised but direction robust.
  5. Management pattern precision exposed at 0.28/0.55 (V1). Wave 2 "management fell" claim was a measurement artifact. Publishable methods-caveat on longitudinal posting studies.
  6. T13 J3 requirements-chars direction flips under alt classifier (V1 + T33). Classifier choice flips the sign at aggregate level, not just J3.
  7. Aggregators post CLEANER JDs than direct employers (T22 ghost-likeness, T31 AI). Counter to typical "aggregators are spammy" prior.
  8. Copilot at 0.10% of postings vs ~33% worker regular-use (T23). Employers under-formalize even the most-adopted AI tool.

docs.title: Wave 3.5-specific surprises
docs.text:
1. Pair-level AI drift EXCEEDS company-level drift (T31 +13.4 pp > T16 +7.7-8.3 pp). Pre-Wave-3.5 prior was pair-level should be tighter (less within-company title noise) and hence smaller; it is larger. Within-firm rewriting is same-title, not title-recomposition.
  2. Two emergent senior archetypes, not one (T34). Pre-Wave-3.5 prior expected a single Applied-AI role; found TWO (Applied-AI/LLM + Data-Platform/DevOps).
  3. AI-oriented senior roles ask MORE experience (T34 median YOE 6 vs 5 for cluster 1). Counter to a "AI lowers the bar" prior; supports sharpened-seniority finding.
  4. Cross-occupation divergence UNIVERSAL across 16/16 subgroups 2024 AND 2026 (T32). Nurse ai_strict at 0.00% on 6,801 postings. Accountant 72× worker-to-employer ratio (larger than SWE 7×).
  5. Hiring-selectivity REJECTED with OPPOSITE direction (T38). Volume-UP firms write LONGER JDs (r=+0.20). JOLTS trough is background, not mechanism.
  6. New-entrant firms are LESS junior-heavy than returning firms (T16 12.8% vs 15.3%). Between-company J3 rise is EXIT-driven, not entry-driven. 4,395 firms exited panel with lower-than-average J3.
  7. Legacy substitution is stack-modernization, not AI-ification (T36). 2024 architect titles map to 2026 engineer titles at 3.6% ai_strict (below 14.4% market rate). 2-4 year YOE drop on substitution — title-inflation reversal.
  8. T13 classifier sensitivity extends to aggregate (T33). Direction flip at full-corpus level, not just J3. SNR 0.97 at noise floor.
  9. Tech ecosystem crystallization is MODEST (T35 +0.029 modularity). LLM-vendor cluster already existed in 2024 pooled. Real novelty: AI dev-tools sub-cluster split-off (⅘ members new).
  10. S4 senior within-firm decline EXCEEDS aggregate (T37 −9.31 pp within-co vs −8.29 pp aggregate on returning cohort). Parallel to T16 "exit-driven junior rise" — now "exit-driven senior decline" too.

docs.title: 4. New post-3.5 hypothesis specifications
docs.text: Two new hypothesis candidates emerge from the full Wave 3 + Wave 3.5 evidence body and merit formal specification for analysis-phase prioritization.

docs.title: H_O — Within-firm AI rewriting intensity correlates with firm's 2024 digital-transformation maturity
docs.text:
Claim statement. The pair-level AI-strict drift (T31) is not uniform across firms: firms with greater 2024 digital-transformation maturity (measured via cloud-spend signals, public API surface, or proprietary tooling presence) exhibit larger within-pair AI rewriting magnitudes in 2026.

Supporting evidence (indirect). - T34 cluster 0 (Applied-AI Engineer) concentrates 30% in T16 ai_forward_scope_inflator employer cluster (vs 11% overall share in 2026 senior postings). Only a subset of firms has pivoted. - T11 top-1% AI-saturated outliers are financial-services dominated (JPM, Citi, Wells Fargo, GEICO, Visa, Solera) — firms with known 2024 enterprise-AI pilot programs. - T31 top-20 pair-drift exemplars are Wells Fargo + Microsoft + Capital One + Walmart + Amazon — all firms with prominent 2024 internal AI infrastructure initiatives.

Proposed analysis-phase test. Join T31 pair-drift data with external firm-level digital-maturity indicators (e.g., Apptopia cloud-share data; LinkedIn company-size + tech-stack labels; Dealroom AI-startup-affiliation flags). Regress pair-level AI-strict Δ on maturity-index + sector + size + pair-count controls. Expected sign: positive. Power consideration: n_pairs = 23-37 is thin; analysis-phase would benefit from a larger overlap-panel construction under relaxed pair-count threshold.

Alternative framings. - (a) Sector-channel: financial services over-represented in AI-forward rewrites; test sector × maturity interaction. - (b) Size-channel: large firms lead AI rewrites; test size × maturity interaction. T38 suggests firm-size alone does not select for AI-rewriting (|r|<0.10 across size buckets), so a pure size-explanation is unlikely.

docs.title: H_P — Applied-AI Engineer archetype concentrates in financial services due to regulatory-compliance AI adoption
docs.text:
Claim statement. T34 cluster 0 (Applied-AI / LLM Engineer) is over-represented in financial services (16.5% of cluster 0 vs ~5-8% of general SWE postings) not because financial-services firms adopt AI earlier, but because compliance-grade AI requires explicit engineering roles (vs. informal Copilot use in other sectors).

Supporting evidence (indirect). - T34 cluster 0 industry top-2: Software Development 44.6%, Financial Services 16.5%; T16 top AI-rich outliers dominated by banks. - T23 Copilot at 0.10% in postings suggests employers do NOT formalize casual AI use. The ones they DO formalize concentrate where informal use is compliance-restricted (bank trading floors, healthcare). - T32 accountant employer ai_strict 0.69% vs worker 50% = 72× gap; the universality-plus-compression pattern supports an under-codification-except-where-compliance-requires hypothesis.

Proposed analysis-phase test. Compare T34 cluster 0 industry composition against external industry-level compliance-density indices (e.g., NIST AI RMF adoption data by sector; FINRA/SEC AI-related enforcement actions). Additionally: qualitative comparison of cluster 0 postings in financial services vs. Software Development — look for compliance-specific language ("model risk management", "MRM", "SR 11-7", "EEOC-compliant AI") as a differentiating signature.

Alternative framings. - (a) Talent-market channel: finance pays more, so AI engineers concentrate there. - (b) Legacy-modernization channel: finance's 2024 legacy stack requires AI-specific engineers to integrate; Software Development doesn't.

docs.title: H_Q (additional identified) — AI mentions at junior tier are wish-list items, not formal requirements
docs.text:
Claim statement. T22's finding that 50.3% of near-AI-token windows contain aspirational language (vs 28.2% far-from-AI) extends to the J3 subset specifically: AI asks in entry-level postings sit in "nice-to-have" / "preferred" sections 2× more than non-AI asks.

Supporting evidence (indirect). - T22 aggregate 50.3% vs 28.2% on 3,543 AI-mentioning 2026 postings. - T22 top-20 ghost entry list: Visa "Software Engineer, New College Grad" with 21 tech_count × 6 scope_kitchen_count = 126 kitchen-sink score, asking new grads for LLM fine-tuning. - T23 copilot J3 rate 4.6% < overall but near-zero formalization: J3 ai_strict 8.52% suggests most are general AI/ML not tool-specific.

Proposed analysis-phase test. Re-run T22 aspiration-share analysis restricted to J3 (YOE≤2) postings with AI mentions. Expected: aspiration-share near-AI ≥ 50% on J3; aspiration-share far-from-AI ≤ 30%. If confirmed, this is a RQ3-adjacent finding — the divergence story has a hedging-at-junior sub-channel.

docs.title: 5. Deferred hypothesis inventory (T24 planned list)
docs.text: Six hypotheses from the pre-Wave-3 T24-planned list were not run in Wave 3.5 or were partially addressed. Priority rankings and analysis-phase data/method needs follow.

docs.title: H_D — Senior IC-as-team-multiplier
docs.text:
Claim. The Applied-AI/LLM Engineer archetype (T34 cluster 0) functions as a team-force-multiplier: one senior AI engineer amplifies the productivity of several junior/mid engineers without being a manager. If true, the cluster 0 + team-composition data should show: (1) companies hiring cluster-0 seniors also hire more juniors at the same company × period; (2) cluster-0 postings correlate with downstream junior-hiring intensity within-firm.

Why deferred. Wave 3.5 T34 characterized the archetype at posting level but did not formalize its role in team composition. Cross-time-series hiring correlation requires a company panel with hiring pipeline data beyond T16's 724-company panel.

Priority: HIGH. Directly tests a mechanism for RQ2 (requirement migration across seniority).

Data / method needs. - Company-month hiring panel (BLS QCEW or proprietary BurningGlass equivalent). - T34 cluster 0 per-firm posting counts as independent variable. - T30 J3 per-firm posting counts as dependent variable. - Fixed-effects regression with firm, sector, and period controls.

docs.title: H_E — Same-co J1 drop + J3 rise regime shift
docs.text:
Claim. The J1 (seniority_final='entry') share rise is a recent regime shift occurring 2025-2026, distinguishable from a slow-trend null. If true, a formal break analysis within the 2026 scraped daily window should show rising J3/S4 share through 2026-03 to 2026-04 OR discontinuity from 2024 baselines.

Why deferred. T19 found zero temporal trend within the 30-day scraped window; T30 showed scraped daily profile is internally stable. Annualized rate ratios are below 1 for seniority metrics (dominated by within-2024 source-instrument variance).

Priority: LOW for cross-period break; MEDIUM for event-timing around major AI model releases. The latter would require finer-grained weekly data across 2024-2026.

Data / method needs. - Expanded temporal panel beyond the current 15-30 day scrape + 2024 snapshots. - Event-study framework with model-release dates as treatment indicators. - Not feasible with current data; defer to longitudinal analysis phase with sustained scraping.

docs.title: H_F — Sunbelt AI surge catchup
docs.text:
Claim. AI-mention rates in Sunbelt metros (Atlanta, Tampa, Miami, Dallas-Fort Worth, Charlotte) will grow faster than traditional tech-hub metros (SF Bay Area, Seattle, NYC, Boston) between 2024 and 2026, with Sunbelt metros closing the gap.

Why partially-addressed. T17 found AI-rise is geographically UNIFORM (SNR ~10, all metros positive) with leaders Atlanta/Tampa/Miami/SLC — not SF/Seattle. The H_F prediction is partially absorbed: Sunbelt DID lead, but SF/Seattle did NOT fall behind — the uniformity is the bigger story than the catchup. A formal catchup-timing test (event study) was not run.

Priority: LOW. T17's uniformity result supersedes the catchup framing; any remaining catchup structure is sub-leading to the broader "AI-rise is geographic-uniform" finding.

Data / method needs. If revived: weekly metro-stratified posting data across the full 2024-2026 window; interacted event-study with tech-hub vs Sunbelt × time. Current data supports within-period (T17) but not continuous trajectory.

docs.title: H_G — Staff-title redistribution
docs.text:
Claim. The 2024→2026 title vocabulary rotated: "senior" title-marker share dropped 12 pp while "staff" doubled and "none" rose 12 pp (T10). This is title-inflation reversal — the 2024 "architect" premium dissolved.

Why partially-addressed. T10 documented the title-rotation finding. T36 substitution map shows 2024 architect → 2026 engineer with 2-4 year YOE drop. Wave 3.5 did not formalize this as a hypothesis test beyond descriptive statistics.

Priority: MEDIUM. The title-rotation is real and corroborated across T10 + T36; a formal analysis-phase test could quantify cross-firm title-inflation elasticity.

Data / method needs. - Firm-level staff-vs-senior-title share panel. - External firm-size / hiring-difficulty controls (proxies for why firms inflate titles). - OLS regression of title-mix Δ on firm covariates.

docs.title: H_I — AI as coordination signal
docs.text:
Claim. Employer AI-mention rate functions as a coordination signal in the labor market: firms use AI-language to signal technical sophistication to candidates, not primarily to specify required skills. If true, AI-mention rate should correlate with firm attractiveness (quit-rate lower, application-rate higher) independent of actual AI-work performed.

Why deferred. Requires labor-supply-side data (application-volume, quit-rate) that is out of scope for the current corpus. Mechanism test, not measurement test.

Priority: MEDIUM. Complements RQ3 divergence (if AI is coordination-signal, the under-formalization makes sense — employers signal without committing). But testing requires interview data (RQ4) and external labor-supply metrics.

Data / method needs. - Interview-side: RQ4 qualitative interview questions on "why we mention AI in JDs" (candidates vs recruiters). - Quantitative side: Glassdoor/Indeed applicant volume data joined with T11_posting_features; not available in current pipeline.

docs.title: H_J — Recruiter-LLM senior-bias mediation
docs.text:
Claim. T29 rejected the general LLM-authorship mediation hypothesis. H_J specifically targets the SENIOR posting subset: recruiter LLM adoption may be concentrated in senior-role drafting (where JDs are longer and more technically detailed), and the senior-role content shifts (orch rise, Applied-AI archetype emergence) may be partially LLM-mediated even if the aggregate is not.

Why deferred. T29 stratified by overall LLM signature density but did not stratify by seniority × LLM interaction. The general rejection of T29 is strong; a senior-specific re-test is a refinement rather than a primary test.

Priority: MEDIUM. Would strengthen T21/T34 senior-role claims by ruling out senior-specific authorship mediation.

Data / method needs. - Re-run T29 authorship-score analysis on senior subset (S4 + director) only. - Compare low-LLM-quartile-within-senior-subset to full-senior-corpus on orch density, ai_binary, mgmt_rebuilt, cluster 0 share. - Existing T29 authorship_scores.csv + T21 cluster_assignments.csv have sufficient joinable data.

docs.title: Tier A (lead-candidate findings)
docs.text: Finding Method used Method suitability Analysis-phase upgrade A1. Cross-occupation divergence universal Proportion DiD + Spearman rank correlation across 16 subgroups (T32) Appropriate. Rank-correlation handles benchmark heterogeneity. Direction robust across 4 worker-usage bands. Analysis-phase: formal DiD with clustered SEs; bootstrap on benchmark uncertainty; include 2025 benchmarks when available. A2. Within-firm AI rewriting same-title Within-between decomposition + pair-level aggregation (T16 + T31 + T37) Appropriate. Three independent panel constructions converge at +7.7-8.3 pp company-level and +10-13 pp pair-level. Analysis-phase: formalize fixed-effects regression with firm × title × period; use T16's overlap_panel.csv as the primary panel. Pre-register the panel construction (n≥3 vs n≥2 threshold) to avoid T31-style replication issues. A3. Seniority boundaries sharpened Unsupervised TF-IDF cosine (T15) + supervised logistic AUC (T20) + YOE×period interaction (T20) Appropriate. Two independent methods converge. T20 OLS interaction p<1e-44 on requirement_breadth. Analysis-phase: hierarchical Bayesian model with firm random effects; report BOTH TF-IDF and supervised AUC as robustness panels. A4. Scope inflation universal, senior>junior, within-domain Length-residualized OLS (T11) + within/between decomposition (T28) Appropriate. Residualization valid (V1 Phase D confirmed). Within-domain dominance 60-85% across 5 metrics. Analysis-phase: shift-share decomposition at finer granularity (firm × archetype). Cross-check with alt residualization (quantile regression on length). A5. Two emergent senior archetypes k-means clustering (T21) + cluster profiling (T34) Partially appropriate. k=5 is ad-hoc; silhouette 0.477 is mid-range. V2 did not test seed sensitivity on the k-means step. Analysis-phase: pre-register k via BIC or gap-statistic; test k=4-7 stability; validate cluster 1 heterogeneity (DE + DevOps + AI-data-contract bundle — a k=6-7 split may separate cleanly). A6. AI-DiD cross-occupation robustness 4 alt control definitions + SWE-tier drop (V2) Appropriate. Direction robust under all 5 variants. Analysis-phase: pre-register control definition; include 2+ additional controls (construction, healthcare-tech-adjacent) to tighten external validity.

docs.title: Tier B (strong supporting)
docs.text: Finding Method used Method suitability Upgrade B1. AI-mention acceleration 2.80× above within-2024 noise Rate-of-change + within-2024 SNR (T19) Appropriate for descriptive use. Cannot attribute to specific model release; timeline spans GPT-4o → Claude 4 Opus → Gemini 2.5 Pro. Analysis-phase: deferred event study needs longer panel; descriptive cite only. B2. Management flat under V1 pattern V1 pattern validation + re-measurement (T21) Appropriate. 1.00 precision on T22 + V2 re-sample. Analysis-phase: no upgrade needed; cite pattern provenance explicitly. B3. Hiring-bar-lowering REJECTED Dual-classifier OLS + narrative sample (T33) Appropriate. Classifier sensitivity is the substantive finding.
