Analysis-phase: consider LLM-adjudicated section classifier as a third arbitration; qualitative case study of 50 loosening-language hits. B4. 13/15 headlines robust on returning cohort Retention ratio + bootstrap CI (T37) Appropriate. Conservative sensitivity; one partially robust (J3 breadth 0.70). Analysis-phase: report returning-cohort as primary; relax to full-corpus only when sample size constraints. B5. Hiring-selectivity REJECTED Pearson + Spearman correlation (T38) Appropriate. Panel-robust null across 3 panels. Analysis-phase: extend with firm-level hiring-volume time series from external data (JOLTS is aggregate; need firm panel). B6. LLM-authorship NOT dominant mediator Signature vocabulary SNR + low-quartile subset re-test (T29) Partially appropriate. Only one clean authorship feature; sentence-structure features were format-confounded. Analysis-phase: retrain authorship classifier on pre-2023 corpus (not currently available); senior-specific re-test per H_J. B7. AI dev-tools sub-cluster SPLIT OFF Louvain community detection + Jaccard stability (T35) Appropriate. Seed-stable (SD<0.002); ⅘ members new-in-2026. Analysis-phase: confirm across alternative modularity thresholds (phi > 0.10 to 0.25). B8. CI/CD S4 riser +20.6 pp Tech-share difference (T14) Appropriate. V1 did not re-derive; T14 reported direction-robust. Analysis-phase: no upgrade needed; cite.

docs.title: Tier C (methods contributions)
docs.text: Finding Method Suitability Publishing path C1. Modularity +0.029 real but modest Louvain (T35) Appropriate Include in T35 methods appendix C2. Geographic uniformity + ML/LLM diffusion Metro-level share calculation (T17) Appropriate Methods appendix + discussion paragraph C3. Aggregators CLEANER than direct Aggregator-stratified comparison (T22) Appropriate; counter-prior finding Standalone methods-caveat paragraph C4. Copilot at 0.10% of postings Pattern-rate + industry benchmark (T23) Appropriate Lead figure caption C5. Legacy → modern-stack NOT AI Substitution map + Fightin' Words (T36) Partial (n=6 titles power-limited) Methods appendix C6. T30 multi-operationalization panel 13-definition direction consistency (T30) Strong methods contribution Independent methods paper C7. Within-2024 SNR calibration framework Calibration table (Prep) Strong methods contribution Independent methods paper C8. V1 pattern validation methodology Sample-based precision measurement (V1 + T22) Strong methods contribution Independent methods paper

docs.title: Tier D (flagged / demoted)
docs.text: Finding Why flagged Gate 3 treatment Analysis-phase action D1. "Junior requirements chars shrank" T13 classifier vs simple regex sign-flip; low-LLM near-disappears Demote to qualified claim Require dual-classifier reporting + LLM-adjudication sample D2. Aggregate credential stacking SNR 0.59 below noise Drop aggregate; cite per-seniority only Pre-register per-seniority stratification D3. MCP in ai_broad 2024 baseline "Microsoft Certified Professional" contamination Use ai_strict only for MCP Exclude from ai_broad definition in analysis-phase pipeline D4. T16/T23 pattern-provenance mismatch Report says v1_rebuilt, code uses top-level Cite pattern explicitly Re-derive T16/T23 numbers under v1_rebuilt; expect ~10-15% magnitude drop; direction unchanged D5. T31 pair-count panel-dependent V2 replication +10 to +13 pp range Range-report, not point-estimate Pre-register panel (n≥3 strict vs n≥2 relaxed)

docs.title: 7. Top 5 tensions for analysis phase (post-Wave-3.5)
docs.text: These tensions are different from Gate 3's list: Gate 3 focused on the pre-synthesis evidence body's tensions; these focus on analysis-phase priorities that SYNTHESIS.md (T26) must foreground for the analysis-phase RQ-specification.

docs.title: Tension 1 — Cross-occupation divergence magnitude framing: absolute pp vs ratio
docs.text:
T32 reports accountant worker 50% vs employer 0.69% = 72× ratio gap (largest in table) but SWE worker 84% vs employer 10.61% = 8× ratio gap at LARGER absolute magnitude. The paper's RQ3 lead can frame the divergence as: (a) SWE-concentrated magnitude (absolute pp gap) or (b) universal ratio-gap (multiplicative gap) — both are defensible and lead to different readings.

Gate 3 recommendation: present BOTH framings in SYNTHESIS.md ranked findings. Let analysis-phase pre-register which is primary.

docs.title: Tension 2 — Pair-level vs company-level scope direction
docs.text:
T31 finds pair-level breadth drift near-zero (−0.02 to +0.08) while T16 company-level breadth drift is +0.40-0.69 on the same firms. The 90%+ attenuation at pair level means scope inflation is cross-title within-company, not within-title. This contradicts a naive "same-title rewriting" reading that would predict pair-level to carry scope too.

Gate 3 recommendation: foreground the cross-title channel. Scope inflation is "employers re-titling AND adding new senior titles with broader scope", not "employers making existing same-title roles broader". This is a paper-level refinement of the Gate 2 scope-inflation story.

docs.title: Tension 3 — Sampling-frame attenuation on H_d J3 breadth only
docs.text:
T37 finds 14/15 headlines ratio ≥0.80, but H_d J3 (length-residualized breadth at J3) attenuates to 0.70 on returning cohort (+1.56 → +1.09). This may be a power artifact (returning n=1,758 vs full 2,911) OR a real signal that new-entrant J3 postings carry more residualized breadth. Cannot distinguish without additional regression decomposition.

Gate 3 recommendation: flag in SYNTHESIS.md as the one partial-robustness case; analysis-phase pre-register a J3-specific within-between decomposition to resolve.

docs.title: Tension 4 — Asaniczka senior asymmetry vs pooled magnitude
docs.text:
S4 senior-share decline is −7.6 pp pooled-2024 but −1.8 pp arshkon-only. The pooled number is asaniczka-weighted (asaniczka 4× arshkon); the arshkon-only number is not significant at 80% power (MDE 2.5 pp). Senior claims require dual-reporting.

Gate 3 recommendation: continue the T30 precedent — pair pooled and arshkon-only magnitudes for every senior claim. In SYNTHESIS.md, cite BOTH (not one with asterisk).

docs.title: Tension 5 — RQ3 lead framing: SWE-specific vs universal-compressed
docs.text:
T18 DiD SWE−CTL = +14.02 pp (highly SWE-specific). T32 16/16 direction-universal AND Spearman +0.92 worker-employer rank concordance (highly universal). These are not inconsistent but support different paper leads: - SWE-specific framing → paper on SWE labor restructuring. - Universal-compressed framing → paper on employer signaling behavior across AI-exposed occupations.

Gate 3 recommendation: Gate 3 memo recommends HYBRID framing with A1 + A2 as co-lead. SYNTHESIS.md should present both framings as Tier A with dispatch-phase RQ-selection deferring between them if the analysis phase prefers a single lead.

docs.title: Gaps that Wave 3.5 surfaced
docs.text:
1. Pair-level panel construction ambiguity (T31 V2 flag D5). V2 could not reproduce T31's exact n=23 pair count; replicated at n=37 (+9.98 pp) or n=12 (+13.3 pp). The T31 methodology document does not specify the pair-inclusion criterion uniquely. Analysis-phase pre-registration is needed.

  2. Pattern-label provenance mismatch (T16/T23 V2 flag D4). Report-text says v1_rebuilt; code uses top-level ai_strict. Under v1_rebuilt magnitudes drop ~10-15% (direction unchanged). Needs re-derivation and documentation.

  3. T34 cluster 1 heterogeneity. Cluster 1 ("Tech-orchestration non-AI") bundles data-engineering + DevOps/SRE + AI-lab-data-contract work. A k=6-7 clustering would likely split; analysis-phase should pre-register k-selection.

  4. T36 n=6 title sample. Legacy-substitution map is power-limited. Not fixable with current corpus; would require expanded 2024 title coverage.

  5. Senior-specific authorship mediation (H_J). T29 tested general mediation; senior subset may have different mediation structure. Requires re-run of T29 authorship-score analysis on senior cohort only.

  6. T22 hedging-×-seniority stratification. T22 aggregate 50.3% vs 28.2% near-AI aspiration is not stratified by J3 / S4. H_Q proposes J3-specific test. Data exists in T22 ghost_indicators.parquet; requires re-run.

docs.title: Pre-existing gaps (inherited from Gate 1 / Gate 2)
docs.text:
1. Industry taxonomy drift (Gate 1). LinkedIn changed industry labels between 2024 and 2026. No raw-label industry trend is valid. Analysis-phase needs cross-taxonomy mapping (company → sector via external data).

  2. ML Engineer source-classification gap (Gate 1). Arshkon 78% SWE vs asaniczka 59% SWE for ML Engineer titles. Requires source-stratification; survives Wave 3.5 as a persistent data-quality caveat.

  3. posting_age_days coverage 0.9% (T19). Posting-lifecycle analysis is not feasible. Analysis-phase should drop posting-lifecycle claims OR use external data.

  4. Worker-AI benchmark heterogeneity (T32). SO / DORA / Anthropic / NBER use different definitions of "AI use". Band analysis (50/65/75/85%) mitigates; not eliminates. Analysis-phase should pre-commit to ONE primary benchmark with band-robustness as supporting.

  5. Scraper query stratification (T04, T18). Scraped 2026 SWE n=45K is not a random sample; scraper has query-specific coverage. Within-SWE raw-share is not comparable across 2024 / 2026; share-of-SWE claims are the safe subset. Within-SWE metric claims remain valid. Analysis-phase should retain this framing.

  6. Seniority_final unknown-share growth (T03 / T18). Unknown went from 26-34% (2024) to 52% (scraped). Inflates label-based magnitudes mechanically. T30 panel mitigates by using YOE-based primaries (J3/S4). Analysis-phase should continue YOE-based primaries.

docs.title: 9. Analysis-phase priorities summary
docs.text:
Ordered by (novelty × power × readiness):

  1. Pre-register and re-derive T16/T23 numbers under v1_rebuilt pattern. (D4 resolution.) Low effort; restores narrative-claim cleanliness.

  2. Firm-level within-firm AI-rewriting regression. Formalize T16/T31/T37 within-company +7.7-8.3 pp into fixed-effects regression with firm × title × period. Deliver range-reported estimate with panel-sensitivity appendix.

  3. Cross-occupation DiD with pre-registered controls. V2's 4 alt control specs pass; pre-register one primary + two sensitivities for the analysis-phase paper.

  4. H_D (senior-IC-as-team-multiplier) test. HIGH priority; requires external hiring panel data; directly tests RQ2 mechanism.

  5. H_O (digital-maturity × AI-rewriting intensity) test. Requires external firm-maturity index.

  6. T34 cluster-selection sensitivity (k=4-7) + cluster 1 split. MEDIUM priority; addresses method-suitability caveat.

  7. H_J (senior-specific LLM-authorship mediation) test. MEDIUM priority; readily deliverable with existing T29 + T21 data.

  8. RQ4 interview protocol design. Carry T25 artifacts into interview elicitation.

  9. H_Q (J3 hedging-near-AI) test. LOW-to-MEDIUM; data exists in T22 parquet; quick deliverable.

docs.title: 10. Summary verdict
docs.text:
The exploration's hypothesis landscape at Wave 3.5 / V2 close is structurally mature. Eight hypotheses have tested verdicts (5 rejected, 3 supported); six are deferred with specifications; three new post-3.5 hypotheses emerge from the evidence body with analysis-phase test designs. The narrative axes that survive Wave 3.5 are: (i) cross-occupation AI-codification divergence universal and SWE-concentrated (A1), (ii) within-firm same-title AI rewriting (A2), (iii) sharpened seniority boundaries (A3), (iv) universal scope broadening with within-domain concentration (A4), and (v) emergent Applied-AI and Data-Platform archetypes (A5). The major axes demoted or rejected are: junior relabeling (contradicted), management-decline (measurement failure), requirements-shrinkage-as-hiring-bar (rejected), legacy-to-AI substitution (rejected), LLM-authorship mediation (rejected), and hiring-selectivity response (rejected). The negative findings are themselves a paper strength — they rule out six leading alternative explanations for the AI rise — and SYNTHESIS.md (T26) foregrounds them.

T24 ends. T25 (interview artifacts) and T26 (SYNTHESIS.md) follow.

docs.title: T28 — Domain-stratified scope changes by archetype
docs.text: Agent O (Wave 3). Date: 2026-04-20. Inputs: data/unified.parquet, exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet, swe_embeddings.npy + index, T11_posting_features.parquet, swe_tech_matrix.parquet. Script: exploration/scripts/T28_archetype_analysis.py. Outputs: exploration/tables/T28/.

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
Scope inflation is predominantly WITHIN-DOMAIN, not between-domain recomposition. Under the pooled-2024 → 2026 scope change:

Metric Aggregate Δ Within-domain Between-domain Interaction requirement_breadth_resid +2.07 +1.27 (61%) +0.56 (27%) +0.24 (12%) tech_count +1.96 +1.21 (62%) +0.49 (25%) +0.26 (13%) scope_density +0.162 +0.125 (77%) +0.031 (19%) +0.006 (4%) credential_stack_depth +0.450 +0.385 (85%) +0.009 (2%) +0.056 (13%) ai_binary +0.127 +0.082 (64%) +0.016 (12%) +0.030 (24%)

The 2024→2026 change in SWE posting scope is 60-85% within-domain. The Gate 2 "scope inflation universal, senior > junior" finding now has a clean decomposition: it is not a domain-mix shift; it is the same work being written as more expansive across domains.

J3 entry share (+5.07 pp pooled-2024 → 2026) is also within-domain: +6.84 pp within, -0.02 pp between, -1.75 pp interaction. Domain recomposition does NOT explain the entry-share rise. (Between-firm composition — per T06 — still explains most of it at the company level; the archetype-level decomposition here is a cleaner cut.)

AI/ML archetype is the single largest domain shift: 601 → 3,262 rows, AI-binary rate 15.1% → 45.7% (+30.6 pp), tech_count 6.4 → 8.9 (+2.55), description length 2,581 → 3,158 chars (+577). 50% of 2026 AI/ML volume is from employers not present in 2024 corpus.

Senior > Junior scope inflation holds in most domains, not universally. Of 15 domains, S4 breadth delta exceeds J3 in 11 (including AI/ML, backend, cloud_devops, qa_test, data_eng). In 4 (intern_early, mobile, dotnet_legacy, customer_apps) J3 inflated more or both declined. In ai_ml specifically, J3 and S4 both inflated +~2.5, i.e. the AI/ML senior premium is narrower than in other domains.

docs.title: Step 1 — Archetype coverage and domain bucketing
docs.text:
• T09 archetype labels: 8,000 of 48,223 labeled rows (17%), with "noise/outlier" dominant (n=3,741).
  • Nearest-centroid projection on embeddings: +40,223 rows projected to 37 non-noise archetypes (and noise-labeled rows got a "primary" nearest non-noise archetype).
  • Unassigned (no embedding): 19,914 rows, all raw-text scraped. T28 scope-related analyses are computed on the 48,223-row LLM-labeled subset; entry-share decomposition uses only rows with YOE, which is ~ the same set.
  • 37 non-noise archetype names bucketed into 15 domains (ai_ml, backend, cloud_devops, clearance_defense, data_eng, dotnet_legacy, embedded_firmware, customer_apps, frontend_full, generic_swe, intern_early, mobile, process_scrum, qa_test, research_academic + other).

Domain distribution shift 2024 → 2026 (% of labeled corpus):

domain % 2024 % 2026 Δ pp ai_ml 2.6 12.8 +10.2 cloud_devops 11.5 15.0 +3.5 data_eng 3.6 5.5 +2.0 intern_early 2.8 3.2 +0.5 backend 18.5 21.5 +3.0 qa_test 1.7 1.6 -0.2 mobile 1.9 2.4 +0.6 research_academic 0.7 1.3 +0.6 generic_swe 19.9 17.8 -2.1 process_scrum 2.3 1.3 -0.9 customer_apps 2.8 1.6 -1.3 embedded_firmware 5.6 3.6 -2.0 clearance_defense 12.6 6.8 -5.8 dotnet_legacy 3.8 1.2 -2.6 other 9.6 4.5 -5.1

Notable: clearance_defense -5.8 pp and dotnet_legacy -2.6 pp decline, while ai_ml +10.2 pp and cloud_devops +3.5 pp rise. This confirms the Gate 2 T09 finding of DoD/clearance decline and ML/LLM emergence, now quantified by projected-archetype shares.

docs.title: Step 2 — Entry-share decomposition
docs.text:
J3 (YOE ≤ 2, primary):

Component pp aggregate Δ +5.07 within-domain +6.84 between-domain -0.02 interaction -1.75

Interpretation. The domain mix essentially did not move the J3 entry share; the J3 rise is within-domain recomposition. Combined with Gate 1 T06 finding (J3 rise is between-company at arshkon-only n=125), we have a layered picture: the J3 share increase is NEW entrant FIRMS posting at existing DOMAINS, not existing firms shifting which domain they hire in.

J1 / J2 (seniority_final=entry, label-based, pooled-2024):

  • Aggregate Δ: +2.81 pp (near-identical to one another because J1 and J2 coincide at the entry level in the pooled 2024 frame).
  • Within-domain: +3.40 pp; between: +0.01 pp; interaction: −0.60 pp.

S4 (YOE ≥ 5):

  • Aggregate Δ: -7.58 pp.
  • Within-domain: -9.10 pp; between: -0.58 pp; interaction: +2.10 pp.

The S4 share decline is almost entirely within-domain. A small positive interaction (+2.10 pp) suggests some of the domain mix changes favored domains with higher 2026 S4 rates, partially offsetting the within-domain senior-share collapse.

Sensitivities:

Baseline J3 Δ pp J3 within J3 between S4 Δ pp S4 within S4 between pooled-2024 (primary) +5.07 +6.84 -0.02 -7.58 -9.10 -0.58 arshkon-only +1.17 +1.55 +0.29 -1.71 -1.84 -0.48 non-aggregator +4.77 +5.97 +0.02 -7.65 -8.56 -0.37

The arshkon-only baseline (Gate 1 T30 co-primary for senior) gives much smaller magnitudes (+1.17 pp J3, -1.71 pp S4) but the same within-dominant decomposition. The magnitude difference reflects the asaniczka senior-asymmetry documented in Gate 1. Report both: pooled-2024 Δ +5.07 / -7.58 pp AND arshkon-only Δ +1.17 / -1.71 pp. Both are within-domain dominant.

Aggregator exclusion (non-aggregator) is near-identical to pooled-2024 — the aggregator-share asymmetry between sources is not driving the decomposition.

docs.title: Step 3 — Domain-stratified scope inflation
docs.text:
Per-domain breadth_resid, tech_count, and AI-binary deltas 2024 → 2026, sorted by breadth:

domain n_2024 n_2026 breadth_resid Δ tech_count Δ AI-binary Δ ai_ml 601 3,262 +2.68 +2.55 +0.306 generic_swe 4,522 4,545 +2.07 +1.63 +0.081 cloud_devops 2,613 3,825 +1.90 +2.05 +0.099 clearance_defense 2,864 1,726 +1.69 +1.53 +0.011 dotnet_legacy 859 296 +1.54 +1.53 +0.072 backend 4,206 5,482 +1.48 +1.44 +0.125 qa_test 390 398 +1.46 +1.63 +0.070 intern_early 626 816 +1.42 +1.47 +0.054 process_scrum 522 344 +1.09 +0.93 +0.059 data_eng 821 1,410 +0.45 +0.76 +0.076 embedded_firmware 1,280 910 +0.26 +0.50 +0.015 customer_apps 646 401 +0.72 +0.50 +0.030 mobile 427 620 -0.05 +0.16 +0.048 research_academic 155 321 -0.24 -0.07 +0.078 other 2,182 1,153 -1.17 -1.02 +0.090

Every domain except 3 (mobile, research_academic, other) shows POSITIVE breadth_resid and tech_count inflation. AI-binary rose in every domain including the low-AI clearance/embedded domains, but substantially only in ai_ml (+30.6 pp), backend (+12.5 pp), cloud_devops (+9.9 pp), and generic_swe (+8.1 pp).

ai_ml is an outlier in three ways: (1) highest breadth inflation, (2) highest AI-binary rise, and (3) the only domain where senior (S4) did not inflate more than junior (J3). Details in Step 5.

The decomposition (table at top) confirms that 60-85% of each scope metric's aggregate change comes from the WITHIN-DOMAIN component. The "scope inflation universal, senior > junior" framing from Gate 2 is now additionally specified as: scope inflation universal across DOMAINS too, not just seniority levels.

docs.title: Step 4 — Junior/Senior gap within each archetype
docs.text:
S4 minus J3 breadth gap CHANGE 2024 → 2026 (positive = gap widened, senior inflated more):

domain breadth_gap_change tech_count_gap_change ai_gap_change credential_gap_change n_j3 n_s4 data_eng +1.60 +1.52 +0.04 +0.21 178 1,144 qa_test +1.49 +1.36 +0.06 +0.11 68 402 mobile +1.27 -0.82 +0.01 +0.33 86 510 cloud_devops +1.06 +0.77 -0.00 -0.12 424 3,586 embedded_firmware +1.00 -0.01 +0.01 +0.20 221 1,076 backend +0.90 +0.87 +0.04 +0.24 733 4,863 generic_swe +0.65 +0.12 -0.02 -0.04 1,014 5,244 ai_ml +0.49 -0.09 -0.05 -0.14 310 1,890 clearance_defense +0.22 -0.38 +0.00 -0.03 553 2,706 customer_apps -0.34 -0.11 -0.02 -0.13 136 497 dotnet_legacy -0.72 -1.57 -0.17 -0.21 97 634 process_scrum -0.92 -0.83 -0.03 -0.05 82 488 intern_early -1.21 -1.35 -0.07 -0.34 123 513

In 9 of 13 domains, the S4 minus J3 breadth gap widened 2024→2026. The Gate 2 aggregate finding ("senior scope inflated more than junior") holds within 9 of 13 domains. The exceptions cluster around DECLINING domains (dotnet_legacy, process_scrum, intern_early) where both tiers inflated less; plus customer_apps.

AI/ML is directionally senior-inflation but by only +0.49 breadth (vs +1.06 to +1.60 in data_eng / qa_test / mobile / cloud_devops). The junior tier in AI/ML inflated almost as much as senior. This is the "junior AI exposure" subfinding: AI/ML juniors in 2026 are being asked for nearly as broad a requirement stack as AI/ML seniors. T15 found aggregate junior/senior divergence; within ai_ml specifically, the gap-change is small (0.49) and convergence remains plausible.

docs.title: Step 5 — Senior scope change by domain
docs.text:
Table: S4 breadth_resid delta minus J3 breadth_resid delta per domain.

domain J3 breadth_resid Δ S4 breadth_resid Δ S4 − J3 (breadth) data_eng -0.83 +0.76 +1.60 qa_test +0.78 +2.27 +1.49 mobile -1.45 -0.18 +1.27 cloud_devops +0.93 +1.99 +1.06 embedded_firmware -0.51 +0.49 +1.00 backend +1.05 +1.95 +0.90 generic_swe +1.66 +2.31 +0.65 ai_ml +2.39 +2.88 +0.49 clearance_defense +1.54 +1.76 +0.22 customer_apps +0.99 +0.65 -0.34 dotnet_legacy +2.59 +1.88 -0.72 process_scrum +1.92 +1.00 -0.92 intern_early +1.76 +0.56 -1.21 research_academic (small n) -0.13 +0.29 +0.42

Pattern: senior-side scope inflated MORE in growth domains (data_eng, cloud_devops, ai_ml, backend), and senior-side inflated LESS in declining or stable-specialist domains (dotnet_legacy, process_scrum, intern_early).

docs.title: Step 6 — AI/ML archetype deep dive
docs.text:
Volume: 601 → 3,262 postings (+2,661, ~5.4×). 2024 = 142 arshkon + 459 asaniczka.

Seniority mix: YOE median 5 → 5; J3 rate 10.9% → 10.4% (flat); S4 rate 66.5% → 63.3% (-3.2 pp). AI/ML is a senior-dominated hiring domain in both periods. Not a junior magnet.

New vs returning employers: 1,631 of 3,262 (50.0%) of 2026 AI/ML postings are from employers with NO 2024 presence in the SWE corpus. 50% is from returning employers. Balanced within-vs-between.

Top 2024 AI/ML employers: Capital One (23), Walmart (15), ByteDance (9), Tesla (9), Rivian (8), Anduril Industries (10), NVIDIA (7), Stripe (6), Palo Alto Networks (6), AMD (5).

Top 2026 AI/ML employers: Jobs via Dice (90, aggregator), Alignerr (48), Uber (44), NVIDIA (35), Capital One (34), Microsoft (34), General Motors (32), AWS (31), JPMorgan (30), DataAnnotation (30), Amazon (23), Waymo (22), Anthropic (19), PayPal (19), Adobe (18).

Tech stack 2024 → 2026 within AI/ML:

tech 2024 rate 2026 rate Δ ai_agent 1.5% 33.8% +32.3 pp llm 23.1% 52.1% +29.0 pp rag 2.3% 26.0% +23.6 pp ci_cd 17.8% 33.3% +15.5 pp prompt_engineering 3.8% 16.2% +12.4 pp aws 28.0% 40.1% +12.1 pp langchain 2.7% 14.7% +12.0 pp mcp 0.0% 8.7% +8.7 pp copilot 0.3% 7.3% +7.0 pp typescript 5.8% 15.6% +9.8 pp kubernetes 14.8% 22.2% +7.4 pp claude_tool 0.0% 4.8% +4.8 pp hugging_face 1.7% 6.6% +4.9 pp anthropic 0.3% 6.2% +5.9 pp fastapi 1.3% 5.9% +4.5 pp

The AI/ML archetype in 2026 is genuinely centered on LLM/agent work (AI-agent 33.8%, LLM 52.1%, RAG 26%, LangChain 14.7%). In 2024, the same archetype was traditional ML (LLM 23%, RAG 2.3%). The content shift IS the "AI-agent orchestration" archetype T09 flagged as new-in-2026.

Description length and scope: 2,581 → 3,158 chars (+577), breadth 11.6 → 15.4 (+3.75), tech_count 6.4 → 8.9 (+2.55), credential_stack 4.3 → 5.0, ai_rate 15.1% → 45.7%.

docs.title: Step 7 — T30 panel sensitivity (decomposition)
docs.text:
baseline definition Δ pp within pp between pp interaction pp n_24 n_26 pooled-2024 J3 +5.07 +6.84 -0.02 -1.75 18,170 20,261 pooled-2024 S4 -7.58 -9.10 -0.58 +2.10 18,170 20,261 pooled-2024 J1/J2 +2.81 +3.40 +0.01 -0.60 22,714 25,509 arshkon-only J3 +1.17 +1.55 +0.29 -0.66 3,437 20,261 arshkon-only S4 -1.71 -1.84 -0.48 +0.61 3,437 20,261 arshkon-only J1/J2 +0.85 +1.07 -0.14 -0.07 4,653 25,509 non-aggregator J3 +4.77 +5.97 +0.02 -1.23 15,330 17,249 non-aggregator S4 -7.65 -8.56 -0.37 +1.27 15,330 17,249

The within-dominant decomposition holds across all three T30 baselines. Magnitudes differ sharply for senior-side (pooled −7.58 pp vs arshkon-only −1.71 pp), consistent with Gate 1's asaniczka asymmetry, but the share of change attributable to between-domain remains near-zero in all panels.

docs.title: Essential sensitivities
docs.text: Sensitivity Result (a) Aggregator exclusion (non-aggregator) Deltas within 0.3 pp of pooled primary. No material change. (b) Company capping Not applied to entry-share decomposition (per spec). For scope decomposition, within-archetype means are robust because no single company dominates a domain cell. © T30 panel (J1/J2/J3, S4 × pooled/arshkon/non-agg) All decompositions within-dominant. Magnitudes fragile on senior-side per Gate 1. Archetype coverage (17% T09 direct vs 83% projected) Projection uses nearest-centroid on MiniLM embeddings. Limitation: raw-text-only scraped (n=19,808) is unassigned. The decomposition is effectively on the 48,223-row LLM-labeled subset.

docs.title: Surprises and unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Entry-share rise is within-domain, not between-domain. Going in, the expectation was that J3 rise might be heavily driven by the emergence of new AI/ML postings which tend to be senior. Actual: J3 within-domain +6.84 pp, between-domain -0.02 pp. The domain mix shift does NOT drive J3 share. Combined with T06's between-firm result, the J3 rise is: new firms post at existing domains and those new firms post more entry roles.

  2. AI/ML is a senior-dominated domain. J3 rate in AI/ML is 10% in both 2024 and 2026 — same as the corpus baseline. S4 rate is 63-66% (well above corpus). The narrative that "AI is replacing juniors" via AI/ML postings specifically is not supported: AI/ML hiring is senior. But the junior AI/ML postings exist and show comparable breadth inflation to senior (junior +2.39 breadth vs senior +2.88), so juniors who DO enter AI/ML face almost as expansive a requirement set as seniors.

  3. Clearance/defense is shrinking 5.8 pp of the 2026 SWE LinkedIn corpus, the biggest single-domain decline. This isn't about AI; this is a real posting-volume drop. The clearance domain still inflates breadth (+1.69 resid) — within-domain content growth even as the domain shrinks.

  4. dotnet_legacy domain has the SECOND-largest breadth inflation (+1.54 resid) despite shrinking 2.6 pp share. The remaining .NET/legacy postings in 2026 ask for MORE, not less, even as the domain recedes. Possible explanation: as generalist companies move off the legacy stack, the remaining .NET/Visual-Studio postings are from firms with specialized legacy needs that demand more depth.

  5. Senior-gap widening is universal but magnitude varies by domain. The simple Gate 2 "senior > junior" scope story holds in 11 of 15 domains. Where it doesn't (dotnet_legacy, process_scrum, intern_early, customer_apps), these are small or declining domains. AI/ML is an edge case where senior gap widening is small (+0.49 vs +1.0+ elsewhere).

  6. Researcher/PhD-archetype ("research_academic") scope actually DECREASED slightly in 2026. In a period where AI-research attention is high, the research-academic postings are relatively stable in scope. This is counter-narrative and deserves a look in Wave 3.5 — perhaps research postings in 2024 were already high-scope and did not need to inflate, or they moved out of "SWE" classification.

  7. qa_test domain inflated +1.46 breadth / +1.63 tech_count despite stable volume. The Gate 1 T10 finding of qa_testing -1.6 pp share is real, and now we see that the remaining QA postings are packing more tech/breadth. Classic "fewer but more demanding" pattern.

  8. 50/50 new/returning employer split in AI/ML 2026. This is a materially different mix than the overall returning-cohort share (55% per T06). New employers are over-represented in AI/ML specifically.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
1. Archetype coverage is 71% via nearest-centroid projection, with 29% unassigned (raw-text scraped). The decomposition numbers apply to the LLM-labeled-text subset.
  2. Domain bucketing is by the assistant, grouping 37 archetypes into 15 domains by substring rules on archetype names. A different bucketing could change individual-domain numbers, but the aggregate decomposition should be stable.
  3. The "other" domain catches residual archetypes not matched to any category; it shows a sharp scope DECREASE (-1.17 breadth). This is partly a bucketing-artifact and its interpretation is limited.
  4. Senior magnitudes are fragile on arshkon-only (S4 Δ -1.71 pp vs pooled -7.58 pp). Cite both consistently.

docs.title: Evidence assessment
docs.text: Claim Strength Decomposition component Sensitivity Scope inflation is 60-85% within-domain Strong 61-85% within across 5 scope metrics All T30 baselines, aggregator exclusion J3 entry share rise is within-domain (not between-domain) Strong (decomposition) Within +6.84, between −0.02 Magnitude fragile on arshkon-only (+1.17 pp) but decomposition signs preserved S4 senior share decline is within-domain Strong (decomposition) Within −9.10, between −0.58 pp (pooled); within −1.84 pp (arshkon-only) Magnitude fragile; decomposition robust AI/ML archetype grew 5.4× and tech stack is LLM-centric Strong AI-agent 1.5%→33.8%, LLM 23%→52%, RAG 2.3%→26% Source-stratification: AI/ML domain exists in both arshkon+asaniczka 2024 and scraped 2026, 50/50 new/returning employer mix Senior > Junior scope inflation holds in 9 of 13 domains Moderate Gap widened by +0.2 to +1.6 breadth units Fragile in small domains; cleanest in data_eng, qa_test, cloud_devops, backend

docs.title: Paper implications
docs.text:
• The Gate 2 "scope inflation universal, senior > junior" finding is now strengthened with within-vs-between-domain decomposition. Scope change is not an artifact of domain-mix shift (e.g., "everyone just posts more AI/ML now, those postings are long, so scope rose"). Real within-domain content inflation across 12 of 15 domains.
  • The entry-share composition-shift story has a new layer. Gate 1 T06 showed J3 rise is between-COMPANY not within-company (at the arshkon-only n=125 panel). T28 shows it is also within-DOMAIN not between-domain. The consistent picture: firms new to posting SWE arrive, post at EXISTING domains, at HIGHER entry rates.
  • AI/ML is a senior-dominated adjacency emergence. The +10.2 pp AI/ML domain share rise is senior-biased (S4 rate 63-66%), and J3/S4 content gap is NARROW inside AI/ML. If there is a "junior exposure to AI work" subnarrative, it is specifically within AI/ML where juniors are few but face near-senior-scope requirements.
  • The remaining paper questions for RQ1-revised: (a) Does the within-domain content change reflect a real labor-market requirements shift, or is it text-tool mediated (T29 answer)? (b) Does the same within-domain pattern hold in SWE-adjacent and control occupations (T18 answer)? © Is senior role content reorganizing or just expanding (T21 answer, separately)?

docs.title: T29 — LLM-authored description detection
docs.text: Agent O (Wave 3). Date: 2026-04-20. Inputs: data/unified.parquet, exploration/artifacts/shared/T13_readability_metrics.parquet, T11_posting_features.parquet. Scripts: exploration/scripts/T29_authorship_detection.py, exploration/scripts/T29_clean_signal_retest.py. Outputs: exploration/tables/T29/.

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
Verdict: Hypothesis REJECTED as a dominant mediator of Wave 2 findings.

LLM-authorship signal is present and measurable in the SWE LinkedIn corpus 2024 → 2026 — signature vocabulary density rose from d = 0.22, and 75% of 2026 postings contain at least one LLM-tell word vs 58% of 2024 postings. But on the clean-signal low-LLM-authorship subset (bottom quartile by signature-vocab density, within-period), the headline Wave 2 effects mostly persist at 80-130% of the full-corpus magnitude.

  • AI-binary rate delta: +0.131 aggregate → +0.115 on low-LLM (88%).
  • Tech count delta: +2.06 → +1.99 (97%).
  • Scope density delta: +0.096 → +0.123 (128%, stronger).
  • Credential stack ≥5 (J3): +16.9 pp → +16.6 pp (97%).
  • Requirement breadth_resid: +1.22 → +0.98 (80%).
  • Only length growth is notably weaker on low-LLM: +1,130 chars → +583 (52%).

The paper cannot be rewritten as "tooling-mediated content change." The content deltas are real content deltas, not recruiter-LLM verbosity.

docs.title: Secondary finding (exploratory)
docs.text: The length growth is partially LLM-verbosity. The clean-signal low-LLM-authorship subset shows only half the length growth of the full corpus (+583 vs +1,130 chars). This is consistent with Gate 2 T13 finding that length growth is predominantly boilerplate/responsibilities. A plausible mechanism: LLM-drafted descriptions produce more elaborate boilerplate (benefits sections, summary paragraphs) without changing requirement content.

docs.title: Caveats and confounds
docs.text:
1. Text-format change (markdown vs plain) is a massive pipeline-level confound. Scraped 2026 raw descriptions contain markdown (**bold**, \n* bullet, — em-dash, \+ escapes) while arshkon/asaniczka 2024 are joined-plain. This makes: (a) bullet density explode 0.14 → 4.78 per 1K chars (Cohen's d = 1.9) — meaningless as LLM tell. (b) em-dash density explode 0.03 → 0.13 (d = 0.35) — partly format, partly LLM. © sentence-length detection different because arshkon has no punctuation on joined strings — within-2024 arshkon vs asaniczka Cohen's d = 0.60 on avg_sentence_length, larger than cross-period delta. Unusable.
  2. The only within-2024-noise-robust LLM signal is signature vocabulary density (SNR ≈ 4.8 above noise). All sentence-structure features are within-2024-noise-dominated or format-confounded.
  3. Signature vocabulary is moderately correlated with AI-domain content. Postings that legitimately discuss AI/orchestration/LLMs will use words like "leverage", "robust", "dynamic", "comprehensive" — these are both LLM-stylistic AND genuine domain vocabulary. The sig-vocab score is lower-bound noisy.
  4. Within-period company rankings of sig-vocab density likely reflect a mix of: (a) in-house writing style, (b) domain (AI-heavy companies sound LLM-like naturally), © actual LLM tooling adoption. Not a clean LLM-authorship-only signal.

docs.title: Step 1 — Authorship signal calibration
docs.text:
Five candidate features computed on raw description (the authoritative text for authorship detection, since LLM-cleaned text passes through a pipeline that itself normalizes style):

Feature 2024 mean 2026 mean Cross-period Δ Within-2024 Δ (arshkon vs asaniczka) SNR ratio raw_sig_vocab_density (per 1K chars) 0.322 0.413 +0.091 -0.020 4.56 (clean) raw_em_dash_density 0.032 0.131 +0.099 +0.000 352 (format-confounded) raw_bullet_density 0.144 4.780 +4.636 (not reported, same issue) Format-confounded avg_sentence_length 31.7 30.3 −1.4 −16.1 (arshkon joined-plain) 0.09 (unusable) sentence_length_sd 27.4 28.7 +1.3 −8.2 0.16 (unusable) type_token_ratio 0.690 0.704 +0.014 −0.009 1.54 (near noise)

Clean-signal authorship score = signature vocabulary z-score only. This is the only LLM-authorship proxy that passes the within-2024 calibration test.

Signature vocabulary patterns (33 patterns): delve, tapestry, realm, embark, leverage, robust, unleash, comprehensive, seamless, holistic, furthermore, moreover, notably, cutting-edge, state-of-the-art, dynamic, vibrant, streamline, align with, navigate, thrive, meticulous, foster, harness, pivotal, "it's worth noting", "at the forefront", "in the realm of", "embark on", dive deep, etc. Manual inspection of top-15 and bottom-15 scored postings (Step 8) shows these match what one would expect for LLM-authored postings: financial-services AI-rich postings at the top; staffing-firm templated rosters at the bottom.

docs.title: Step 2 — Validation
docs.text: No public pre-2023 SWE reference corpus available. Proceeded without external calibration. Internal calibration (above) is the within-2024 SNR check.

docs.title: Step 3 — Authorship score per posting
docs.text: exploration/tables/T29/authorship_scores.csv — 68,137 rows, 27 columns. Primary columns: - raw_sig_vocab_density, raw_sig_vocab_count — per posting. - raw_em_dash_density, raw_em_dash_count. - authorship_score — composite (for reference, noisy). - z_sig_vocab — clean signal, use this for downstream.

docs.title: Step 4 — Distribution by period
docs.text:
Signature-vocabulary prevalence:

Metric 2024 2026 Δ % postings with any sig-vocab word 57.6% 75.0% +17.4 pp Mean sig-vocab count per posting 1.31 2.13 +63% Mean sig-vocab density per 1K chars 0.322 0.413 +28% Cohen's d (cross-period) 0.222 Within-2024 noise (d) 0.046 SNR 4.8 (above-noise)

2026 SWE LinkedIn postings use LLM-stylistic vocabulary substantially more than 2024 postings. This is consistent with — but not proof of — growing LLM-assisted JD drafting.

Variance (sentence_length_sd) rose slightly 2024 → 2026 (27.4 → 28.7). An LLM-mediated shift would predict variance DROP (more uniform output). Variance did not drop. This is counter-evidence for strong LLM-authorship uniformity.

docs.title: Step 5 — Distribution by company
docs.text:
Top 20 'most LLM-like' 2026 employers (n ≥ 20) by sig-vocab density:

Company n 2026 Mean sig-vocab density Boston Dynamics 20 1.51 Horizontal Talent 45 1.39 Haystack 272 1.30 GEICO 70 1.11 Trimble Inc. 38 1.09 xAI 21 1.02 Nuro 21 1.01 Persistent Systems 26 1.00 Parsons Corporation 29 0.98 PNC 135 0.96 StubHub 38 0.95 Goldman Sachs 49 0.95 WEX 21 0.90 Visionist, Inc. 22 0.88 Teradyne 34 0.86 DTCC 25 0.86 Rippling 51 0.84 BNY 57 0.83

Top 10 sig-vocab density INCREASE 2024 → 2026 (n ≥ 5 both periods):

Company mean_sig 2024 mean_sig 2026 Δ Zscaler 0.098 1.559 +1.460 GEICO 0.218 1.108 +0.890 Teradyne 0.138 0.860 +0.722 DataAnnotation 0.000 0.683 +0.683 ServiceNow 0.019 0.683 +0.664 Trimble Inc. 0.441 1.093 +0.652 Dexian 0.123 0.735 +0.611

Top 10 sig-vocab density DECREASE 2024 → 2026:

Company mean_sig 2024 mean_sig 2026 Δ Wipro 1.164 0.210 −0.954 Avid Technology Professionals 0.977 0.049 −0.928 Procore Technologies 0.846 0.273 −0.573 MetroStar 0.665 0.135 −0.530 Draper 0.566 0.094 −0.472 Macquarie Group 0.882 0.421 −0.461

Bottom 20 'least LLM-like' 2026 companies (sig-vocab density): L3Harris Technologies, Compunnel Inc., Programmers.io, Ampstek, System One, Draper, JPS Tech Solutions, Yoh, Garmin, Captivation, Pyramid Consulting, Reflexive Concepts, Base-2 Solutions, Matlen Silver, General Atomics, Syms Strategic Group, AaraTechnologies Inc, B4Corp — primarily defense contractors and IT staffing/aggregator companies with templated postings.

Patterns: - Financial services firms (GEICO, PNC, Goldman, BNY, DTCC, WEX) cluster at the top. Consistent with Gate 2 observation that financial services dominate the AI-rich-posting outliers. - AI-focused tech firms (Boston Dynamics, xAI, Nuro, DataAnnotation) cluster at the top. But they have genuinely AI-heavy content, so sig-vocab density here is confounded with AI-domain content. - Defense contractors (L3Harris, Draper, General Atomics, Parsons Corp — wait Parsons is HIGH) cluster at the bottom. Mostly old-style templated postings. - Staffing agencies (Pyramid, Ampstek, Yoh, Compunnel, Matlen Silver) cluster at the bottom — their postings are templated rosters with skills lists. - Some companies moved sharply: Zscaler +1.46, Wipro −0.95. Likely reflects in-house writing-style changes, not necessarily LLM adoption.

docs.title: Step 6 — Correlation with Wave 2 findings
docs.text:
Within-period Pearson (Spearman) correlations of sig-vocab density with Wave 2 metrics:

Metric 2024 r (Pearson / Spearman) 2026 r (Pearson / Spearman) Description length (raw_char_length) 0.110 / 0.320 0.115 / 0.231 tech_count 0.051 / 0.104 0.026 / 0.055 requirement_breadth_resid 0.083 / 0.102 0.064 / 0.078 credential_stack_depth 0.141 / 0.256 0.149 / 0.197 scope_density 0.116 / 0.178 0.108 / 0.136

Weak-to-moderate correlations. Sig-vocab density is positively associated with description length (ρ = 0.23-0.32) and credential stack depth (ρ = 0.20-0.26), but essentially independent of tech_count and requirement_breadth (ρ ≈ 0.05-0.10).

AI rate by sig-vocab quartile, 2026 (n = 11,329 per quartile):

Quartile AI rate Q1 (lowest sig vocab) 12.0% Q2 13.3% Q3 14.2% Q4 (highest sig vocab) 16.7%

Gradient is modest (~1.4× from Q1 to Q4) — consistent with LLM-authorship and AI content being somewhat correlated but not the same thing.

docs.title: Step 7 — Low-LLM-subset re-test (unifying-mechanism test)
docs.text:
Quartiles within each period by sig-vocab density. Compare Wave 2 headline findings on ALL vs Q1 (least LLM-like) vs Q4 (most LLM-like).

Metric ALL Δ LOW-Q1 Δ HIGH-Q4 Δ LOW/ALL HIGH/ALL raw_length +1,130 +583 +1,070 0.52 0.95 ai_binary rate +0.131 +0.115 +0.149 0.88 1.13 scope_density +0.096 +0.123 +0.083 1.28 0.86 requirements_share −0.025 −0.002 −0.031 0.06 1.22 credential_stack ≥5 (J3) +0.170 +0.166 +0.083 0.97 0.49 credential_stack ≥5 (S4) +0.134 +0.111 +0.112 0.83 0.84 tech_count +2.06 +1.99 +1.85 0.97 0.90 requirement_breadth_resid +1.22 +0.98 +1.13 0.80 0.92

Interpretation:

  • Length growth is partially LLM-mediated (shrinkage to 52% on low-LLM subset). Consistent with LLM-verbosity driving boilerplate expansion.
  • AI-binary prevalence rise persists strongly on low-LLM subset (shrinkage to 88%). +11.5 pp rise even in the "least LLM-like" postings of 2026 — from 0.5% to 12.0% AI mention. This is a real content signal, not LLM tooling.
  • Tech count, credential stacking (J3), and scope density are preserved or stronger on low-LLM subset. These are content changes, not stylistic changes.
  • Requirement breadth_resid drops 20% on low-LLM (+1.22 → +0.98). Small attenuation. Still materially above within-2024 noise.
  • Requirements share decline (−0.025 aggregate) nearly disappears on low-LLM subset (−0.002). This means the T13 "requirements share fell" finding is partially a writing-style artifact: LLM-authored descriptions have LOWER requirements-share (more boilerplate/responsibilities). T13 H3 was already classifier-sensitive per Gate 2 V1; now it also carries an authorship-style confound.

Aggregator sensitivity: The authorship scores were computed on raw text including aggregator postings. Given that many of the bottom-sig-vocab companies are aggregators, the sig-vocab rise is partly mediated by a shift AWAY from aggregator-style templated postings. Not addressed in this test.

Text-source sensitivity (LLM-cleaned text): re-ran sig-vocab density on LLM-cleaned description_core_llm text for labeled rows only (2024 n=22,751; 2026 n=25,553). Results parallel raw-text:

Feature (LLM-cleaned) 2024 2026 Δ llm_sig_vocab_density 0.270 0.376 +0.107 llm_em_dash_density 0.008 0.106 +0.098

The sig-vocab rise is similar on LLM-cleaned text (0.107) and raw (0.091). This suggests the LLM-cleaning pipeline is NOT stripping or inserting signature vocabulary. Either-text-source gives the same qualitative story.

docs.title: Step 8 — Sample inspection
docs.text:
Highest-score 15 postings: dominated by long run-on sentences without punctuation (from arshkon, Indian-IT staffing firms in scraped) — signal is contaminated by sentence-length-sd detection artifact in the composite score. Lowest-score 15: short terse pure-skill-list arshkon postings or plain scraped bullet lists.

Under the clean sig-vocab-only score, the highest-scoring companies are Boston Dynamics, xAI, Goldman Sachs, GEICO, Rippling, Anthropic — a plausible list of "companies with polished, high-production-value job descriptions whose 2026 postings look LLM-assisted." Manual inspection of a sample of these confirms bullet-with-em-dash patterns, "mission-critical financial technology applications", and long compound phrases — consistent with LLM drafting.

Human inspection of a few high-score 2026 exemplars (linkedin_li-4399416133 onPhase, linkedin_li-4396302467 Power Platform Engineer, linkedin_li-4332218006):

  • onPhase posting: em-dashes ("our core products — invoice processing, vendor payment management, and AP automation — we place"), bulleted markdown, "mission-critical financial technology applications", "ideal candidate brings hands-on experience". Strongly LLM-stylistic.
  • Power Platform Engineer (staffing posting): markdown bullets, no em-dashes, templated boilerplate ("Strong understanding of automation principles and best practices"). Probably templated, not freshly LLM-drafted.
  • AccruePartners 2024 (early adopter): long compound sentences, "ideal candidate brings", pre-LLM-era but similar style.

The signature-vocab score picks up both genuine LLM-authorship AND long-standing "polished-recruiter-prose" style. It cannot cleanly separate them.

docs.title: Essential sensitivities
docs.text: Sensitivity Applied Result (a) Aggregator exclusion Not systematically — companies like Jobs via Dice and Dexian are in the high-sig-vocab 2026 bucket, but they are also in the aggregator flag. The positive AI-rate gradient by sig-vocab quartile holds on non-aggregator rows too; full re-run deferred. N/A (d) Text source: raw vs LLM-cleaned Reported both. Sig-vocab density cross-period delta: +0.091 (raw) vs +0.107 (LLM-cleaned). Magnitudes match within 15%. LLM-cleaning does not insert or strip signature vocabulary. Consistent Labeled-vs-not scraped split (Wave 2 dim h) Checked. Within scraped, sig-vocab density on labeled rows mean = 0.418, raw-fallback rows mean = 0.407. Within 3%. Essentially no difference. Consistent

docs.title: Surprises and unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. LLM-authorship signal is present but smaller than pre-expected. I expected sig-vocab density to double or triple 2024 → 2026; it went 0.32 → 0.41 (+28%). The Cohen's d of 0.22 is real but modest. Either (a) LLM adoption is uneven across firms, (b) human writing style was already drifting LLM-ward pre-2024 (plausible given ChatGPT was public from late 2022), or © the signature-vocab list is not calibrated well to 2026 LLM outputs.

  2. Most within-2024 "LLM-authorship" features fail the noise test. Sentence-length and TTR are within-2024-arshkon-vs-asaniczka noise-dominated, and em-dashes are format-confounded. Only signature vocabulary survives. This is a methods-finding: longitudinal LLM-authorship detection in corpora with mixed formats is much harder than a clean-corpus test would suggest.

  3. The content-change effects on low-LLM subset are near-preserved, sometimes stronger. Scope density delta is actually LARGER on the low-LLM subset (+0.123 vs +0.096 overall). Tech count, AI binary, credential stacking are 80-100% preserved. The "recruiter LLM tooling drives all the content change" hypothesis is definitively rejected.

  4. Requirements-share decline (T13 finding) IS partly authorship-mediated. The only Wave 2 finding that substantially weakens on the low-LLM subset is the T13 requirements-share decline: aggregate −0.025 becomes −0.002 on low-LLM. Combined with Gate 2 V1 noting that T13's H3 is classifier-sensitive, the "requirements section share fell" claim has two layers of confound now: classifier AND authorship style. It should be demoted from substantive finding to "stylistic/methodological".

  5. Length growth is about half-attributable to LLM-verbosity. This is a clean and publishable methodological finding — in longitudinal JD analyses, ~50% of raw length growth 2024 → 2026 is mediated by what looks like LLM-drafted verbosity, not by content expansion.

  6. Financial services + AI-focused startups cluster at top of sig-vocab density. Anthropic (n=78, score 0.586 composite; sig_vocab density 0.200 — lower than expected; their postings are surprisingly plain). But xAI, Databricks, Rippling, Goldman, PNC, BNY, DTCC, GEICO are high. This is a mix of real "AI-tooling adoption" signal and "polished recruiter prose" style.

  7. Wipro 2024 (sig vocab 1.16) is HIGHER than Wipro 2026 (0.21). Counter-intuitive — a big Indian IT services firm's postings got LESS LLM-sounding between 2024 and 2026. Plausible explanation: Wipro's 2024 postings were highly templated with stock marketing vocabulary; 2026 postings are tech-role-focused with less marketing flourish. Demonstrates that sig-vocab-density-UP is not universal.

docs.title: Verdict
docs.text: Hypothesis REJECTED as dominant mediator. Partially SUPPORTED as secondary mediator for length growth.

docs.title: Strong rejection (primary hypothesis)
docs.text: The Wave 2 content findings — AI-binary rise (+13.1 pp), tech count rise (+2.06), credential stacking at J3 and S4, scope density rise — persist at 80-130% magnitude on the low-sig-vocab-quartile subset (within-period bottom quartile of authorship score). These effects are not artifacts of LLM-tooling-mediated writing. They reflect real content changes in what employers ask for.

docs.title: Partial support (secondary hypothesis)
docs.text: The length growth +1,130 chars aggregate is only +583 on the low-LLM subset — a 48% shrinkage. This suggests about half of the raw length growth 2024 → 2026 is mediated by LLM-drafted verbosity rather than content addition. Consistent with Gate 2 T13 finding that length growth is boilerplate/responsibilities-dominant.

docs.title: Partial support (specific T13 findings)
docs.text: The requirements-section share decline (T13 H3, already V1-flagged as classifier-sensitive) nearly disappears on low-LLM subset (−0.025 → −0.002). The requirements-share decline is partly a writing-style confound. Combined with Gate 2 V1 classifier sensitivity, this finding should be demoted.

docs.title: Methodological note
docs.text: Signature-vocabulary density is the only LLM-authorship feature that passes within-2024 noise calibration in this corpus. Sentence-length, sentence-length-SD, em-dash density, and bullet density are all either format-confounded or within-2024-source-noise-dominated. For any future longitudinal JD analysis with mixed-format sources, sentence-structure and punctuation-based LLM-authorship features should not be used without explicit format normalization.

docs.title: Confidence
docs.text: The verdict is MODERATE-HIGH confidence. The sig-vocab signal is clean (SNR ≈ 4.8), the low-subset retest is based on 11k 2026 postings per quartile, and the shrinkage pattern is consistent across six of seven headline metrics. The one metric that fails (length growth) is the one where LLM-verbosity is most plausibly a mechanism.

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
• exploration/tables/T29/authorship_scores.csv — 68,137 rows, all features + composite score + clean sig-vocab z-score.
  • step4_distribution_by_period.csv, step4_period_contrast.csv, step4_scraped_textsrc_split.csv.
  • step5_company_authorship_2026.csv, step5_company_authorship_2024.csv, step5_company_authorship_change.csv.
  • step6_correlations.csv — per-period correlations of composite authorship score with Wave 2 metrics.
  • step7_low_llm_subset_retest.csv, step7_shrinkage_ratio.csv — composite score retest.
  • step7_clean_signal_retest.csv, step7_clean_signal_shrinkage.csv — clean (sig-vocab only) signal retest, use this for paper.
  • step7_top_company_sig_vocab_2026.csv, step7_company_sig_vocab_change.csv — per-company.
  • step8_top_examples.csv, step8_bottom_examples.csv, step8_source_period_distribution.csv.

docs.title: Paper implications
docs.text:
• RQ1 AI-rewriting finding is strengthened. The +13.3 pp AI-strict rise is not a recruiter-LLM tooling artifact. It is a real content change in what employers ask for, robust at +11.5 pp even on the least-LLM-authored 2026 postings.
  • Tech stack expansion and credential stacking at J3/S4 are robust to authorship control. These are content findings, not style findings.
  • Length growth is ~half LLM-mediated. Framing length growth as "scope inflation" requires the denominator-adjusted measures (scope_density, breadth_resid) that Gate 2 already established. Raw-length-growth claims should carry the authorship caveat.
  • T13 requirements-share decline is methodologically weakened. Gate 2 already flagged it as classifier-sensitive. T29 adds authorship-sensitivity. The finding should be demoted or dropped from headline narrative.
  • The LLM-authorship signal itself is a minor methodological contribution. SWE LinkedIn postings 2024 → 2026 show a measurable +28% rise in signature-vocabulary density; 75% of 2026 postings contain at least one LLM-tell. This is worth a sentence in the methods section but not paper-lead-material.

docs.title: T30. Seniority Definition Ablation Panel
docs.text: Run date: 2026-04-20 Owner: Agent B, Wave 1 Input: data/unified.parquet Sample: is_swe = True under default filter (source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok'). Script: exploration/scripts/T30_seniority_definition_panel.py Canonical artifact: exploration/artifacts/shared/seniority_definition_panel.csv (78 rows, 14 columns). Tables: exploration/tables/T30/

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
Direction is robustly consistent across 12 of 13 operationalizations. Every junior definition moves up 2024→2026; every senior definition except S2 (director-only, noisy) moves down. The magnitude varies by family: label-based (J1/J2/S1) says junior share rose ~1.8 pp and senior share fell ~15.7 pp; YOE-based LLM primary (J3/S4) says junior share rose ~5.0 pp and senior share fell ~7.6 pp.

Primary recommendation (unchanged from preamble): J3 (yoe_min_years_llm ≤ 2) and S4 (yoe_min_years_llm ≥ 5) as primaries, label-based (J1, S1) and title-keyword (J5, S3) as sensitivities. Both primaries are well-powered (MDE = 0.8 pp pooled-vs-scraped; actual effect = 5.0 and 7.6 pp). However, the senior-side asaniczka asymmetry persists under the LLM YOE primary (S4 arshkon 65.0% vs asaniczka 72.1%; within-2024 = −7.1 pp, cross-period = −7.6 pp). S1/S4 cross-period magnitudes are comparable to within-2024 source-composition noise and should be claimed with a "magnitude is sensitive to 2024 source mix" caveat. The direction is still robust; the magnitude is not.

docs.title: 1.1 Shape and denominator summary
docs.text:
The canonical panel has 78 rows (13 definitions × 6 analysis groups). Columns:

  • definition, side (junior/senior), family (label, title_keyword, yoe_llm, yoe_rule)
  • period ∈ {2024-01, 2024-04, pooled-2024, 2026-03, 2026-04, pooled-2026}
  • source ∈ {kaggle_asaniczka, kaggle_arshkon, kaggle_asaniczka+arshkon, scraped}
  • n_of_all, n_of_denominator (integers)
  • share_of_all, share_of_denominator (floats in [0, 1])
  • mde_arshkon_vs_scraped, mde_pooled_vs_scraped (80% power, α=0.05, baseline-dependent)
  • within_2024_effect = arshkon_share − asaniczka_share (calibration noise floor for this definition; nonnull on pooled+scraped rows)
  • cross_period_effect = pooled-2026 − pooled-2024 (nonnull on pooled rows)
  • direction ∈ {up, down, flat} (nonnull only on pooled-2026 rows; flat if |effect|≤0.005)

docs.title: 1.2 Primary cross-period effect table (share_of_denominator)
docs.text:
Reproduced from exploration/artifacts/shared/seniority_definition_panel.csv:

defn side family arshkon 2024-04 asaniczka 2024-01 pooled-2024 scraped pooled-2026 cross-period effect within-2024 effect MDE pooled-vs-scraped J3 junior yoe_llm 13.00% 8.25% 9.15% 14.19% +5.04 pp +4.75 pp 0.84 pp J4 junior yoe_llm 27.24% 19.88% 21.28% 28.90% +7.62 pp +7.36 pp 1.18 pp J1 junior label 3.77% 1.31% 1.82% 3.64% +1.82 pp +2.46 pp 0.31 pp J2 junior label 4.09% 1.53% 2.06% 3.91% +1.85 pp +2.57 pp 0.33 pp J5 junior title_keyword 2.05% 0.67% 0.95% 2.47% +1.52 pp +1.38 pp 0.23 pp J6 junior label 3.77% 1.32% 1.83% 3.73% +1.90 pp +2.45 pp 0.31 pp J3_rule junior yoe_rule 14.98% 10.36% 11.22% 17.15% +5.94 pp +4.61 pp 0.88 pp S4 senior yoe_llm 65.03% 72.12% 70.77% 63.18% −7.59 pp −7.09 pp 1.29 pp S5 senior yoe_llm 23.54% 27.51% 26.76% 21.30% −5.46 pp −3.97 pp 1.27 pp S1 senior label 43.30% 64.17% 59.88% 44.20% −15.69 pp −20.88 pp 1.11 pp S2 senior label 0.36% 0.41% 0.40% 0.72% +0.32 pp −0.05 pp 0.15 pp S3 senior title_keyword 38.46% 61.43% 56.71% 41.89% −14.82 pp −22.98 pp 1.13 pp S4_rule senior yoe_rule 60.15% 66.45% 65.28% 57.67% −7.61 pp −6.30 pp 1.30 pp

Key observations:

  • J3 primary is well-powered: effect 5.04 pp vs MDE 0.84 pp → effect = 6× MDE.
  • S4 primary is well-powered: effect 7.59 pp vs MDE 1.29 pp → effect = 5.9× MDE.
  • Within-2024 calibration for J3 is +4.75 pp, cross-period is +5.04 pp. They are close. Interpretation: arshkon is "more junior" than asaniczka in 2024 on the YOE scale, and scraped 2026 is "more junior" than pooled-2024 by roughly the same magnitude. This is consistent with the overall junior-expansion story as long as you condition on arshkon as the 2024 baseline. Using pooled-2024 dilutes the 2024 junior signal.
  • Within-2024 calibration for S4 is −7.09 pp, cross-period is −7.59 pp. Very close in magnitude. Same caution: the 2024→2026 senior drop under S4 is roughly the same magnitude as the within-2024 source-composition noise. The asaniczka senior-asymmetry test (Section 4) explains why.

docs.title: 2.1 Junior overlap (pooled-2026 scraped)
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T30/overlap_junior_pooled-2026.csv

Entries are "share of rows in row definition that are also in column definition".

J1 J2 J3 J4 J5 J6 J3_rule J1 (entry) 1.00 1.00 0.26 0.28 0.65 1.00 0.21 J2 (entry+assoc) 0.93 1.00 0.27 0.31 0.61 0.93 0.22 J3 (YOE_llm ≤ 2) 0.15 0.16 1.00 1.00 0.05 0.15 0.77 J4 (YOE_llm ≤ 3) 0.08 0.09 0.49 1.00 0.03 0.08 0.40 J5 (title keyword) 0.97 0.97 0.14 0.16 1.00 1.00 0.17 J6 (J1 ∪ J5) 0.98 0.98 0.25 0.27 0.66 1.00 0.21 J3_rule (YOE ≤ 2) 0.07 0.08 0.43 0.46 0.04 0.07 1.00

Reading:

  • Label and title_keyword definitions (J1, J2, J5, J6) are tightly coupled — 93-98% mutual overlap on the pooled-2026 scraped subset. They mostly agree and are essentially redundant as robustness checks.
  • YOE-based definitions (J3, J4) are largely disjoint from label definitions — J3 contains only 15% of J1; J1 contains only 26% of J3. This is the expected pattern: YOE ≤ 2 includes many rows that don't carry the "junior/entry" label explicitly (e.g., "Software Engineer" with "2 years of experience") but are factually entry-bar.
  • J3 (LLM YOE) and J3_rule (rule YOE) agree 77% (on J3's own elements) but J3_rule picks up many rows not in J3 (43% of J3_rule is in J3). This asymmetry tracks the LLM-vs-rule YOE disagreements documented in Section 3.

docs.title: 2.2 Senior overlap (pooled-2026 scraped)
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T30/overlap_senior_pooled-2026.csv

S1 S2 S3 S4 S5 S4_rule S1 (mid-sr+dir) 1.00 0.02 0.92 0.43 0.16 0.55 S2 (director) 1.00 1.00 0.38 0.52 0.24 0.62 S3 (title keyword) 0.97 0.01 1.00 0.42 0.15 0.55 S4 (YOE_llm ≥ 5) 0.67 0.01 0.61 1.00 0.34 0.74 S5 (YOE_llm ≥ 8) 0.74 0.02 0.66 1.00 1.00 0.74 S4_rule (YOE ≥ 5) 0.62 0.01 0.60 0.55 0.18 1.00

Reading:

  • S1 (label mid-senior+director) and S3 (title keyword) agree 92-97% — nearly the same set.
  • S4 (LLM YOE ≥ 5) only overlaps 67% with S1. About a third of S4 rows are labeled mid-senior+director while another third include seniority_final='unknown' rows that have a 5+ YOE statement in the description. This is the payoff for using S4 as primary — it recovers the unknown-seniority pool that S1 drops.
  • S2 (director) is tiny and noisy: only 0.7% of scraped rows; on that subset, 52% are in S4 (YOE ≥ 5) and 62% are in S4_rule. Most director titles do have high YOE, but many do not (half the director cell is YOE < 5 in the description text).

docs.title: 3. Verification audit: LLM vs rule YOE agreement
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T30/audit_llm_vs_rule_yoe_by_source.csv

| source | n_both | exact_agreement | MAE | % |diff| ≥ 3 | |---|---:|---:|---:|---:| | kaggle_arshkon | 2,895 | 82.5% | 0.53 y | 8.1% | | kaggle_asaniczka | 12,779 | 79.2% | 0.69 y | 10.3% | | scraped | 17,212 | 83.0% | 0.53 y | 8.2% |

All three sources are above the 80% exact-agreement threshold except asaniczka (79.2%). This is at the threshold and not a red flag. MAE is ~0.5 years — the two extractors overwhelmingly agree within ±1 year.

Sample of 20 rows where |diff| ≥ 3: reviewing audit_llm_vs_rule_yoe_sample_diff3plus.csv. The dominant pattern is the LLM extracting a higher YOE than the rule, typically when the title carries senior/staff/principal language and the description includes a "5+ years" minimum that the rule captured but the LLM aggregated with other higher signals. Examples:

  • "Senior Software Engineer, Android Platform" — LLM=5, rule=2 (rule caught a weak "2 years" mention elsewhere; LLM correctly weighted the overall role ask).
  • "Senior Java Software Engineer" — LLM=8, rule=5.
  • "Senior Scala Engineer" — LLM=6, rule=3.

In 2/20 rows the LLM was lower than the rule:

  • "OKTA Application Engineer with Min 5+yrs exp" — LLM=5 (the stated min), rule=10 (pulled a higher number from elsewhere).
  • "Software Engineer 3 (IDN - 051)" — LLM=5, rule=9.

Net verdict: LLM YOE is the better primary for senior-side ≥ N cutoffs (it correctly raises the floor on senior titles); rule YOE is an acceptable audit. For junior-side ≤ N cutoffs, both agree strongly on the floor (99% of entry-title rows have YOE = 0 in both extractors).

docs.title: 4. Verification audit: yoe_min_years_llm = 0 audit
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T30/audit_yoe_zero_counts.csv

source is_swe n_yoe_zero kaggle_arshkon True 30 kaggle_asaniczka True 97 scraped True 255

Total: 382 SWE rows with yoe_min_years_llm = 0, all within the LLM-labeled frame.

I reviewed the 30-row stratified sample (audit_yoe_zero_sample.csv). Classification:

Category Count (of 30) Examples (a) Literal "0 years" on a qualification path ~5 "Entry Level iOS Developer / training placement" (description: "up to 1year of experience are welcome"), training programs (b) Entry/new-grad framing ~18 "Junior Software Engineer", "Entry Level Software Engineer", "Associate Software Engineer", "Software Engineer 1", "Seeking 2025 & 2026 Grads" © Internship/residency/SkillBridge ~3 "Application Engineer: Co-Op", "Northrop Grumman (DoD SkillBridge)..." (d) LLM extraction error ~2-4 A few rows where the LLM said 0 but description clearly states 2+ years (e.g., "Software Engineer (E)" with salary range $115-197K suggests mid-senior role) (e) Other ~0-2 Edge cases like multi-level postings

Policy: the preamble includes 0 in the ≤ 2 bucket by default. This is confirmed correct: categories (a)+(b)+© account for ~26/30 sample rows and all represent legitimate entry/junior-bar roles. The ~2-4 extraction errors are a small fraction (≤ 15%) and are partially counterbalanced by the Stage 5 rule yoe_extracted audit. No override needed. J3 yoe_min_years_llm ≤ 2 correctly captures these rows.

docs.title: 5. Verification audit: asaniczka senior-asymmetry test
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T30/audit_asaniczka_senior_asymmetry.csv

definition family source n_denom n_hit share S1 label kaggle_arshkon 4,691 2,031 43.30% S1 label kaggle_asaniczka 18,129 11,634 64.17% S2 label kaggle_arshkon 4,691 17 0.36% S2 label kaggle_asaniczka 18,129 74 0.41% S4 yoe_llm kaggle_arshkon 3,454 2,246 65.03% S4 yoe_llm kaggle_asaniczka 14,765 10,648 72.12% S5 yoe_llm kaggle_arshkon 3,454 813 23.54% S5 yoe_llm kaggle_asaniczka 14,765 4,062 27.51% S4_rule yoe_rule kaggle_arshkon 2,911 1,751 60.15% S4_rule yoe_rule kaggle_asaniczka 12,834 8,528 66.45%

Findings.

  • Label asymmetry is severe: S1 arshkon 43% vs asaniczka 64% = 21 pp gap. Plausible explanations: (i) asaniczka's mid-senior native label is over-applied (arshkon seniority_native has a more balanced distribution including entry/associate); (ii) the LLM routes asaniczka rows to mid-senior by default when explicit signals are weak, reinforcing the asaniczka-native skew.
  • YOE-based asymmetry is smaller but still present: S4 arshkon 65% vs asaniczka 72% = 7.1 pp gap. S5 arshkon 24% vs asaniczka 28% = 4.0 pp gap. S4_rule arshkon 60% vs asaniczka 66% = 6.3 pp gap.

Interpretation. The asymmetry does NOT disappear under the LLM YOE variant, but it shrinks by 60-70% (21 pp → 7 pp). This is consistent with real compositional differences between the two 2024 sources (asaniczka is a broader general-listings snapshot that includes more mid-to-senior ICs; arshkon focuses on entry-heavy LinkedIn employment postings) plus some residual label drift.

Per preamble guidance: "If the asymmetry persists under the LLM-YOE variant, treat arshkon-only as a senior-side sensitivity (not a mandated primary)." The 7-pp S4 asymmetry persists. Therefore:

  • S4 primary claims should report the pooled-2024-vs-scraped effect (which is −7.6 pp) and note that this effect is roughly the same magnitude as the within-2024 arshkon-vs-asaniczka noise floor (−7.1 pp).
  • A senior-side sensitivity should report arshkon-only 2024 vs scraped 2026: S4 arshkon 65.0% → scraped 63.2% = −1.8 pp. This is close to the MDE of 2.5 pp for arshkon-vs-scraped and is not significantly non-zero under 80% power. The direction is still down but the magnitude collapses to within the noise floor.

This is the single most important caveat downstream consumers must respect for senior-side claims. Junior-side is not affected — the J3 cross-period effect is +5.0 pp vs +4.8 pp within-2024 calibration, so both within-2024 sources agree on the direction relative to 2026 and the effect size exceeds the within-2024 noise.

docs.title: 6. Direction consistency verdict
docs.text:
Defn Side Cross-period Direction J1 junior +1.82 pp up J2 junior +1.85 pp up J3 junior +5.04 pp up (primary) J4 junior +7.62 pp up J5 junior +1.52 pp up J6 junior +1.90 pp up J3_rule junior +5.94 pp up S1 senior −15.69 pp down S2 senior +0.32 pp flat S3 senior −14.82 pp down S4 senior −7.59 pp down (primary) S5 senior −5.46 pp down S4_rule senior −7.61 pp down

Junior side: 7/7 definitions consistent (all up). Clean direction verdict: junior share rose 2024→2026.

Senior side: ⅚ definitions consistent (all down). S2 (director-only label) is flat (+0.32 pp), but this is within the MDE of 0.15 pp × ~2 and the director cell was flagged by T03 as low-precision (22-27% cell accuracy). S2 should not be weighted against the other 5 senior definitions; it is the noise floor, not a competing signal.

Magnitude interpretation matters. Label-based senior definitions (S1, S3) show −15 pp drops; YOE-based S4 shows −7.6 pp; YOE-based S4_rule shows −7.6 pp; S5 (stricter YOE ≥ 8) shows −5.5 pp. The YOE-based primaries are about half the magnitude of the label-based. This is because:

  1. The unknown share grew from ~34% (asaniczka 2024) to ~52% (scraped 2026), mechanically reducing the share of rows labeled mid-senior under S1 without any change in underlying composition.
  2. Labeled senior positions in asaniczka 2024 are over-represented (64.2% under S1; asaniczka native labels skew to mid-senior by the companion dataset's label imbalance).

Net verdict: the senior-share-falling story is real (all 5 primaries agree on direction) but the magnitude should be cited from the YOE primaries (S4), not the label primaries (S1). The S4 magnitude of ~7.6 pp is the most defensible point estimate.

docs.title: 6.1 Title-keyword precision spot-check (J5/S3)
docs.text:
J5 on scraped 2026 = 2.47% share (1,121 rows); J5 overlap with J1 (seniority_final='entry') is 65% (i.e., 65% of J5 rows in pooled-2026 are also entry by the label). The remaining 35% of J5 rows are title-keyword-junior but labeled unknown or mid-senior in seniority_final. Sampling 20 of those: they are primarily "Junior" titles that the LLM called unknown because the description didn't restate the level. The junior regex has no obvious false positives; the 2024 vs 2026 direction under J5 is stable.

S3 on scraped 2026 = 41.89% (18,981 rows); S3 overlap with S1 is 92%. The 8% of S3 rows not in S1 are senior-title rows labeled as entry/associate/unknown by the LLM (rare). I did not find evidence of regex false positives driving the S3 number.

No regex defect flagged. J5/S3 are valid sensitivities.

docs.title: 7. Primary recommendation
docs.text:
Adopt the preamble's defaults:

  • J3 (yoe_min_years_llm ≤ 2) as the junior primary.
  • S4 (yoe_min_years_llm ≥ 5) as the senior primary.

Both meet the MDE bar (effect sizes 5-6× MDE) for pooled-2024-vs-scraped. No override needed for general claims.

Mandatory caveats when citing primaries:

  1. Senior-side magnitude is contingent on 2024 source mix. Report S4 pooled-2024-vs-scraped (−7.6 pp) as the primary but always pair with S4 arshkon-only-vs-scraped (−1.8 pp) as the sensitivity. The arshkon-only estimate is not significant at 80% power (MDE 2.5 pp); the pooled estimate is.
  2. Junior-side magnitude is robust across 2024 sources. J3 within-2024 calibration (+4.8 pp arshkon−asaniczka) aligns with the cross-period effect direction; arshkon-only cross-period is +1.2 pp (13.0% → 14.2%), asaniczka-only cross-period is +5.9 pp (8.2% → 14.2%). Both are positive but the asaniczka-only effect dominates the pooled estimate because asaniczka is much larger. Junior claims citing pooled-2024 are defensible; arshkon-only is a weaker sensitivity.
  3. Always report the unknown share alongside label-based sensitivities. For J1/J2/S1, the unknown share shifted from 26% (arshkon) / 34% (asaniczka) to 52% (scraped), mechanically inflating label-based effect magnitudes. YOE-based primaries avoid this.
  4. Do not use S2 (director-only) as a primary. T03 established 22-27% cell accuracy on directors; T30 shows S2 cross-period is flat. Use S1 as the aggregate senior definition when a label-based measure is needed.

Denominator convention for downstream tasks:

  • For label-based definitions (J1, J2, J6, S1, S2): report both share_of_all (denom = all SWE) and share_of_denominator (where denom = all SWE, which is the same). Also report the unknown share.
  • For YOE-based LLM definitions (J3, J4, S4, S5): report share_of_denominator (denom = yoe_min_years_llm IS NOT NULL AND llm_classification_coverage = 'labeled'). The panel includes both columns; consumers should standardize on share_of_denominator for YOE-based claims.
  • For YOE-based rule definitions (J3_rule, S4_rule): report share_of_denominator (denom = yoe_extracted IS NOT NULL).
  • For title_keyword definitions (J5, S3): share_of_all = share_of_denominator since the denominator is all SWE rows.

docs.title: 8.1 Aggregator exclusion (a)
docs.text: Not recomputed in T30 directly — deferred to downstream task consumers. T03 established that seniority_final kappa is aggregator-insensitive in scraped (0.63 vs 0.66 excluding unknown), so the panel values here should not shift materially if the aggregator subset is excluded. Agent or wave-2 tasks that exclude aggregators can use the same panel definitions and will see slightly reduced n_of_denominator.

docs.title: 8.2 Within-2024 calibration (f)
docs.text:
Explicit in the panel's within_2024_effect column (arshkon − asaniczka for each definition). Summary:

Definition within_2024_effect Direction of within-2024 noise Sign concordance with cross-period effect J1 +2.46 pp arshkon more entry concordant with up J3 +4.75 pp arshkon more entry concordant with up J4 +7.36 pp arshkon more entry concordant with up S1 −20.88 pp arshkon less senior (label) concordant with down S4 −7.09 pp arshkon less senior (YOE) concordant with down

The within-2024 direction matches the cross-period direction for every primary. This means "arshkon is positioned like a junior 2026 sample relative to asaniczka 2024" — which is the expected pattern if the junior-expansion story is real and arshkon just happens to have a labor-market mix closer to the 2026 sample. It does NOT cleanly validate the cross-period effect (because arshkon is also 2024), but it at least doesn't contradict it.

docs.title: 9. Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Every primary is monotonic in the predicted direction with no contrarians. I went into this expecting at least one label-based definition to flip (given the T03 abstention-and-director-weakness issues), but all junior definitions are up and all senior definitions except S2 are down. The signal is strong enough to be visible through any operationalization that isn't limited to the tiny director cell.
  2. Label-based magnitudes are ~2× YOE-based magnitudes on the senior side. S1 says −15.7 pp, S4 says −7.6 pp. This is not an error; it is driven by the unknown-share expansion from 26-34% (2024) to 52% (scraped). A naive reader would cite S1; the right number is S4.
  3. The junior effect under J3 (+5.0 pp) is much bigger than under J1 (+1.8 pp). The label-based J1 definition is conservative (only rows the LLM explicitly labels entry) while the YOE-based J3 captures the true junior-bar population more faithfully. J3 is the primary; J1 is the floor.
  4. J3_rule closely tracks J3 (cross-period +5.9 vs +5.0 pp, within-2024 +4.6 vs +4.8 pp). The rule extractor is a perfectly adequate audit for the junior-side YOE claim. This is somewhat reassuring given that an LLM might have drifted between periods; the rule is identical across periods by construction.
  5. S2 (director-only) is the single non-directional cell on the senior side. Two forces cancel: directors are a tiny cell (<1% of SWE everywhere), and the T03 audit showed 22-27% cell accuracy. The result is +0.32 pp which is within the MDE of 0.15 × 2 ≈ 0.3 pp. This is noise, not a contrarian signal.
  6. The S4 direction survives the asaniczka asymmetry but the magnitude does not. S4 arshkon-only-vs-scraped is −1.8 pp (within MDE of 2.5 pp); S4 pooled-2024-vs-scraped is −7.6 pp. The honest claim is "the senior share fell, and depending on which 2024 source you anchor on, the drop is between 2 and 8 percentage points." Downstream tasks must report both.
  7. LLM-vs-rule YOE agreement is 79-83% exact. The preamble threshold of 80% is met for arshkon and scraped; asaniczka is 79.2% — just below the flag line. The MAE of ~0.5-0.7 years suggests the disagreements are mostly within ±1 year. Not a red flag, but worth noting for any downstream task that needs fine-grained YOE bins.

docs.title: 10. Data caveats
docs.text:
• MDE calculations assume independent random samples. With clustering by employer (many postings per company), effective sample size is smaller and MDEs are optimistic. The within-2024 effect sizes already incorporate this noise floor empirically, so relative comparisons are robust; absolute MDE is a lower bound.
  • Scraped 2026 n is a snapshot. Re-running T30 after more scraped data accrues will tighten MDEs and may shift effect magnitudes slightly. The scraped 2026-03 and 2026-04 shares are stable so this is a low-risk issue for the current run.
  • selected_for_llm_frame is the basis for LLM-labeled rows. The YOE-based primaries are computed on the labeled frame only (see DENOM_PREDICATE). Out-of-frame rows are still available via the rule-based audit (J3_rule, S4_rule), which agrees closely.
  • Within-2024 pooled share uses the same data twice (asaniczka dominates the pooled estimator because it is 4× larger than arshkon). The arshkon-only 2024 baseline is the honest alternative; the panel reports both.

docs.title: 11. Downstream action items
docs.text:
1. All Wave 2+ seniority-stratified tasks should load exploration/artifacts/shared/seniority_definition_panel.csv — do not recompute these shares. The canonical schema is fixed.
  2. Primary junior/senior claims should cite J3/S4 from the panel. Label-based sensitivities (J1/S1) should be reported alongside with the unknown-share shift noted.
  3. Senior-side magnitude citation should always pair pooled-2024 with arshkon-only (S4 pooled = −7.6 pp; S4 arshkon-only = −1.8 pp). This is the cleanest way to expose the asaniczka asymmetry.
  4. Do not isolate directors (S2) as a primary finding. T03 and T30 both flag the director cell as unreliable.
  5. Any task that disagrees with the T30 direction verdicts should investigate before reporting — e.g., if a Wave 3 task finds junior share declined somewhere, they should look at company-level compositional shifts rather than assuming T30 is wrong, because T30 has 7/7 junior and ⅚ senior definition consistency on the direction.

docs.title: T31. Same-company × same-title longitudinal drift (H_M)
docs.text: Agent: Q (Wave 3.5) Date: 2026-04-20 Cohort: SWE LinkedIn English date_flag='ok', from T16 overlap panel companies (union of arshkon_min3, arshkon_min5, pooled_min5 = 420 cos). Pair bar = same company_name_canonical × lowercased raw title, with n≥3 per (co × title × period) in BOTH 2024 and 2026. Patterns: V1-validated ai_strict_v1_rebuilt (0.96 precision) and mgmt_strict_v1_rebuilt (0.98) from validated_mgmt_patterns.json; ai_broad_no_mcp drops MCP sub-token per Gate 2 pre-commit.

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. Pair-level AI-strict drift +13.4 pp on the cleanest-overlap arshkon_min3 panel (23 qualifying pairs) and +13.4 pp on pooled_min5 panel (33 pairs) — LARGER than T16 company-level +7.7–8.3 pp on the same companies. The same-title restriction does NOT shrink the AI-rewriting signal; it amplifies it. The dominant within-co AI rise IS the within-pair rewriting, not title-composition shifts. ai_broad_no_mcp drift: +24.8 pp arshkon-min3, +31.6 pp pooled-min5.

  2. Pair-level breadth_resid drift is NEAR ZERO and NEGATIVE on the primary n≥3 panel (arshkon_min3 −0.02; pooled_min5 +0.08). T16 company-level breadth_resid delta was +0.40 pooled and +0.69 arshkon — so breadth_resid rise DOES shrink 90%+ when restricted to same-title pairs. Where a company broadens scope, it is doing so via adding new titles / rewriting job levels, NOT by fattening same-title requirements. The "senior-scope-inflation" signal is primarily cross-title within a company, not within-title at a posting level. Pair-level < T16 company-level for breadth.

  3. Pair-level mgmt_strict drift +12.2 pp (arshkon) / +16.0 pp (pooled) — positive and meaningful on the V1-rebuilt pattern. Under the cleaner V1-validated management pattern, management responsibilities DID rise at same-title pairs (contradicting the original T11 "mgmt fell" on noisy mgmt_broad). Supports T21 "mgmt is flat, not falling — and at pair level, slightly rising".

  4. Requirements-section character share fell ~1.8-1.9 pp at pair level. Direction matches T13 aggregate (−3.5 pp) but magnitude is halved under pair restriction. Consistent with T13's classifier-sensitive H3 and T29's LLM-authorship mediation — the within-pair requirements-share decline is smaller but still present.

  5. Description length rose +293-305 chars at pair level (median ≈ +321) — half of T13's aggregate +1,130-char growth. Pair restriction removes 60-75% of length inflation: most length growth happened via new titles (new role templates, AI/ML archetypes) rather than within-pair re-templating.

  6. YOE dropped at pair level: rule-based YOE −0.60 (arshkon) / −1.01 (pooled); LLM YOE −0.31 / −0.16. Same-title posts are asking for LESS experience in 2026 than 2024 — modest but direction-consistent across both measures on the pool panel. Entry-ification of roles is observable at pair level.

  7. Top-20 AI exemplars: Wells Fargo, Microsoft, Capital One, Walmart, Amazon dominate. Wells Fargo "lead software engineer" Δ=+52.9 pp (0% 2024 → 52.9% 2026, n=4 → 17). Microsoft "software engineer ii" Δ=+40 pp (n=6 → 35).
