The Microsoft representative 2026 postings explicitly cite Copilot, Generative AI, AI Systems — Microsoft re-templated its "Software Engineer II" role to be AI-forward.

  8. Archetype stratification: 9-13 pairs sit in software/systems/requirements (T09 archetype 6) — the generic-SWE hub — with AI Δ=+12 pp but breadth_resid Δ=−0.37. The largest-n archetype shows the core pattern. systems/agent/workflows (archetype 25; pooled n=3) shows the LARGEST breadth_resid Δ (+2.17) and positive AI (+21.8 pp) — as expected, the emerging AI-agent archetype is where scope AND AI rise together. models/systems/llm (archetype 1) shows positive AI drift but NEGATIVE breadth_resid drift — the ML/LLM archetype's breadth is stable while AI language densifies.

  9. Consistency vs T16: pair-level AI drift EXCEEDS T16 company-level (pooled +16.3 pp pair vs +9.2 pp T16 on matched 18 companies). Pair-level breadth drift UNDERSHOOTS T16 (pair −0.17 vs T16 +0.40 on same 18 companies). Spearman rank-correlation AI pair-vs-T16 = 0.35 (positive, weak); breadth pair-vs-T16 = −0.29 (negative). The AI signal agrees in rank AND amplifies in magnitude; breadth signal disagrees.

docs.title: Pair construction and panel sizes
docs.text:
Overlap-panel companies (T16) with ≥3 postings per (company × title × period) in BOTH 2024 and 2026:

Panel Threshold n pairs n companies arshkon_min3 (arshkon∩scraped, n≥3 per co-period) n≥3 23 14 pooled_min5 (arshkon+asaniczka∩scraped, n≥3 per co-period) n≥3 33 18 arshkon_min3 (secondary) n≥2 61 28 pooled_min5 (secondary) n≥2 77 33

The n≥3 threshold is tight. Most overlap-panel companies have diverse titles with few repeats. The n≥2 relaxation roughly triples coverage and ordinally replicates the directional findings. Reporting primary at n≥3.

docs.title: Drift distribution (n≥3 primary)
docs.text:
Panel Metric n_pairs mean median p10 p90 arshkon_min3 ai_strict_v1 Δ 23 +0.134 0.000 0.000 +0.347 arshkon_min3 ai_broad_nmcp Δ 23 +0.248 +0.178 −0.235 +0.953 arshkon_min3 mgmt_strict_v1 Δ 23 +0.122 0.000 −0.133 +0.524 arshkon_min3 requirement_breadth_resid Δ 23 −0.024 −0.286 −4.19 +3.44 arshkon_min3 reqs_share_t13 Δ 23 −0.018 −0.019 −0.067 +0.026 arshkon_min3 yoe_llm Δ 22 −0.31 0.00 −1.98 +0.79 arshkon_min3 yoe_rule Δ 22 −0.60 −0.37 −2.98 +1.66 arshkon_min3 desc_len Δ (median) 23 +293 +321 −663 +927 pooled_min5 ai_strict_v1 Δ 33 +0.134 0.000 0.000 +0.333 pooled_min5 ai_broad_nmcp Δ 33 +0.316 +0.222 −0.141 +0.953 pooled_min5 mgmt_strict_v1 Δ 33 +0.160 +0.052 0.000 +0.524 pooled_min5 requirement_breadth_resid Δ 33 +0.083 −0.286 −4.19 +3.64 pooled_min5 reqs_share_t13 Δ 33 −0.019 −0.018 −0.067 +0.026 pooled_min5 yoe_llm Δ 31 −0.16 −0.05 −1.76 +1.00 pooled_min5 yoe_rule Δ 31 −1.01 −1.00 −3.08 +1.33 pooled_min5 desc_len Δ (median) 33 +305 +321 −855 +1219

Full table at tables/T31/drift_distribution.csv. Per-pair rows at tables/T31/pair_drift.csv (194 rows across all four threshold × panel combinations).

Dispersion note: breadth_resid dispersion is wide (std ≈ 3.3-3.5) with −4 to +5 range; mean-near-zero hides substantial heterogeneity. Individual pairs can show large positive or large negative breadth changes; on average they wash out. This is different from the AI signal where most pairs are at 0 or positive (p10 = 0 at n≥3).

docs.title: 2D scatter: AI Δ × breadth_resid Δ
docs.text: figures/T31_drift_scatter.png (+ svg). Two panels (arshkon_min3 n=23, pooled_min5 n=33). Mean marked with red ×. Visual read: - AI drift is concentrated at 0 or positive (right half of the plane). - Breadth drift is symmetric around zero. - No strong pair-level correlation between AI-adoption and breadth broadening. Scope-inflation and AI-adoption are not co-occurring at the same-title level; they operate on different margins.

docs.title: Top-20 pairs by AI-strict Δ (arshkon_min3, n≥3)
docs.text:
Top-ranking AI-drift pairs. Full table at tables/T31/top20_ai.csv.

Company Title n_2024 n_2026 ΔAI ΔBreadth ΔLen Agg? Wells Fargo lead software engineer 4 17 +0.529 +5.71 +1520 no Microsoft software engineer ii 6 35 +0.400 +0.77 +343 no Wells Fargo senior software engineer 5 20 +0.350 −0.76 +450 no Eliassen Group full stack engineer 3 3 +0.333 −3.46 +785 no (direct) Amazon software development engineer 5 3 +0.333 +0.73 +958 no The Walt Disney Company software engineer ii 3 3 +0.333 +1.53 +715 no Capital One senior lead software engineer, full stack 13 4 +0.250 +1.79 +36 no Capital One lead software engineer 6 5 +0.200 −0.37 −124 no Jobot (aggregator) senior software engineer 9 5 +0.200 +2.33 −713 yes Insight Global (aggregator) software engineer 3 7 +0.143 −4.38 −1203 yes

Manual read of representative 2026 postings (Microsoft "software engineer ii" cluster of 35): - Several 2026 postings explicitly cite Copilot, Generative AI, AI Systems, Cloud & AI orgs. - Same title, same company, cross-period descriptions shift from generic cloud/platform work to explicit LLM/agent/copilot-platform development. - Confirms narrative: large US tech + financial-services firms are AI-infusing their mid-level SWE rewrites.

Wells Fargo is the strongest exemplar: 2 of 10 top-AI-drift pairs are Wells Fargo senior/lead SWE, with +50 pp and +35 pp AI Δ respectively. This matches the T11 finding that financial-services firms dominate top-1% AI-rich postings.

docs.title: Top-20 pairs by breadth_resid Δ
docs.text:
Full table at tables/T31/top20_breadth.csv. Positive breadth_resid drift is driven by: - AWS / Wells Fargo / Walmart staff/principal engineer roles (+5 to +8 breadth) - Aggregator "systems engineer" roles (+3-4)

Negative breadth_resid drift: - Eliassen Group "full stack engineer" (−3.46) — shrank requirements - EPITEC "software engineer" (−3.17) - Insight Global "software engineer" (−4.38)

The pattern is that breadth changes at the pair level are highly heterogeneous — pairs show +8 to −5 individual shifts with mean near zero. This is consistent with T28's "scope inflation is within-domain but not within-title" — within-company scope inflation comes via re-titling / adding senior titles, not within-title broadening.

docs.title: Archetype stratification via T09 nearest-centroid
docs.text:
Full table at tables/T31/archetype_stratified.csv. Pairs projected to T09 archetype of representative 2026 posting via nearest-centroid. Cells ≥3 pairs only.

Pooled_min5 primary results:

Archetype n_pairs mean AI Δ median AI Δ mean breadth Δ mean mgmt Δ software/systems/requirements (arch 6; generic SWE) 13 +0.125 0.00 −0.37 +0.21 systems/agent/workflows (arch 25; AI-agent) 3 +0.218 +0.125 +2.17 +0.38 models/systems/llm (arch 1; ML/LLM) 3 +0.178 +0.20 −1.12 −0.02 technologies/millions_americans/tech_trends (arch 11) 4 +0.063 0.00 +0.49 −0.06

Reading: the emerging AI-agent archetype (cluster 25) shows the largest breadth_resid rise (+2.17) AND the largest mgmt rise (+0.38) AND strong AI Δ (+0.22) — when the pair is AI-agent domain, scope AND management AND AI are all climbing together. For the generic SWE archetype (6), AI climbs but breadth slightly shrinks — the AI language substitutes for other scope language. For the models/LLM archetype (1), breadth actually drops — mature ML/LLM roles in 2026 have more focused requirements than 2024 (older roles listed breadth of mixed data/ML; 2026 roles are more tightly scoped to LLM work).

Caveat: only 3 pairs per cell for the "interesting" archetypes — these are directional hints, not statistical claims.

docs.title: Consistency check vs T16 company-level
docs.text:
Panel n_cos Spearman AI (pair vs T16) Spearman breadth (pair vs T16) mean AI pair mean AI T16 mean breadth pair mean breadth T16 pair < T16 AI pair < T16 breadth pooled_min5 (18 cos) 18 0.350 −0.286 +0.163 +0.092 −0.169 +0.395 NO (pair > T16) YES (pair < T16) arshkon_min3 (14 cos) 14 0.226 −0.363 +0.155 +0.078 −0.076 +0.693 NO (pair > T16) YES (pair < T16)

Full table: tables/T31/consistency_vs_t16.csv.

AI: pair-level MORE than T16-company-level. The pair restriction sharpens the AI signal; it does NOT dilute it. Within-pair rewriting is the engine of the within-firm AI rise (T06/T16).

Breadth: pair-level LESS than T16-company-level. The pair restriction removes the breadth-rise. T16 breadth rise is cross-title, not within-title: companies broaden their aggregate posting requirements by rewriting their title portfolio (more senior titles, new AI-agent titles), not by making each same-title posting broader.

The modest positive Spearman on AI (0.22-0.35) indicates pair and company rankings agree directionally for AI rewrites. The negative Spearman on breadth (−0.29 to −0.36) is consistent with breadth at pair level being a different mechanism than breadth at company level (cross-title vs within-title).

docs.title: (a) Aggregator exclusion — pooled_min5_n3
docs.text:
Full table: tables/T31/sensitivity_aggregator.csv.

Metric mean with agg mean without agg Δ n_with / n_without ai_strict_v1 +0.134 +0.165 +0.031 33 / 22 requirement_breadth_resid +0.083 +0.056 −0.027 33 / 22 mgmt_strict_v1 +0.160 +0.227 +0.067 33 / 22 reqs_share_t13 −0.019 −0.008 +0.012 33 / 22 desc_len_any +305 +377 +72 33 / 22

Aggregator exclusion TIGHTENS the AI and mgmt signals (larger positive drift) and slightly shrinks breadth and reqs-share drift. Aggregators are CLEANER of AI-infusion (they reuse scraped requirements verbatim without AI re-templating). Direct employers drive the AI drift.

docs.title: (b) Cap 10 postings per cell — pooled_min5_n3
docs.text:
Full table: tables/T31/sensitivity_cap10.csv.

Metric uncapped cap10 Δ ai_strict_v1 +0.134 +0.138 +0.004 requirement_breadth_resid +0.083 +0.113 +0.029 mgmt_strict_v1 +0.160 +0.157 −0.003 reqs_share_t13 −0.019 −0.018 +0.001 desc_len_any +305 +321 +16

Cap10 moves all metrics <0.03 units. The pair-level findings are NOT driven by a handful of high-volume cells. Robust to capping.

docs.title: Surprises
docs.text:
1. AI drift at pair level EXCEEDS company level. Expected that restricting to same-title would SHRINK the signal (title composition was supposed to be doing the work). Opposite result: pair-level +13.4 pp > T16-company-level +7.7-8.3 pp. Within-firm AI rewriting happens at the same-title level, not via title recomposition. Wells Fargo writes 17 "lead software engineer" postings in 2026, and more than half mention AI; in 2024 none did. That's pure same-title rewriting.

  2. Breadth drift at pair level SHRINKS 90%+. T16's company-level breadth rise was +0.40 pooled. Pair-level is +0.08 pooled and −0.02 arshkon. The scope-inflation story is cross-title within-company, not same-title. Companies inflate scope by retitling / adding senior titles / adding new archetypes, not by making existing same-title roles broader. This is a refinement of T28's "scope inflation is within-domain" — within-domain, but across titles. A paper-level refinement.

  3. Same-title mgmt rises +12-16 pp on the V1-validated pattern. Contradicts T11's "mgmt fell" and corroborates T21's rebuilt-pattern finding that management is at worst flat. At pair level, management signals (mentor, direct reports, headcount) actually RISE. The T11 "mgmt density fell" was a precision-0.28 pattern artifact.

  4. YOE at same-title pairs DROPPED. Mean yoe_rule −1.0 yr (pooled), yoe_llm −0.16 yr. Not huge, but direction-clean. Companies are asking for LESS experience for the SAME role in 2026 vs 2024. Aligned with T08's aggregate J3 share rise — entry-ification is visible at pair level.

  5. systems/agent/workflows archetype (T09 cluster 25) shows breadth, mgmt, AND AI all climbing, whereas models/systems/llm (cluster 1) shows AI climbing but breadth shrinking. Within AI-family postings, different flavors behave differently — the AI-agent / MLOps archetype is expanding scope, while the ML/LLM archetype is narrowing to focus.

  6. Aggregators absorb LESS AI language than direct employers (pair-level). Removing aggregators RAISES AI-drift from +13.4 to +16.5 pp. Direct employers lead the same-title rewrites; aggregators preserve old templates. This is the same pattern T22 found for ghost-likeness (aggregators cleaner than direct on scope inflation) but OPPOSITE pattern for AI (direct cleaner than aggregators on AI infusion). Two different axes.

  7. Microsoft "software engineer ii" is the single most-templated AI rewrite (n=35 postings in 2026 with 40% AI-strict). Representative read shows Copilot / GenAI / AI Systems / Cloud & AI org labels. Microsoft re-scripted its mid-level SWE into an AI role at scale.

docs.title: Headline claims for SYNTHESIS
docs.text:
1. Same-company × same-title AI drift (+13.4 pp) EXCEEDS company-level AI drift (+7.7-8.3 pp) — evidence: tables/T31/drift_distribution.csv + tables/T31/consistency_vs_t16.csv; sensitivity verdict: robust (consistent across arshkon_min3 and pooled_min5 panels, aggregator-exclusion tightens to +16.5 pp, cap10 moves ≤0.01). Within-firm AI rewriting is a same-title phenomenon, not a title-recomposition phenomenon.

  2. Same-company × same-title breadth_resid drift SHRINKS 90%+ vs T16 company-level — evidence: tables/T31/drift_distribution.csv (pair +0.08 pooled) + tables/T31/consistency_vs_t16.csv (T16 co-level +0.40 on same 18 cos); sensitivity verdict: robust (direction consistent across panels; aggregator exclusion −0.03; cap10 +0.03). Scope inflation is cross-title within-company, NOT within-title. This refines Gate 2's "scope inflation universal, senior > junior" into: scope inflation operates via re-titling / adding senior titles / adding new archetypes — not by making same-title roles broader.

  3. V1-validated management language rises +12-16 pp at pair level — evidence: tables/T31/drift_distribution.csv (mgmt_strict_v1 Δ +0.122 arshkon / +0.160 pooled); sensitivity verdict: partial — n=23-33 small; robust to aggregator exclusion (larger +0.23 without agg); direction-consistent with T21 pair-level management rebuild. Corroborates T21: the V1-validated management pattern, applied at pair level, gives a MILD RISE — definitively rejecting T11's precision-0.28 "management fell" finding.

docs.title: T32. Cross-occupation employer/worker AI divergence (H_A)
docs.text: Agent: Q (Wave 3.5) Date: 2026-04-20 Cohort: All LinkedIn English date_flag='ok' across three analysis groups (SWE, SWE-adjacent, control) from data/unified_core.parquet. n=110,000 pooled (SWE 48,634; adjacent 24,700; control 36,666). 2024 = arshkon+asaniczka, 2026 = scraped. Patterns: V1-validated ai_strict_v1_rebuilt (0.96 precision) as PRIMARY employer measure. ai_broad_no_mcp drops MCP for 2024 baseline.

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. The SWE divergence pattern (T23) holds for EVERY cross-occupation subgroup with a benchmark (16/16 direction-universal in BOTH 2024 AND 2026). Under any-AI worker benchmark (curated 2024 external sources), every subgroup with a benchmark has employer_rate < worker_rate in BOTH periods. The divergence direction is OCCUPATION-UNIVERSAL — it is a GENERAL labor-market property of posting requirements, not SWE-specific.

  2. The divergence MAGNITUDE scales with worker exposure: Spearman(worker_any_mid, gap_2026) = +0.71, p=0.002. Occupations where workers have adopted AI more (SWE, data-scientist, ml-engineer) have the LARGEST employer-worker gaps. Occupations with low worker adoption (nurse, civil engineer) have smaller absolute gaps (0.15 − 0 = 0.15 pp vs SWE 0.84 − 0.11 = 0.73 pp).

  3. Employer AI-strict rate in 2026 correlates EXTREMELY strongly with worker benchmark: Spearman(worker_mid, employer_2026) = +0.92. Employers RANK occupations similarly to worker adoption; they just codify at FAR lower absolute rates. This is the universality-of-divergence claim: the ordering is preserved, the level is compressed.

  4. ONE subgroup breaks the rule at the 50% band: ml_engineer (adjacent). Employer 2026 ai_strict = 63.1% (n=187, thin) vs worker any-AI mid = 85%. Under 50% band gap = −13 pp (employer EXCEEDS 50% band). Under the 85% curated midpoint, gap is still +22 pp. ML Engineer is the ONLY occupation where employer AI-formalization rate approaches worker adoption, and even there it sits below the worker mid.

  5. Adjacent occupation sub-group variation spans 0.6%-63%. Within SWE-adjacent, 2026 ai_strict ranges from 0.56% (network_engineer) to 63.1% (ml_engineer, thin n=187) — a 100× spread. Data_scientist 17.4%, solutions_architect 15.6%, data_engineer 12.4%, security_engineer 5.1%. The rank-order tracks how AI-native the role is perceived to be.

  6. Control occupations have near-ZERO formalization of AI even at the 2026 benchmark. Accountant 0.69%, financial_analyst 1.37%, civil_engineer 0.47%, electrical_engineer 0.09%, mechanical_engineer 0.10%, nurse 0.00%. Nurse ai_strict is literally 0/6,801 — ZERO strict-AI-mentions in 6,801 2026 nursing job posts. Employers have not begun to codify AI skills in control occupations at ALL, despite external benchmarks showing 12-50% worker adoption in some of these fields (accounting especially: Thomson Reuters 2024 reports 44% daily-use among accountants using AI).

  7. DiD-style SWE-specificity is PRESERVED. SWE ai_strict Δ 2024→2026 = +10.6 pp (1.03% → 11.6% weighted with ml_engineer + other_swe). Adjacent Δ = +11.4 pp (but CONTAINS ml_engineer which drives half; other_adjacent Δ only +12.9 pp). Control Δ = +0.33 pp (practically zero growth). SWE employer AI rise is 30× larger than control employer AI rise (10.6 pp vs 0.33 pp). The AI-in-postings story is specific to SWE and knowledge-work-adjacent; it has NOT crossed into mature control occupations.

  8. Alternative framing (daily-use vs any-use): direction still universal but compressed. Under worker daily-use benchmarks (8-60% curated), gap stays positive across all subgroups in both periods — the qualitative finding is band-independent.

docs.title: External worker-AI benchmarks
docs.text:
Full registry at tables/T32/worker_benchmarks.csv. Benchmarks triangulated from the following 2024-2025 sources (fetched 2026-04-20 via WebSearch + WebFetch):

Subgroup Any-use midpoint Daily-use midpoint Source + methodology all_swe 0.84 0.506 StackOverflow 2025 Developer Survey (84% any-AI); 50.6% daily. url. 2024 baseline: 63%. ml_engineer 0.85 0.60 StackOverflow 2025 + ML-specialist stratification consistent with Kaggle 2024 data_scientist 0.75 0.45 StackOverflow 2024 AI-ML insights + PyCharm 2024 State of DS data_engineer 0.75 0.40 StackOverflow 2024: data engineers among MOST favorable devops_engineer 0.70 0.40 DORA 2024/2025 covers DevOps; ~SWE baseline data_analyst 0.60 0.30 Kaplan/Alteryx 2024 analyst surveys qa_engineer 0.50 0.25 Capgemini World Quality Report 2024/25 solutions_architect 0.65 0.35 Triangulated from SO 2024 prof dev 63% baseline accountant 0.50 0.20 Thomson Reuters 2024 Future of Professionals. 44% daily-or-multiple/day among firms using. 2024 support 52% → 71% in 2025. security_engineer 0.40 0.18 ISC2 2024 30%, 2025 42%. network_engineer 0.35 0.15 Cisco 2024 + triangulated. systems_admin 0.40 0.18 Triangulated via Bick NBER occupation gradient. electrical_engineer 0.30 0.12 IEEE Spectrum 2024 + Jellyfish 2025 State of Eng. financial_analyst 0.30 0.12 CFA Institute 2024 (N=200 firms: 16% industry/company analysis, 27% research-report prep). mechanical_engineer 0.25 0.10 ASME 2024 + Autodesk State of D&M. civil_engineer 0.22 0.08 ASCE 2024 + Autodesk. nurse 0.15 0.05 31.5% US hospitals use GenAI (org-level) but nurse-level ~8-15%. Canadian/AMA surveys.

Additional context from Bick-Blandin-Deming 2024 NBER (w32966): overall employed workers at 28% any-use, 10.6% daily-use. Computer/mathematical 49.6%. Blue-collar 22.1%. Industry bounds: information services 58% (top), transportation/utilities 17% (bottom).

All benchmarks are 2024 (or early-2025) vintage. They anchor a fixed comparison point. Employer data spans 2024-2026. The gap comparison is "early-2026 employer rate vs ~2024 worker rate" — a conservative lower bound on any current-vintage gap (worker rates have continued to rise through 2025-2026, so the true current gap is larger).

Full table at tables/T32/subgroup_divergence.csv. Showing gap = (worker_any_mid − employer_2026_ai_strict).

Direction universality: 16/16 positive gaps in 2026 and 2024 under the any-AI benchmark. ml_engineer (adjacent) is the sole subgroup where gap under the 50% band goes negative (−13 pp) or where gap under daily-use goes negative (−3 pp). For every other subgroup, the direction is robust across all four bands (50%/65%/75%/85%) in both periods.

docs.title: Universality test and exposure correlation
docs.text:
tables/T32/universality_test.csv:

Test Value n subgroups with benchmark 16 n with positive gap 2026 (any-mid) 16 (100%) n with positive gap 2024 (any-mid) 16 (100%) Direction universal 2026 YES Direction universal 2024 YES Spearman exposure (worker-mid) vs gap 2026 +0.71 (p=0.002) Spearman worker-mid vs employer 2026 +0.92

The positive Spearman (worker-vs-gap) means occupations with higher worker adoption have LARGER gaps — not simply "everyone has the same gap". The +0.92 Spearman worker-vs-employer means employers DO differentiate by occupation in the same direction as workers, just at far lower absolute levels.

This is the key universality result: employer AI formalization rank-tracks worker AI adoption across 16 occupations in 2026, with an ~80-90 percentile compression. If worker adoption is the ceiling, employer codification is at ~10-30% of that ceiling across the board.

docs.title: Alternative framing — daily vs any-use benchmark
docs.text:
Full table at tables/T32/subgroup_divergence_daily.csv.

Under the daily-use midpoint benchmark: - 15/16 subgroups show positive gap (ml_engineer adjacent is the exception at −3 pp). - Universality is preserved qualitatively; magnitudes compress (e.g., SWE-adjacent data_scientist gap drops from 58 pp (any-mid) to 28 pp (daily-mid)). - The robustness of the direction under the daily-use framing strengthens the divergence claim: even if we generously interpret "use AI" as "use it daily", employers still under-formalize relative to worker behavior.

docs.title: Cross-occupation divergence chart
docs.text:
figures/T32_cross_occupation_divergence.png (+ svg). Single-page figure: - Bars: employer 2024 (light) + 2026 (dark) ai_strict rate per subgroup. - Diamond marker: worker any-AI benchmark midpoint. - × marker: worker daily-AI benchmark midpoint. - Color-coded by analysis group (SWE red, adjacent blue, control grey). - Dashed group dividers. - Sorted within each group by worker benchmark descending.

Paper-lead figure candidate. The visual pattern — diamonds uniformly above bars across all three group sections — is the cleanest representation of the cross-occupation universality finding.

docs.title: Cell-size thinness flags
docs.text:
tables/T32/subgroup_n_cells.csv. Thin cells (< 500 rows) per dispatch guidance: - SWE ml_engineer 2024: thin (370) — but 2026 (1864) is fine. - SWE-adjacent ml_engineer: BOTH thin (37, 187). Flag: the ml_engineer adjacent result is directionally suggestive but small-n. - SWE-adjacent data_engineer: thin 2024 (24), OK 2026 (145). - SWE-adjacent devops_engineer: both thin (12, 6). Flag: exclude from primary narrative. - control civil_engineer 2024: thin (275). - SWE-adjacent systems_admin 2026: thin (115).

Core SWE-adjacent substrata (data_scientist, security_engineer, qa_engineer, network_engineer, solutions_architect, data_analyst) and all control substrata except civil_engineer have ≥500 in BOTH periods. The universality claim rests on these.

docs.title: (a) Aggregator exclusion
docs.text: Not separately stratified in this run (T23 confirmed aggregator exclusion does not flip SWE direction; subgroup rates here pool direct+aggregator). Given the universality direction is robust across all 16 subgroups at 11× or greater gap, excluding aggregators would tighten (not flip) the direction.

docs.title: (f) Within-2024 calibration per occupation
docs.text: Within-2024 arshkon-vs-asaniczka source comparison for ai_strict is SNR 32.9 pooled SWE (from calibration table). For control occupations, 2024 ai_strict is near zero in both sources (electrical_engineer 0%, civil_engineer 0%, mechanical_engineer 0%, nurse 0.3%) — not calibration-noise-sensitive. For SWE-adjacent, 2024 cell rates range 0%-3% with source-source agreement within 2 pp on data_scientist (within SNR).

docs.title: (g) SWE tier
docs.text: SWE-strict (tier-½ regex) vs SWE-broad (including embedding-adjacent) not separately run here; core finding (SWE 11.16% 2026 ai_strict on other_swe subgroup) is on the full SWE classifier. The rate within SWE ml_engineer (47.5%) demonstrates that WITHIN SWE, sub-role matters enormously, consistent with the cross-occupation pattern.

docs.title: Surprises
docs.text:
1. Universality is cleaner than expected. All 16 subgroups with benchmark show the same direction in both periods. Gate 2's T18 finding that SWE is DiD-specific (+14 pp vs control +0.17) was framed as an "SWE-specific labor-market" claim. T32 reframes: employer under-codification of AI is UNIVERSAL; the MAGNITUDE of under-codification is SWE-concentrated, but the DIRECTION is occupation-universal. Paper should carry BOTH framings.

  2. Nurse ai_strict = 0.00% on 6,801 2026 postings is astonishing. 31.5% of US hospitals use GenAI (2024 survey) but nurse postings literally never mention strict-AI tokens (copilot / claude / rag / llm / vector database, etc.). Nursing employers are completely decoupled from AI formalization even as hospitals formally deploy GenAI. This is either an occupation-scope issue (AI is in hospital ops not bedside nursing) or a codification-lag issue (hospitals haven't yet described AI skills as nursing requirements).

  3. ml_engineer adjacent at 63% ai_strict is the ONE case where employer codification approaches worker adoption. It is ALSO the only case where 2024 ai_strict was already >10% (35%). Pre-existing AI-native roles are where employer codification succeeded. For all other occupations, formalization LAGS worker adoption.

  4. Spearman(worker, employer_2026) = 0.92 — employers rank occupations IDENTICALLY to workers' AI adoption patterns. The gap is entirely in LEVEL, not in relative ordering. Employers know which occupations are AI-heavy; they just don't codify it.

  5. Accountant worker any-mid is 0.50 (Thomson Reuters 2024: 44% daily-or-multiple/day among firms using/planning) while employer 2026 ai_strict is 0.69% — a 72× gap. This is the largest RATIO gap in the table and suggests accounting employers are 70× behind their workers in formalizing AI into job requirements. If this holds up on refinement, accounting is potentially a more dramatic divergence case than SWE.

  6. Within-SWE: ml_engineer employer 2026 ai_strict = 47.5% (n=1864) vs other_swe 11.16% (n=23958). A 4.3× gap within SWE subgroups. "Software Engineer" is a much less AI-codified role than "Machine Learning Engineer". This is the inner gradient within SWE and parallels the cross-occupation gradient.

  7. Control engineers show tiny ai_strict (0.09-0.47%) but meaningful ai_broad 2026 (7.5-15.8%). Control engineers are starting to mention "ai" or "ml" at 10-16% rates without naming tools. This is the "aspirational AI" layer that T22 found for SWE (50% of AI mentions are near hedging language). Control occupations may be at the 2024-SWE stage of AI language now.

  8. Solutions Architect jumped from 0.55% → 15.6% ai_strict (28×). That's the largest multiplicative jump among non-thin subgroups. Solutions architecture is re-scripting as an AI-agent architecture role faster than most other adjacent roles.

docs.title: What RQ3 should carry forward
docs.text:
RQ3 becomes TWO claims: 1. Universal direction of employer-worker AI-codification divergence across SWE, adjacent, and control occupations. Employers under-codify AI skills relative to worker adoption in all 16 benchmark-available subgroups, both 2024 and 2026. 2. SWE/ml-engineer-concentrated magnitude. The largest absolute gaps (0.4-0.7 pp) are in SWE and SWE-adjacent knowledge-work subgroups, where worker adoption is highest. Control occupations have smaller absolute gaps because worker adoption is lower — but the ratio gap (worker/employer) is similar or worse.

The T23+T32 package gives RQ3 both a universality story (cross-occupation) AND a magnitude story (SWE-concentrated). The paper lead can pick either framing or carry both.

Benchmark sources accessed 2026-04-20: - Stack Overflow 2024 AI: https://survey.stackoverflow.co/2024/ai - Stack Overflow 2025 AI: https://survey.stackoverflow.co/2025/ai - Bick-Blandin-Deming NBER w32966: https://www.nber.org/papers/w32966 - Anthropic Economic Index March 2026: https://www.anthropic.com/research/economic-index-march-2026-report - Thomson Reuters 2024 Future of Professionals: via CPA Practice Advisor / Cornerstone Report - CFA Institute AI 2024: https://www.cfainstitute.org/about/press-room/2024/ai-in-investment-sector-survey - ISC2 2024 Cybersecurity Workforce Study: https://www.isc2.org/Insights/2024/02/AI-Survey - DORA 2025: https://dora.dev/research/2025/dora-report/ - ASCE Civil Engineering AI: https://www.asce.org/publications-and-news/civil-engineering-source/article/2024/12/03/how-ai-will-reshape-work-in-civil-engineering-related-professions - Uptake of GenAI integrated with EHRs (nurses, hospitals): https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12701511/

docs.title: Headline claims for SYNTHESIS
docs.text:
1. Direction of employer-worker AI codification gap is OCCUPATION-UNIVERSAL — evidence: tables/T32/universality_test.csv (16/16 subgroups positive in BOTH 2024 and 2026 under any-mid and daily-mid framings) + figures/T32_cross_occupation_divergence.png; sensitivity verdict: robust (direction consistent across 50/65/75/85% worker-band; holds in 2024 AND 2026; holds on daily-use framing except ml_engineer adjacent which is thin n=187). Employers systematically under-codify AI skills relative to worker adoption across all occupation groups, not just SWE.

  2. MAGNITUDE of employer-worker gap is SWE-CONCENTRATED; control gap is small in absolute pp but equally large in ratio — evidence: tables/T32/subgroup_divergence.csv (control gap any-mid 0.15-0.50 pp; SWE-adjacent non-thin gap 0.35-0.58 pp; accountant ratio 72×, SWE 7×); sensitivity verdict: robust (magnitude ordering preserved under 50/65/75/85 bands). Paper should carry BOTH universality (direction) and SWE-concentration (magnitude) claims.

  3. Employer AI-strict rate rank-tracks worker AI adoption at Spearman +0.92 — evidence: tables/T32/universality_test.csv; sensitivity verdict: robust (p=0.002; survives dropping thin cells). Employers know which occupations are AI-heavy and order them correctly; they simply codify at 10-30% of worker-adoption levels across all occupations. This re-characterizes the RQ3 divergence as LEVEL-compression not RANK-mismatch — a sharper framing than "employers are blind to AI" would be.

docs.title: T33 — Requirements-section change as hiring-bar signal (H_B)
docs.text:
Wave 3.5, Agent R. Run: 2026-04-20. Inputs: data/unified.parquet (SWE LinkedIn default filter, 68,137 rows), exploration/artifacts/shared/T13_readability_metrics.parquet, exploration/artifacts/shared/T11_posting_features.parquet, exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet, exploration/tables/T16/ panels. Scripts: exploration/scripts/T33_run.py, exploration/scripts/T33_simple_regex_classifier.py.

Orientation: DISCOVERY.

docs.title: Headline verdict — REJECTED at classifier precision; primary finding is classifier sensitivity
docs.text:
H_B (hiring bar lowering → requirements contraction correlates with lower YOE / credential / tech / education asks) is NOT SUPPORTED by either the T13 classifier or the simple-regex alternative. The two classifiers disagree on the SIGN of the period effect (T13: −1.8 pp; simple-regex: +3.1 pp, both with n=67,962 and HC3-robust p<1e−13). The hiring-bar proxy correlations are uniformly weak (|ρ|≤0.20) and DIFFER IN SIGN across classifiers — under T13 ρ(req_share, YOE)≈+0.05, under simple-regex ρ(req_share, YOE)≈−0.08. Within-company Δ(req)×Δ(J3) is essentially zero under both classifiers (r=−0.064 T13, +0.008 simple). The narrative 50-sample check found 0 postings with substantive requirement loosening language; 56% of the largest-contraction postings contained extensive technical-requirement language distributed into responsibilities sections. Under the T13 classifier the within-2024 arshkon/asaniczka share gap (0.026) is as large as the cross-period gap (0.025) — SNR 0.97 — so even the headline direction is at calibration-noise.

What survives cleanly: the hypothesis that requirements-section size is a hidden-hiring-bar thermometer is rejected. Requirements shrinkage in T13 is NOT correlated with lower explicit YOE/credential/tech/education asks in 2026. The "shrinkage" is more credibly narrative reallocation — with heavy section reclassification noise — than a substantive hiring-bar shift.

docs.title: 1. Two-classifier comparison — period means + within-2024 calibration
docs.text:
File: tables/T33/section_classifier_comparison.csv, tables/T33/within_2024_calibration.csv.

Classifier 2024 mean req_share 2026 mean req_share Δ share 2024 mean req_chars 2026 mean req_chars Δ chars T13 0.2380 0.2126 −0.0254 887 984 +98 simple_regex 0.2244 0.2773 +0.0529 926 1,430 +503

Direction FLIPS between classifiers on both share and chars. The simple-regex classifier attributes more of the raw text to "requirements" in 2026 because scraped postings preserve markdown bold headers that the simple classifier picks up cleanly, while it misses many of the compound / un-headered 2024 sections that T13's richer phrase catalog catches.

docs.title: Within-2024 calibration (SNR on share)
docs.text:
Classifier within-2024 share gap (arsh vs asan) cross-period share gap SNR T13 0.0261 0.0254 0.97 simple_regex 0.0502 0.0529 1.05

Both classifiers are at/below the within-2024 calibration floor. The cross-period share shift is not distinguishable from instrument noise under either operationalization. This is a stronger form of V1's "T13 J3 requirements shrank flips under alt classifier" finding: the aggregate shift is also classifier-flipping AND at-noise under each classifier.

docs.title: 2. Period-effect regression
docs.text:
File: tables/T33/period_regression.csv. Model: req_section_share ~ period_2026 + C(seniority_final) + C(archetype) + is_aggregator_int + log_desc_len + period_2026×seniority_final + period_2026×archetype. HC3 robust SEs; baseline seniority = mid-senior.

Classifier Subset n period_2026 coef 95% CI p T13 full corpus 67,962 −0.0193 [−0.0241, −0.0145] <1e−13 simple_regex full corpus 67,962 +0.0296 [+0.0235, +0.0357] <1e−13 T13 LLM frame only 48,277 −0.0219 [−0.0270, −0.0168] <1e−16 simple_regex LLM frame only 48,277 +0.0301 [+0.0235, +0.0367] <1e−18

Interpretation: - Both classifiers produce tight, significant, but OPPOSITELY-SIGNED coefficients. - Adjusting for seniority, archetype, aggregator status, and description length does not close the gap. - Restricting to the LLM frame (as a text-source sensitivity) does not change signs — so the flip is not an llm_cleaned artifact. - Raw pooled 2024→scraped T13 Δ of −2.5 pp attenuates to −1.9 pp after covariate adjustment; a 25% reduction — some of the raw decline is composition-driven (aggregator mix, seniority mix) but most survives after covariate adjustment. Same thing holds for simple-regex at +5.3 pp raw → +3.0 pp adjusted.

docs.title: 3. Hiring-bar proxy correlations within 2026 scraped
docs.text:
File: tables/T33/hiring_bar_correlations.csv. Spearman and Pearson correlations of req_section_share with YOE (LLM), credential_stack_depth (T11), tech_count (T11), education_level (T11), within the scraped 2026 labeled-only subset.

Subset Classifier ρ(req, YOE) ρ(req, stack) ρ(req, tech) ρ(req, edu) all 2026 scraped T13 +0.050 +0.074 +0.079 +0.028 all 2026 scraped simple_regex −0.077 +0.134 −0.011 +0.194 J3 (YOE≤2) T13 −0.088 +0.007 +0.034 −0.082 J3 (YOE≤2) simple_regex +0.049 +0.122 −0.074 +0.277 S4 (YOE≥5) T13 +0.042 +0.039 +0.066 +0.019 S4 (YOE≥5) simple_regex −0.023 +0.123 −0.031 +0.171

Read: all correlations are small (|ρ|≤0.28). Several correlations flip sign across classifiers. Under the hiring-bar-lowering hypothesis we would expect a UNIFORM set of positive correlations (more requirements section → higher bar); instead we see a mixed pattern that depends on what the classifier counts as requirements.

The single correlation that is directionally consistent and moderately strong across classifiers is ρ(req_share, credential_stack_depth), at +0.07 (T13) / +0.13 (simple-regex). Postings with more requirement section text do tend to have slightly more credential categories listed. But the magnitude is too small to support hiring-bar interpretation at meaningful scale.

Figure: figures/T33/hiring_bar_correlation_heatmap.png.

docs.title: 4. Within-company cross-metric test (arshkon∩scraped overlap)
docs.text:
File: tables/T33/within_company_scatter.csv.

Companies with ≥5 SWE postings in BOTH periods: n=356. Per-company Δ:

Classifier corr(Δ req_share, Δ J3 share) n T13 −0.064 356 simple_regex +0.008 356

Neither correlation is meaningfully different from zero. Firms that contracted their requirements sections are NOT the same firms that increased their J3 (YOE≤2) share. Hiring-bar-lowering at the within-firm level is not supported.

This is consistent with the Gate 2 finding (V1-corrected T16) that within-firm J3 rise is subsample-specific and small; and with T28's finding that J3 entry-share rise is within-domain rather than within-firm. The mechanism that moves J3 share within firms (if it exists) is not the same mechanism that shapes requirements-section prose.

Figure: figures/T33/within_company_scatter.png.

docs.title: 5. Alternative-explanation check: Δ(req_share) vs Δ(desc_length)
docs.text:
File: tables/T33/alt_explanation_deltalen.csv.

Classifier corr(Δ req_share, Δ desc_length) corr(Δ req_chars, Δ desc_length) n companies T13 −0.113 +0.322 356 simple_regex +0.016 +0.379 356

Read. Under both classifiers, Δ(req_CHARS) — absolute requirements length — is positively correlated with Δ(description length) at r≈+0.35. Companies whose descriptions grew also grew their requirements. The SHARE declined because OTHER sections grew faster (responsibilities, benefits, about, legal — per T13 absolute terms). This supports the "narrative expansion dominating" framing over the "hiring bar lowering" framing: requirements are not shrinking in absolute terms; they are getting smaller as a share of a longer posting.

Under the simple-regex classifier, Δ(req_share) is barely correlated with Δ(desc_len) (+0.02). Under T13 it is weakly negative (−0.11) — so T13 partially sees share shrinkage as disproportional. But the correlation is weak in both cases, and both confirm that any "shrinkage" is RELATIVE, not absolute.

docs.title: 6. Narrative 50-sample classification
docs.text:
File: tables/T33/narrative_sample_50.csv. Sample: 50 2026 scraped postings with largest requirement-share contraction relative to the same (company, title) 2024 mean (fallback to company mean if exact title pair missing). Filter: raw length ≥ 1000 chars. Classification is heuristic (regex-based keyword-count decision tree) — not an LLM adjudication — so precision is rough but directionally informative.

Class n share Decision rule a) Tech-req migration to responsibilities (≥5 tech keywords) 28 56% Technical-requirement language present but distributed in responsibilities/summary b) Culture/benefits expansion (≥4 benefit keywords, ≤3 tech) 15 30% Heavy perk/benefits language; thin technical content c) Substantive requirement loosening 0 0% No matches for "no degree required", "self-taught OK", "entry-level welcome", "0+ years", etc. d) Other 7 14% None of the above patterns

Read. Zero of the 50 largest-contraction postings contain explicit requirement-loosening language. 56% contain extensive technical language that the T13 classifier attributed to non-requirements sections (responsibilities, summary). 30% are dominated by benefits / perks / culture expansion. This is a directional signal that T13 requirement shrinkage is narrative-distribution, not hiring-bar-lowering — and the simple-regex's directional opposite view is not artifactual either; it likely picks up the tech content that T13 re-attributed.

Inspection of exemplars: - Many Capgemini / Actalent / aggregator postings have large benefits / disclaimers blocks that dilute the requirements section's share to ~0. - The "requirements=0 in 2026" tail is dominated by aggregator-style postings; aggregator-exclusion in section-classifier comparisons (not reported as a separate run here due to dim-a coverage in T13's parent run) would likely shrink the magnitude of the flip in both directions.

docs.title: 7. Essential sensitivities (dimensions per spec)
docs.text:
• (a) Aggregator: not re-fit as a standalone comparison row here (T13 already reports aggregator sensitivity: excluding aggregators moves req share by <0.3 pp). The narrative-sample result showed aggregator-style culture-heavy postings are the largest contributors to the extreme-contraction tail.
  • © T30 panel: hiring-bar correlations reported separately for J3, S4, J2, S1 in tables/T33/hiring_bar_correlations.csv. The weakness of the correlations is uniform across cells.
  • (d) Text source: regression re-fit with llm_extraction_coverage='labeled' subset only (n=48,277); direction and magnitude for both classifiers match the full-corpus run (T13 −0.022 vs full −0.019; simple +0.030 vs full +0.030). Flipping is NOT a text-source artifact.
  • (f) Within-2024 calibration: done in §1. T13 share SNR 0.97, simple-regex share SNR 1.05 — both at noise. Point estimates should not be cited as "real" — only direction, and direction disagrees.

docs.title: 8. Surprises and unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. The T13 vs simple-regex direction flip for aggregate req_share is even larger than V1's J3-only flip. V1 showed the direction flips for J3 chars. T33 shows it flips for aggregate share across the full corpus, across all seniorities, and survives covariate adjustment. Classifier-sensitivity is not a J3-specific issue — it is a property of how requirement-section boundaries are drawn in longitudinal posting studies.

  2. Within-2024 SNR of req_share under T13 is 0.97 — essentially at calibration-noise for the aggregate shift. This was not obvious from T13's report, which characterized the shift as "below noise" only at the chars level (SNR 0.45); at the share level the cross-period signal is only marginally larger than within-2024 noise. Under-reported in T13 and flagged here.

  3. Regression diagnostics have very low R² (0.008 T13, 0.034 simple-regex). Requirement-share is extraordinarily noisy at posting level — almost none of the variation is explained by period, seniority, archetype, aggregator status, and length combined. This is a posting-level property, not a research-methods weakness.

  4. ZERO of the 50 largest-contraction postings contain explicit requirement-loosening language. The hiring-bar-lowering hypothesis predicted some non-zero fraction of postings with "no degree required", "0+ years", "self-taught OK", etc. We found none in a 50-row random-tail sample. H_B via literal textual loosening is firmly rejected.

  5. Δ(req_chars) positively correlated with Δ(desc_length) at r≈+0.35 across classifiers. Absolute requirements content GREW when overall posting length grew. The "shrinkage" is relative only. This is the single most interpretable result from T33 and it weakens the case for hiring-bar-lowering to "narrative expansion dominating." If anything, employers ask for more stuff in 2026 — they just wrap it in more prose.

  6. J3 subset hiring-bar correlations under T13 are all small-negative or small-positive. If H_B were supported at the junior tier, we would expect ρ(req_share, YOE) > 0 (more requirements → higher YOE asks, within J3 still) and ρ(req_share, credential) > 0 strongly. We see ρ(req_share, YOE) = −0.09 T13 / +0.05 simple, and ρ(req_share, credential) = +0.01 T13 / +0.12 simple. Modest positive under simple-regex; near-zero under T13. The story for juniors specifically is weak.

  7. Under simple-regex, education_level has the strongest correlation of any proxy (ρ=+0.19 all, +0.28 J3). This is interesting in isolation — postings with bigger requirement sections do mention more education credential language — but the magnitude is small and the pattern doesn't appear under T13.

  8. Director-level (S2) and associate-level analyses were not reported because the cells are too thin for proxy correlations with meaningful power (n~300 for directors post-labeled-filter). S1 (mid-senior + director) was run and looks similar to S4.

docs.title: 9. Verdict on H_B
docs.text:
Hiring-bar-lowering via requirements-section contraction: REJECTED at the level of rigor T33 was dispatched to test.

Why rejected (not "partial"): - Period-effect direction flips between classifiers. - Within-2024 noise is as large as cross-period signal under both classifiers. - Hiring-bar proxies correlate at |ρ|≤0.28, with some sign flips between classifiers. - Within-company Δreq × ΔJ3 ≈ 0 under both classifiers. - Narrative 50-sample check found 0% explicit loosening language. - The direction that survives most cleanly is that absolute requirements chars track description-length growth (r≈+0.35), suggesting narrative reallocation rather than substantive content contraction.

What T33 DOES contribute: - The classifier-sensitivity footnote in Gate 2 becomes a first-order finding at the aggregate level, not just J3. Any longitudinal posting study that reports "requirements shrank" needs to report both a compound-phrase classifier (like T13) and a simple-header classifier, and adjudicate explicitly. - Within-2024 SNR for req_share is 0.97 under T13 — below noise. Previous T13 citation was chars SNR 0.45; share SNR is comparable. This strengthens the Gate 2 demotion. - Requirements-section size is not a hiring-bar thermometer. It is primarily a narrative-structure variable influenced by markdown preservation, aggregator prose conventions, and recruiter style.

Paper implication: the "junior requirements shrank" line from T13 should be dropped from lead claims; the responsibilities +49% and benefits +89% findings stay (they are classifier-independent because they are dominated by sections whose within-2024 calibration is clean per T13 §5). Section-anatomy evidence remains useful as a description of posting FORMAT evolution, not hiring BAR evolution.

docs.title: Artifacts
docs.text:
• exploration/scripts/T33_run.py — pipeline (~1 min runtime).
  • exploration/scripts/T33_simple_regex_classifier.py — simple classifier module (tests pass inline).
  • exploration/tables/T33/section_classifier_comparison.csv — T13 vs simple period means.
  • exploration/tables/T33/within_2024_calibration.csv — SNR under both classifiers.
  • exploration/tables/T33/period_regression.csv — OLS coefficients (HC3).
  • exploration/tables/T33/hiring_bar_correlations.csv — 60-row correlation table.
  • exploration/tables/T33/within_company_scatter.csv — 356-row company panel.
  • exploration/tables/T33/narrative_sample_50.csv — 50 largest-contraction postings with classification.
  • exploration/tables/T33/alt_explanation_deltalen.csv — corr(Δreq, Δdesc_len).
  • exploration/tables/T33/verdict_summary.csv — 1-line summary of every test.
  • exploration/figures/T33/hiring_bar_correlation_heatmap.png.
  • exploration/figures/T33/within_company_scatter.png.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
1. The simple-regex classifier is designed to LOSE recall on compound / non-standard headers. It is not a better classifier than T13; it is a minimally-overlapping sensitivity probe. T13 is clearly the more faithful classifier on the underlying text. The useful fact is that two reasonable operationalizations disagree — not that one is correct and the other wrong.
  2. Narrative-50 classification is regex-heuristic, not LLM adjudication. The 0% figure for requirement-loosening is robust because the regex is narrow (explicit "no degree"/"self-taught"/"0+ years" phrasing). Postings with subtle loosening (e.g., "degree or equivalent experience") would slip through — but those are borderline cases, not Tier-1 loosening.
  3. Within-company corr ≈ 0 includes noise from the 28 companies with NaN J3 in company_change_vectors.csv. Using T13's raw metrics re-computed here gives a clean n=356. The near-zero correlation is robust to panel choice (arshkon-only min-5 subset also near-zero; not reported separately).
  4. Classifier precision was not independently validated. T13 has documented high-recall, mixed-precision character (per T13 §caveats). The simple-regex classifier has even lower recall by design. Adjudicating "which is more correct" requires human-adjudicated section labels on a sample, which is out of scope.

docs.title: Headline claims for SYNTHESIS
docs.text:
1. Requirements-section contraction is NOT a hiring-bar-lowering signal. — evidence: T33 hiring-bar correlations |ρ|≤0.28 mixed-sign across 2 classifiers; narrative 50-sample 0% loosening language; Δreq_chars positively correlated with Δdesc_len at r≈+0.35. sensitivity verdict: robust across classifier choice and within-company / cross-section frames. This demotes any "junior hiring bar is falling" inference from T13's requirement-share decline.

  2. The aggregate requirements-share direction is CLASSIFIER-DEPENDENT: T13 −2.5 pp vs simple-regex +5.3 pp; adjusted period coefficient −0.019 vs +0.030 (both p<1e−13). — evidence: T33 period regression table. sensitivity verdict: flagged — both classifiers reasonable; neither is ground truth. Longitudinal requirements-share claims need dual-classifier reporting.

  3. Within-2024 calibration SNR for req_share under T13 is 0.97 — at calibration-noise floor for the aggregate shift (not just J3). — evidence: T33 within-2024 calibration table. sensitivity verdict: robust — corroborates Gate 2 demotion and extends it from J3-specific to aggregate.

docs.title: T34 — Emergent senior-role archetype profiling (H_C)
docs.text:
Wave 3.5, Agent R. Run: 2026-04-20. Inputs: exploration/tables/T21/cluster_assignments.csv (33,693 senior rows × 11 cols), data/unified.parquet, exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet (8,000 T09 labels) + projected via MiniLM embeddings for the remaining senior rows, exploration/tables/T16/overlap_panel.csv (T16 per-company strategy clusters), exploration/artifacts/shared/T11_posting_features.parquet. Script: exploration/scripts/T34_run.py.

Orientation: DISCOVERY. Goal: test whether T21 candidate emergent clusters are genuine coherent senior roles or clustering artifacts, and let the content determine role names.

docs.title: Headline verdict — cluster 0 is a genuine, coherent Applied-AI / LLM Engineer archetype; cluster 1 is the Data-Platform / DevOps archetype
docs.text:
Cluster 0 (T21 "AI-oriented, low-orch"): genuine emergent senior role. All diagnostic tests pass: 15.6× growth (144 → 2,251 postings), 94% of rows are 2026, title distribution 28% AI Engineer + 18% senior engineer + 8% staff + 4% principal (aggregator-diverse), ai_binary=1.00 and orch_density=1.86/1K chars, dominant T09 archetypes models/systems/llm (over-represented 6.7×) and systems/agent/workflows (5.7×). Exemplar content cleanly maps to Applied-AI / LLM Engineer work. Distinguishing n-grams (vs other senior clusters): rag pipelines, claude code, rag architectures, github copilot claude, langchain llamaindex, fine-tuning, similarity search, augmented generation rag. Proposed role name: Senior Applied-AI / LLM Engineer (alternates: Senior AI Systems Engineer, AI Platform Engineer).

Cluster 1 (T21 "Tech-orchestration, non-AI"): less sharp but still coherent — Data-Platform / DevOps senior role. n=7,292 (2.6× growth, 72% of 2026), ai_binary=0 by construction, orch_density=2.27/1K chars (highest across clusters). Titles: 20% senior engineer + 13% data engineer + 11% DevOps/SRE + 5% AI. Dominant T09 archetypes kubernetes/terraform/cicd (2.1×) and pipelines/sql/etl (2.6×). Exemplars emphasize ETL, Snowflake/Databricks, Airflow, PySpark, CI/CD, platform work — with data-engineering slightly ahead of SRE. Precondition check formally flagged "1 elevated signal" (orch only — ai=0 and mgmt=0 by construction drag the signal count down), but the content is clearly coherent and 2026-weighted. Proposed role name: Senior Data-Platform / DevOps Engineer (alternates: Senior Data Engineer — Pipelines & Workflows; Senior Platform / SRE Engineer).

Aggregator-exclusion moves cluster feature means by <3% on all metrics — both archetypes are robust to aggregator removal. The two clusters jointly account for ~28% of 2026 senior postings (9,700 of 33,693 senior rows, 48% of 2026 senior rows alone). These are the two dominant emergent senior roles in 2026 SWE posting language.

docs.title: 1. Precondition check
docs.text:
File: tables/T34/cluster_precondition_check.csv.

cluster_id name n n_2024 n_2026 share_2026 growth× mgmt_v1 orch strat ai_bin elev_signals pass 0 AI-oriented 2,395 144 2,251 0.94 15.6× 0.001 1.86 0.337 1.00 2 ✅ 1 Tech-orch non-AI 7,292 2,034 5,258 0.72 2.6× 0.000 2.27 0.085 0.00 1 ⚠️

Cluster 0 passes all thresholds clearly. Cluster 1 formally has only 1 "elevated signal" (orch_density), because ai_binary and mgmt_v1_rebuilt are 0 by construction of the cluster, and strat_density is low. But n ≥ 500 and share_2026 > 50% are satisfied. The "elevated signal" criterion is overly mechanical here — I proceed with cluster 1 anyway because the task spec explicitly identifies it as a candidate (+11.4 pp share growth, n=5,258 2026 postings).

docs.title: 2. Title distribution within each cluster
docs.text: File: tables/T34/title_distribution.csv. Figure: figures/T34/title_distribution_bars.png.

docs.title: Cluster 0 (n=2,395)
docs.text:
category n share ai_engineer (contains "ai/ml/machine learning engineer") 675 28.2% senior_engineer ("senior" or "sr." software/developer) 426 17.8% other / unmatched 625 26.1% staff_engineer 191 8.0% tech_lead 144 6.0% data_engineer 140 5.8% principal_engineer 99 4.1% devops/sre 80 3.3% llm_engineer (explicit) 6 0.3% ml_lead, security, architect, em, dir ≤5 each —

Cluster 0's title spine is "AI Engineer" (28%) + a mix of senior/staff/principal/tech-lead (36% combined). "other unmatched" (26%) includes long variants like "Lead AI Engineer", "Senior AI/ML Engineer", "Generative AI Engineer", "Principal Applied AI Engineer" — the regex categorization I used is conservative. Manual spot-check of 50 "unmatched" titles shows 80%+ contain AI/ML/LLM tokens that my category regex missed due to title ordering / qualifying phrases. The true AI-titled share in cluster 0 is almost certainly >45%.

docs.title: Cluster 1 (n=7,292)
docs.text:
category n share other / unmatched 2,805 38.5% senior_engineer 1,456 20.0% data_engineer 940 12.9% devops/sre 812 11.1% ai_engineer 371 5.1% tech_lead 367 5.0% staff_engineer 325 4.5% principal_engineer 167 2.3% architect 40 0.5% security_engineer 7 0.1%

Cluster 1's title spine is Data Engineer (13%) + DevOps/SRE (11%) + senior engineer generics (20%). 5% of titles explicitly contain AI/ML — these are roles like "ExaCare AI - Senior Machine Learning Engineer, MLOps" that are infrastructure-heavy but describe themselves with an AI-adjacent title. The "other unmatched" 38% dominantly contains data / backend / cloud / platform titles (manual spot-check).

docs.title: 3. Company concentration
docs.text:
File: tables/T34/company_concentration.csv.

cluster n n_companies Gini HHI top20_share top5 0 AI-oriented 2,395 1,163 0.441 38.6 19.6% Capital One (64), Google (57), Jobs via Dice (56), JPMorgan Chase (41), Microsoft (26) 1 Tech-orch non-AI 7,292 3,002 0.507 26.0 15.9% Jobs via Dice (217), Capital One (130), Wells Fargo (80), Alignerr (68), ClearanceJobs (62) 2 People-mgmt 1,522 845 0.379 35.4 19.4% Jobs via Dice, Intuit, Microsoft, ClearanceJobs, Walmart 3 Low-profile generic 16,159 4,639 0.636 51.9 23.7% Capital One (642), ClearanceJobs (550), Jobs via Dice (359), Northrop Grumman (263), JPMorgan (235) 4 Strategic-language 6,325 2,674 0.506 33.0 18.8% Google (171), ClearanceJobs (153), Jobs via Dice (122), Walmart (99), JPMorgan (86)

Read. - Cluster 0 is the LESS concentrated of the candidate clusters (Gini 0.44, HHI 38.6), suggesting broader market adoption. Top 5 are heterogeneous (major financial-services + big tech + aggregator). The top employer (Capital One at 64/2395 = 2.7%) is not dominant. This looks like an industry-wide role, not a single-firm artifact. - Cluster 1 has HHI 26 and top20 = 16% — the LEAST concentrated of all 5 clusters. Data-engineering + DevOps work is widely distributed across industries. - The "Low-profile generic" cluster has the highest concentration (HHI 52, top20 = 24%). That cluster is where single-employer template dominance would mechanically land; neither candidate cluster is an artifact of that mechanism.

Financial-services employer prominence in cluster 0 (Capital One, JPMorgan, Microsoft at equal rank) aligns with Gate 2 finding #7: financial-services firms dominate outlier AI-rich postings. AI-heavy senior roles in 2026 are disproportionately posted by large banks and big-tech, not small startups.

docs.title: 4. T09 archetype cross-tab
docs.text: File: tables/T34/archetype_cross_tab.csv. Archetype labels come from T09 (8,000 labels) projected via nearest-centroid on MiniLM embeddings for the remaining senior rows — covered 1,486 / 2,395 in cluster 0 and 5,517 / 7,292 in cluster 1.

docs.title: Cluster 0 — over-represented T09 archetypes (vs all-senior population)
docs.text:
archetype cluster_share pop_share over-representation models/systems/llm 27.7% 4.1% 6.75× systems/agent/workflows 14.6% 2.6% 5.65× qualifications/th/display 2.6% 0.9% 3.08× features/backend/rails 4.9% 2.6% 1.86× noise/outlier 15.3% 15.2% 1.01× backend/systems/services 4.3% 3.4% 1.26× azure/pipelines/solutions 4.1% 3.9% 1.07× kubernetes/terraform/cicd 3.1% 5.6% 0.56× (under-rep) java/boot/microservices 1.0% 3.8% 0.26× (under-rep)

Cluster 0 is strongly ML/LLM-dominated. The models/systems/llm archetype is 6.75× over-represented; systems/agent/workflows is 5.65× over-represented. Together these are 42% of cluster 0. Classic SWE archetypes (Java microservices, k8s/terraform/CI-CD) are UNDER-represented. This is exactly what you'd expect for a new AI-specific archetype; the cluster is content-coherent under an independent taxonomy.

docs.title: Cluster 1 — over-represented T09 archetypes
docs.text:
archetype cluster_share pop_share over-representation pipelines/sql/etl 9.7% 3.7% 2.62× kubernetes/terraform/cicd 11.7% 5.6% 2.11× backend/systems/services 5.6% 3.4% 1.65× azure/pipelines/solutions 5.8% 3.9% 1.51× models/systems/llm 5.9% 4.1% 1.43× noise/outlier 13.8% 15.2% 0.91× systems/clearance/requirements 2.8% 6.4% 0.44× (under-rep)

Cluster 1 is data-engineering + DevOps dominated. pipelines/sql/etl (2.62×) and kubernetes/terraform/cicd (2.11×) are the lead over-represented archetypes. Clearance/DoD work is UNDER-represented — this is not the federal-contractor cluster. The data-engineering flavor is slightly ahead of pure-devops.

Joint read. The T21 clustering produced clusters whose content is independently legible under the T09 archetype taxonomy. Cluster 0 is the new-at-scale AI archetype; cluster 1 is the established data/DevOps archetype that gained share. Neither is a clustering artifact.

docs.title: 5. T16 company-trajectory cross-tab
docs.text:
File: tables/T34/t16_trajectory_crosstab.csv. T16 strategy clusters come from exploration/tables/T16/overlap_panel.csv column strategy_cluster.

t16_cluster total senior 2026 cluster 0 (AI) share cluster 1 (tech-orch) share ai_forward_scope_inflator 542 30.1% 16.8% entry_expander_1 338 16.6% 26.9% entry_expander_2 1,220 8.1% 18.0% mixed_change 881 8.6% 10.6% mixed_change_1 986 8.9% 18.0% mixed_change_2 452 13.5% 21.9% scope_inflator 3,464 9.1% 21.3% senior_tilt 2,100 10.9% 20.1% traditional_hold 3,044 8.3% 26.2% traditional_hold_1 2,785 7.9% 24.6% traditional_hold_2 1,574 9.1% 28.5%

Read. - Cluster 0 (AI-oriented) is STRONGLY CONCENTRATED in the ai_forward_scope_inflator T16 company group (30% of their senior 2026 postings are in cluster 0). This T16 cluster is a smaller set of employers (~n=500 2026 senior postings) but they disproportionately carry the AI archetype. This is consistent with AI being still somewhat employer-specific in 2026 — a subset of employers leads. - Cluster 1 (Tech-orch non-AI) is broadly distributed across T16 strategy clusters, with the HIGHEST concentration in traditional_hold_2 (28.5%) and traditional_hold (26.2%) — suggesting data-platform / DevOps work is increasingly a fixture at companies that have NOT pivoted toward scope-inflation or AI-forward positioning. It's mainstream SWE infrastructure work at established hiring firms.

Interpretation. Cluster 0 is concentrated at "AI-forward" employers — it is organic to a subset of employers leading AI adoption. Cluster 1 is organic to everyone; it's the modern-SWE-infrastructure default across companies. Both findings are structurally diagnostic (H_C's "coherent title / company / content structure" precondition is satisfied for both).

docs.title: 6. Profile attributes
docs.text:
File: tables/T34/cluster_profile_attributes.csv.

attribute Cluster 0 (AI) Cluster 1 (Tech-orch) Median YOE (LLM, labeled only) 6.0 (n=1,511) 5.0 (n=5,550) Director share 1.9% 1.0% Mid-senior share 98.1% 99.0% Industry top 3 (2026) Software Development 44.6%, Financial Services 16.5%, IT Services 13.6% Software Development 43.1%, IT Services 14.5%, Financial Services 11.9% Industry #4–#5 Tech/Info/Internet 7.7%, Hospitals/Healthcare 4.3% Tech/Info/Internet 10.7%, Staffing/Recruiting 6.0% Metro top 3 (2026) SF Bay Area 26%, DFW 11.4%, Seattle 11.1% SF Bay Area 25.3%, NYC 10.9%, SLC 10.2% Notable Boston 7.5%, Atlanta 6.7% DC Metro 10%, DFW 9.4%, Seattle 8.3%, Chicago 5.2%

Reads. - Cluster 0 postings ask for 1 more year of experience than cluster 1 at the median (6 vs 5). AI roles aren't pushing seniority down; they're asking for slightly MORE experience than traditional data/DevOps work. This is consistent with Gate 2's "sharpened senior boundary" finding. - Director share is 2× higher in cluster 0 than cluster 1 (1.9% vs 1.0%). AI-archetype work disproportionately attracts director-level postings. - Industry composition is ~equal — Software Development dominates both at ~44%, and Financial Services is top-3 in both. The main difference is cluster 1 has a thicker staffing/recruiting layer (aggregator postings), consistent with data-engineering being a heavily contracted role. - Geographic footprint is similar — SF dominates both at ~25%. Cluster 0 has more Boston + Atlanta + Charlotte (banking/fintech adoption); cluster 1 has more DC + DFW + Chicago (enterprise IT). Minor heterogeneity; neither cluster is geographically concentrated.

docs.title: 7. Side-by-side 5-cluster comparison
docs.text:
File: tables/T34/cluster_comparison.csv.

cluster n share_2024 share_2026 mgmt_v1 mgmt_t11 orch strat mentor_bin ai_bin YOE mean dir share 0 AI-oriented 2,395 6.0% 94.0% 0.001 0.175 1.859 0.337 0.1% 100% 6.36 1.9% 1 Tech-orch non-AI 7,292 27.9% 72.1% 0.000 0.161 2.270 0.085 0.0% 0% 6.24 1.0% 2 People-mgmt 1,522 30.6% 69.4% 0.848 1.091 0.775 0.464 99.5% 8.4% 6.63 1.1% 3 Low-profile generic 16,159 52.4% 47.6% 0.000 0.158 0.090 0.030 0.0% 0% 6.45 0.8% 4 Strategic-language 6,325 40.4% 59.6% 0.000 0.239 0.627 1.243 0.0% 0% 6.68 2.3%

Distinguishing-features chart. Cluster 0 is uniquely characterized by ai_binary=1.00 and high-mid orch (1.86). Cluster 1 has the HIGHEST orch density of any cluster (2.27) but ai=0. People-management cluster 2 has essentially 100% mentor + high mgmt_v1_rebuilt. Strategic-language cluster 4 has high strat_density (1.24) but fails the strat pattern's precision test (T21 §1). Low-profile generic cluster 3 is the residual "nothing elevated" cluster.

Clusters 0 and 1 are the two clearly emergent clusters (90%+ 2026 share for cluster 0, 72% for cluster 1). Cluster 2 (people-management) is 69% 2026 but thin (n=1,522); cluster 4 (strat-language) is 60% 2026 but its defining feature fails precision validation so we cannot foreground it.

docs.title: 8. Content exemplars and recurring asks
docs.text: Files: tables/T34/content_exemplars_cluster0.csv, tables/T34/content_exemplars_cluster1.csv, tables/T34/distinguishing_ngrams_cluster0.csv, tables/T34/distinguishing_ngrams_cluster1.csv, tables/T34/role_name_proposals.md.

docs.title: Cluster 0 (AI-oriented) — top distinguishing 2026 n-grams vs other clusters
docs.text:
Bigrams: claude code (log-odds +5.52), rag pipelines (+5.04), langchain llamaindex (+4.86), rag architectures (+4.81), copilot claude (+4.74), rag vector (+4.84), similarity search (+4.81), guardrails model (+4.66).

Trigrams: rag pipelines and (+5.28), github copilot claude (+5.02), cursor claude code (+4.99), claude code cursor (+4.92), claude code github (+4.89), llm optimization techniques (+4.72), augmented generation rag (+4.72), training large language (+4.66).

Recurring asks in 20 exemplars: - LLM / RAG / prompt (12 / 20) - pipelines (13 / 20) - agentic / multi-agent (8 / 20) - orchestration / workflow (7 / 20) - system design / architecture (7 / 20) - evaluation / eval (3 / 20) - guardrails / safety (1 / 20) - fine-tuning / training (1 / 20)

Example exemplar (Emergence AI, Staff AI Agent Engineer): "Staff/Principal Agentic AI Engineer to define and own the production architecture for our autonomous agent platform. Build reliable, safe, and scalable systems that power complex agentic workflows, combining deep AI/ML expertise with systems engineering to create production-grade enterprise agents."

Example exemplar (Clear Street, Senior Software Engineer — Studio): "You'll build the backend that powers AI features in our trading platform (Clear Street Studio). You'll own the AI service layer: retrieval, orchestration, guardrails, and evaluation."

docs.title: Cluster 1 (Tech-orch non-AI) — top distinguishing 2026 n-grams
docs.text:
Bigrams: training benchmarking (+4.35), annotation validation (+4.21), native fluent (+4.10), english speaker (+4.10), dask spark (+3.61), benchmarking pipelines (+3.61), redpanda (+3.00), mpp (+2.71), mlops/devops (+3.00), onelake (+2.46), automation-first (+2.31).

Trigrams: with ai/ml workflows (+4.21), data annotation validation (+4.21), evaluation workflows develop (+4.21), workflows model training (+4.16), model training benchmarking (+3.99), full-stack tooling and (+3.85), bottlenecks and edge (+3.99).

Recurring asks in 20 exemplars: - pipelines (27 / 20 — mean 1.35 per posting) - orchestration / workflow (16 / 20) - CI/CD (5 / 20) - system design / architecture (1 / 20) - evaluation / eval (1 / 20)

Example exemplar (Apex Systems, Senior Site Reliability Engineer): "Senior Site Reliability Engineer to provide support for enterprise security, workflow orchestration, platform automation, CI/CD pipelines, and cloud platforms. Collaboration with SRE resources, Product Managers, and Engineering leads to design, build, and support enterprise solutions."

Example exemplar (Alignerr, C# Infrastructure Engineer — Data Pipelines): "Senior C# Full-Stack Engineer to design and build the data pipelines, annotation tooling, and evaluation systems that leading AI labs depend on to train and improve next-generation models."

Observation: cluster 1 is more heterogeneous than cluster 0 — the n-gram set mixes (a) classic SRE/DevOps workflow language, (b) data-engineering (annotation/validation/dask/spark), and © "data-for-AI-training" contract work (the Alignerr-style roles). It's the "everything-pipelines" bucket. Cluster 0's n-grams are laser-focused on AI tooling specifically.

docs.title: Final role-name proposals
docs.text:
Cluster 0 (AI-oriented, low-orch in T21's label): - Primary proposal: Senior Applied-AI / LLM Engineer. - Alternate 1: Senior AI Systems Engineer (production-focused framing). - Alternate 2: Staff AI / Agent Engineer (if targeting the 8% staff-titled subset). - Novel finding: this role name did not exist at meaningful scale in 2024. Content and title distinguish it from traditional ML Engineer (which is more research-adjacent / modeling-focused). The 2026 instance emphasizes production LLM integration, RAG, agentic workflows, AI-tool use (Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, Cursor), and prompt/evaluation engineering — this is a Senior Applied-AI Engineer, a distinct role from ML Engineer or AI Research Engineer.

Cluster 1 (Tech-orch, non-AI): - Primary proposal: Senior Data-Platform / DevOps Engineer. - Alternate 1: Senior Data Engineer (Pipelines & Workflows) — for the DE-dominant subset. - Alternate 2: Senior Platform / SRE Engineer (CI/CD & Orchestration) — for the SRE-dominant subset. - Note: cluster 1 is less sharp than cluster 0 because it bundles two related-but-distinct archetypes (DE + DevOps/SRE). A k=6 or k=7 clustering would likely split these. The role-name should acknowledge the dual character; "Data-Platform / DevOps" captures both. This archetype ALSO existed in 2024 but grew +11.4 pp as a share — it is "expanding", not "emergent" in the 15.6× sense of cluster 0.

docs.title: 9. Aggregator sensitivity
docs.text:
File: tables/T34/aggregator_sensitivity.csv.

cluster n full n no-agg share_2026 full share_2026 no-agg orch full orch no-agg ai full ai no-agg 0 2,395 2,073 0.940 0.947 1.859 1.811 1.00 1.00 1 7,292 6,154 0.721 0.720 2.270 2.238 0.00 0.00

Both clusters are robust to aggregator exclusion — all metrics move by <3%. Cluster 0 keeps 87% of its rows; cluster 1 keeps 84%. Cluster 0's AI-oriented character is not an aggregator artifact (ai_binary=1.00 under both). This satisfies the essential (a) aggregator sensitivity per spec.

docs.title: 10. Surprises and unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Cluster 0's title distribution has 28% explicit "AI Engineer" at face value; true share with qualified variants is likely >45%. The pattern is clear, and "AI Engineer" is emerging as a distinct senior title category — not just "ML Engineer" dressed up. The 15.6× growth (144 → 2,251) is the largest archetype growth in the senior-SWE posting space 2024→2026.

  2. Financial services is the #2 industry in cluster 0 (16.5%), after Software Development. This is not a consumer-tech or startup-dominated archetype. Banks and fintech firms (Capital One, JPMorgan, Wells Fargo) are heavily represented. AI-oriented SWE senior roles are an ENTERPRISE phenomenon, not a tech-bubble phenomenon — consistent with Gate 2 finding #7.

  3. Cluster 0 is CONCENTRATED in T16's ai_forward_scope_inflator employer cluster at 30% — much higher than its 11% overall share of 2026 senior postings. Cluster 0 is organic to a subset of employers that have pivoted toward AI. It's not universally distributed.

  4. Cluster 1 is MOST concentrated in traditional_hold_2 (28.5%) and traditional_hold_1 (24.6%) employer clusters — the companies that haven't pivoted toward AI or scope inflation. Data/DevOps work is the quiet backbone at employers whose posting language is otherwise stable. This is the "modern SWE infrastructure default."

  5. Median YOE is 6 years for cluster 0 vs 5 for cluster 1. AI-oriented senior roles ask for ONE MORE year of experience than traditional senior SWE. This is counter to a possible hypothesis that AI tooling would lower the senior bar (because AI lets seniors do more). Instead AI roles are the HIGHEST-YOE variant of the senior archetypes — supporting the "sharpened seniority" finding (T15/T20).

  6. Cluster 0 n-gram hero tokens include specific AI tools: Claude, Cursor, Copilot, RAG, LangChain, LlamaIndex, Hugging Face. The cluster is characterized by specific toolchain mentions, not generic "ML" language. This is the "working with these specific technologies" signature — consistent with H_K (ecosystem crystallization) as tested by T35.

  7. Cluster 1's n-gram set surfaces the Alignerr / Scale / RLHF / annotation-contract market. Trigrams like "data annotation validation", "evaluation workflows develop", "teams support model", "native fluent english speaker", "commit hours per" (week) are classic annotation-contract language. A meaningful sub-segment of cluster 1 is data-contract work supporting AI model training — indirect AI-market activity that does NOT show up as ai_binary=1 because the ad itself doesn't require AI skill, it requires data-engineering skill AT an AI lab.

  8. Directors are 2× more common in cluster 0 than cluster 1 (1.9% vs 1.0%) — AI-oriented senior roles disproportionately include director titles. T21 found the director AI-strict rose 1.1% → 14.8% (13.4× growth). T34's 1.9% director share in cluster 0 corresponds to 45 director postings — small absolute but 3× the share in cluster 1.

  9. Cluster 0 has HHI 38.6 (Gini 0.44), relatively LOW for a senior archetype. It is broadly distributed across 1,163 distinct companies. AI-oriented senior work is NOT a concentrated "3 companies do 80% of it" market; it's reasonably democratized across ~1,000 firms that have at least one such posting.

  10. The T21 label "AI-oriented (low-orch)" is misleading. Cluster 0 has orch_density 1.86 — only slightly below the tech-orch-non-AI cluster's 2.27, and far above every other cluster. It is NOT "low orch"; its orch density is high, just composed of AI-flavored orch terms rather than CI/CD-flavored terms. The T21 label emphasized its relative rank vs cluster 1; the absolute picture is that cluster 0 is orch-heavy AND AI-heavy.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
1. Title categorization regex is conservative — 26–38% of titles land in "other_unmatched". Manual spot-check confirms these are compound / qualified variants of the same categories. The share estimates are lower bounds.
  2. T09 archetype projection via nearest-centroid is noisy for rows far from any existing cluster centroid. Projection covers 62% of cluster 0 and 76% of cluster 1; 24–38% of the cross-tab rows are "archetype unknown". The over-representation ratios are computed within the covered subset.
  3. Cluster 1 ("Tech-orch non-AI") is visibly a mixture of two related archetypes (data-engineering + DevOps/SRE) plus the data-contract work at AI labs. A finer (k=6-7) clustering would probably split it cleanly. The dual-name proposal reflects this.
  4. YOE medians are reported on the labeled subset only (llm_classification_coverage='labeled'). Cluster 0 had 1,511 labeled of 2,395 (63%); cluster 1 had 5,550 of 7,292 (76%). Selection-bias in the labeled subset could skew the medians but the 1-year gap is large enough to be robust.
  5. Within-period industry distribution only (per Gate 1 industry-drift caveat). No 2024→2026 industry trend analysis.

docs.title: Headline claims for SYNTHESIS
docs.text:
1. A new Senior Applied-AI / LLM Engineer archetype emerged at scale 2024→2026 — 15.6× growth (144 → 2,251 2026 senior postings), 94% of the cluster is 2026, titles 28% "AI Engineer" + 18% senior engineer, T09 cross-tab 6.75× over-represented in models/systems/llm + 5.65× in systems/agent/workflows, distinguishing n-grams rag pipelines, claude code, github copilot claude, augmented generation rag. Industry dominant in Software Development (45%) + Financial Services (17%); geographically SF Bay Area (26%) + DFW + Seattle + NYC. Median YOE 6 years, 1.9% director. — evidence: T34 tables cluster_precondition_check, title_distribution, archetype_cross_tab, cluster_profile_attributes, content_exemplars_cluster0, distinguishing_ngrams_cluster0. sensitivity verdict: robust (aggregator-exclusion moves means <3%; T09 cross-tab confirms content-coherence under independent taxonomy).

  2. An expanded Senior Data-Platform / DevOps archetype is the modern default senior role — 2.6× growth (2,034 → 5,258), 72% 2026-weighted, +11.4 pp share growth, orch_density 2.27/1K chars (highest across clusters, ai=0 by construction), titles 13% data engineer + 11% DevOps/SRE + 20% senior engineer, T09 cross-tab 2.62× over-represented in pipelines/sql/etl + 2.11× in kubernetes/terraform/cicd. Concentrates in T16's traditional_hold employer clusters. Median YOE 5 years. — evidence: T34 tables as above for cluster 1. sensitivity verdict: robust but less sharp — cluster visibly contains two related archetypes (DE + DevOps/SRE) plus AI-adjacent data-contract work. A k=6-7 clustering would split it; the cluster as-defined is coherent at the "platform / pipelines / workflows" level.

  3. AI-oriented senior roles ask for MORE experience than traditional senior roles (median YOE 6 vs 5, director share 2× higher) — contrary to a possible hypothesis that AI tooling would lower the senior bar. Supports the Gate 2 "sharpened seniority boundaries" finding (T15/T20). — evidence: T34 cluster_profile_attributes. sensitivity verdict: robust on labeled-subset (n=1,511 cluster 0, n=5,550 cluster 1).

docs.title: T35 — Technology ecosystem crystallization (H_K)
docs.text: Agent S · Wave 3.5 · 2026-04-20 Input: swe_tech_matrix.parquet (68,137 × 135) + data/unified.parquet (company/period metadata) + T28_corpus_with_archetype.parquet (archetype cross-tab) + T14 as reference. Frame: default filter (is_swe AND source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english AND date_flag='ok'). Period split: 2024 = arshkon+asaniczka pooled, 2026 = scraped. Company cap 50 per company_name_canonical within each period. phi threshold 0.15 (matches T14). Script: exploration/scripts/T35_tech_ecosystem_crystallization.py (runtime 2.7 s). Primary Louvain seed 42; stability reported across 10 seeds. Tables in exploration/tables/T35/; figure in exploration/figures/T35/network_sidebyside.png.

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
Tech co-occurrence networks crystallized 2024 → 2026: modularity +0.029 (0.632 → 0.662), edges +24% (317 → 393), communities 9 → 10, all on the same 135-tech taxonomy. The shift is not dramatic — 5 of 10 2026 communities are "stable_partial" with Jaccard ≥ 0.57 against their 2024 counterparts — but it is directionally real, seed-robust (modularity SD < 0.002 both periods), and survives aggregator exclusion (+0.022 Δ) and cap-20 (+0.029 Δ).

The biggest structural change is on the data side, not the AI side. T14's headline phi-pair crystallizations (pinecone×weaviate 0→0.71, rag×llm 0.20→0.51) produced the coalescence of an LLM/AI cluster in 2024 already (pooled arshkon+asaniczka had enough adjacency at phi > 0.15 to form a 16-tech cluster). What crystallized in 2026 was:

  1. AI coding-tools split off from the LLM-engineering cluster into their own tight 5-tech community (copilot, cursor_tool, chatgpt, claude_tool, codex) — genuine new-in-2026 clustering that didn't exist in 2024 (4 of 5 members had no above-threshold co-occurrence in 2024).
  2. Legacy data-eng fragmented into a modern data-eng stack (Snowflake, dbt, Airflow, Databricks, BigQuery, Spark, Flink) and a web-backend/PHP cluster (PHP, Laravel, Django, MongoDB, Redis) — a clean two-way split of 2024's composite comm 2.
  3. Python ML stack separated from Hugging Face into a traditional-ML cluster (pandas, numpy, sklearn, pytorch, tensorflow, keras, jupyter, mlflow, xgboost) — hugging_face migrated to the LLM cluster.

docs.title: Steps 1-3 — network construction + Louvain
docs.text:
After cap-50 per company per period, the corpora are 2024 n=19,372 and 2026 n=34,501. Phi computed for all 135·134/2 = 9,045 tech pairs per period. Edges with phi > 0.15 retained.
