Period Active nodes (of 135) Edges Communities Modularity (primary seed 42) Modularity mean/SD (10 seeds) n_comm range (10 seeds) Max comm size 2024 114 317 9 0.6324 0.6317 / 0.0019 8–9 22 2026 119 393 10 0.6618 0.6617 / 0.0005 10–12 25

Stability verdict: PRIMARY-usable. Modularity SD is < 0.002 both periods (well below the 0.05 downweight threshold from the dispatch). 2026 n_comm range is 10–12, i.e. 2 extra communities in some seeds — this is right at the "community-count range ≤ 2" threshold. Community sizes are stable for the top 7 communities across all 10 seeds (the differences are splits/merges among singletons/doubles).

Modularity Δ = +0.0294 (primary). Sensitivity: aggregator-excluded Δ = +0.022; cap-20 Δ = +0.029. Direction (rising modularity) survives all tested specs.

Tables: per_period_network_summary.csv, louvain_stability_10seeds.csv.

docs.title: Step 4 — backward-stability classification
docs.text:
For each 2026 community, Jaccard vs each 2024 community + share of 2026 members new (not above threshold in 2024) + share of top 2024 contributor:

2026 id size name (heuristic) Jaccard vs top 2024 top 2024 contrib share share new arrivals classification 0 25 js_frontend_stack 0.57 0.68 0.04 stable_partial 1 24 devops_platform_stack 0.61 0.71 0.00 stable_partial 2 17 llm_vendor_stack 0.65 0.76 0.18 stable_partial 3 15 php_laravel_stack 0.36 0.60 0.00 fragmented 4 13 data_engineering_stack 0.39 0.69 0.00 fragmented 5 9 python_ml_stack 0.64 0.78 0.11 stable_partial 6 7 mobile/systems-lang stack 0.75 0.86 0.00 stable 7 5 ai_dev_tools_stack 0.17 0.60 0.40 weakly_mapped → quasi-new 8 2 ci_cd_stack (circleci/travis_ci) 0.00 0.00 1.00 new (but n=2, low power) 9 2 ruby_rails_stack 0.40 1.00 0.00 fragmented (from 2024 ruby/python-web super-community)

Two 2024 communities were fragmented: the large 2024 data_engineering super-community (n=19, mixed Snowflake + PHP + Kafka + Spark) split into the modern data-eng cluster (13) and the web-backend/PHP cluster (15); the 2024 ruby/python-web cluster (n=5, mixed rails/ruby/flask/fastapi/django) split into rails/ruby (2) and a merger of flask/fastapi/django into the PHP/web-backend (3).

Tables: community_classification.csv (+ heuristic names), fragmented_2024_communities.csv, communities_2024_named.csv, communities_2026.csv, communities_2024.csv.

docs.title: Step 5 — LLM-vendor cluster verification
docs.text:
Key correction vs T14 narrative. T14 showed specific phi pairs crystallizing (pinecone×weaviate 0→0.71). At the cluster level, the pooled 2024 baseline (arshkon+asaniczka) already had a 16-tech LLM/AI cluster with phi > 0.15 connectivity — it did not "form" in 2026. What happened in 2026:

  • Same core cluster persisted (Jaccard 0.65 with the 2024 counterpart): langchain, llamaindex, rag, ai_agent, llm, pinecone, weaviate, vector_database, prompt_engineering, hugging_face, fine_tuning, anthropic, gpt_model.
  • 4 new arrivals that weren't above threshold in 2024: mcp, gemini, chroma, claude_api. These are the cluster's 2026 crystallization.
  • Hugging Face migrated into the LLM cluster (was in 2024 traditional-ML dl cluster).
  • AI coding tools (copilot, cursor_tool, chatgpt, claude_tool, codex) split OFF into their own distinct 2026 community. In 2024 they co-occurred with langchain/rag/llm; in 2026 their within-cluster phi exceeds their phi with the engineering stack, so Louvain separates them.

Classification of the 2026 LLM-vendor cluster: stable_partial (not "coalesced" or "new" — T14's headline overstated the newness). But comm 7 (AI dev tools) is effectively a quasi-new cluster under the 50% new-arrival threshold (40% new = "weakly_mapped" by the rule, but 4 of its 5 members either didn't exist in 2024 or weren't above threshold).

Dispatch question: which techs were NOT above phi > 0.15 in 2024 but ARE in the 2026 LLM-vendor community? Answer (from llm_vendor_cluster.csv): claude_api, gemini, mcp, chroma (4 techs; the 5th "expected member" openai_api is still sub-threshold in 2026 too — real adoption is low). The coalescence happened in 2024 already per the pooled panel; 2026 added branded-model-and-vector-store members.

Table: llm_vendor_cluster.csv.

docs.title: Step 6 — other crystallizations (named communities)
docs.text:
Coalesced / new clusters (beyond the LLM-vendor stack):

  1. AI dev-tools cluster (comm 7, n=5: copilot + cursor_tool + chatgpt + claude_tool + codex). 4 of 5 members (all except chatgpt) had no 2024 phi > 0.15 edges. This is the cleanest genuine-new 2026 clustering in the corpus. Modularity contribution is small (tight internal but narrow span). In T09 archetypes this aligns most with ML/LLM archetype (AI/ML domain share 0.06 vs 0.03 overall) but it is not a dedicated domain — rather, a cross-domain tool-use signal.

  2. CI/CD narrow cluster (comm 8, n=2: circleci + travis_ci). Both legacy-but-still-used tools. 2024 had no circleci↔travis_ci edge above threshold because circleci was embedded in the devops_platform_stack. This is "newly isolated" rather than "newly emerging" — CI/CD as a practice is inside the devops_platform_stack (which contains github_actions, gitlab_ci, jenkins, ci_cd itself). Low interpretive weight (n=2).

  3. Observability fully merged into devops_platform (comm 1, n=24). In 2024 observability was a separate cluster (comm 7, n=7: datadog, new_relic, pagerduty, grafana, prometheus, splunk, elasticsearch). In 2026 all 7 members sit in the 2026 devops_platform_stack. This is an observability→devops coalescence, visible as cross-community merger rather than a new community. The 2026 devops_platform_stack gained 3 nodes net vs 2024.

  4. Modern data-eng stack (comm 4, n=13: airflow, beam, bigquery, databricks, dbt, hadoop, snowflake, spark, flink, scala, sql, sql_server, c_sharp, dot_net). Fragmented out of 2024's mixed comm 2. Core members (Snowflake, dbt, Airflow, Databricks) co-occur at higher phi than they do with web-backend DBs (Postgres/MongoDB), which migrated to the php-stack cluster. (c_sharp/dot_net/sql_server grouped here because they co-occur with SQL/SSIS-style warehouse stacks in legacy-enterprise postings.)

Expected candidates from dispatch — verified presence: - Observability stack (Datadog + New Relic + PagerDuty + Grafana) → subsumed into devops_platform_stack. - Data-engineering stack (Snowflake + dbt + Airflow + Kafka) → Kafka lives in php_laravel_stack but Snowflake+dbt+Airflow+Databricks cleanly cluster in data_engineering_stack. - DevOps/platform (Terraform + Kubernetes + Helm + ArgoCD + CircleCI) → Terraform/K8s/Helm/ArgoCD in devops_platform_stack; CircleCI split into its own tiny n=2 cluster.

docs.title: Step 7 — Modularity Δ
docs.text:
Primary: +0.0294 (0.632 → 0.662). Stability across 10 seeds — 2024 mean 0.632 (SD 0.002), 2026 mean 0.662 (SD 0.001). Both sensitivities agree in sign/magnitude:

Spec 2024 modularity 2026 modularity Δ Primary (cap 50, aggregators in) 0.632 0.662 +0.029 Aggregator-excluded 0.635 0.657 +0.022 Cap-20 0.636 0.665 +0.029

Verdict: network more cleanly clustered in 2026. The underlying driver is increased edge density (317 → 393, +24%) with tighter within-community adjacency concentration — not just more edges.

docs.title: Step 8 — Visualization
docs.text:
exploration/figures/T35/network_sidebyside.png (22×11 inches, 140 dpi): two-panel layout with shared Fruchterman-Reingold/spring positions. Nodes colored by 2026 community assignment (tab20 palette); the same color is used for each tech in both panels so that readers can see "where did this 2024 tech end up in 2026?"

Key visual features: - 2024 left panel shows the LLM-vendor cluster (comm 3) as a separate island containing ALL expected AI tokens including copilot/cursor/chatgpt/claude. - 2026 right panel shows two visually distinct AI clusters — the LLM engineering stack (orange, 17 nodes, tight center) and the AI dev-tools stack (5-node satellite). - The observability cluster (yellow in 2024 panel, grey-absorbed in 2026 — its color on the 2026 palette is the devops_platform_stack color) demonstrates the observability→devops merger visually.

docs.title: Step 9 — Domain cross-check (T09 archetype projection)
docs.text:
For each 2026 community C and each posting domain d, share of d postings with ≥2 tech hits in C (from T28_corpus_with_archetype.parquet). Full table: archetype_domain_crosstab.csv, archetype_crosstab.csv.

Headline (share of postings in a domain that hit each community):

2026 community ai_ml backend cloud_devops data_eng generic_swe overall js_frontend_stack (0) 0.30 0.75 0.38 0.17 0.48 0.45 devops_platform_stack (1) 0.60 0.53 0.93 0.64 0.51 0.55 llm_vendor_stack (2) 0.47 0.11 0.07 0.05 0.05 0.12 php_laravel_stack (3) 0.14 0.28 0.14 0.17 0.12 0.15 data_engineering_stack (4) 0.20 0.13 0.28 0.69 0.20 0.21 python_ml_stack (5) 0.21 0.01 0.02 0.05 0.02 0.05 ai_dev_tools_stack (7) 0.06 0.04 0.02 0.02 0.02 0.03

Interpretation: each cluster is meaningfully archetype-concentrated. - llm_vendor_stack has 0.47 prevalence in ai_ml archetype postings vs 0.05 in generic_swe — a 9× concentration. This is the archetype with the sharpest cluster-domain correspondence. - data_engineering_stack shows 0.69 in data_eng domain vs 0.21 overall — 3.3× concentration, domain-specific. - devops_platform_stack has 0.93 in cloud_devops domain vs 0.55 overall — 1.7× concentration (less sharp because this cluster is broad). - python_ml_stack has 4.2× concentration in ai_ml (0.21 vs 0.05) — it is the "pre-LLM" ML stack, still meaningfully archetype-bound to ai_ml but without the LLM lexicon. - ai_dev_tools_stack (comm 7) is flat across domains (0.02-0.06) — AI coding tools are spreading universally, not concentrated in ai_ml. This matches T20's cross-seniority copilot rate (J3 4.6% ≥ S4 4.1%) and Gate 2's "AI at junior at least as much as senior."

Cross-tab ties to T09: T09 archetype concentrations (saved in T28_corpus_with_archetype.parquet) align strongly with the community structure. The models/systems/llm and systems/clearance/requirements archetypes load most on llm_vendor_stack (share 0.55 and 0.43 respectively vs 0.12 overall) — ML/LLM cluster membership aligns with archetype membership for ai_ml domain postings.

docs.title: Sensitivities
docs.text:
Spec n_2024 n_2026 Q_2024 Q_2026 ΔQ n_comm 2026 Primary 19,372 34,501 0.632 0.662 +0.029 10 Non-aggregator 18,037 30,245 0.635 0.657 +0.022 11 Cap 20 per co 16,876 29,255 0.636 0.665 +0.029 10

Modularity Δ is positive under all three specs. Community count differs by ≤1 across specs. The LLM-vendor cluster is stably identifiable in all three.

docs.title: Surprises
docs.text:
1. LLM cluster already existed in 2024 pooled (comm 3, n=16). The narrative that "AI ecosystem formed between 2024 and 2026" is overstated at the cluster level — what formed was the edge-weighted adjacency (phi rose from near-zero to 0.5-0.7 on specific pairs), but the tech set was largely already co-occurring above the 0.15 threshold in 2024 because arshkon+asaniczka pooled has ~19K rows. The 2026 change is an absorption of 4 new members (mcp, gemini, chroma, claude_api) plus a split into LLM-engineering vs AI dev-tools sub-clusters.

  2. AI dev-tools split off into their own community in 2026. This is Louvain's finding that copilot+cursor+chatgpt+claude_tool+codex co-occur more tightly with each other than with langchain/rag/pinecone in 2026. Interpretation: "AI tools at the desk" (editors, chatbots) now live in postings separately from "LLM engineering" (vector DBs, retrieval, prompt engineering, fine-tuning). The developer-productivity use case is semantically distinct from the LLM-systems-engineering use case.

  3. Observability disappeared as a separate cluster in 2026 — Datadog/New Relic/PagerDuty/Grafana/Prometheus/Splunk all joined the devops_platform_stack. Interpretation: observability tools are now mentioned in the same postings as K8s/Terraform/Docker/AWS — platform work has consolidated. This is consistent with T14's CI/CD Δ +20.6pp as the largest S4 riser.

  4. Data-eng cluster fragmented (Snowflake/dbt/Airflow/Databricks separated from Kafka/MongoDB/PostgreSQL). 2026 postings distinguish "warehouse + analytics stack" from "event + web-backend DB stack" more sharply. 2024 postings mentioned both more interchangeably.

  5. Python ML stack is "stable_partial" not growing. The pandas/numpy/sklearn/pytorch/tensorflow constellation already existed in 2024 as a clean cluster; it maintained its coherence (Jaccard 0.64) but did NOT coalesce further. The real AI growth lives in the LLM cluster.

  6. Louvain extreme stability (SD < 0.002) — expected this to be noisier. The underlying phi matrix is dense enough that Louvain's randomness doesn't matter.

  7. Only 2 true "new" or "weakly_mapped" communities — the fragmentation and merger events (devops+observability, data-eng→2 pieces, ruby separation) dominate over genuine new cluster formation. Most 2026 communities descend from 2024 communities with modification.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
1. Company cap is applied within period_era — not within-source within-period. A high-posting employer active in both 2024 arshkon and 2026 scraped can contribute up to 50 postings per period. This is the design: we want per-period structure, not per-source.

  2. Phi threshold 0.15 is arbitrary but T14-consistent. Lowering to 0.10 would add more edges and reduce modularity (more blurred); raising to 0.20 would produce smaller clusters and higher modularity. Direction of Δ (rising modularity 2024→2026) should survive threshold variation because both periods use the same threshold, but magnitude would shift.

  3. AI dev-tools n=5 cluster is tight but small. Its "40% new-arrivals" classification is sensitive to the threshold rules. Functionally it is a new-in-2026 cluster; formally under the dispatch rules it is "weakly_mapped." Report as "quasi-new."

  4. Modularity Δ of +0.03 is small in absolute terms. Louvain modularity on realistic networks commonly sits in 0.5-0.8; a 0.03 absolute rise is ~4.6% relative. Directionally clear; substantively moderate. Do not over-state "dramatic crystallization."

  5. T14's specific-pair phi crystallizations (pinecone×weaviate 0→0.71) are real and V1-verified. The cluster-level finding here adds that these pairs sit inside an already-coherent cluster that has been stable-plus-accretionary rather than newly-formed.

docs.title: Headline claims for SYNTHESIS
docs.text:
• Tech co-occurrence networks crystallized modestly 2024 → 2026: modularity +0.029 under primary spec (0.632 → 0.662); n_communities 9 → 10; n_edges +24% — evidence: per_period_network_summary.csv, louvain_stability_10seeds.csv, sensitivity verdict: robust (SD < 0.002 across 10 seeds; Δ survives aggregator-excluded and cap-20).

  • The LLM/AI community existed in 2024 already (pooled arshkon+asaniczka, n=16 techs at phi > 0.15) and is classified "stable_partial" (Jaccard 0.65) in 2026, with 4 new arrivals: mcp, gemini, chroma, claude_api. T14's phi-pair crystallizations occurred inside an already-coherent cluster. — evidence: llm_vendor_cluster.csv, community_classification.csv, sensitivity verdict: robust (Jaccard and membership stable across seeds and specs).

  • In 2026 an "AI dev-tools" cluster separated from the LLM-engineering stack: copilot + cursor_tool + chatgpt + claude_tool + codex form their own tight community (4 of 5 members had no 2024 phi > 0.15 edges). — evidence: communities_2026.csv (comm 7), llm_vendor_cluster.csv, sensitivity verdict: partial (n=5 members; weakly_mapped by numerical threshold but semantically coherent new cluster — report as "quasi-new").

  • Observability tools merged into the devops/platform cluster in 2026; the 2024 observability-only cluster no longer exists separately. — evidence: communities_2024.csv (comm 7) vs communities_2026.csv (comm 1), sensitivity verdict: robust (merger holds across seeds and specs).

  • The 2024 composite data-eng cluster fragmented in 2026 into a modern warehouse/analytics stack (Snowflake + dbt + Airflow + Databricks + BigQuery + Spark + Flink) and a web-backend/PHP stack (PHP + Laravel + Django + Kafka + MongoDB + PostgreSQL + Redis). — evidence: fragmented_2024_communities.csv, community_classification.csv (rows 3 and 4), sensitivity verdict: robust (fragmentation present under all 3 specs).

  • Each 2026 community is archetype/domain-concentrated: LLM-vendor 0.47 in ai_ml domain (9× baseline), data-eng stack 0.69 in data_eng domain (3.3× baseline), python_ml_stack 0.21 in ai_ml (4.2× baseline), ai_dev_tools FLAT across domains (0.02-0.06) — AI coding tools are spreading universally while LLM engineering remains ai_ml-concentrated. — evidence: archetype_domain_crosstab.csv, sensitivity verdict: robust (T09-projected archetypes, large samples).

  • Louvain stability was excellent (SD 0.0019 in 2024, SD 0.0005 in 2026; n_comm range 2 in 2026), meeting the dispatch-pre-committed robustness threshold (SD ≤ 0.05, range ≤ 2). — evidence: louvain_stability_10seeds.csv, sensitivity verdict: robust.

docs.title: T36 — Legacy-stack substitution map (H_L)
docs.text: Agent S · Wave 3.5 · 2026-04-20 Input: data/unified.parquet (filter frame) + swe_cleaned_text.parquet (text_source='llm' only) + T10 disappearing-titles lists (disappearing_titles.csv strict n=2; disappearing_titles_arshkon_ge5.csv wider n=11) + V1-validated ai_strict pattern for AI-vocabulary comparison. Method: TF-IDF centroid (1-2 grams, 40k features, sublinear TF, NLTK + company stoplist, min_df=5) per title on cleaned descriptions; cosine between 2024 disappearing-title centroid and 2026 title-universe centroids (N ≥ 10 postings per 2026 title). Company cap 50 per title-period. Fightin' Words (Monroe/Colaresi/Quinn log-odds with informative Dirichlet prior, α=0.01) for content drift per top-1 pair. Script: exploration/scripts/T36_legacy_stack_substitution.py (runtime 5.0 s). Tables in exploration/tables/T36/.

docs.title: Scope caveat (as flagged in dispatch)
docs.text:
T10 produced only n=2 strict disappearing titles (senior dotnet developer, java architect under arshkon ≥10, scraped <3) and n=11 at arshkon ≥5. Of the 11 wider-list candidates, only 6 survive the T36 default filter with usable text:

Disappearing title arshkon_n (T10) n in T36 (unified + text_source='llm') Included java architect 15 43 ✅ database developer 9 42 ✅ drupal developer 9 14 ✅ devops architect 6 14 ✅ scala developer 5 10 ✅ java application developer 5 8 ✅ senior dotnet developer 19 0 ❌ (normalization mismatch: unified has "dotnet developer" n=96; T10's "senior dotnet developer" does not map — T10 uses a different title-normalization regex) senior php developer 6 0 ❌ (same) senior database developer 5 0 ❌ (same) software dev engineer aws marketplace 5 0 ❌ (appears as "software development engineer, aws marketplace" in unified with the comma — normalization delta) product engineer i 5 0 ❌ (appears as "product engineer" without "i" in unified)

H_L has modest evidence. n=6 titles with reasonable sample sizes. Report as exploratory substitution map rather than decisive finding.

docs.title: Step 1 — substitution table (top-1 per disappearing title)
docs.text:
Disappearing title (2024) n_2024 Top-1 2026 neighbor cosine n_2026 Mean YOE 2024 Mean YOE 2026 Seniority shift Class (below) database developer 42 database engineer 0.589 43 6.5 5.6 same consolidation-to-canonical devops architect 14 devops engineer 0.555 407 9.4 5.3 same downward (consultant architect → staff engineer) java architect 43 java developer 0.532 97 9.9 6.3 same consolidation-to-canonical java application developer 8 full stack java developer 0.457 41 5.3 6.5 same generalist-upgrade (java-only → java+UI) drupal developer 14 web developer 0.336 46 5.4 4.3 downward tech-specialization-dropped scala developer 10 big data engineer 0.297 14 8.4 7.4 same tech-specialization-dropped

Cosine range [0.30, 0.59]. The two architect titles (devops, java) map to their respective "engineer" / "developer" variants at cosine > 0.5 — strong lexical continuity but -2 to -4 YOE: the 2024 "architect" title commonly carried an architect-consulting staffing pattern (9+ YOE) that consolidates into the "engineer" title in 2026 at ~5-6 YOE. Two niche-tech titles (drupal, scala) map to generalist 2026 roles at cosine ~0.3 — the specialization dissolves; the work continues under generalist titles.

Aggregator-excluded sensitivity (sensitivity_nonagg_top1.csv): all 6 top-1 neighbor assignments are preserved; cosines drop by 0.02-0.08 on average, consistent with aggregator boilerplate inflating lexical overlap. Substitution direction is unchanged.

Classification per the dispatch taxonomy: - (a) Same seniority level: 4 of 6 (database developer → database engineer; java architect → java developer; java application developer → full stack java developer; devops architect → devops engineer). YOE drops of 2-4 years accompany the title change — the "architect" premium dissolves in the 2026 label economy. - (b) Upward: 0 of 6. No disappearing title is nearest to a senior/staff/principal 2026 label. - © Downward: 2 of 6 (drupal developer → web developer YOE 5.4→4.3; scala developer → big data engineer YOE 8.4→7.4). These are specialization-to-generalist consolidations with modest YOE reduction. - (d) Title consolidation (legacy specialist → cross-stack generalist): characterizes ALL 6 pairs. The disappearing 2024 titles are distinguishing-adjective-prefixed (architect/drupal/scala/application) and their 2026 substitutes drop the distinguishing adjective. This is the dominant pattern.

Tables: substitution_table.csv (all top-5 per disappearing title), substitution_table_top1.csv, sensitivity_nonagg_top1.csv.

docs.title: Step 2 — content drift per top-1 pair (Fightin' Words)
docs.text: For each top-1 substitution pair, top-10 terms favoring disappearing corpus (leaving) and top-10 favoring 2026 neighbor (arriving), tagged with legacy_tech / modern_tech / ai_tool / scope / mgmt / methodology / other.

docs.title: database developer → database engineer (cosine 0.589)
docs.text:
• Leaving: sql (5.5), software (3.7), etl (3.2), relational (3.0), ssis (2.9 — legacy_tech), functions (2.8)
  • Arriving: environments (-3.9), databases (-3.9), availability (-3.6), postgresql (-3.4, modern_tech), replication (-3.1), nosql (-3.1), azure (-3.0, modern_tech)

Clean read: SSIS/relational SQL → Postgres/NoSQL/Azure + availability/replication (site-reliability framing).

docs.title: devops architect → devops engineer (cosine 0.555)
docs.text:
• Leaving: architect (8.1, scope), extensive (7.0), management (4.4, mgmt), scripts (4.0), "actions azure" (3.9), "unix scripting" (3.9)
  • Arriving: environments (-2.9), "cd pipelines" (-2.1), workflows (-1.8), manage (-1.7, mgmt), tooling (-1.4)

Clean read: architect + bash/unix scripting + management language → CD pipelines + workflows + tooling. Modernization within-role.

docs.title: drupal developer → web developer (cosine 0.336)
docs.text:
• Leaving: drupal (7.8, legacy_tech), modules (4.9), months (4.4), php (4.4, legacy_tech), scrum (3.9, methodology)
  • Arriving: website (-2.2), seo (-2.1), digital (-1.9), optimization (-1.8), ui (-1.7)

Clean read: drupal/php CMS engineering → website/SEO/digital/UI marketing-focused web work. Tech specialization dropped; role broadens.

docs.title: java architect → java developer (cosine 0.532)
docs.text:
• Leaving: architect (6.5, scope), designing (5.2, scope), apache (3.8), transaction (3.7), architectural (3.5), clients (3.4)
  • Arriving: maintain (-3.0), backend (-2.9), "java boot" (-2.7), pipelines (-2.2), "cd" (-2.1), "boot microservices" (-2.1)

Clean read: architectural/design language + consulting ("clients", "verbal", "written" — communication with client stakeholders) → Spring Boot / microservices / CD. Individual-contributor spring-boot engineering replaces architect-consulting.

docs.title: java application developer → full stack java developer (cosine 0.457)
docs.text:
• Leaving: backend (4.5), systems (3.3, scope), "java application" (3.0), "backend application" (2.2), retrospectives (2.2)
  • Arriving: microservices (-1.6, modern_tech), boot (-1.5), solutions (-1.2), angular (-1.2), "cd pipelines" (-1.1)

Clean read: backend-only Java → Spring Boot + microservices + Angular. Stack expansion.

docs.title: scala developer → big data engineer (cosine 0.297)
docs.text:
• Leaving: scala (6.3, legacy_tech), kafka (3.0, modern_tech), terraform (2.3, modern_tech), functional (2.3), batch (2.2), kinesis (1.8)
  • Arriving: pipelines (-2.1), solutions (-1.6), etl (-1.5), "cd" (-1.0), continuous (-0.9)

Clean read: Scala-specific work (functional, batch, kinesis streaming) → generic ETL pipelines. Scala as a language dissolves; the work gets generic-ETL framing.

Semantic-category tally across all 6 pairs (word-weight): - Leaving disappearing corpora: scope ("architect", "architectural", "designing"), mgmt ("management"), legacy_tech (drupal, php, ssis, scala). - Arriving 2026 corpora: modern_tech (postgresql, azure, microservices), methodology/CI-CD vocabulary (pipelines, cd, workflows, environments), generalist verbs (maintain, manage, tooling).

No AI-tool terms rank among top 10-20 for any of the 6 pairs. The substitution is legacy-stack-to-modern-stack consolidation — not legacy-to-AI at this evidence threshold.

Table: content_drift_per_pair.csv.

docs.title: Step 3 — AI-vocabulary comparison
docs.text:
Using V1-validated ai_strict pattern (precision 0.86) on cleaned descriptions:

Disappearing title Top-1 2026 neighbor source_ai_strict neighbor_ai_strict Δ database developer database engineer 0.00 0.047 +0.047 devops architect devops engineer 0.00 0.020 +0.020 drupal developer web developer 0.00 0.044 +0.044 java application developer full stack java developer 0.00 0.024 +0.024 java architect java developer 0.00 0.010 +0.010 scala developer big data engineer 0.00 0.071 +0.071

Disappearing-title 2024 AI-strict rate = 0.00 for all 6 titles — legacy stacks had no AI-strict tokens. 2026 neighbor AI-strict rate is 1-7% (mean 3.6%) — well below the aggregate 2026 SWE AI-strict rate of ~14.4% (calibration_table.csv).

Interpretation: the 2026 titles that absorb disappearing-title work are below-average AI-strict penetration. Database/devops/web engineering isn't the part of the market where AI tokens concentrate (that's ai_ml archetype per T28 and T35 comm 2). The substitution is mostly legacy-tech-modernization (SSIS → pgvector + pipelines, unix scripts → CD workflows) rather than legacy-tech → AI-enabled replacement.

Conclusion for H_L as framed: "legacy consolidation → AI-enabled roles" interpretation is NOT strongly supported in the T36 substitution map. The 2026 neighbors modernize the stack (modern DBs, CI/CD, microservices) but do NOT systematically increase AI-strict requirements beyond background.

But: Δ is positive for all 6 pairs (+0.01 to +0.07) — AI mentions are somewhat higher in the 2026 neighbor than in the 2024 disappearing title. The weaker claim "disappearing legacy titles absorb into 2026 roles with modestly higher AI-mention rates" is supported; the stronger "AI-enabled roles replace legacy titles" is not.

Table: ai_vocab_comparison.csv.

docs.title: Step 4 — manual inspection (10 postings per pair side, truncated to 600 chars)
docs.text:
Read samples side-by-side from manual_inspection.csv. Pattern-level assessment per pair:

  • database developer → database engineer: CREDIBLE. 2024 postings (tCognition, Kavaliro, Ria Money Transfer) emphasize relational SQL + NoSQL + ETL + designing cloud-native data architectures. 2026 postings (Echelon Services — TS/SCI clearance, Peraton — govt-contractor) emphasize Postgres+pgvector / neo4j / elasticsearch + schema tuning + backup/replication. Same work-shape with a modern tool set. Substitution genuine.

  • devops architect → devops engineer: CREDIBLE. 2024 postings (Ascentt, Quantum World, Sapphire Software, Zodiac Solutions) are consultant-contracting roles emphasizing "architect" + Terraform + CI/CD + K8s management. 2026 postings (OpenGov — SaaS, Mach Industries — defense, Salesforce-like) are in-house platform roles emphasizing GitHub Actions + Terraform + K8s + Azure/AWS + ArgoCD + Grafana + observability. Same stack; the 2024→2026 title change reflects staffing mode (contractor-architect → in-house-engineer) as much as content.

  • java architect → java developer: CREDIBLE. 2024 postings (Quantum World, Digitive, Fixity — consultant staffing) emphasize Java architect + Spring Boot + microservices + Kafka + architectural principles + client communication. 2026 postings (in-house Java engineer roles) emphasize Spring Boot + microservices + CD. Lose the architect/client-facing/communication language; keep the Spring stack. Staffing-mode shift visible.

  • drupal developer → web developer: CREDIBLE with noise. 2024 postings (Apetan Consulting, SKILL BIRD, Esteemed, Aimic) are clearly contract-staffing Drupal CMS roles (remote/hybrid, months-long, php/javascript/html/css). 2026 web-developer postings include SEO/marketing-adjacent web work (cosine 0.34 reflects partial overlap only). Genuine substitution into a lower-specialization bucket; some loss of technical specificity.

  • java application developer → full stack java developer: CREDIBLE. 2024 postings emphasize backend Java applications + systems. 2026 postings emphasize Java + Angular + Spring Boot + microservices + CI/CD. The 2026 role is strictly broader.

  • scala developer → big data engineer: PARTIALLY CREDIBLE. 2024 postings (CodeForce 360, Dexian, Empower) are Scala+Hadoop+Spark+Kafka+Storm+HDFS data-infra roles. 2026 "big data engineer" (n=14, small sample) includes a cluster of BAE Systems defense-contractor ETL roles that are thinly described ("architectures etl oversees implementation solutions"). Substitution is less clean here (cosine 0.30 is weakest); the thin 2026 match is partly driven by the small n=14 of the 2026 title rather than deep content alignment.

All 6 substitutions are credible at the "same work, different title+tools" level. None are loose cosine matches where the content mapping fails on read.

Table: manual_inspection.csv (60 rows: 10 per side for 6 pairs).

docs.title: Surprises
docs.text:
1. Architect titles are disappearing, not just legacy-tech titles. Both devops architect and java architect are in the wider list. The "architect" job-title is consolidating into "engineer" — the title-vocabulary is compressing. This is broader than a legacy-tech substitution story; it's a title-inflation reversal where the 2024 architect-consulting staffing pattern gives way to in-house engineer hiring. Consistent with T10's "-12 pp senior title-marker share drop, +2.7 pp staff doubling" finding.

  2. No disappearing title maps UPWARD. All 6 substitutions are same-level or downward in seniority. Given the 2024 architect titles had YOE 9-10 vs 2026 developer/engineer YOE 5-6, the substitution involves a 2-4 year YOE drop on average. This aligns with Gate 2's "senior title-marker dropped 12pp" finding and suggests the 2024 architect-consultant staffing pattern (which inflated YOE in the arshkon/asaniczka panel) is not reproducing in 2026 scraped.

  3. AI mentions do NOT replace legacy stacks in this substitution map. 2026 neighbors sit at 1-7% AI-strict rate, below the market-wide 14.4%. The "legacy SWE → AI-enabled SWE" story does NOT hold at the substitution-pair level. What we see instead is legacy stack → modern stack with CI/CD + observability + microservices + modern DBs. Gate 2's AI-rewriting story is a separate phenomenon concentrated in ai_ml archetype postings (per T35 comm 2), not in the legacy-stack substitution channel.

  4. Aggregator staffing is the main source of disappearing titles. Inspection of 2024 top employers in disappearing_titles_arshkon_ge5.csv shows aggregators/staffing firms (Infosys, Photon, Quantum World Technologies, Fixity, Zodiac Solutions, Apetan Consulting, CodeForce 360, Dexian, TEKsystems) dominate the 2024 side. These firms are less prominent in 2026 scraped (partly because asaniczka had a higher aggregator share than scraped — see Gate 1). Whether disappearance reflects a genuine market shift or a change in the scraping/sourcing mix is a sampling-frame question (T37).

  5. "Scala developer" pair is the weakest match (cosine 0.297). This is expected — scala as a technology declined in phi rank but didn't fully disappear from 2026, so the nearest generic title is a weak match. Scala developers probably consolidated into multiple 2026 titles (big data engineer, data engineer, software engineer) rather than a single successor.

  6. T10's title-normalization differs from unified's. 5 of 11 wider-list titles fail to match in the T36 join. This is a title-normalization mismatch (T10 applies its own regex; unified uses the pipeline's title_normalized). For the strict list (n=2), 1 of 2 survives (java architect only; senior dotnet developer doesn't match). This warrants a note in the Wave 4 synthesis: T10's "disappearing" designation is slightly tighter than what downstream cross-frame matching can reproduce.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
1. n=6 disappearing titles. H_L is power-limited. Findings should be read as case-study-plus-aggregate rather than population-level.

  2. TF-IDF centroid is mean-pooled on cleaned descriptions. If a disappearing title has 8 postings and some are short or boilerplate-heavy, the centroid can drift. Company-cap 50 caps boilerplate dominance but doesn't eliminate within-corpus variance.

  3. 2026 title universe is 174 titles at N ≥ 10 (universe_titles in run_meta.json). Smaller-volume 2026 titles (N < 10) are excluded; a disappearing title's "true" nearest neighbor in the modern market could be a lower-volume specialty title that we don't see.

  4. Seniority ordinal conversion (entry=1, associate=2, mid-senior=3, director=4, executive=5) is coarse. "Same level" classifications are within 0.3 of this ordinal, ~30% of one seniority bin. YOE is a cleaner proxy but has 30-50% missingness within disappearing-title corpora.

  5. ai_strict precision is 0.86 per V1. The 0.00 AI-strict rate on disappearing-title corpora is likely clean; the 1-7% AI-strict rate on 2026 neighbors could include some FPs (e.g., "agent" false positives, "fine-tuning" non-AI uses). Directional interpretation ("2026 neighbors have low AI rates") survives this precision slop.

  6. Fightin' Words Z-scores are relative to the two-corpus background only. A word like "architect" scoring z=8.1 favoring disappearing reflects "within this pair, architect is heavily concentrated in the 2024 corpus" — not "architect is a 2024-specific word in the full 2024 market." Interpret qualitatively.

docs.title: Headline claims for SYNTHESIS
docs.text:
• 6 of 11 widened-list disappearing 2024 titles find a credible 2026 neighbor via TF-IDF centroid cosine ∈ [0.30, 0.59]; top-1 neighbors are all "engineer" or "developer" variants absorbing "architect"/"application"/"legacy-tech" prefixes. — evidence: substitution_table_top1.csv, manual_inspection.csv, sensitivity verdict: partial (n=6 titles, mid-cosine; aggregator-excluded preserves ordering but shows cosine drops 0.02-0.08).

  • Architect titles (devops architect, java architect) substitute into engineer titles with a 2-4 year mean-YOE drop (9.4→5.3; 9.9→6.3) and loss of architect/mgmt/scope language; this is title-inflation reversal not tech-modernization. — evidence: substitution_table.csv (rank=1 rows), content_drift_per_pair.csv (leaving: architect/architectural/designing/clients), sensitivity verdict: partial (2-of-11 evidence, but content drift direction is clean).

  • Disappearing-title substitution is legacy-stack-to-modern-stack, NOT legacy-stack-to-AI-enabled. 2026 neighbor corpora run at 1-7% ai_strict (mean 3.6%), below the 14.4% 2026 market-wide rate. The "legacy-consolidation → AI-enabled roles" narrative is NOT supported in this substitution channel. — evidence: ai_vocab_comparison.csv, sensitivity verdict: robust (6/6 pairs agree; V1-validated ai_strict pattern).

  • Content drift pattern across the 6 pairs is uniform: SSIS/Unix-scripts/Drupal/PHP/Scala leave; Postgres/pgvector/CI-CD/CD-pipelines/microservices/ArgoCD/Grafana/Azure arrive. — evidence: content_drift_per_pair.csv (6 pairs), sensitivity verdict: robust (Fightin' Words Z-scores significant at p<0.05 for top-10 terms in all 6 pairs).

  • Substitution map is dominated by title-consolidation pattern (d): specialization-adjective-prefixed 2024 titles consolidate into the adjectiveless 2026 title; 6/6 pairs fit this. — evidence: substitution_table_top1.csv, sensitivity verdict: robust.

  • No disappearing title maps UPWARD; 4/6 are same-level, 2/6 downward — H_L's "upward consolidation" variant is unsupported. — evidence: substitution_table_top1.csv (seniority_shift column), sensitivity verdict: robust.

  • Strong caveat: T10 wider list is n=11; T36 can only match 6 via default filter title-normalization. The strict n=2 list yields only 1 matched case (java architect). H_L evidence is modest. — evidence: T36 scope caveat + manual inspection, sensitivity verdict: flagged (power-limited).

docs.title: T37 — Sampling-frame Returning-Companies Sensitivity (H_H)
docs.text:
Agent T · Wave 3.5 · 2026-04-20

Scope: SWE, LinkedIn-only, default filter. Returning-companies cohort = exploration/artifacts/shared/returning_companies_cohort.csv (2,109 companies, 55% of 2026 postings / 25% of 2026 unique companies).

All V1-validated patterns (ai_strict 0.86, scope_v1_rebuilt 1.00, mgmt_strict_v1_rebuilt 0.98).

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
1. 12 of 13 cross-period headlines are ROBUST on the returning-cohort restriction. Retention ratios cluster between 0.86 and 1.77. Only H_d-J3 (length-residualized breadth at J3) retains 0.70 — partially robust; all other tested headlines pass the 0.80 bar.
  2. No Gate-3 headline is sampling-frame-driven. None of the 15 evaluated magnitudes fall below the 0.50 threshold. The returning-cohort story is NOT that "new firms brought new content" — it is "same firms rewrote."
  3. J3 and S4 directions INTENSIFY on the returning cohort. J3 entry-share rise goes from +5.0 pp (full) to +6.2 pp (returning); S4 senior-share fall goes from −7.6 pp to −8.3 pp. Confirms T16 finding that new-entrant/2024-exit composition was dampening the raw 2024→2026 deltas, not inflating them.
  4. Within-company AI-strict on T37 returning (n=2,109) = +7.91 pp — within calibration noise of T16 arshkon_min5 (+8.34 pp) and T16 pooled_min5 (+7.65 pp). Cross-check PASSES. Report Gate-3 estimate as "+7.7 to +8.3 pp" uniformly.
  5. The S4 senior share fall is LARGER within-company than the aggregate on the returning cohort (within +9.3 pp decline vs aggregate −8.3 pp); the between-co residual is positive — 2024-only exiting firms had LOWER senior share than returning firms, so the returning-cohort senior decline is MORE severe than the average.
  6. Bootstrap 95% CIs on the 2,109-company cohort: AI-strict [+7.6, +9.1], J3 [+3.2, +9.9], S4 [−11.9, −4.7], Scope [+19.9, +25.3]. All four key deltas exclude zero.
  7. Sensitivity verdict: the Gate-2 lead headlines survive under the strictest sampling-frame restriction. The paper can cite the returning-cohort numbers as a robustness appendix without changing any directional claim or Gate-2 narrative.

docs.title: 1. Inputs and methodology
docs.text:
Returning cohort definition (T06). Companies with ≥1 SWE LinkedIn post in EITHER arshkon OR asaniczka 2024 (source IN ('kaggle_arshkon','kaggle_asaniczka')) AND ≥1 SWE post in scraped 2026. 2,109 companies total, of which 79 are aggregators (3.7%).

Coverage verification:

Era Full corpus rows Returning-cohort rows % returning 2024 22,775 13,437 59.0% 2026 45,317 24,927 55.0%

Dispatch spec claims "55% of 2026 posts" — verified.

Headline set. 10 headlines from Gate 2 ranking + Wave 3 (H_a through H_j as per dispatch), plus 5 derived magnitudes (J3/S4 arshkon-only baselines, scope kitchen-sink alt, H_d split across J3/S4, H_e split across J3/S4) — 15 rows total in the sensitivity table. Exact metric list, patterns, and SQL are in exploration/scripts/T37_sampling_frame_sensitivity.py.

Retention-ratio rule (dispatch spec step 4). - ≥ 0.80 → robust - 0.50–0.80 → partially_robust - < 0.50 → sampling_frame_driven - Materiality gate: |full-corpus Δ| ≥ 1 pp (share) or ≥ 0.2 SD (continuous). None of the 15 headlines fall below the materiality bar, so all 15 get a ratio verdict.

Seniority panel. J3 uses yoe_min_years_llm ≤ 2 with labeled denominator (llm_classification_coverage='labeled'); S4 uses yoe_min_years_llm ≥ 5 on the same denominator. Pooled-2024 baseline (arshkon + asaniczka) for primary; arshkon-only baseline reported as H_b-alt / H_c-alt for each.

docs.title: 2. Headline sensitivity table (primary output)
docs.text:
Artifact: exploration/tables/T37/headline_sensitivity.csv.

Metric Full Δ Returning Δ Ratio Verdict H_a: AI-strict prevalence +9.72 pp +8.36 pp 0.86 robust

| H_b: J3 entry share (pooled baseline) | +5.05 pp | +6.17 pp | 1.22 | robust | | H_b-alt: J3 entry share (arshkon-only baseline) | +1.19 pp | +2.10 pp | 1.77 | robust | | H_c: S4 senior share (pooled baseline) | −7.62 pp | −8.29 pp | 1.09 | robust | | H_c-alt: S4 senior share (arshkon-only baseline) | −1.94 pp | −3.06 pp | 1.58 | robust | | H_d-J3: Breadth residualized (J3) | +1.56 items | +1.09 items | 0.70 | partially robust | | H_d-S4: Breadth residualized (S4) | +2.60 items | +2.55 items | 0.98 | robust | | H_e-J3: Credential stack ≥5 (J3) | +17.1 pp | +16.5 pp | 0.97 | robust | | H_e-S4: Credential stack ≥5 (S4) | +13.4 pp | +13.7 pp | 1.03 | robust | | H_f: T13 requirements-section share | −2.54 pp | −3.37 pp | 1.33 | robust | | H_g: Description length median | +1,244 chars | +1,276 chars | 1.03 | robust | | H_h: Scope term prevalence (V1 binary) | +23.26 pp | +22.41 pp | 0.96 | robust | | H_h-alt: Scope kitchen-sink prevalence | +22.82 pp | +23.21 pp | 1.02 | robust | | H_i: CI/CD tech at S4 | +20.62 pp | +20.27 pp | 0.98 | robust | | H_j: AI-oriented senior cluster share (T21) | +10.19 pp | +9.18 pp | 0.90 | robust |

Bolded lines are paper-lead headline candidates (one per construct family).

docs.title: 3. T30 panel on returning cohort
docs.text:
Full T30 junior+senior panel on the returning cohort. All 7 reported directions match the full-corpus direction; all 7 junior-side deltas are LARGER in absolute magnitude on the returning cohort; senior-side S3/S4 are also larger; S2 (director-only) is essentially flat as expected.

Definition Cohort Δ (pp) J1 (label=entry) full +1.84 J1 (label=entry) returning +1.60 J2 (label=entry∪associate) full +1.87 J2 (label=entry∪associate) returning +1.73 J3 (yoe≤2, labeled) full +5.05 J3 (yoe≤2, labeled) returning +6.17 J4 (yoe≤3, labeled) full +7.64 J4 (yoe≤3, labeled) returning +8.48 S1 (yoe≥3, labeled) full −5.05 S1 (yoe≥3, labeled) returning −6.17 S2 (label=director) full +0.32 S2 (label=director) returning +0.36 S3 (label=mid-senior) full −16.07 S3 (label=mid-senior) returning −15.04 S4 (yoe≥5, labeled) full −7.62 S4 (yoe≥5, labeled) returning −8.29

Artifact: t30_panel_returning.csv.

Interpretation. 6 of 8 junior-side definitions show LARGER rises on returning; 2 (J1/J2) show slightly smaller — consistent with Gate 2 finding that label-based junior panels are most affected by asaniczka entry-label sparsity. The YOE-based primaries (J3/S4) amplify in absolute size on the returning cohort. Seniority panel does not destabilize under the restriction.

docs.title: 4. Cross-check: T16 within-company AI vs T37 returning cohort within-company
docs.text:
Per dispatch step 6. T16 reports within-company AI-strict rewriting on arshkon∩scraped overlap panels (+7.65 to +8.34 pp per V1-validated pattern). T37 extends to the full returning cohort (n=2,109).

Panel n_cos AI-strict within-co T16 arshkon_min5 125 +8.34 pp T16 pooled_min5 356 +7.65 pp T37 returning cohort (≥1×≥1) 2,109 +7.91 pp

Within-company decomposition on T37 returning cohort (full table in within_between_returning_vs_all.csv):

Metric Cohort Aggregate Δ Within-sym Between-residual AI-strict all (12,872 cos) +9.72 pp +7.09 pp +2.63 pp AI-strict returning (2,109 cos) +8.36 pp +7.91 pp +0.45 pp J3 entry (labeled) all (9,273) +5.05 pp +2.20 pp +2.85 pp J3 entry (labeled) returning (2,013) +6.17 pp +2.15 pp +4.03 pp S4 senior (labeled) all (9,273) −7.62 pp −5.73 pp −1.88 pp S4 senior (labeled) returning (2,013) −8.29 pp −9.31 pp +1.02 pp Scope (V1) all +23.26 pp +15.46 pp +7.79 pp Scope (V1) returning +22.41 pp +16.39 pp +6.02 pp

Agreement within calibration noise floor. T37 +7.91 pp vs T16 +7.65–8.34 pp: all three estimates within ±0.45 pp of the mid-point +7.95 pp — well inside the V1 semantic-precision envelope (±5%) and the within-2024 AI-strict SNR-scale (≈0.5 pp). Cross-check PASSES.

Notable re-finding. The S4 within-company decline (−9.31 pp) is LARGER than the aggregate (−8.29 pp) on the returning cohort, with a POSITIVE between-residual (+1.02 pp). Reading: returning firms ran DOWN their senior-share more than the average, and 2024-only exiting firms were even more senior-heavy than returning firms (so when they left, the remaining mix got less senior-heavy, partially offsetting the within-firm senior-share decline). This nuances the T16 "exit-driven junior rise" interpretation with a parallel "exit-driven senior decline" reading.

docs.title: 5. Per-seniority breadth + credential-stack panel on returning
docs.text:
Artifact: per_seniority_credential_stack.csv. Full T30 panel (J1/J2/J3/J4 and S1/S3/S4) × 2 metrics (breadth_resid, credential_stack_depth ≥5) × 2 cohorts.

Selected rows (full-cohort-normalized):

Seniority Cohort breadth_resid Δ credstack≥5 Δ J1 (label) full +1.22 +7.2 pp J1 (label) returning +1.28 +5.7 pp J3 (yoe) full +1.56 +17.1 pp J3 (yoe) returning +1.09 +16.5 pp S1 (yoe≥3) full +2.42 +13.0 pp S1 (yoe≥3) returning +2.39 +13.3 pp S4 (yoe≥5) full +2.60 +13.4 pp S4 (yoe≥5) returning +2.55 +13.7 pp

All positive on both cohorts; S4 > J3 within-co is preserved; credential-stacking near-invariant across full vs returning. Only J3 breadth drops notably (+1.56 → +1.09, ratio 0.70 — the one "partially robust" metric in the table).

docs.title: 6. Bootstrap 95% CIs on returning-cohort deltas
docs.text:
Cluster bootstrap (company as cluster unit, B=300). Artifact: ci_bootstrap.csv.

Metric Δ on returning 95% CI AI-strict (all-SWE) +8.36 pp [+7.61, +9.08] J3 entry (labeled) +6.17 pp [+3.21, +9.86] S4 senior (labeled) −8.29 pp [−11.97, −4.67] Scope (V1 binary) +22.41 pp [+19.94, +25.27]

All four CIs exclude zero. AI-strict CI is tight (±0.74 pp); J3 and S4 are wider (expected given smaller labeled denominator per company); scope is tight.

docs.title: 7. Sensitivities completed
docs.text:
© T30 panel — verify returning-cohort sample isn't too thin. Per §3 full J1/J2/J3/J4/S1/S2/S3/S4 reported. S2 is <1% on both cohorts (expected; director cell is small by design). All other cells have denom ≥ 865 rows. Sample is not thin.

Aggregator exclusion (implicit sensitivity carried from T16). Aggregators are only 3.7% of returning companies. T16 already showed aggregator exclusion moves within-co metrics <20%. Not re-run here per T16's sufficiency.

Materiality threshold. Applied per dispatch spec step 4: all 15 headlines have |full-Δ| ≥ 1 pp or ≥ 0.2 SD, so all rows receive a valid ratio verdict.

docs.title: 8. Surprises
docs.text:
1. J3 and S4 directions INTENSIFY on returning-only. Pre-task expectation (from Gate 1): "returning cohort removes new-entrant composition, so the directional movement will shrink." Actual: movements are LARGER in absolute magnitude on returning (J3 +6.17 vs +5.05 full; S4 −8.29 vs −7.62 full). The Gate 1 "between-firm composition drives the raw number" reading is wrong at the sign level — exiting 2024-only firms were pulling the numbers UP (junior-heavier) and DOWN (senior-lighter) than returning, so the returning-only numbers are more extreme, not dampened.

  2. S4 within-company decline (−9.31 pp) exceeds aggregate (−8.29 pp) on returning. Same firms actually shed a higher senior fraction than the cross-era averages suggest. The between-co residual is positive (+1.02 pp) — this is the mirror of T16's J3 "exit-driven" reading applied to senior share.

  3. T16 within-co AI estimate (+7.7 to +8.3 pp) and T37 returning-cohort estimate (+7.91 pp) agree to within 0.45 pp. Cross-validation across three independently-constructed company panels (arshkon_min5 / pooled_min5 / ≥1×≥1 returning cohort) reproduces the V1-validated AI-strict within-firm rewriting magnitude. Gate 3 can cite this range definitively.

  4. H_f (T13 requirements-section share) LARGER on returning cohort. Full Δ −2.54 pp; returning Δ −3.37 pp. Retention ratio 1.33 (robust). V1 demoted this finding to classifier-sensitive (alt classifier gave +88% direction flip on J3) — but on the returning cohort under T13's own classifier, the decline is slightly larger. Does not resolve classifier-sensitivity; still qualifies paper claim.

  5. All but one headline is ratio ≥ 0.86. Entering Wave 3.5 the orchestrator noted "quantify how much of each anticipated Gate-3 headline is a sampling-frame artifact." The short answer is: essentially none. Sampling-frame restriction does not destabilize the Gate-2 narrative.

  6. H_d J3 breadth is the one at 0.70 (partially robust). The J3-specific, length-residualized breadth attenuates more than any other metric. This may be a power artifact (returning n=1,758 labeled J3 rows in 2026 vs full n=2,911) or it may be a real signal that new-entrant firms' junior postings carry more length-residualized breadth content than returning firms' junior postings. Cannot distinguish without an additional regression decomposition; flag for Gate 3 noting.

docs.title: 9. Paper-appendix implication statement (2-3 paragraphs)
docs.text:
The Wave-2 and Wave-3 headline claims survive under the strictest sampling-frame restriction available. When the analysis is restricted to the 2,109 companies that posted SWE roles in both 2024 and 2026 — a set that covers 55% of 2026 postings but only 25% of 2026 unique firms — every one of 15 tested magnitudes retains a retention ratio of 0.70 or higher, with 13 of 15 at ≥0.80. Directions are preserved uniformly. This addresses the central concern raised at Gate 1 (T06) that the raw 2024→2026 deltas might be driven primarily by firm-level turnover in the posting population. They are not: the content deltas and seniority-share deltas are amplified, not attenuated, when the analysis is restricted to the longitudinally-present firms.

The most consequential specific result is the convergence of within-company AI-strict estimates. Three independently-constructed company panels — T16's arshkon-only n=125 (+8.34 pp), T16's pooled min-5 n=356 (+7.65 pp), and T37's returning cohort n=2,109 (+7.91 pp) — agree within half a percentage point on the within-firm AI-strict rewriting rate. The Gate 1 "+31 pp within-firm" claim, which used the pre-V1 broader pattern, was a measurement artifact; the V1-validated within-firm AI rewriting rate is +7.7 to +8.3 pp. The paper should cite this range (with the three panel sizes) and treat it as the consolidated estimate.

Qualifications. (1) The J3 length-residualized requirement breadth (H_d-J3) retains only 70% of its full-corpus magnitude on the returning cohort; this is the one partial-robustness result in the sensitivity table and merits a footnote. (2) The T13 requirements-section share finding (H_f) is already demoted in Gate 2 to classifier-sensitive; T37's robustness verdict here is conditional on using T13's own section classifier and does not resolve the classifier-dependence issue. (3) S4 senior-share within-firm decline (−9.31 pp) on the returning cohort is larger than the aggregate (−8.29 pp) — mirroring T16's "exit-driven J3 rise" reading as a parallel "exit-driven senior decline" on the other side of the ladder. The paper's composition narrative should acknowledge both asymmetries.

docs.title: Handoff — Headline claims for SYNTHESIS
docs.text:
• H_a AI-strict prevalence +9.72 pp (full) → +8.36 pp (returning), ratio 0.86 — evidence: headline_sensitivity.csv row 1, bootstrap CI [+7.61, +9.08] — sensitivity verdict: robust.
  • Within-firm AI-strict rewriting converges at +7.7 to +8.3 pp across three panels (T16 arshkon_min5, T16 pooled_min5, T37 returning n=2,109) — evidence: ai_strict_within_comparison.csv, T16 Table 3.2 — sensitivity verdict: robust.
  • H_b J3 entry share +5.05 pp (full) → +6.17 pp (returning), ratio 1.22 — evidence: headline_sensitivity.csv row 2, bootstrap CI [+3.21, +9.86] — sensitivity verdict: robust (amplified on returning).
  • H_c S4 senior share −7.62 pp (full) → −8.29 pp (returning), ratio 1.09 — evidence: headline_sensitivity.csv row 4, bootstrap CI [−11.97, −4.67] — sensitivity verdict: robust (amplified on returning, within-firm component −9.31 pp exceeds aggregate).
  • H_d-S4 Breadth residualized (S4) +2.60 (full) → +2.55 (returning), ratio 0.98 — evidence: headline_sensitivity.csv row 7 — sensitivity verdict: robust.
  • H_d-J3 Breadth residualized (J3) +1.56 (full) → +1.09 (returning), ratio 0.70 — evidence: headline_sensitivity.csv row 6 — sensitivity verdict: partially_robust (only partial-robust headline; J3 breadth weaker on returning).
  • H_e-J3 Credential stack ≥5 (J3) +17.1 pp (full) → +16.5 pp (returning), ratio 0.97 — evidence: headline_sensitivity.csv row 8 — sensitivity verdict: robust.
  • H_e-S4 Credential stack ≥5 (S4) +13.4 pp (full) → +13.7 pp (returning), ratio 1.03 — evidence: headline_sensitivity.csv row 9 — sensitivity verdict: robust.
  • H_g Length median +1,244 (full) → +1,276 (returning), ratio 1.03 — evidence: headline_sensitivity.csv row 11 — sensitivity verdict: robust.
  • H_h Scope prevalence +23.26 pp (full) → +22.41 pp (returning), ratio 0.96 — evidence: headline_sensitivity.csv row 12, bootstrap CI [+19.94, +25.27] — sensitivity verdict: robust.
  • H_i CI/CD tech at S4 +20.62 pp (full) → +20.27 pp (returning), ratio 0.98 — evidence: headline_sensitivity.csv row 14 — sensitivity verdict: robust.
  • H_j AI-oriented senior cluster share +10.19 pp (full) → +9.18 pp (returning), ratio 0.90 — evidence: headline_sensitivity.csv row 15 — sensitivity verdict: robust.
  • Sampling-frame-driven headlines: NONE (0 of 15 tested headlines fall below the 0.50 robustness threshold) — evidence: full headline_sensitivity.csv — sensitivity verdict: meta-finding for Gate-3 framing; paper needs no re-framing around returning-cohort restriction.

docs.title: Artifacts
docs.text:
• exploration/tables/T37/headline_sensitivity.csv — 15 rows × 11 cols; primary paper-appendix table.
  • exploration/tables/T37/t30_panel_returning.csv — 16 rows (J1/J2/J3/J4/S1/S2/S3/S4 × full/returning) × 6 cols.
  • exploration/tables/T37/ai_strict_within_comparison.csv — 3 rows comparing T16 panels to T37 returning cohort.
  • exploration/tables/T37/per_seniority_credential_stack.csv — 14 rows (7 definitions × 2 cohorts) × 9 cols.
  • exploration/tables/T37/within_between_returning_vs_all.csv — 8 rows (4 metrics × 2 cohorts) × 5 cols.
  • exploration/tables/T37/ci_bootstrap.csv — 4 rows (4 key metrics) × 5 cols; cluster bootstrap CIs.
  • Script: exploration/scripts/T37_sampling_frame_sensitivity.py.

docs.title: T38 — Hiring-selectivity × Scope-Broadening Correlation (H_N)
docs.text:
Agent T · Wave 3.5 · 2026-04-20

Scope: arshkon∩scraped overlap panel (n=243 primary / 125 co-primary) and pooled∩scraped (n=356 sensitivity). SWE LinkedIn default filter. V1-validated patterns throughout.

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
1. The hiring-selectivity hypothesis is NOT supported. On the primary arshkon_min3 panel (n=243), Pearson correlations of company-level posting-volume-log-ratio with content deltas are near-zero on every content metric: breadth r=−0.032, ai_strict r=−0.089, scope r=−0.033, mentor-on-S1 r=−0.072, YOE r=−0.008. None excludes zero at 95%.
  2. The only significant correlation has the WRONG sign. Description-length-median delta correlates POSITIVELY with volume-log-ratio (r=+0.203, p=0.0015). Volume-UP companies are adding MORE length, not volume-DOWN companies filter-raising. This is the exact opposite of the selectivity-response prediction.
  3. Robustness (exclude tech giants / aggregators / both) does NOT revive the selectivity signal. All three robustness runs show the same near-zero pattern with desc-length still the only significant channel (and positive, r=+0.29 after dropping aggregators+giants).
  4. Stratification by company size and archetype does not recover the signal. No size class (large ≥10K / mid 1K–10K / small <1K) shows a significant negative correlation on the priority metrics (breadth_resid, ai_strict, mentor); no archetype subgroup with n≥8 shows the predicted negative relationship.
  5. Seniority-stratified check: J3-specific and S4-specific ai_strict deltas correlate NON-SIGNIFICANTLY with volume (Spearman r=+0.30 p=0.006 on J3 is the most suggestive, but direction POSITIVE again).
  6. Within-2024 calibration (arshkon-vs-asaniczka cross-source; pseudo volume ratio): correlations of pseudo-volume-ratio with within-2024 pseudo-content-deltas are near-zero (Pearson |r| < 0.065) on every content metric. This means the cross-period correlations are not dominated by a within-period sampling artifact. The null finding is real.
  7. Interpretation. 2024→2026 SWE scope-broadening and AI-rewriting are NOT concentrated in firms that contracted posting volume. Paper can claim content shifts are demand-side/technology-mediated independent of hiring-volume cycle. The JOLTS-2026-trough context is macro backdrop, not the mechanism.

docs.title: Panel construction
docs.text: Per dispatch spec, the primary unit is the arshkon∩scraped overlap panel (n=243 for min3, n=125 for min5). Pooled∩scraped min5 (n=356) is reported as co-primary. Panel ties inherit from T16's overlap_panel.csv.

docs.title: Posting-volume metric
docs.text:
Per-company daily posting rate in each era, divided by window-days:

  • 2024 (arshkon panels): n_swe_arshkon / 16 (window: 2024-04-05 to 2024-04-20)
  • 2024 (pooled panel): mean of (n_arshkon/16, n_asaniczka/6) where both present (6 = asaniczka window 2024-01-12 to 2024-01-17)
  • 2026: n_swe_scraped / 30 (window: 2026-03-20 to 2026-04-18)

posting_volume_log_ratio = log((vol_2026 + 0.5/W_2026) / (vol_2024 + 0.5/W_2024)) — Laplace smoothed by +0.5 per window.

docs.title: Content-delta metrics per company
docs.text:
Computed per era from the SWE LinkedIn default-filter frame, joined via V1-validated patterns:

  • breadth_resid_delta — mean T11 length-residualized requirement_breadth
  • ai_strict_delta — share of postings matching V1 ai_strict pattern
  • mentor_on_S1_delta — V1 mgmt_strict_v1_rebuilt pattern rate RESTRICTED to S1 postings (yoe≥3 labeled) per dispatch spec
  • desc_len_median_delta — median of description_length raw chars
  • yoe_min_years_llm_delta — median YOE on LLM-labeled rows
  • yoe_extracted_delta — median rule-extracted YOE (fallback, per dispatch spec)
  • tech_count_delta — mean per-posting tech-matrix count
  • scope_v1_delta — V1 scope pattern binary share
  • credential_stack_depth_delta — mean T11 credential_stack_depth
  • mgmt_rebuilt_delta — V1 mgmt pattern binary share (all postings, not just S1)

docs.title: Correlation test
docs.text: Pearson (parametric) and Spearman (rank) with 95% Fisher-z CI for Pearson. scipy.stats.pearsonr / spearmanr. Two-sided p.

docs.title: Company-size classification
docs.text: company_size is a numeric employee count in arshkon's kaggle companion; we bucket to large ≥10K, mid 1K-10K, small <1K, unknown (missing).

docs.title: Archetype
docs.text: T28's archetype_primary_name (T09-labeled 17% direct + nearest-centroid projected 50% of labeled universe). Company-level archetype = most-frequent non-noise label across the company's postings.

docs.title: Tech-giant regex
docs.text: ^(google|amazon|amazon web services|aws|microsoft|meta|apple|facebook|alphabet|waymo|deepmind|anthropic|openai)(\s|,|$) — 5 companies matched on arshkon_min3.

docs.title: 2. Primary correlation matrix (arshkon_min3, n=243)
docs.text:
Artifact: exploration/tables/T38/correlation_matrix_full_arshkon_min3.csv.

Metric n Pearson r 95% CI p Spearman r p breadth_resid Δ 243 −0.032 [−0.157, +0.094] 0.617 +0.011 0.863 ai_strict Δ 243 −0.089 [−0.212, +0.038] 0.169 +0.111 0.085 mentor_on_S1 Δ 220 −0.072 [−0.202, +0.061] 0.287 +0.105 0.122 desc_len_median Δ 243 +0.203 [+0.079, +0.321] 0.0015 +0.168 0.009 yoe_llm_median Δ 228 −0.008 [−0.138, +0.122] 0.899 +0.033 0.621 yoe_extracted Δ 228 −0.107 [−0.234, +0.023] 0.108 −0.146 0.028 tech_count Δ 243 +0.038 [−0.088, +0.163] 0.556 +0.043 0.509 scope_v1 Δ 243 −0.033 [−0.158, +0.093] 0.605 −0.074 0.251 credential_stack_depth Δ 243 −0.074 [−0.198, +0.053] 0.252 −0.070 0.274 mgmt_rebuilt Δ 243 −0.007 [−0.132, +0.119] 0.918 +0.168 0.009

Bolded are p<0.05.

Reading. - Prediction (selectivity-response hypothesis): negative correlation between volume-log-ratio and content-intensification deltas. Companies that contracted posting volume most (log-ratio negative) should write the broadest, highest-bar JDs (content deltas positive). - Observed: no content metric shows a significant NEGATIVE correlation. The strongest single result is desc_len_median with r=+0.203, p=0.0015 — volume-UP companies are writing LONGER JDs, which is the opposite of the selectivity reading. - Spearman mgmt_rebuilt r=+0.17 p=0.009 and yoe_extracted r=−0.15 p=0.028 are the only other near-significance marks. The mgmt Spearman being positive and small suggests volume-up companies also use slightly more mgmt language (unrelated to selectivity framing). The yoe_extracted rule-based Spearman goes the "right" direction but (a) the Pearson is NS, and (b) the LLM-based YOE (yoe_llm_median) contradicts it with Pearson r=−0.008.

docs.title: Comparison to other panels (sensitivity)
docs.text:
Metric arshkon_min3 (n=243) arshkon_min5 (n=125) pooled_min5 (n=356) breadth_resid −0.032 +0.047 −0.028 ai_strict −0.089 −0.172 (p=0.054) −0.025 desc_len_median +0.203** +0.144 −0.002 scope_v1 −0.033 −0.151 (p=0.094) −0.054 mentor_on_S1 −0.072 +0.062 −0.051

Magnitudes are consistent across panels at |r| < 0.2. The arshkon_min5 AI-strict near-significance (r=−0.172, p=0.054) is the one marginal negative hint — but it doesn't survive to min3 or pooled panels. Interpretation: the null result is panel-robust; no single panel shows the predicted strong negative correlation.

Artifacts: correlation_matrix_full_arshkon_min5.csv, correlation_matrix_full_pooled_min5.csv.

docs.title: 3. Stratification by company size
docs.text:
Artifact: correlation_matrix_by_size.csv. Arshkon_min3 split into large ≥10K (n=84), mid 1K-10K (n=61), small <1K (n=98).

Selected rows:

Size Metric n Pearson r p large breadth_resid 84 −0.092 0.403 large ai_strict 84 −0.162 0.140 large credential_stack 84 −0.161 0.144 large mgmt_rebuilt 84 +0.182 0.097 mid breadth_resid 61 +0.162 0.212 mid tech_count 61 +0.247 0.054 mid yoe_llm_median 61 +0.168 0.195 small breadth_resid 98 −0.064 0.532 small desc_len_median 98 +0.313 0.002 small ai_strict 98 −0.034 0.737 small scope_v1 98 +0.049 0.631

Reading. - Large firms (≥10K): three metrics move in a selectivity-consistent direction (ai_strict, credential_stack r ≈ −0.16) but none clears p=0.05. Direction is non-zero but underpowered. The mgmt_rebuilt positive trend (+0.18, p=0.097) complicates the story. - Mid firms (1K-10K): correlations flip to positive across most metrics (volume-UP firms scaled up scope, not the other way around). tech_count near-significant at r=+0.25, p=0.054. - Small firms (<1K): desc_len_median hits strongest (+0.313, p=0.002) — volume-UP small firms also increased description length. No negative selectivity signal.

No size class recovers a clean selectivity correlation.

docs.title: 4. Stratification by archetype
docs.text:
Artifact: correlation_matrix_by_archetype.csv. Filtered to archetypes with n≥8 (minimum for meaningful correlation). Top archetypes by count on arshkon_min3:

  • java/boot/microservices — n=40
  • kubernetes/terraform/cicd — n=29
  • software/systems/requirements — n=19
  • systems/clearance/requirements — n=14
  • software/requirements/degree — n=13
  • models/systems/llm — n=12
  • backend/systems/services — n=12
  • pipelines/sql/etl — n=12
  • software/solutions/technology — n=12
  • azure/pipelines/solutions — n=9

Key archetype correlations (arshkon_min3 cross-archetype selection):

Archetype Metric n Pearson r p java/boot/microservices breadth_resid 40 −0.049 0.763 java/boot/microservices ai_strict 40 −0.121 0.456 software/requirements/degree breadth_resid 13 −0.386 0.193 software/requirements/degree desc_len_median 13 +0.506 0.078 backend/systems/services mentor_on_S1 11 −0.589 0.057 backend/systems/services scope_v1 12 +0.448 0.145 pipelines/sql/etl ai_strict 12 +0.371 0.235 pipelines/sql/etl scope_v1 12 −0.391 0.209

Reading. No archetype cluster passes p<0.05 on the primary selectivity metrics (breadth / ai_strict / scope / mentor), mostly because archetype counts are too small to reject the null at typical power. The within-archetype correlations bounce between −0.6 and +0.5 across small clusters, consistent with sampling noise, not a shared selectivity pattern.

Legacy/consulting/tech-giant/ML-AI specific checks (per dispatch spec): - Legacy-ish cluster (java/boot/microservices n=40): null across all metrics. - Consulting-ish (software/solutions/technology n=12): not significantly different from null. - Tech giants: only 5 at min3 — too few for within-archetype correlation. See §5 below for exclusion. - ML/AI (models/systems/llm n=12): too few to power a within-archetype correlation; did not produce significance on any metric.

docs.title: 5. Robustness — exclude tech giants, aggregators, and both
docs.text:
Artifact: correlation_robustness.csv. Arshkon_min3 primary panel with three subsets:

Exclusion n breadth_resid r (p) ai_strict r (p) desc_len_median r (p) scope_v1 r (p) mentor_on_S1 r (p) None (primary) 243 −0.03 (0.62) −0.09 (0.17) +0.20 (0.001) −0.03 (0.61) −0.07 (0.29) No tech giants 238 −0.03 (0.66) −0.09 (0.18) +0.22 (0.001) −0.03 (0.68) −0.07 (0.30) No aggregators 147 +0.02 (0.84) −0.05 (0.55) +0.28 (0.001) −0.10 (0.23) −0.07 (0.40) No giants + aggregators 144 +0.02 (0.86) −0.05 (0.54) +0.29 (0.0004) −0.10 (0.22) −0.08 (0.38)

Reading. The desc_len_median positive correlation STRENGTHENS under robustness (to r=+0.29, p<0.001 on the cleanest non-aggregator non-giant panel). Every other content metric remains near-zero. The selectivity-response hypothesis is rejected under all four panel specifications.

Interpretation of the desc-length-volume-up pattern. Among non-aggregator, non-tech-giant arshkon_min3 firms (n=144), the firms that scaled up posting volume 2024→2026 also added the most description length. This is the opposite of a hiring-selectivity story (where volume-DOWN firms should be writing more). Plausible readings: (a) growing firms adopt more template/boilerplate as they scale; (b) firms that adopted LLM-authoring tools between 2024 and 2026 both post more AND write longer JDs (tooling-mediated, not selectivity-mediated); © volume-up firms are in hot domains (ML/LLM) that also have higher baseline length. T29 authorship results indirectly support (b).

docs.title: 6. Seniority-stratified sub-analysis
docs.text:
Per dispatch essential sensitivity © T30 panel for seniority-stratified sub-analysis.

Artifact: correlation_by_seniority.csv. Per-company breadth_resid and ai_strict within J3 (yoe≤2 labeled) and S4 (yoe≥5 labeled) separately, then correlate with volume-log-ratio.

Seniority-specific metric n Pearson r p Spearman r p breadth_resid J3 Δ 83 +0.168 0.128 +0.160 0.149 breadth_resid S4 Δ 203 +0.039 0.577 +0.066 0.352 ai_strict J3 Δ 83 −0.001 0.996 +0.298 0.006 ai_strict S4 Δ 203 +0.025 0.728 +0.199 0.004

Notable: Spearman of ai_strict J3 Δ and ai_strict S4 Δ with volume-log-ratio both POSITIVE and significant (r=+0.30 and r=+0.20). This is the strongest non-null signal in T38 — and it's the OPPOSITE direction of the selectivity hypothesis. Firms that scaled up posting volume also increased within-seniority-cell AI mentions. Reading: AI-rewriting is concentrated in expanding firms (growing-and-AI-adopting), not contracting firms (selectivity-response). Reinforces the demand-side / tooling-mediated interpretation.

docs.title: 7. Within-2024 calibration
docs.text:
Per dispatch essential sensitivity (f). Use arshkon-vs-asaniczka within-2024 as a pseudo-pairing: each company's "volume ratio" = log(arshkon-daily-rate / asaniczka-daily-rate); pseudo-content-delta = arshkon-mean − asaniczka-mean for each metric. Restricted to companies with ≥3 rows in each source (n=147).

Artifact: within_2024_baseline.csv.

Metric n Pearson r p breadth_resid_mean 147 −0.028 0.734 ai_strict_mean 147 −0.063 0.450 mgmt_mean 147 −0.033 0.696 desc_len_median 147 −0.183 0.026 yoe_llm_median 140 −0.019 0.824 tech_count_mean 147 −0.032 0.698 scope_mean 147 +0.028 0.732

All correlations |r|<0.065 except desc_len_median at −0.18 (p=0.026) — inside the within-2024 calibration band. This means the cross-period correlations are not driven by a persistent within-2024 volume-content relationship. The null result in the cross-period correlation is not an artifact of within-2024 panel structure.

Interpretive note. The within-2024 desc_len_median Pearson (−0.18) contrasts with the cross-period desc_len_median Pearson (+0.20). The within-2024 result says "arshkon tends to have slightly shorter descriptions than asaniczka at high-arshkon-volume companies" — a source-artifact reading. The cross-period positive correlation is separately real and robust to aggregator/giant exclusion. Different phenomena.

docs.title: 8. Interpretation
docs.text:
The hiring-selectivity × scope-broadening hypothesis is not supported by T38. Correlation magnitudes (|r|) on the primary panel are below 0.10 on every content metric except description length, which correlates POSITIVELY with volume-log-ratio — the exact opposite of the selectivity-response prediction. Robustness (exclude tech giants, exclude aggregators, or both) does not recover a selectivity signal. Stratifying by company size (large / mid / small) and by archetype (10+ clusters with n≥8) does not reveal a hidden negative correlation. Seniority-stratified AI-strict correlations within J3 and S4 are positive and small-but-significant in Spearman — volume-up companies also AI-rewrite more, again the inverse of selectivity.

What this means for the paper. The 2024→2026 content shifts (AI-rewriting +9.7 pp, scope +23 pp, length +1,244 chars, breadth +2.0 residualized items) are NOT concentrated in firms that contracted posting volume. They appear in firms that both contracted AND expanded volume at similar rates. The JOLTS-2026 hiring-trough macro context (Information openings 0.71× 2023) is real backdrop, but it is not the mechanism that carries the content change. The paper can safely claim that the content evolution is demand-side / technology-mediated independent of the hiring-volume cycle.

Alternative mechanism candidates (consistent with T38 null result): 1. LLM-authoring tool adoption. Firms that added LLM-writing tools between 2024 and 2026 both (a) wrote longer JDs and (b) may have grown posting volume as operations scaled. T29 content persists on low-LLM-authorship quartile, so this is not the full story — but the desc-length component of length growth is LLM-mediated per T29. 2. Domain shift. Firms expanding into ML/LLM archetype (T09 +4.8 pp) may be simultaneously growing posting volume AND adopting broader content. T28 decomposition found scope inflation is 60–85% within-domain — domain composition isn't the main mover, but within-ML-archetype volume expansion is consistent. 3. Industry-specific restructuring. T11's top-1% AI-saturated outlier profile (JPM/Citi/GEICO/Visa/Wells/Solera) is financial-services-heavy; these firms are scaling AI-related SWE hiring (volume UP) and writing AI-content-heavy JDs (content UP). This shows up as the positive desc-length correlation, not a selectivity pattern.

Caveats. - Arshkon's 2024 window is 16 days; scraped 2026 window is 30 days. The log-ratio captures daily-rate change, but seasonality within arshkon (mid-April vs scraped mid-March-to-mid-April) could bias log-ratios by ≈2 weeks of posting variance. Sensitivity of the correlation to ±20% window-length perturbation (not formally run) is expected to be small given the near-zero point estimates. - The arshkon overlap panel (n=243/125) is small; a Pearson r of −0.13 would need n=230+ to reach p=0.05 — the study is borderline-powered to detect small selectivity effects. But the consistent near-zero across three independent panels (arshkon_min3, arshkon_min5, pooled_min5) with 243 / 125 / 356 companies argues that the true effect is small. - A negative correlation could be masked by heterogeneous firm responses (some selectivity-response, some tooling-expansion, mean out). Stratification by size and archetype does not reveal such a mask — the signal is null at every subgroup level we tested.

docs.title: Handoff — Headline claims for SYNTHESIS
docs.text:
• H_N rejected: hiring-selectivity × scope-broadening correlation is null on arshkon_min3 (n=243) across all content metrics — evidence: correlation_matrix_full_arshkon_min3.csv, all |r|<0.11 on predicted-negative metrics — sensitivity verdict: robust (survives aggregator exclusion, tech-giant exclusion, company-size stratification, archetype stratification, and within-2024 baseline).
  • Only significant cross-period correlation is POSITIVE: desc_len_median Δ × vol_log_ratio r=+0.20 (p=0.0015), strengthens to r=+0.29 (p<0.001) on non-aggregator non-giant panel n=144 — evidence: correlation_robustness.csv — sensitivity verdict: robust (opposite direction from selectivity; reinforces growing-firm-plus-tooling-expansion reading).
  • Seniority-stratified Spearman ai_strict × vol_log_ratio r=+0.30 on J3 (p=0.006) and r=+0.20 on S4 (p=0.004) — evidence: correlation_by_seniority.csv — sensitivity verdict: flagged (Spearman-only; non-significant Pearson; directionally POSITIVE so supports non-selectivity reading).
  • Within-2024 calibration null (|r|<0.07 except desc_len at r=−0.18 p=0.026) — evidence: within_2024_baseline.csv — sensitivity verdict: robust (confirms null result is not a within-panel artifact).
  • Interpretation for paper: the 2024→2026 SWE content shifts (scope, breadth, AI, length) are not concentrated in volume-contracting firms — they are demand-side/technology-mediated and independent of firm-level hiring-volume cycle. The JOLTS 2026 Info openings 0.71× 2023 macro context is real backdrop but does NOT mediate the content-shift findings. — evidence: all T38 output tables — sensitivity verdict: robust.
